26 "§ 115C-107.3. Child find.
27 (a) The Board shall require an annual census of all children with disabilities residing in
28 the State, subdivided for "identified" and "suspected" children with disabilities, to be taken in
29 each school year. Suspected children are those in the formal process of being evaluated or
30 identified as children with disabilities. The census shall be conducted annually and shall be
31 completed by October 15, submitted to the Governor and General Assembly and made available
32 to the public by January 15 annually. The census submitted to the General Assembly may be a
33 copy of any information or any report submitted to the federal government as part of compliance
34 with the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act pursuant to 20 U.S.C. § 1418.
35 (b) In taking the census, the Board requires the cooperation, participation, and assistance
36 of all local educational agencies. Therefore, each local educational agency shall cooperate and
37 participate with and assist the Board in conducting the census.
38 (c) The census shall include the number of children identified and suspected with
39 disabilities, their age, the nature of their disability, their county or city of residence, their local
40 school administrative unit residence, whether they are being provided special educational or
41 related services and if so by what local educational agency, the identity of each local educational
42 agency having children with disabilities in its care, custody, management, jurisdiction, control,
43 or programs, the number of children with disabilities being served by each local educational
44 agency, and any other information or data that the Board requires. The census shall be of children
45 with disabilities between the ages three through 21 but is not required to include children with
46 disabilities that have graduated from high school."

48 CHANGES TO LITERACY INSTRUCTION
49 SECTION 7.13.(a) G.S. 115C-83.6 reads as rewritten:
50 "§ 115C-83.6. Facilitating early grade reading proficiency.
Page 80 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Kindergarten, first, second, and third Kindergarten through fifth grade students shall
2 be assessed with valid, reliable, formative, and diagnostic reading assessments made available to
3 local school administrative units by the State Board of Education pursuant to
4 G.S. 115C-174.11(a). Difficulty with reading development identified through administration of
5 formative and diagnostic assessments shall be addressed with literacy interventions outlined in
6 the student's Individual Reading Plan. Parents or guardians of first and second grade students
7 offered a reading camp as a literacy intervention shall be encouraged to enroll their student in the
8 reading camp provided by the local school administrative unit. Parents or guardians of a student
9 identified as demonstrating reading comprehension below grade level shall make the final
10 decision regarding a student's reading camp attendance.
11 (a1) Kindergarten through third fifth grade reading assessments shall yield data that can
12 be used with the Education Value-Added Assessment System (EVAAS) to analyze student data
13 to identify root causes for difficulty with reading development and to determine actions to address
14 them.
15 (a2) The Department of Public Instruction shall provide for EVAAS analysis all formative
16 and diagnostic assessment data collected pursuant to this section for kindergarten through third
17 fifth grade. The Department shall use a uniform template for all data collected, and the template
18 shall be used each time data is provided. The template shall include clear designations for each
19 data component reported.
20 (b) Formative and diagnostic assessments and resultant literacy interventions shall
21 address oral language, phonological and phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and
22 comprehension using developmentally appropriate practices. These assessments may be
23 administered by computer or other electronic device.
24 (c) Local school administrative units are encouraged to partner with community
25 organizations, businesses, and other groups to provide volunteers, mentors, or tutors to assist
26 with the provision of literacy interventions that enhance reading development and proficiency."
27 SECTION 7.13.(b) G.S. 115C-83.6B(a) reads as rewritten:
28 "(a) An Individual Reading Plan (IRP) shall be developed for any student in kindergarten
29 through third fifth grade demonstrating difficulty with reading development based on the results
30 of either (i) the first diagnostic or formative assessment of the school year or (ii) the first
31 diagnostic or formative assessment of the second semester of the school year. The IRP shall be
32 continually adjusted based on multiple data sources as prescribed by the Department of Public
33 Instruction, indicating that the student is not progressing toward grade-level standards in one or
34 more major reading areas. Based on the most recently collected data, the IRP shall include the
35 following information, specific to the identified student:
36 (1) The specific reading skill deficiencies identified by assessment data.
37 (2) Goals and benchmarks for growth.
38 (3) The means by which progress will be monitored and evaluated.
39 (4) The specific additional literacy interventions the student will receive.
40 (5) The Science of Reading-based instructional programming the teacher will
41 implement.
42 (6) Any additional services the teacher deems appropriate to accelerate the
43 student's reading skill and development."
44 SECTION 7.13.(c) G.S. 115C-83.9(a) reads as rewritten:
45 "(a) Parents or guardians shall be notified in writing, and in a timely manner, that the
46 student shall be retained, unless he or she is exempt from mandatory retention for good cause, if
47 the student is not demonstrating reading proficiency by the end of third grade. Parents or
48 guardians shall receive this notice when a kindergarten, first, second, or third grade student (i) is
49 demonstrating difficulty with reading development; or (ii) is not reading at grade level.
50 Additionally, parents or guardians shall receive notice when a fourth or fifth grade student is
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 81
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 demonstrating difficulty with reading development or is not reading on grade level as determined

41 FISCAL RESPONSIBILITY AND K-12 TECH PLANNING
42 SECTION 7.14.(a) Part 3A of Article 8 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is
43 amended by adding new sections to read:
44 "§ 115C-102.10. Technology costs considerations.
45 The State Board of Education shall adopt rules requiring all public school units to evaluate
46 the following when acquiring technology, computer hardware, and software:
47 (1) The long-term cost of ownership, including costs of repairing the technology,
48 computer hardware, or software.
49 (2) Any flexibility for innovation during the life of the technology, computer
50 hardware, or software.
Page 82 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (3) Any anticipated resale or salvage value at the end of the target life cycle for
2 the technology, computer hardware, or software based on the average resale
3 or salvage value of similar technology, computer hardware, or software as a
4 percentage of the initial cost of purchase.
5 "§ 115C-102.11 Break/fix rate reporting requirement.
6 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions apply in this section:
7 (1) Break/fix rate. – The percentage obtained by dividing the number of school
8 technology devices reported as malfunctioning or needing repair due to
9 physical damage, hardware failure, or other breakage incidents prior to the
10 stated life cycle period, not covered by insurance or a policy plan period, by
11 the total number of school technology devices in operation during that period.
12 (2) School technology device. – Any electronic or computerized equipment
13 provided for educational purposes in a public school unit, including
14 computers, tablets, interactive whiteboards, and similar devices or anything
15 considered a digital device for purposes of the digital learning dashboard
16 pursuant to G.S. 115C-102.9.
17 (b) Each governing body of a public school unit shall submit a report on the following
18 information to the State Board of Education by August 15 annually:
19 (1) The break/fix rate of the school technology devices in the public school unit
20 for the previous school year.
21 (2) The total number of school technology devices currently in operation in the
22 public school unit.
23 (3) The total number of school technology devices in the public school unit
24 requiring repair that (i) underwent repair or (ii) were no longer in service
25 during the previous school year.
26 (4) The total amount of funds spent to repair or replace school technology devices
27 during the previous school year.
28 (c) The State Board of Education shall report to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight
29 Committee by November 15 annually on the break/fix rate of school technology devices across
30 all public school units based on the reports submitted by the governing bodies in accordance with
31 subsection (b) of this section. This report shall include a summary of the data reported by each
32 governing body and recommendations to reduce break/fix rates in the future."
33 SECTION 7.14.(b) The first reports from governing bodies of public school units
34 required by G.S. 115C-102.11(b), as enacted by this section, shall be submitted no later than
35 August 15, 2026, based on data collected during the 2025-2026 school year. The first report from
36 the State Board of Education required by G.S. 115C-102.11(c), as enacted by this section, shall
37 be submitted no later than November 15, 2026.

7 SECTION 7.14.(f) Part 2 of Article 14A of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is
8 amended by adding a new section to read:
9 "§ 115C-218.33. School technology.
10 (a) A charter school shall adopt a policy requiring the evaluation of technology costs
11 considerations adopted by the State Board of Education pursuant to G.S. 115C-102.10.
12 (b) A charter school shall report annually to the State Board of Education on the break/fix
13 rate of school technology devices used in the school in accordance with G.S. 115C-102.11."

33 AUTOMATIC ENROLLMENT IN ADVANCED ENGLISH LANGUAGE ARTS
34 COURSES
35 SECTION 7.15.(a) G.S. 115C-81.36 reads as rewritten:
36 "§ 115C-81.36. Advanced courses in mathematics.mathematics and English Language
37 Arts.
38 (a) When practicable, local boards of education shall offer advanced learning
39 opportunities in mathematics in grades three through five, and advanced courses in mathematics
40 in all grades six and higher. For the purposes of this section, advanced learning opportunities are
41 those services and curricular modifications in mathematics and English Language Arts for
42 academically or intellectually gifted students approved as part of the local plan, as required by
43 G.S. 115C-150.7.G.S. 115C-150.7, and advanced courses are advanced courses in mathematics
44 and English Language Arts.
45 (a1) When advanced learning opportunities are offered in mathematics in grades three
46 through five, any student scoring at the highest level on the corresponding end-of-grade test shall,
47 for the next school year, be provided advanced learning opportunities in mathematics approved
48 for that student's grade level. No student who qualifies under this subsection shall be removed
49 from the advanced learning opportunity provided to the student unless a parent or guardian of the
50 student provides written consent for the student to be excluded or removed after being adequately
Page 84 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 informed that the student's placement was determined by the student's achievement on the
2 previous end-of-grade test.
3 (b) When advanced courses are offered in mathematics in grades six and higher, any
4 student scoring at the highest level on the corresponding end-of-grade or end-of-course test for
5 the mathematics course in which the student was most recently enrolled shall be enrolled in the
6 advanced course for the next mathematics course in which the student is enrolled. A student in
7 seventh grade scoring at the highest level on the seventh grade mathematics end-of-grade test
8 shall be enrolled in a high school level mathematics course in eighth grade. Local boards of
9 education may provide supplemental content enrichment, which may include the administration
10 of diagnostic assessments, to students enrolled in a high school level mathematics course. No
11 student who qualifies under this subsection shall be removed from the advanced or high school
12 mathematics course in which the student is enrolled unless a parent or guardian of the student
13 provides written consent for the student to be excluded or removed from that course after being
14 adequately informed that the student's placement was determined by the student's achievement
15 on the previous end-of-grade or end-of-course test.
16 (b1) When a high school mathematics course is offered in eighth grade, a student in
17 seventh grade scoring at the highest level on the seventh grade mathematics end-of-grade test
18 shall be enrolled in a high school level mathematics course in eighth grade. Local boards of
19 education may provide supplemental content enrichment, which may include the administration
20 of diagnostic assessments, to students enrolled in a high school level mathematics course.

23 Committee containing data collected for the current school year on the number and demographics
24 number, demographics, and socioeconomic status of students who were eligible for advanced
25 mathematics courses under this section, including high school level mathematics courses in
26 eighth grade, and of those students, the number and demographics number, demographics, and
27 socioeconomic status of those who were placed in advanced mathematics courses and were not
28 placed in advanced mathematics courses. The report shall include information on the type and
29 format of advanced mathematics courses provided and shall also include any feedback provided
30 by local boards of education on the implementation of this section.
31 (d) The Department of Public Instruction shall provide guidance to local boards of
32 education on how to best develop programming and courses to ensure all impacted students
33 receive rigorous, academically appropriate instruction in mathematics.mathematics and English
34 Language Arts.
35 (e) No student who qualifies for advanced learning opportunities or advanced courses
36 under this section shall be removed from the advanced learning opportunity or advanced course
37 provided to the student unless a parent or guardian of the student provides written consent for the
38 student to be excluded or removed after being adequately informed that the student's placement
39 was determined by the student's achievement on the previous end-of-grade or end-of-course test.
40 Local boards of education may provide supplemental content enrichment, which may include the

10 COMPETENCY-BASED HIGH SCHOOL/HEALTHCARE AND HIGH-TECH
11 PATHWAYS PROGRAM
12 SECTION 7.17.(a) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the
13 Competency-Based High School/Healthcare and High-Tech Pathways Program (Program). The
14 purpose of the Program is to create pathways that will utilize competency-based education
15 (CBE). Pathways will result in obtaining either an associate degree or an industry recognized
16 credential, certification, or licensure based on the student's goal of employment or enrollment.
17 Students will decide their college or career track at the end of their junior year with an initial
18 emphasis on healthcare preparation.
19 SECTION 7.17.(b) Participants. – Mooresville Graded School District (MGSD)
20 shall partner with Mitchell Community College (MCC) to implement the Program.
21 SECTION 7.17.(c) Program Time Line. – MGSD, in collaboration with MCC, shall
22 contract with an organization with demonstrated expertise in designing and implementing
23 learner-centered, modular, and competency-based high school programs that align with emerging
24 and high-tech career pathways. The organization shall have experience in co-designing stackable
25 credentials with local education agencies and higher education partners and a documented track
26 record of developing digital infrastructure that supports student-paced progression,
27 interdisciplinary learning, and real-world skill acquisition. This expert partner shall assist in
28 developing a framework for the Program, including course design, sequencing, credentialing
29 structure, and other elements necessary for personalized student progression during the
30 2025-2026 school year. Students will have the opportunity to participate in a pathway provided
31 by the Program by the 2027-2028 school year at the latest.
32 SECTION 7.17.(d) Program Flexibilities. – Notwithstanding any provision of law
33 to the contrary, the following flexibilities shall be available to Program participants:
34 (1) MGSD may offer Credit by Demonstrated Mastery assessments and CTE
35 Proof of Learning assessments outside of existing State testing windows.
36 (2) MCC may enroll MGSD students in community college courses prior to their
37 eleventh grade year without the student meeting the requirements for ninth
38 and tenth grade students pursuant to subdivision (4) of G.S. 115D-20.
39 (3) Students participating in the Program shall have access to all community
40 college courses at MCC, regardless of pathway selection.
41 (4) MGSD may replace any high school graduation credit requirement, except
42 those outlined in this subdivision, with either community college courses
43 offered by MCC or locally developed CBE courses that are consistent with
44 the student's pathway. MGSD shall not replace any graduation credit
45 requirements in the following subject areas:
46 a. English.
47 b. Mathematics.
48 c. Science.
49 d. Social Studies.
Page 86 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (5) MGSD may operate any high school in the local school administrative unit
2 consistent with the flexibilities of a school operating under a restart model
3 pursuant to G.S. 115C-105.37B(a)(2).
4 SECTION 7.17.(e) Selection of Third-Party Vendor. – MGSD shall select a
5 third-party vendor (Vendor) that is an educational support provider with a nationally recognized,
6 research-based instructional and leadership framework, including a High Reliability School
7 model, a taxonomy of educational objectives aligned to classroom strategies, and a system for
8 implementing personalized competency-based education. The provider shall have published
9 extensively in peer-reviewed and practitioner literature, have an established track record of
10 working directly with K-12 schools and districts across multiple states, and offer professional
11 development services, implementation support, and instructional resources developed and led by
12 a founding education researcher with over four decades of influence in curriculum, instruction,
13 and assessment.
14 SECTION 7.17.(f) MGSD Partnership with Vendor. – MGSD shall partner with the
15 Vendor selected pursuant to subsection (e) of this section to develop standards-aligned
16 proficiency scales for all content areas in grades nine through 12 to ensure educators in the
17 participating schools have the tools necessary to successfully transition to CBE approaches. The
18 Vendor shall provide professional development and coaching to the schools in MGSD to ensure
19 educators can engage in learning about CBE approaches, including implementation of the
20 proficiency scales developed by MGSD.
21 SECTION 7.17.(g) Funding. – Of the funds appropriated to the Department of Public
22 Instruction allocated to MGSD pursuant to this section, MGSD shall use the funds for the
23 following purposes:
24 (1) To contract with experts in CBE and designing stackable credentials pursuant
25 to subsection (c) of this section.
26 (2) To hire one additional staff member at MGSD to oversee implementation of
27 the Program.
28 (3) To partner with a Vendor pursuant to subsection (f) of this section.
29
30 LIGHTHOUSE MATH PROJECT
31 SECTION 7.18.(a) Project Established; Purpose. – There is established the
32 Lighthouse Math Project (Project). The purpose of the Project is to increase the percentage of
33 high school students who are (i) eligible to complete college level mathematics upon graduation
34 from high school and (ii) able to complete college level math either through dual enrollment
35 while in high school or within the first year of college enrollment.
36 SECTION 7.18.(b) Project Participants. – Wake Technical Community College
37 (Wake Tech) shall partner with Wake County Public School System (WCPSS) for high school
38 students at East Wake High School and Knightdale High School to be eligible to participate in
39 the Project at the Wake Tech East campus of Wake Tech. Wake Tech and WCPSS shall partner
40 with SparkNC to align the Project participants' ongoing efforts at the Wake Tech East campus
41 with the design and implementation of the Project.
42 SECTION 7.18.(c) Project Commitments. – WCPSS, in collaboration with Wake
43 Tech, shall contract with Khan Academy, Inc. (Khan), for use of the artificial intelligence student
44 tutoring program, Khanmigo. Students enrolling at participating high schools shall have access
45 to Khanmigo from the time of enrollment in the high school and continuing through their
46 attendance at Wake Tech if the student chooses to enroll at Wake Tech East. Wake Tech shall
47 collaborate with Khan to do the following:
48 (1) Develop and deliver at least one Khanmigo-centered gateway college math
49 course to be offered to high school students at participating high schools to be
50 completed as a college level math course. This course shall also be available
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 87
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 to students who graduate from participating high schools and enroll in the
2 courses at Wake Tech East.
3 (2) Develop a Khanmigo-centered developmental math course sequence that will
4 be provided by Wake Tech faculty to students from participating high schools
5 in one of the following formats:
6 a. As part of a Career and College Promise Career Ready Pathway.
7 b. As supplement to a Career and College Promise Pathway.
8 c. As a summer bridge program available to students prior to or
9 subsequent to their junior and senior years of high school.
10 d. As a community college course during the first semester of enrollment
11 at Wake Tech.
12 SECTION 7.18.(d) Modular Math Course. – As part of the Project, Khan, in
13 consultation with Wake Tech and WCPSS, shall work to develop a self-paced, competency-based
14 modular math course with appropriate student diagnostics and student assessments based on the
15 college readiness math competencies provided as part of the Project. It is the intent that students
16 successfully demonstrating college readiness math competencies will be eligible to enroll in
17 college level math courses.
18 SECTION 7.18.(e) Funding; DPI. – Of the funds appropriated from the General
19 Fund to the Department of Public Instruction, the sum of up to five hundred thousand dollars
20 ($500,000) in nonrecurring funds for the 2025-2026 fiscal year shall be used to contract with
21 Khan for licenses for the Khanmigo application and to create the course developed pursuant to
22 subdivision (1) of subsection (c) of this section. Funds appropriated for the purposes laid out in
23 this section shall not revert but shall remain available to accomplish the purposes of this section
24 until the end of the 2026-2027 fiscal year.
25 SECTION 7.18.(f) Funding; NCCCS. – Of the funds appropriated to the Community
26 Colleges System Office for the 2025-2026 fiscal year, the sum of one million dollars
27 ($1,000,000) in nonrecurring funds shall be used to establish time-limited positions at Wake
28 Tech. Two positions shall be assigned to work in each participating high school to assist in the
29 implementation of the courses offered by the Project. Funds appropriated for the purposes laid
30 out in this section shall not revert but shall remain available to accomplish the purposes of this
31 section until the end of the 2028-2029 fiscal year.
32
33 COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION AND HIGH SCHOOL REDESIGN
34 STRATEGIC NETWORK
35 SECTION 7.19. Mooresville Graded School District, Mitchell Community College,
36 Wake Technical Community College, Wake County Public School System, Fayetteville
37 Technical Community College, Wilkes Community College, and SparkNC shall collaborate to
38 create a Competency-Based Education and High School Redesign Strategic Network (Network).
39 The president of SparkNC, or the president's designee, shall serve as the chair of the Network.
40 The chair shall coordinate the operations of the Network and provide administrative and technical
41 support for the Network to the extent such support is necessary. The Network shall provide a
42 means for each entity to share what it has learned and developed regarding CBE and high school
43 redesign. The goals of the Network shall be to advance the goals of developing a CBE approach
44 to education across the State.
45
46 NORTH CAROLINA COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION INNOVATION GRANT
47 PROGRAM
48 SECTION 7.20.(a) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the North
49 Carolina Competency-Based Education Innovation Grant and Network Program (Program). The
50 purpose of the program is to provide funding and support to schools and districts to support the
51 transition to competency-based education (CBE) models that focus on mastery of learning rather
Page 88 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 than instructional time. The grant aims to promote innovative instructional practices, flexible
2 assessment models, and systemic shifts to ensure all students progress based on demonstrated
3 proficiency.
4 SECTION 7.20.(b) Program Objectives. – The objectives of the Program are as
5 follows:
6 (1) Redesign learning pathways. – Support schools and districts in developing
7 personalized, CBE models that allow students to progress at their own pace.
8 (2) Assessment innovation. – Utilize formative and performance-based
9 assessments that measure student mastery of skills and knowledge, including
10 the use of AI learning tools.
11 (3) Educator capacity building. – Provide common professional development for
12 educators on instructional strategies and assessment practices aligned with
13 transitioning to CBE.
14 (4) Develop strategies for statewide scaling and best practices. – Develop and
15 share resources, implementation strategies, and lessons learned to expand
16 CBE across North Carolina.
17 SECTION 7.20.(c) Phase One. – The first phase of the Program shall begin with the
18 2025-2026 school year and continue until the conclusion of the 2028-2029 school year. Public
19 school units shall submit applications and complete any planning or organizational duties during
20 the 2025-2026 school year with implementation of CBE in participating schools beginning with
21 the 2026-2027 school year.
22 SECTION 7.20.(d) Application. – The Department of Public Instruction shall create
23 an application for the Program and make the application available to all public school units no
24 later than 60 days after this act becomes law. Public school units shall submit their applications
25 to the Department no later than 60 days after the application becomes available. The Department
26 shall select 10 schools to participate in the Program no later than 60 days after the close of the
27 application period. When selecting schools to participate in the Program, the Department shall
28 prioritize schools that show a strong commitment to implementing CBE and innovation in
29 assessment. The Department shall develop criteria to determine which schools are most ready to
30 transition to CBE. The application shall include at least the following:
31 (1) A comprehensive plan outlining the school's vision for competency-based
32 education.
33 (2) A description of instructional and assessment shifts to be implemented.
34 (3) A budget for use of grant funds and a sustainability plan to continue utilizing
35 CBE after the conclusion of Phase One.
36 (4) Commitments from public school units leadership and stakeholders.
37 SECTION 7.20.(e) Grant Funds; Use; Training. – Grant awards shall be in the
38 amount of three hundred thirty thousand dollars ($330,000) to each participating school.
39 Participating schools shall use the sum of ninety-five thousand dollars ($95,000) each school
40 year to receive training on the implementation of CBE in the school. The remaining grant funds
41 shall be used for costs associated with the transition to CBE, including travel costs and CBE
42 resources. Grant funds shall remain available until the end of the 2028-2029 fiscal year.
43 SECTION 7.20.(f) Selection of Third-Party Vendor. – The Department shall select
44 a third-party vendor (Vendor) that is an educational support provider with a nationally
45 recognized, research-based instructional and leadership framework, including a High Reliability
46 School model, a taxonomy of educational objectives aligned to classroom strategies, and a system
47 for implementing personalized competency-based education. The provider must have published
48 extensively in peer-reviewed and practitioner literature, have an established track record of
49 working directly with K-12 schools and districts across multiple states, and offer professional
50 development services, implementation support, and instructional resources developed and led by
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 89
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a founding education researcher with over four decades of influence in curriculum, instruction,
2 and assessment.
3 SECTION 7.20.(g) Department Partnership with Vendor. – The Department shall
4 partner with the Vendor selected pursuant to subsection (f) of this section to develop publicly
5 available, standards-aligned proficiency scales for all content areas and grade levels to ensure
6 educators in the participating schools have the tools necessary to successfully transition to CBE
7 approaches. The Vendor shall provide annual professional development events throughout the
8 regions of the State to ensure educators can engage in learning about CBE approaches, including
9 implementation of the proficiency scales developed by the Department.
10 SECTION 7.20.(h) Department Duties. – The Department of Public Instruction shall
11 do the following as part of the Program:
12 (1) Develop, in collaboration with the Vendor, a CBE Network to support the
13 long-term transition to CBE. The CBE Network shall do the following:
14 a. Provide strategic guidance and policy recommendations for scaling
15 CBE statewide.
16 b. Facilitate collaboration among grant recipients, educators,
17 policymakers, and CBE industrial leaders.
18 c. Develop a long-term roadmap for CBE implementation statewide.
19 d. Offer professional learning communities and shared online resources
20 to support ongoing innovation and capacity building.
21 e. Develop methods of measuring proficiency for all courses offered as
22 part of the standard course of study in kindergarten through grade 12,
23 with support from the Vendor.
24 f. Disseminate developed proficiency measures to all public school units
25 and post standards developed to align with the proficiency measures
26 online on a publicly accessible website.
27 g. Conduct research and evaluation to inform best practices and
28 continuous improvement in participating schools.
29 (2) Provide support and technical assistance, professional learning opportunities,
30 and access to the CBE network for all grant recipients.
31 SECTION 7.20.(i) Reporting; Participants. – Each participating school shall submit
32 a quarterly progress report to the Department detailing implementation of CBE in the school. The
33 Department shall establish the due dates and mechanism for the submission of the progress
34 reports. Progress shall include available student learning outcome data, including evidence of
35 progress toward content mastery.
36 SECTION 7.20.(j) Reporting; Department. – The Department of Public Instruction
37 shall submit a final report on the outcomes of Phase One of the Program to the Joint Legislative
38 Education Oversight Committee by December 1, 2029. The report shall include the following:
39 (1) A summary of student outcomes reported by participating schools.
40 (2) Any challenges experienced by participating schools or the Department with
41 implementation of the Program.
42 (3) Recommendations for scaling the Program statewide.
43 (4) Any other information the Department deems relevant.
44 (5) Any information requested by the Committee.
45
46 AI ACADEMIC SUPPORT PROGRAM
47 SECTION 7.21. Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended by adding a new
48 Article to read:
49 "Article 42.
50 "Academic Support Programs.
51 "§ 115C-650. Establish AI Academic Support Program.
Page 90 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Purpose; Program. – There is established the AI Academic Support Program
2 (Program) to allow public school units to contract with Khan Academy, Inc., (Khan Academy)
3 for use of the academic support service Khanmigo, in grades six through 12. Khanmigo is an
4 application that uses artificial intelligence to support teachers with lesson plan development and
5 to support students with lesson comprehension.
6 (b) Funding. – Funds shall be allocated to each public school unit on the basis of average
7 daily membership (ADM) in grades six through 12 in the public school unit for the purposes set
8 out in this section.
9 (c) Funding Use. – Public school units that utilize funds pursuant to this section shall use
10 all funds to contract with Khan Academy for use of the Khanmigo service, any associated
11 professional development, and the rostering programs required pursuant to subsection (e) of this
12 section.
13 (d) Contract Term. – Public school units entering into contracts with Khan Academy shall
14 contract for a term of one year. At the end of each school year, each public school unit shall
15 evaluate the use of Khanmigo services in the public school unit prior to contracting with Khan
16 Academy for the subsequent school year. The evaluation shall include all of the information
17 required to be reported on by the Department of Public Instruction in subdivisions (2) through
18 (7) of subsection (h) of this section. Public school units shall submit their evaluations to the
19 Department of Public Instruction by July 15 of each year that funds are used pursuant to this
20 section.
21 (e) Class Rostering for Khanmigo. – All public school units that contract with Khan
22 Academy pursuant to this section shall use a secure, cloud-based, single sign-on, and class
23 rostering platform to manage access to digital instructional resources and student information
24 systems and to facilitate automated data integration between educational applications and school
25 district systems. Class rostering products used pursuant to this section must be approved by the
26 Department.
27 (f) Juvenile Justice. – The Department shall contract with Khan for 500 Khanmigo
28 licenses and training for use in schools operated by the Division of Juvenile Justice of the
29 Department of Public Safety. The Department of Public Instruction shall provide technical
30 support to the Division of Juvenile Justice for the use of Khanmigo. The Division of Juvenile
31 Justice shall comply with evaluation requirements pursuant to subsection (d) of this section.
32 (g) Study. – The Office of Learning Research at the North Carolina Collaboratory (OLR)
33 shall design and conduct a study to measure the effectiveness of using Khanmigo, including any
34 impact on student performance and growth. As part of the study, the Department of Public
35 Instruction shall aggregate and report to OLR on the evaluations submitted by public school units
36 and the Division of Juvenile Justice pursuant to subsections (d) and (f) of this section. OLR shall
37 collaborate with Khan Academy to aggregate data on student performance by teacher and by
38 subgroup, to the extent allowed by law. OLR shall provide the results of this study to the Joint
39 Legislative Education Oversight Committee no later than April 1, 2028.
40 (h) Report. – No later than February 15 of each year in which funds are made available
41 for the Program, the Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint Legislative
42 Education Oversight Committee at least the following information:
43 (1) The number public school units that contracted with Khan Academy pursuant
44 to this article.
45 (2) The number of students using the Khanmigo application.
46 (3) The average number of minutes and interactions students had with the
47 Khanmigo application weekly.
48 (4) The number of teachers using the application.
49 (5) The average number of minutes and interactions teachers had with the
50 Khanmigo application weekly.
51 (6) Any identified impact on student outcomes.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 91
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (7) Any identified impact on teacher performance.
2 (8) The number of moderation flags generated by the application."

4 SECTION 7.24.(a) G.S. 115C-264 is amended by adding a new subsection to read:
5 "(a1) A local board of education that is operating a school nutrition program shall provide

7 grade levels that qualify for reduced-price meals under the federal National School Lunch
8 Program or School Breakfast Program. If funds from alternate sources are insufficient to provide
9 school meals at no costs to students for students that qualify for reduced-price meals, the
10 Department of Public Instruction may use funds appropriated to the State Aid for Public Schools
11 Fund for this purpose."

40 SCHOOL BUSINESS SYSTEMS MODERNIZATION PLANS
41 SECTION 7.26.(a) For a public school unit to receive an allocation from the
42 nonrecurring funds appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction in this act for the
43 2025-2026 fiscal year for business systems modernization, the Department shall require the
44 public school unit to submit a letter of intent to the Department no later than September 1, 2026.
45 The letter of intent must (i) verify that the public school unit has not previously received funds
46 for business systems modernization, (ii) specify the platform the public school unit intends to
47 use, and (iii) state the projected implementation time line. Any platform chosen must integrate
48 with the Department's Licensure and Uniform Education Reporting System and analytics system.
49 SECTION 7.26.(b) Notwithstanding G.S. 143C-1-2(b), any funds that have been
50 appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction for the purpose of business systems
51 modernization that have not been obligated by June 30, 2031, shall revert to the General Fund.

11 FORMALIZE THE DIAPER BANK OF NORTH CAROLINA'S ROLE AS PROVIDER
12 OF FEMININE HYGIENE PRODUCTS FOR PUBLIC SCHOOLS
13 SECTION 7.28. G.S. 115C-377 reads as rewritten:
14 "§ 115C-377. Feminine Hygiene Products Grant Program.
15 (a) Program; Purpose. – The Department of Public Instruction shall establish the
16 Feminine Hygiene Products Grant Program (Program) to assist public school units participating
17 in the Program in providing provide students with feminine hygiene products at no charge to the
18 student. The Department shall run the Program in accordance with this section in each year in
19 which funds are made available for the purpose.
20 (b) Grants. – To the extent funds are made available for the Program, the Department of
21 Public Instruction shall award public school units grants of up to five thousand dollars ($5,000)
22 on a first-come, first-served basis, and the Department shall prioritize awarding grants to public
23 school units that did not receive an award pursuant to the Program in the previous fiscal year. No
24 public school unit shall receive more than one grant per fiscal year.
25 (b1) Participation. – The Department of Public Instruction shall develop a process through
26 which public school units can elect to participate in the Program for each school year.
27 (b2) Contract for Products. – The Department of Public Instruction shall contract with the
28 Diaper Bank of North Carolina to provide feminine hygiene products to all public school units
29 that elect to participate in the Program on a pro rata basis based on the number of female students
30 in grades six through 12 in the participating public school unit.
31 (c) Reporting. – No later than March 15, 2023, and every year thereafter that funds are
32 made available for 15 of each year of the Program, the Department shall report to the Joint
33 Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the public school units receiving grants products
34 under the Program, the specific number of feminine hygiene products purchased with the grant
35 funds, provided through the Program, the number of students served by the Program, and the
36 impact of the Program on student health and well-being."

40 SPARKNC EXTENSION
41 SECTION 7.33. Section 7.62 of S.L. 2023-134, as amended by Section 3J.14 of S.L.
42 2024-57, reads as rewritten:
43 "SECTION 7.62.(a) There is established the SparkNC Pilot Program (Program) for
44 beginning with the 2023-2025 fiscal biennium. biennium continuing through the 2031-2033
45 fiscal biennium. The pilot program Program authorizes SparkNC, a North Carolina nonprofit
46 corporation, in partnership with selected public school units, to develop a nontraditional,
47 student-driven pathway through which students may select and complete modular learning
48 experiences that, when aggregated, will provide a competency-based equivalency to a traditional
49 elective course credit. SparkNC shall provide a menu of modular learning experiences that
50 include opportunities for work-based learning. For purposes of this section, "Learning
51 Accelerator" is either a High-Tech or Health Science Learning Accelerator. The
Page 98 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 competency-based elective credit shall be denoted on student transcripts as a Learning
2 Accelerator credit and be focused on science, technology, engineering, and mathematics
3 (STEM). A student may earn up to four credits in Learning Accelerators but may not complete
4 the same learning experience more than once for credit.
5 "SECTION 7.62.(b) Each public school unit partnering with SparkNC in accordance with
6 this section (partnering public school units) shall enter a memorandum of understanding with
7 SparkNC to meet certain requirements for the Program. These requirements shall include the
8 provision of a physical learning lab staffed by a learning lab facilitator, operated on a schedule
9 agreed upon by the public school unit and SparkNC, that will provide a site for collaborative
10 learning and networking. Learning lab facilitators shall facilitate interdistrict instruction, provide
11 student advising, design learning experiences, coordinate with industry partners, and validate
12 student work.
13 "SECTION 7.62.(c) Notwithstanding any State Board of Education rules, partnering public
14 school units shall award the elective credit in a Learning Accelerator to any student who
15 completes a combination of learning experiences determined by SparkNC to provide the
16 competency-based elective credit in that course upon verification of successful completion of the
17 learning experiences and integrity of student work products by the learning lab facilitator. The
18 elective credit shall be denoted as achieved competency on the student's transcript. A student's
19 participation in learning experiences but failure to earn elective credit shall not be denoted as a
20 fail on the student's transcript.
21 "SECTION 7.62.(d) The following provisions shall apply to the Program:
22 (1) Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-295 and any related State Board of Education
23 rules, learning lab facilitators shall not be required to hold teacher licensure
24 but shall meet the standards established by the memorandum of
25 understanding. Learning lab facilitators shall be the teacher of record for
26 students enrolled in the Program. Additional non-licensed personnel may be
27 contracted with on a full- or part-time basis for the purpose of providing
28 timely, real-world content, industry expertise, and student learning
29 experiences. Learning lab facilitators and contract personnel with the Program
30 shall be subject to the requirements of Part 6 of Article 22 of Chapter 115C of
31 the General Statutes (Criminal History Checks).
32 (2) For the purposes of student participation in the Program, the requirements of
33 Part 2 of Article 8 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes (Calendar) shall
34 not apply. Students may continue to participate in the Program and aggregate
35 learning experiences throughout the time the students are enrolled in the
36 public school unit and shall not be limited to a semester or school year.
37 Learning experiences may be provided to students in person, remotely, or
38 through asynchronous learning experiences.
39 (3) Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-316 or any other law or rule to the contrary,
40 public school units shall not be required to pay learning lab facilitators in
41 accordance to the salary schedule used for other teachers employed by the
42 public school unit.
43 (4) If a course in computer science is required for high school graduation,
44 completion of the competency-based elective credit of a High-Tech Learning
45 Accelerator shall be deemed to satisfy the requirement.
46 "SECTION 7.62.(e) For the 2023-2024 and 2024-2025 school years, all All public school
47 units are eligible to participate in the Program.
48 "SECTION 7.62.(f) The nonrecurring funds appropriated to the Department of Public
49 Instruction in the 2023-2024 and 2024-2025 fiscal years for the purposes of this section shall be
50 allocated as a directed grant to SparkNC to provide students a nontraditional pathway to earn
51 competency-based Learning Accelerator elective credits. Funds allocated pursuant to this section
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 99
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 shall not revert to the General Fund but shall remain available until the end of the 2025-2026
2 2026-2027 fiscal year. SparkNC shall utilize the grant to partner with and provide services in the
3 maximum number of public school units possible.

47 STUDENT USE OF WIRELESS COMMUNICATION DEVICES
48 SECTION 7.34.(a) Article 7B of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
49 by adding a new Part to read:
50 "Part 8. Miscellaneous Requirements.
51 "§ 115C-76.100. Regulation of wireless communication devices.
Page 100 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) The following definitions apply in this section:
2 (1) Wireless communication device. – Any portable wireless device that has the
3 capability to provide voice, messaging, or other data communication between
4 two or more parties, including the following:
5 a. Cellular telephones.
6 b. Tablet computers.
7 c. Laptop computers.
8 d. Paging devices.
9 e. Two-way radios.
10 f. Gaming devices.
11 (2) Wireless communication policy. – A policy governing the use of wireless
12 communication devices by students.
13 (b) The governing body of a public school unit shall establish a wireless communication
14 policy. At a minimum, except as permitted in subsection (c) of this section, the policy shall
15 prohibit students from using, displaying, or having a wireless communication device turned on
16 during instructional time.
17 (c) A wireless communication policy shall allow student use of a wireless communication
18 device during instructional time as follows:
19 (1) If authorized by a teacher for educational purposes or for use in the event of
20 an emergency. The governing body may establish parameters to be followed
21 by a teacher in granting authorizations.
22 (2) As required by the student's individualized education program or section 504
23 (29 U.S.C. § 794) plan.
24 (3) As required to manage a student's health care, in accordance with a
25 documented medical condition.
26 (d) The governing body shall establish the consequences for violations of the wireless
27 communication policy, which may include confiscation of the wireless communication device
28 and disciplinary measures under the public school unit's Code of Student Conduct.
29 (e) No later than September 1 of the year in which this section becomes effective, every
30 public school unit shall send to the Department of Public Instruction a copy of the public school
31 unit's wireless communication policy. The public school unit shall provide the Department of
32 Public Instruction with a copy of the wireless communication policy anytime it is changed. No
33 later than October 1 of each year, the Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint
34 Legislative Education Oversight Committee the number of public school units that are in
35 compliance with this section and the name of any public school unit which is not in compliance
36 with this section."
37 SECTION 7.34.(b) This section applies beginning with the 2025-2026 school year.

2 intends to establish residency in the State.
3 (2)b. Upon request by the local school administrative unit where the student
4 seeks to register to enroll, a parent or legal guardian provides (i) a copy
5 of the official military order transferring to a military installation or
6 reservation located in the State.State or (ii) an official military
7 document showing an anticipated date of separation. Each local school
8 administrative unit may determine the document or documents
9 sufficient to establish an anticipated date of separation so long as the
10 document required is easily accessible by the parent or legal guardian.
11 (3)c. A parent or legal guardian completes and submits the local school
12 administrative unit's required enrollment forms and documentation,
13 except that proof of residency and documentation related to
14 disciplinary actions pursuant to G.S. 115C-366(a4) shall not be
15 required until the student transfers into the local school administrative
16 unit, at which time they shall be required prior to commencing
17 attendance.
18 (2) Once registered to be enrolled, the following apply:
19 a. A local school administrative unit shall make available to a student
20 who registers to enroll pursuant to this subsection the same
21 opportunities available to a student enrolled contemporaneously with
22 domicilia, such as requesting or applying for school assignment,
23 registering for courses, and applying for any other programs that
24 require additional request or application.
25 b. A student enrolled pursuant to this subsection may not attend school
26 in the local school administrative unit until proof of residency is
27 provided in accordance with the requirements of the local school
28 administrative unit.the later of (i) one year from the parent or legal
29 guardian's reporting-for-duty or separation date or (ii) the end of the
30 school year in which the student began attending the school. The
31 parent or legal guardian shall provide proof of residency before the
32 expiration of the allotted period of attendance provided in this
33 sub-subdivision.
34 c. If a student begins attending a school pursuant to this subsection in
35 grade 11 or 12, the local school administrative unit shall extend the
36 period of attendance allowed under sub-subdivision b. of this
37 subdivision to allow the student to attend the school through high
38 school graduation.
39 Nothing in this subsection shall be construed to curtail a local school administrative unit's
40 authority pursuant to G.S. 115C-366(a5)."

42 EXTENDED LEARNING AND INTEGRATED STUDENT SUPPORTS COMPETITIVE
43 GRANT PROGRAM
44 SECTION 7.36. Article 16 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended by
45 adding a new Part to read:
46 "Part 7A. Extended Learning and Integrated Student Supports Grant Program.
47 "§ 115C-238.35. Program; purpose.
48 (a) Program; Purpose. – There is established the Extended Learning and Integrated
49 Student Supports Grant Program (Program). Nonprofit corporations and nonprofit corporations
50 working in collaboration with local school administrative units operating relevant programs may
51 apply for the Program. The purpose of the Program is to fund high-quality, independently
Page 102 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 validated extended learning and integrated student support service programs for at-risk students
2 that raise standards for student academic outcomes by focusing on the following:
3 (1) Use of an evidence-based model with a proven track record of success.
4 (2) Inclusion of rigorous, quantitative performance measures to confirm
5 effectiveness of the program.
6 (3) Deployment of multiple tiered supports in schools to address student barriers
7 to achievement, such as strategies to improve chronic absenteeism, antisocial
8 behaviors, academic growth, and enhancement of parent and family
9 engagement.
10 (4) Alignment with State performance measures, student academic goals, and the
11 North Carolina Standard Course of Study.
12 (5) Prioritization in programs to integrate clear academic content, in particular,
13 science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) learning
14 opportunities or reading development and proficiency instruction.
15 (6) Minimization of student class size when providing instruction or instructional
16 supports and interventions.
17 (7) Expansion of student access to high-quality learning activities and academic
18 support that strengthen student engagement and leverage community-based
19 resources, which may include organizations that provide mentoring services
20 and private-sector employer involvement.
21 (8) Utilization of digital content to expand learning time, when appropriate.
22 (b) Program Requirements. – In each year in which sufficient funds are available, the
23 Department of Public Instruction shall administer the Program in accordance with this Part.
24 (c) Program Funding. – The Department shall use up to seven million dollars
25 ($7,000,000) from the At-Risk Student Services Alternative School Allotment each fiscal year
26 to fund the Program. Of the funds used to fund the Program, the Department may use up to two
27 hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) for each fiscal year for expenses of administering the
28 Program.
29 "§ 115C-238.36. Awards; eligible uses.
30 (a) Eligible Uses. – Grants shall be used to award funds for new or existing eligible
31 programs for at-risk students operated by nonprofit corporations and nonprofit corporations
32 working in collaboration with local school administrative units. Programs should focus on
33 serving (i) at-risk students not performing at grade level as demonstrated by statewide
34 assessments, (ii) students at risk of dropout, and (iii) students at risk of school displacement due
35 to suspension or expulsion as a result of antisocial behaviors. Priority consideration shall be given
36 to applications demonstrating models that focus services and programs in schools that are
37 identified as low-performing pursuant to G.S. 115C-105.37.
38 (b) Awards; Required Match. – Grant participants are eligible to receive grants for up to
39 two years in an amount of up to five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) each year. A grant
40 participant shall provide certification to the Department of Public Instruction that the grants
41 received under the Program shall be matched on the basis of three dollars ($3.00) in grant funds
42 for every one dollar ($1.00) in nongrant funds. Matching funds shall not include other State funds.
43 The Department shall also give priority consideration to an applicant that is a nonprofit
44 corporation working in partnership with a local school administrative unit resulting in a match
45 utilizing federal funds under Part A of Title I of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of
46 1965, as amended, or Title IV of the Higher Education Act of 1965, as amended, and other federal
47 or local funds. Matching funds may include in-kind contributions for up to fifty percent (50%)
48 of the required match.
49 (c) A nonprofit corporation may act as its own fiscal agent for the purposes of this
50 Program.
51 "§ 115C-238.37. Reporting requirements.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 103
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Recipient Reporting. – No later than July 15 of each year in which a grant recipient
2 is participating in the Program, the recipient shall report to the Department of Public Instruction
3 on the expenditure of grant funds and the progress of the Program, including alignment with State
4 academic standards, data collection for reporting student progress, the source and amount of
5 matching funds, and other measures, before receiving funding for the next fiscal year. Grant
6 recipients shall also submit a final report on key performance data, including statewide test
7 results, attendance rates, graduation rates, and promotion rates, and financial sustainability of the
8 Program.
9 (b) Department Reporting. – No later than September 15 of each year of the Program, the
10 Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight
11 Committee on the Program, including recommendations regarding effective program models,
12 standards, and performance measures based on student performance, leveraging of
13 community-based resources to expand student access to learning activities, academic and
14 behavioral support services, and potential opportunities for the State to invest in proven models
15 for future grant programs."

17 TEACHER APPRENTICESHIP PROGRAM
18 SECTION 7.37.(a) Article 17D of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
19 by adding a new section to read:
20 "§ 115C-269.33. Teacher Apprenticeship Program.
21 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions shall apply in this section:
22 (1) Advanced Teaching Roles unit. – As defined in G.S. 115C-310.3(6).
23 (2) Apprentice. – A person who is employed as an apprentice by an apprenticeship
24 employer and meets all of the following criteria:
25 a. Holds a bachelor's degree.
26 b. Is eligible to hold or holds one of the following:
27 1. An emergency license.
28 2. A residency license.
29 3. A permit to teach issued by the Department in accordance with
30 rules adopted by the State Board of Education.
31 c. Submitted a Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA).
32 (3) Apprenticeship employer. – An Advanced Teaching Roles unit that meets all
33 of the following criteria:
34 a. Has a registered apprenticeship program under requirements
35 established by the United States Department of Labor.
36 b. Employs apprentices under the Program.
37 (4) Department. – The Department of Public Instruction.
38 (5) Program. – The Teacher Apprenticeship Program.
39 (6) RFP. – Request for proposals.
40 (b) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the Teacher Apprenticeship
41 Program as a competitive grant program for the purpose of increasing the number of
42 professionally licensed teachers in the State and improving teacher competency, student
43 outcomes, and teacher retention in the State. The Department of Public Instruction shall
44 administer the Program in collaboration with ApprenticeshipNC as set forth in this section.
45 (c) Request for Proposals. – No later than November 1 of each year, ApprenticeshipNC
46 shall issue an RFP for the Program. Advanced Teaching Roles units may submit proposals by
47 January 1 of the following calendar year. Proposals shall include at least the following
48 information:
49 (1) A plan to establish a registered teacher apprenticeship program in
50 collaboration with ApprenticeshipNC, including at least the following
51 information:
Page 104 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a. The number of apprentices the unit intends to employ using grant
2 funds for State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment
3 expenses, or both.
4 b. Specific subject areas and grade levels in the local school
5 administrative unit with teacher shortages.
6 (2) A system of supports that would be provided for apprentices, including
7 qualifications of mentor teachers and a schedule of supervision.
8 (3) Alternative sources of funding to support apprenticeships that could be paired
9 with State funds received under the Program, including federal workforce
10 development funds.
11 (4) An explanation of how the unit would incorporate its registered teacher
12 apprenticeship program with its advanced teaching roles program to enhance
13 the learning environment for apprentices.
14 (5) Strategies to encourage candidates to accept an apprenticeship instead of
15 directly entering the teacher profession on a Residency License, Emergency
16 License, or permit to teach.
17 (d) Selection of Recipients. – By March 15 of each school year in which proposals are
18 submitted, ApprenticeshipNC shall review the proposals and select local school administrative
19 units to participate in the Program, beginning in the subsequent school year. ApprenticeshipNC
20 shall notify the Department of its selections, and the Department shall allocate funds to the
21 selected Advanced Teaching Roles units in accordance with subsection (e) of this section.
22 (e) Allocation of State Grant Funds. – To the extent funds are appropriated by the General
23 Assembly for this purpose, the Department shall allocate the funds for grants to apprenticeship
24 employers based on the number of apprentices the unit intends to employ receiving grant funds
25 for State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment expenses, or both, up to a
26 maximum of 10 total apprentices per unit, as follows:
27 (1) Up to seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) per apprentice per year,
28 plus any additional funds necessary for benefits, to provide salary supplements
29 for apprentices in accordance subdivision (3) of subsection (f) of this section.
30 (2) Up to five thousand dollars ($5,000) per apprentice per year for the costs of
31 enrollment of an apprentice in an institution of higher education in accordance
32 with subdivision (5) of subsection (f) of this section.
33 (f) Program Requirements. – The following minimum requirements shall apply to
34 apprentices and apprenticeship employers under the Program:
35 (1) Number of apprentices. – An apprenticeship employer shall employ no more
36 than a maximum of 10 total apprentices who receive grant funds for
37 State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment expenses, or both.
38 An apprenticeship employer may employ additional apprentices receiving
39 funds for salary supplements or education expenses from other sources.
40 (2) Funds for positions. – An apprenticeship employer may employ apprentices
41 using State funds allotted for teacher assistant positions, with roles and
42 responsibilities modified to meet the requirements of the Program, or other
43 available funds.
44 (3) Salary supplements. – As follows:
45 a. An apprenticeship employer shall provide the following salary
46 supplements:
47 1. For each apprentice, up to a maximum of seven thousand five
48 hundred dollars ($7,500) per apprentice.
49 2. For each mentor teacher, up to a maximum of five thousand
50 dollars ($5,000) per mentor teacher.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 105
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 b. An apprenticeship employer may provide a salary supplement for any
2 other teacher employed by the local school administrative unit who
3 agrees to become the teacher of record for additional students to
4 facilitate the operation of the registered apprenticeship program in that
5 unit, up to a maximum of three thousand dollars ($3,000).
6 (4) Mentors. – Each apprentice shall work full time in a classroom with a mentor
7 teacher who is a member of a team of teachers that is led by a teacher with an
8 advanced teaching role. For purposes of this subdivision, a mentor teacher
9 may include a classroom excellence teacher as defined in G.S. 115C-310.3(7).
10 (5) Enrollment costs. – An apprentice shall enroll in or remain enrolled in a
11 recognized educator preparation program. An apprenticeship employer may
12 provide up to a maximum of five thousand dollars ($5,000) per apprentice per
13 year for the costs of enrollment in an institution of higher education. These
14 funds shall only be used after the apprentice has exhausted all other
15 scholarships or grants covering the cost of attendance at an institution of
16 higher education, including federal Pell grants.
17 (6) Limitations. – An apprentice shall not do any of the following:
18 a. Serve as the teacher of record for any students.
19 b. Engage in substitute teaching for more than eight hours per week.
20 c. Work as an apprentice for more than three years.
21 (g) Term. – The Department shall award grant funds to selected Advanced Teaching
22 Roles units to serve as apprenticeship employers for one or more terms of three years. Prior to
23 the conclusion of a term, ApprenticeshipNC shall evaluate the success of the Program at the unit
24 and the compliance of the unit with the requirements of this section. At the conclusion of the
25 evaluation, ApprenticeshipNC may, in its discretion, renew the apprenticeship employer for an
26 additional term. Throughout the Program, an apprenticeship employer shall provide any
27 information or access requested by ApprenticeshipNC to evaluate the registered apprenticeship
28 program pursuant to this section.
29 (h) Emergency Position Conversion. – Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-105.25(b), an
30 apprenticeship employer may convert one position allocated to the unit for classroom teachers to
31 its dollar equivalent at the salary on the first step of the "A" Teachers Salary Schedule for every
32 one apprentice employed by the unit who is receiving State grant funds if all of the following are
33 met:
34 (1) The apprentice receiving State grant funds would have been eligible to fill the
35 vacant position using a residency license, emergency license, or permit to
36 teach but was instead hired into the apprentice position.
37 (2) The funds are only used for one or more of the following purposes in
38 accordance with the requirements of the Program:
39 a. Salary supplements for apprentices.
40 b. Salary supplements for mentor teachers.
41 c. Costs of enrollment in an institution of higher education.
42 d. Salary supplements for teachers identified in sub-subdivision b. of
43 subdivision (3) of subsection (f) of this section.
44 (i) Administration. – Of the funds appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction
45 for the Program for each fiscal year, the Department shall allocate the greater of fifteen percent
46 (15%) or three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000) to ApprenticeshipNC to do all of the
47 following in consultation with the Department:
48 (1) Outline the duties and responsibilities of apprentices, including on-the-job
49 training requirements.
Page 106 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Collaborate with recognized educator preparation programs to establish
2 education requirements for apprentices and revise curriculum requirements
3 for student teaching to include apprenticeships under the Program.
4 (3) Create minimum competencies for apprentices that reflect the progressive
5 acquisition of ability.
6 (4) Create resources that can be used by apprenticeship employers to select and
7 train mentor teachers, including the responsibilities of a mentor teacher and
8 background information on teacher apprenticeship programs.
9 (5) Develop a process to monitor apprentices in their first years of teaching after
10 successful completion of the Program to evaluate the qualities of teacher
11 candidates that correlate to successful outcomes and lower teacher turnover
12 rates.
13 (6) Assist apprenticeship employers with the following:
14 a. Combining State and federal funds to maximize the number of
15 apprentices in the Program.
16 b. Complying with applicable State and federal law.
17 (7) Develop a training module for mentor teachers that establishes standards for
18 mentor teachers under the Program and incorporates, where applicable, any
19 preexisting standards for mentor teachers.
20 (j) Report. – No later than March 15 of each year, ApprenticeshipNC shall report to the
21 Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the Program, including at least the
22 following information:
23 (1) The impact of the Program for each apprenticeship employer on the following:
24 a. The number of teachers, disaggregated by licensure type.
25 b. Student outcomes.
26 c. Teacher retention.
27 (2) Successful strategies and best practices used by apprenticeship employers.
28 (3) Any barriers to expanding the Program."

38 SECTION 7A.1.(b) Salary Supplements for Teachers Paid on This Salary Schedule.
39 –
40 (1) Licensed teachers who have NBPTS certification shall receive a salary
41 supplement each month of twelve percent (12%) of their monthly salary on
42 the "A" salary schedule.
43 (2) Licensed teachers who are classified as "M" teachers shall receive a salary
44 supplement each month of ten percent (10%) of their monthly salary on the
45 "A" salary schedule.
46 (3) Licensed teachers with licensure based on academic preparation at the
47 six-year degree level shall receive a salary supplement of one hundred
48 twenty-six dollars ($126.00) per month in addition to the supplement provided
49 to them as "M" teachers.
50 (4) Licensed teachers with licensure based on academic preparation at the
51 doctoral degree level shall receive a salary supplement of two hundred
Page 110 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

1 fifty-three dollars ($253.00) per month in addition to the supplement provided
2 to them as "M" teachers.
3 (5) Certified school nurses shall receive a salary supplement each month of ten
4 percent (10%) of their monthly salary on the "A" salary schedule.
5 (6) School counselors who are licensed as counselors at the master's degree level
6 or higher shall receive a salary supplement each month of one hundred dollars
7 ($100.00).
8 SECTION 7A.1.(c) For school psychologists, school speech pathologists who are
9 licensed as speech pathologists at the master's degree level or higher, and school audiologists
10 who are licensed as audiologists at the master's degree level or higher, the following shall apply:
11 (1) The first step of the salary schedule shall be equivalent to the sixth step of the
12 "A" salary schedule.
13 (2) These employees shall receive the following salary supplements each month:
14 a. Ten percent (10%) of their monthly salary, excluding the supplement
15 provided pursuant to sub-subdivision b. of this subdivision.
16 b. Three hundred fifty dollars ($350.00).
17 (3) These employees are eligible to receive salary supplements equivalent to those
18 of teachers for academic preparation at the six-year degree level or the
19 doctoral degree level.
20 (4) The twenty-sixth step of the salary schedule shall be seven and one-half
21 percent (7.5%) higher than the salary received by these same employees on
22 the twenty-fifth step of the salary schedule.
23 SECTION 7A.1.(d) Beginning with the 2014-2015 fiscal year, in lieu of providing
24 annual longevity payments to teachers paid on the teacher salary schedule, the amounts of those
25 longevity payments are included in the monthly amounts under the teacher salary schedule.
26 SECTION 7A.1.(e) A teacher compensated in accordance with this salary schedule
27 for the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall receive an amount equal to the greater of the following:
28 (1) The applicable amount on the salary schedule for the applicable school year.
29 (2) For teachers who were eligible for longevity for the 2013-2014 school year,
30 the sum of the following:
31 a. The salary the teacher received in the 2013-2014 school year pursuant
32 to Section 35.11 of S.L. 2013-360.
33 b. The longevity that the teacher would have received under the longevity
34 system in effect for the 2013-2014 school year provided in Section
35 35.11 of S.L. 2013-360 based on the teacher's current years of service.
36 c. The annual bonus provided in Section 9.1(e) of S.L. 2014-100.
37 (3) For teachers who were not eligible for longevity for the 2013-2014 school
38 year, the sum of the salary and annual bonus the teacher received in the
39 2014-2015 school year pursuant to Section 9.1 of S.L. 2014-100.
40 SECTION 7A.1.(f) As used in this section, the term "teacher" shall also include
41 instructional support personnel.

43 CONSOLIDATED TEACHER BONUS PROGRAM
44 SECTION 7A.2.(a) Article 20 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
45 by adding the following new section to read:
46 "§ 115C-302.9. Teacher bonuses.
47 (a) Program. – The State Board of Education shall establish a consolidated teacher bonus
48 program to reward teacher performance and encourage student learning and improvement. To
49 attain this goal, to the extent funds are made available for this purpose, the Department of Public
50 Instruction shall administer bonus pay to qualifying teachers whose salaries are supported from
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 111
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 State funds in January of each year, based on data from the prior school year, in accordance with
2 this section.
3 (b) Definitions. – For purposes of this section, the following definitions shall apply:
4 (1) Eligible advanced course teacher. – A teacher of Advanced Placement
5 courses, International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme courses, or the
6 Cambridge Advanced International Certificate of Education (AICE) program
7 who meets the following criteria:
8 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, one or more
9 of the following:
10 1. A qualifying public school unit.
11 2. The North Carolina Virtual Public School program.
12 b. Taught one or more students who received a score listed in subsection
13 (c) of this section.
14 (2) Eligible career and technical education (CTE) teacher. – A teacher who meets
15 the following criteria:
16 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a qualifying
17 public school unit.
18 b. Taught one or more students who attained approved industry
19 certifications or credentials consistent with G.S. 115C-156.2.
20 (3) Eligible growth teacher. – A teacher who meets at least one of the following
21 criteria:
22 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a qualifying
23 public school unit and meets one of the following criteria:
24 1. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
25 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for third
26 grade reading from the previous school year.
27 2. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
28 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth
29 or fifth grade reading from the previous school year.
30 3. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
31 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth,
32 fifth, sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics from the
33 previous school year.
34 b. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a local school
35 administrative unit and meets one of the following criteria:
36 1. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
37 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
38 to the EVAAS student growth index score for third grade
39 reading from the previous school year.
40 2. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
41 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
42 to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth or fifth
43 grade reading from the previous school year.
44 3. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
45 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
46 to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth, fifth,
47 sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics from the previous
48 school year.
49 c. Was employed by a local school administrative unit that employed in
50 the previous school year three or fewer total teachers in that teacher's
51 grade level as long as the teacher has an EVAAS student growth index
Page 112 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 score from the previous school year of exceeded expected growth in
2 one of the following subject areas:
3 1. Third grade reading.
4 2. Fourth or fifth grade reading.
5 3. Fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics.
6 (4) EVAAS. – The Education Value-Added Assessment System.
7 (5) Predecessor bonus programs. – All of the following:
