34 (14a) Prior authorization. – The process by which insurers and UROs determine

37 …
38 (16a) Urgent health care service. – A health care service, including mental and

1 Prospective and concurrent determinations shall be communicated to The time line for
2 completion of a prospective or concurrent utilization review is as follows:
3 (1) Non-urgent health care services. – If an insurer requires a prior authorization
4 review of a healthcare service, then the insurer or its URO shall both render a
5 prior authorization review determination or noncertification and notify the
6 covered person and the covered person's provider within three business days
7 after the insurer obtains all necessary information about the admission,

9 determination or noncertification.
10 (2) Urgent health care services. – An insurer or its URO shall both render a
11 utilization review determination or noncertification concerning urgent health
12 care services and notify the covered person and the covered person's provider
13 of that utilization review determination or noncertification not later than 24
14 hours after receiving all necessary information needed to complete the review

18 this subdivision shall not apply and the utilization review will be subject to

24 covered person's provider and covered person. In person that is in compliance with subsection
25 (h) of this section.
26 (f2) Concurrent Review Liability. – For concurrent reviews, the insurer shall remain liable
27 for health care healthcare services until the covered person has been notified of the
28 noncertification.
29 …
30 (j1) Requirements Applicable to Appeals Reviews. – All of the following requirements
31 apply to an appeals review:
32 (1) Except as otherwise provided, appeals shall be reviewed by a licensed
33 physician who meets all of the following criteria:
34 a. Possesses a current and valid non-restricted license to practice
35 medicine in any United States jurisdiction.
36 b. Has practiced for a period of at least three consecutive years in the
37 same or similar specialty as a medical doctor who typically manages
38 the medical condition or disease for which prior authorization review
39 is required or whose training and experience meets all of the following
40 criteria:
41 1. Includes treatment of the same condition as the condition of
42 the covered person.
43 2. Includes treatment of complications that may result from the
44 service or procedure that is the subject of the appeal.
45 3. Is sufficient for the medical doctor to determine if the service
46 or procedure is medically necessary or clinically appropriate.
47 c. Had no direct involvement in making the prior adverse determination
48 or noncertification that is the subject of the appeal.
49 d. Has no financial interest, or other conflict of interest, in the outcome
50 of the appeal.

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Appeals initiated by a licensed mental health professional for a service
2 provided by a licensed mental health professional may be reviewed by a
3 licensed mental health professional rather than a medical doctor. The
4 requirements of subdivision (1) of this subsection shall apply to the reviewing
5 licensed mental health professional in the same manner that they apply to a
6 medical doctor.
7 (3) The medical doctor or licensed mental health professional shall consider all
8 known clinical aspects of the healthcare service under review, including all
9 pertinent medical records and any medical literature that have been provided
10 by the covered person's provider or by a health care facility.
11 …
12 (m) Disclosure of Utilization Review Requirements. – All of the following apply to an
13 insurer's responsibility to disclose any utilization review procedures:
14 (1) Coverage and member handbook. – In the certificate of coverage and member
15 handbook provided to covered persons, an insurer shall include a clear and
16 comprehensive description of its utilization review procedures, including the
17 procedures for appealing noncertifications and a statement of the rights and
18 responsibilities of covered persons, including the voluntary nature of the
19 appeal process, with respect to those procedures. An insurer shall also include
20 in the certificate of coverage and the member handbook information about the
21 availability of assistance from the Department's Health Insurance Smart NC,
22 including the telephone number and address of the Program. program.
23 (2) Prospective materials. – An insurer shall include a summary of its utilization
24 review procedures in materials intended for prospective covered persons.
25 (3) Membership cards. – An insurer shall print on its membership cards a toll-free
26 telephone number to call for utilization review purposes.
27 (4) Website. – An insurer shall make any current prior authorization requirements
28 and restrictions readily accessible on its website.
29 (m1) Changes to Prior Authorization. – If an insurer intends either to implement a new
30 prior authorization review requirement or restriction or to amend an existing requirement or
31 restriction, then the new or amended requirement shall not be in effect unless and until the
32 insurer's website has been updated to reflect the new or amended requirement or restriction. A
33 claim shall not be denied for failure to obtain a prior authorization if the prior authorization
34 requirement or amended requirement was not in effect on the date of service of the claim.
35 …
36 (n1) Prior Authorization Determination Validity. – All of the following apply to the length
37 of time an approved prior authorization shall remain valid under certain circumstances:
38 (1) If a covered person enrolls in a new health benefit plan offered by the same
39 insurer under which the prior authorization was approved, then the previously
40 approved prior authorization remains valid for the initial 90 days of coverage
41 under the new health benefit plan. This section does not require coverage of a
42 service if it is not a covered service under the new health benefit plan.
43 (2) If a healthcare service, other than for in-patient care, requires prior
44 authorization and is for the treatment of a covered person's chronic condition,
45 then the prior authorization shall remain valid for no less than six months from
46 the date the healthcare provider receives notification of the prior authorization
47 approval.
48 …
49 (o) Violation. – A In accordance with this Chapter, a violation of this section subjects an
50 insurer and an agent of the insurer to G.S. 58-2-70.

1 (p) Federal Rule Alignment. – No later than January 1, 2028, an insurer offering a health

24 THE NINJA'S LAW
25 SECTION 9L.2.(a) Article 2 of Chapter 90 of the General Statutes is amended by
26 adding a new section to read:
27 "§ 90-30.3. Reports of adverse events.
28 (a) A dentist who holds a permit to administer general anesthesia or sedation shall submit
29 an adverse event report to the Board within 72 hours after each adverse event if the patient dies
30 or has permanent organic brain dysfunction within 24 hours after the administration of general
31 anesthesia or sedation. Sedation permit holders shall cease administration of sedation until the
32 Board has investigated the death or permanent organic brain dysfunction and approved
33 resumption of permit privileges. General anesthesia permit holders shall cease administration of
34 general anesthesia and sedation until the Board has reviewed the adverse event report and
35 approved resumption of permit privileges.
36 (b) A dentist who holds a permit to administer general anesthesia or sedation shall submit
37 an adverse event report to the Board within 30 days after each adverse event if the patient is
38 admitted to a hospital on inpatient status for a medical emergency or physical injury within 24
39 hours after the administration of general anesthesia or sedation.
40 (c) A dentist who holds a permit to administer general anesthesia or sedation shall submit
41 an adverse event report to the Board within 30 days after each adverse event.
42 (d) The adverse event report shall be in writing and shall include, at a minimum, all of
43 the following:
44 (1) Dentist's name, license number, and permit number.
45 (2) Date and time of the occurrence.
46 (3) Facility where the occurrence took place.
47 (4) Name and address of the patient.
48 (5) Surgical procedure involved.
49 (6) Type and dosage of sedation or anesthesia utilized in the procedure.
50 (7) Circumstances involved in the occurrence.
51 (8) The entire patient treatment record, including anesthesia records.
Page 224 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (e) Upon receipt of an adverse event report under this section, the Board shall investigate
2 and shall take disciplinary action if the evidence demonstrates that a licensee has violated this
3 Article.
4 (f) For the purposes of this section, the term "adverse event" means any of the following
5 clinical emergencies: anaphylaxis, aspiration, cardiac arrest, or unplanned advanced airway
6 placement."
7 SECTION 9L.2.(b) The North Carolina Policy Collaboratory at the University of
8 North Carolina at Chapel Hill shall conduct a study to evaluate whether a second health care
9 provider who is qualified to provide anesthesia services is needed when utilizing drugs that can
10 lead to deep sedation, such as propofol. The Collaboratory shall submit a report to the Joint
11 Legislative Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services by April 1, 2026, with any
12 legislative recommendations addressing this issue.
13 SECTION 9L.2.(c) Subsection (a) of this section becomes effective October 1, 2025.
14 The remainder of this section is effective when it becomes law.

4 GENERAL PROVISIONS
5 SECTION 9M.1.(b) Information to be Included in Block Grant Plans. – The
6 Department of Health and Human Services shall submit a separate plan for each Block Grant
7 received and administered by the Department, and each plan shall include the following:
8 (1) A delineation of the proposed allocations by program or activity, including
9 State and federal match requirements.
10 (2) A delineation of the proposed State and local administrative expenditures.
11 (3) An identification of all new positions to be established through the Block
12 Grant, including permanent, temporary, and time-limited positions.
13 (4) A comparison of the proposed allocations by program or activity with two
14 prior years' program and activity budgets and two prior years' actual program
15 or activity expenditures.
16 (5) A projection of current year expenditures by program or activity.
17 (6) A projection of federal Block Grant funds available, including unspent federal
18 funds from the current and prior fiscal years.
19 (7) The required amount of maintenance of effort and the amount of funds
20 qualifying for maintenance of effort in the previous year delineated by
21 program or activity.
22 SECTION 9M.1.(c) Changes in Federal Fund Availability. – If the Congress of the
23 United States increases the federal fund availability for any of the Block Grants or contingency
24 funds and other grants related to existing Block Grants administered by the Department of Health
25 and Human Services from the amounts appropriated in this act, the Department shall allocate the
26 increase proportionally across the program and activity appropriations identified for that Block
27 Grant in this section. In allocating an increase in federal fund availability, the Office of State
28 Budget and Management shall not approve funding for new programs or activities not
29 appropriated in this act.
30 If the Congress of the United States decreases the federal fund availability for any of
31 the Block Grants or contingency funds and other grants related to existing Block Grants
32 administered by the Department of Health and Human Services from the amounts appropriated
33 in this act, the Department shall develop a plan to adjust the Block Grants based on reduced
34 federal funding.
35 Notwithstanding the provisions of this subsection, for fiscal years 2025-2026 and
36 2026-2027, increases in the federal fund availability for the Temporary Assistance to Needy
37 Families (TANF) Block Grant shall be used only for the North Carolina Child Care Subsidy
38 program to pay for child care and shall not be used to supplant State funds.
39 Prior to allocating the change in federal fund availability, the proposed allocation
40 must be approved by the Office of State Budget and Management. If the Department adjusts the
41 allocation of any Block Grant due to changes in federal fund availability, then a report shall be
42 made to the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services and the Fiscal
43 Research Division.
44 SECTION 9M.1.(d) Except as otherwise provided, appropriations from federal
45 Block Grant funds are made for each year of the fiscal biennium ending June 30, 2027, according
46 to the schedule enacted for State fiscal years 2025-2026 and 2026-2027, or until a new schedule
47 is enacted by the General Assembly.
48 SECTION 9M.1.(e) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (e1) of this section,
49 all changes to the budgeted allocations to the Block Grants or contingency funds and other grants
50 related to existing Block Grants administered by the Department of Health and Human Services
51 that are not specifically addressed in this section shall be approved by the Office of State Budget
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 235
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 and Management. The Office of State Budget and Management shall not approve funding for
2 new programs or activities not appropriated in this section. Additionally, if budgeted allocations
3 are decreased, the Office of State Budget and Management shall not approve any reduction of
4 funds designated for subrecipients in subsection (a) of this section under (i) Item 03 of the
5 Substance Use Prevention, Treatment, and Recovery Services Block Grant or (ii) Item 01 or 02
6 of the Maternal and Child Health Block Grant. The Office of State Budget and Management shall
7 consult with the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services for
8 review prior to implementing any changes. In consulting, the report shall include an itemized
9 listing of affected programs, including associated changes in budgeted allocations. All changes
10 to the budgeted allocations to the Block Grants shall be reported immediately to the Joint
11 Legislative Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services and the Fiscal Research
12 Division. This subsection does not apply to Block Grant changes caused by legislative salary
13 increases and benefit adjustments.
14 SECTION 9M.1.(e1) The Department of Health and Human Services shall have the
15 authority to realign appropriated funds under subsection (a) of this section for Item 01 or 02 in
16 the Maternal and Child Health Block Grant to maintain federal compliance and programmatic
17 alignment, so long as the realignment does not result in a reduction of funds designated for
18 subrecipients under subsection (a) of this section. The Department of Health and Human Services
19 is authorized to realign appropriated funds between the Maternal and Child Health Block Grant
20 categories as provided in this subsection without prior consultation with the Joint Legislative
21 Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services or without exceeding the total amount
22 appropriated for the items.
23 SECTION 9M.1.(f) Except as otherwise provided, the Department of Health and
24 Human Services shall have flexibility to transfer funding between the Temporary Assistance for
25 Needy Families (TANF) Block Grant and the TANF Emergency Contingency Funds Block Grant
26 so long as the total allocation for the line items within those Block Grants remains the same.

36 appropriated funds from Work First Family Assistance for electing counties to the Work First

11 SECTION 9M.1.(u) The sum of fifty-six million three hundred sixty-nine thousand
12 two hundred eighty-one dollars ($56,369,281) for each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium
13 appropriated in this act in the Low-Income Energy Assistance Block Grant to the Department of
14 Health and Human Services, Division of Social Services, shall be used for Energy Assistance
15 Payments for the households of (i) elderly persons age 60 and above with income up to one
16 hundred fifty percent (150%) of the federal poverty level and (ii) disabled persons eligible for
17 services funded through the Division of Aging.
18 County departments of social services shall submit to the Division of Social Services
19 an outreach plan for targeting households with 60-year-old household members no later than
20 August 1 of each year. The outreach plan shall comply with the following:
21 (1) Ensure that eligible households are made aware of the available assistance,
22 with particular attention paid to the elderly population age 60 and above and
23 disabled persons receiving services through the Division of Aging.
24 (2) Include efforts by the county department of social services to contact other
25 State and local governmental entities and community-based organizations to
26 (i) offer the opportunity to provide outreach and (ii) receive applications for
27 energy assistance.
28 (3) Be approved by the local board of social services or human services board
29 prior to submission.

1 U.S.C. § 710), for the 2025-2026 fiscal year or the 2026-2027 fiscal year, then those funds shall

33 USE OF CHILD CARE AND DEVELOPMENT BLOCK GRANT FUNDS/FAMILY
34 CHILD CARE HOME DIRECT SUPPORT PILOT
35 SECTION 9M.2.(a) Of the funds appropriated in this act from the federal Child Care
36 and Development Block Grant under Section 9M.1 of this act to the Department of Health and
37 Human Services, Division of Child Development and Early Education, for quality and
38 availability initiatives, the sum of three million dollars ($3,000,000) for each year of the
39 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall be allocated in equal amounts to the councils of governments in
40 Alamance, Harnett, and Johnston Counties to establish a two-year pilot program coordinated by
41 those councils of governments to build child care capacity in those counties. Each designated
42 council of governments shall issue a request for application (RFA) for a vendor to contract with
43 the respective council of governments to administer the pilot program, and each vendor selected
44 shall have experience providing support and assistance to early child care providers. To receive
45 funds, the vendor shall partner with the councils of governments in the respective county to (i)
46 increase the supply of child care programs by recruiting and coaching prospective child care
47 providers through the initial business planning and implementation process and (ii) ensure
48 sustainability by executing a two-year mentorship program for the new child care programs
49 created pursuant to this section.
50 SECTION 9M.2.(b) The councils of governments participating in the pilot program
51 may use (i) a portion of these funds for additional solutions provided by the vendor within the
Page 240 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 early childhood education space to meet localized needs and in support of recovery,
2 rehabilitation, and ongoing needs of their member communities and (ii) up to five percent (5%)
3 of the funds allocated to the respective councils of governments under this act for administrative
4 costs.
5 SECTION 9M.2.(c) The councils of governments participating in the pilot program
6 shall select a vendor that has all of the following qualifications:
7 (1) Experience and active or successful contracts to establish new family child
8 care homes in at least three other states.
9 (2) Technology to operate a substitute teacher pool that matches teachers with
10 providers and facilitates payments and quality control, and experience in
11 creating an active substitute teacher pool in one state.
12 (3) Experience successfully establishing family child care homes in rural
13 communities and addressing child care access in underserved areas.
14 (4) Technology that (i) allows for the recruitment of child care providers via
15 microsites, (ii) allows the onboarding of child care providers via a licensing
16 checklist, (iii) allows coaches to interface with and communicate with child
17 care providers, (iv) supports child care providers with enrollments via a
18 website and enrollment marketplace, (v) supports the recruitment of teachers
19 for the programs, (vi) provides billing for the programs, (vii) provides ongoing
20 business coaching, and (viii) allows all such technology to be connected and
21 communicate seamlessly.
22 (5) Demonstrated successful experience establishing new family child care homes
23 at scale on time lines of six months or less.
24 SECTION 9M.2.(d) Each vendor selected to participate in the pilot program shall
25 do each of the following:
26 (1) Perform a child care needs analysis to determine where child care providers
27 and substitute teachers are needed.
28 (2) Recruit new potential child care providers and substitutes and plan, staff, and
29 execute in-person and virtual recruitment events for new child care providers
30 in areas of need.
31 (3) Implement technology that meets the requirements of subdivision (c)(4) of
32 this section.
33 (4) Implement technology to operate a substitute teacher pool that matches
34 teachers with providers and facilitates payments and quality control.
35 (5) Develop informational materials that assist in-home family child care
36 providers with marketing, advertising, and parental outreach.
37 (6) Create child care slots and implement a substitute teacher pool available to
38 child care providers in the councils of governments' respective counties.
39 (7) Craft an implementation strategy to meet community and workforce needs,
40 including establishing child care for nontraditional hours and days, as needed.
41 (8) Provide a dashboard that allows for government leaders to track vendor
42 progress and get feedback from child care providers along with real-time
43 reporting.
44 (9) Provide support and resources and offer in-home family child care providers
45 coaching and training that includes in-person group training sessions, on-site
46 coaching visits, community forums, and events for a minimum of two years.
47 (10) Report all necessary information as required by this section.
48 SECTION 9M.2.(e) The councils of governments participating in the pilot program
49 shall submit an initial progress report by March 1, 2026, and additional progress reports every
50 six months thereafter for the duration of the pilot program to the Joint Legislative Oversight
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 241
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 Committee on Health and Human Services, the Fiscal Research Division, and the Division of
2 Child Development and Early Education. The reports shall include, at a minimum, the following:
3 (1) The number of child care programs created through the pilot program, by
4 county.
5 (2) The number of child care programs created that are child care centers and the
6 number that are family child care homes.
7 (3) The number of new child care slots created by the pilot program.
8 (4) The costs associated with creating the child care programs, including any
9 administrative costs.

11 PART X. AGRICULTURE AND CONSUMER SERVICES

13 ADDITIONAL 2024 AGRICULTURAL DISASTER CROP LOSS PROGRAM FUNDING
14 SECTION 10.1.(a) Of the funds appropriated from the Stabilization and Inflation
15 Reserve in this act, the nonrecurring sum of two hundred fifty million dollars ($250,000,000) for
16 the 2025-2026 fiscal year shall be allocated to the Department of Agriculture and Consumer
17 Services (Department) for the Agricultural Disaster Crop Loss Program (Program), established
18 in Section 2D.2(a) of S.L. 2025-2, for verifiable losses from an agricultural disaster in 2024,
19 excluding Hurricane Helene.
20 SECTION 10.1.(b) Funds allocated to the Program under this section shall be subject
21 to all requirements of Section 2D.2 of S.L. 2025-2. The Department shall include these funds in
22 the reporting requirements set forth in Section 2D.2(i) of S.L. 2025-2. Section 2D.2(h) of S.L.
23 2025-2 does not apply to these funds.
24 SECTION 10.1.(c) Funds allocated to the Program under this section that are not
25 expended or encumbered on the date the Program expires shall revert to the General Fund.
26
27 CONTINUATION OF AGRICULTURE MANUFACTURING AND PROCESSING
28 INITIATIVE
29 SECTION 10.2.(a) Findings and Purpose. – The General Assembly finds that the
30 lack of capacity for value-added processing of agricultural commodities near the farms where
31 those commodities are produced in the State creates competitive disadvantages to North Carolina
32 farmers by imposing increased transportation costs to remote commodity processing facilities
33 and presenting economic barriers to farmers who wish to participate in the market for higher
34 profit margin processed food products. The General Assembly further finds that grants to increase
35 agricultural processing opportunities in the State will create jobs and increase local property tax
36 bases in this State, will benefit agricultural and farming operations in the State with decreased
37 costs and increased profit options, and are consistent with promoting agricultural operations, a
38 vital sector of the State's economy. The purpose of this section is to fund and promote the
39 establishment of value-adding agricultural manufacturing and food processing facilities across
40 the State to fill existing gaps in the processing of agricultural products and to create a diverse and
41 economically competitive array of high value-added goods and products manufactured in this
42 State from agricultural products grown or produced in this State.
43 SECTION 10.2.(b) Funding. – Funds appropriated in this act to the Department of
44 Agriculture and Consumer Services (Department) for the North Carolina Agricultural
45 Manufacturing and Processing Initiative (NCAMPI) shall be used to provide grants to agriculture
46 manufacturing facilities for site development, infrastructure (including water, wastewater, or
47 transportation improvements), building construction or rehabilitation costs, or equipment. As a
48 part of the application, applicants must demonstrate in a manner determined by the Department
49 that they have applied for or otherwise sought other sources of applicable funding for the
50 proposed project. New facilities and expansions of existing facilities will be eligible for grants
51 under this subdivision. Shared-use facilities and incubators are ineligible for grants under this
Page 242 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 subdivision. Before entering into a grant agreement, the Department must find that the total
2 benefits of the project to the State outweigh its anticipated costs and render the grant appropriate
3 for the project.
4 SECTION 10.2.(c) Administration of Initiative. – In consultation with the nonprofit
5 corporation with which the Department of Commerce contracts pursuant to G.S. 143B-431.01(b),
6 the Department shall develop guidelines related to the administration of NCAMPI. The
7 guidelines shall require a finding that a grant under this section is necessary for the construction
8 or expansion of a facility to be used by a business entity (as that term is defined in G.S. 55-1-40)
9 that will engage in agricultural manufacturing or processing activities in this State. At least 20
10 days before the effective date of any guidelines or nontechnical amendments to the guidelines,
11 the Department shall publish the proposed guidelines on its website and provide notice to persons
12 who have requested notice of proposed guidelines. In addition, the Department shall accept oral
13 and written comments on the proposed guidelines and shall, in its discretion, consider those
14 comments before finalizing the guidelines. Guidelines adopted under this section shall not be
15 subject to the requirements of Article 2A of Chapter 150B of the General Statutes and shall
16 include all of the following:
17 (1) Criteria for evaluating grant applicants, including job creation, concentration
18 of production of the agricultural product the facility will process in proximity
19 to the proposed location, and reductions in (i) transportation costs and (ii)
20 estimated damage rates for agricultural products created as a result of greater
21 geographical proximity to the proposed manufacturing or processing facility.
22 (2) Criteria for determining grant eligibility, the amounts of awards, not to exceed
23 five million dollars ($5,000,000) per facility, and the required cost-share for
24 grant recipients. The Department may consider the economic development tier
25 of the county of a grant recipient under G.S. 143B-437.08 in setting cost-share
26 amounts.
27 SECTION 10.2.(d) Report. – Until all funds allocated by this section have been
28 expended, the Department shall annually report no later than October 1 on NCAMPI activities
29 during the prior fiscal year to the chairs of the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on
30 Agriculture and Natural and Economic Resources and the Fiscal Research Division. The report
31 shall include, at a minimum, all of the following:
32 (1) Total amount of grants awarded.
33 (2) A list of award recipients and the amount awarded to each recipient.
34 (3) Matching funds required and provided by grant recipients.
35 (4) Activities to ready sites and associated costs.
36 (5) Any major employers located at an improved or acquired site.
37 (6) Any unallocated amount for grants remaining in the NCAMPI Fund.
38 (7) Assessment of additional remaining needs for agricultural manufacturing and
39 processing facilities in the State.
40 SECTION 10.2.(e) Availability of Funds and Administrative Expenses. –
41 Notwithstanding any provision of G.S. 143C-1-2(b) to the contrary, these funds shall not revert
42 at the end of the fiscal year in which they are appropriated but shall remain available for the
43 purposes set forth in this section. The Department may use up to three percent (3%) of the funds
44 allocated by this section for administrative costs.

18 SECTION 11.1.(b) Availability Reduction. – If federal funds are reduced below the
19 amounts specified in this section after the effective date of this act, then every program in each
20 of these federal block grants shall be reduced by the same percentage as the reduction in federal
21 funds.
22 SECTION 11.1.(c) Availability Increase. – Any block grant funds appropriated by
23 the Congress of the United States in addition to the funds specified in this section shall be
24 expended as follows: each program category under the Community Development Block Grant
25 shall be increased by the same percentage as the increase in federal funds.
26 SECTION 11.1.(d) Reallocation. – The Department of Commerce shall consult with
27 the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations prior to reallocating Community
28 Development Block Grant Funds. Notwithstanding the provisions of this subsection, whenever
29 the Director of the Budget finds either of the following conditions exists:
30 (1) If a reallocation is required because of an emergency that poses an imminent
31 threat to public health or public safety, then the Director of the Budget may
32 authorize the reallocation without consulting the Commission. The
33 Department of Commerce shall report to the Commission on the reallocation
34 no later than 30 days after it was authorized and shall identify in the report the
35 emergency, the type of action taken, and how it was related to the emergency.
36 (2) If the State will lose federal block grant funds or receive less federal block
37 grant funds in the next fiscal year unless a reallocation is made, then the
38 Department of Commerce shall provide a written report to the Commission
39 on the proposed reallocation and shall identify the reason that failure to take
40 action will result in the loss of federal funds. If the Commission does not hear
41 the issue within 30 days of receipt of the report, the Department may take the
42 action without consulting the Commission.
43 SECTION 11.1.(e) Report. – By October 1, 2025, and September 1, 2026, the
44 Department of Commerce shall report to the chairs of the House of Representatives
45 Appropriations Committee on Agriculture and Natural and Economic Resources; the chairs of
46 the Senate Appropriations Committee on Agriculture, Natural, and Economic Resources; the
47 chairs of the Joint Legislative Economic Development and Global Engagement Oversight
48 Committee; and the Fiscal Research Division on the use of Community Development Block
49 Grant Funds appropriated in the prior fiscal year. The report shall include the following:
50 (1) A discussion of each of the categories of funding, including information on
51 the statewide need in each category.

6 SECTION 11.1.(f) Neighborhood Revitalization. – Funds allocated to the
7 Neighborhood Revitalization Category in subsection (a) of this section shall be made available
8 as grants for eligible activities listed in this subsection. The funds available for grants under this
9 category may be used for all of the following, subject to the national objectives and eligible
10 activities allowed under guidance issued by the United States Department of Housing and Urban
11 Development (HUD):
12 (1) Essential repairs to prevent abandonment and deterioration of housing in
13 low- and moderate-income neighborhoods.
14 (2) Demolition and rehabilitation of buildings and improvements.
15 (3) Public improvements, including parks, streets, sidewalks, and water and sewer
16 lines.
17 SECTION 11.1.(g) Economic Development. – Funds allocated to the Economic
18 Development Category in subsection (a) of this section shall be made available as grants for
19 eligible activities listed in this subsection. The funds available for grants under this category may
20 be used for all of the following, subject to the national objectives and eligible activities allowed
21 under guidance issued by HUD:
22 (1) Acquisition of real property.
23 (2) Demolition and rehabilitation of buildings and improvements.
24 (3) Removal of material and architectural barriers.
25 (4) Public improvements, including parks, streets, sidewalks, and water and sewer
26 lines.
27 (5) Loans and grants to public or private nonprofit entities for construction and
28 rehabilitation activities.
29 (6) Assistance to private, for-profit entities for economic development.
30 (7) Technical assistance to public or nonprofit entities for neighborhood
31 revitalization or economic development activities.
32 (8) Assistance to for-profit and nonprofit entities to facilitate economic
33 development activities.
34 SECTION 11.1.(h) Infrastructure. – For purposes of this section, eligible activities
35 under the Infrastructure Category in subsection (a) of this section shall be defined as provided in
36 the HUD State Administered Community Development Block Grant definition of the term
37 "infrastructure." Notwithstanding the provisions of subsection (d) of this section, funds allocated
38 to the Infrastructure Category in subsection (a) of this section shall not be reallocated to any other
39 category.
40 SECTION 11.1.(i) Rural Community Development. – Funds allocated for the Rural
41 Community Development Category in subsection (a) of this section shall be made available as
42 grants for eligible activities listed in this subsection. These funds shall provide grants that support
43 community development and comprehensive growth projects to be awarded by the Department
44 of Commerce. The Rural Community Development Category will provide grants to units of local
45 government in development tier one and development tier two areas, as defined in
46 G.S. 143B-437.08, and in rural census tracts, as defined in G.S. 143B-472.127(a)(2), in any other
47 area to support projects that promote broad-based community development activities, increased
48 local investment and economic growth, and stronger and more viable rural neighborhoods. In
49 awarding grants under this section, preference shall be given to projects in development tier one
50 areas, as defined in G.S. 143B-437.08. The funds available for grants under this category may be
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 245
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 used for all of the following, subject to the national objectives and eligible activities allowed
2 under guidance issued by HUD:
3 (1) Essential repairs to prevent abandonment and deterioration of housing in
4 low- and moderate-income neighborhoods.
5 (2) Public improvements, including parks, streets, sidewalks, and water and sewer
6 lines.
7 (3) Public facilities, including neighborhood and community facilities and

16 SECTION 11.1.(j) Deobligated Funds. – Throughout each year, deobligated funds
17 arise in the various funding categories and program years of the Community Development Block
18 Grant (CDBG) program as a result of (i) projects coming in under budget, (ii) projects being
19 canceled, or (iii) projects being required to repay funds. Surplus federal administrative funds in
20 the CDBG program may vary from year to year based upon the amount of State-appropriated
21 funds allocated and the amount of eligible in-kind funds identified. To allow the Department of
22 Commerce and the Department of Environmental Quality to quickly deploy deobligated and
23 surplus federal administrative funds as they are identified throughout the program year, the
24 following shall apply to the use of deobligated CDBG funds and surplus federal administrative
25 funds:
26 (1) All surplus federal administrative funds shall be divided proportionally
27 between the Departments of Commerce and Environmental Quality and shall
28 be used as provided in subdivisions (2) and (3) of this subsection.
29 (2) All deobligated funds allocated to the Department of Commerce and any
30 surplus federal administrative funds, as provided for in subdivision (1) of this
31 subsection, may be used by the Department for all of the following:
32 a. To issue grants in the CDBG Economic Development or
33 Neighborhood Revitalization Program Category.
34 b. For providing training and guidance to local governments relative to
35 the CDBG program, its management, and administrative requirements.
36 c. For any other purpose consistent with the Department's administration
37 of the CDBG program if an equal amount of State matching funds is
38 available.
39 (3) All deobligated funds allocated to the Department of Environmental Quality
40 and any surplus federal administrative funds, as provided for in subdivision
41 (1) of this subsection, may be used by the Department for all of the following:
42 a. To issue grants in the CDBG Infrastructure Category.
43 b. For any other purpose consistent with the Department's administration
44 of the CDBG program if an equal amount of State matching funds is
45 available.

23 NC BIOTECHNOLOGY CENTER
24 SECTION 11.3.(a) Except for the funds appropriated in subsection (b) of this
25 section, funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Commerce for the North Carolina
26 Biotechnology Center (Center) for each fiscal year in the 2025-2027 biennium shall be allocated
27 for the following purposes in the following proportions:
28 (1) Twenty-one percent (21%) for job creation, including funding for the
29 AgBiotech Initiative, economic and industrial development, and related
30 activities.
31 (2) Sixty-five percent (65%) for science and commercialization, including
32 science and technology development, Centers of Innovation, business and
33 technology development, education and training, and related activities.
34 (3) Fourteen percent (14%) for Center operations, including administration,
35 professional and technical assistance and oversight, corporate
36 communications, human resource management, financial and grant
37 administration, legal, and accounting.
38 SECTION 11.3.(b) Of the funds appropriated in this act to the Department of
39 Commerce for the Center, one million dollars ($1,000,000) of recurring funds in each fiscal year
40 of the biennium shall be used to support funding for early stage loans to North Carolina
41 agricultural technology companies.
42 SECTION 11.3.(c) The Center shall not use any of the recurring funds allocated in
43 subsection (b) of this section for administrative costs and shall report on the expenditure of those
44 funds each year pursuant to Section 11.2 of this act.
45 SECTION 11.3.(d) The Center shall prioritize funding and distribution of loans over
46 funding and distribution of grants.
47 SECTION 11.3.(e) Up to ten percent (10%) of the sum of each of the allocations in
48 subsection (a) of this section may be reallocated to subdivision (a)(1) or subdivision (a)(2) of this
49 section if, in the judgment of Center management, the reallocation will advance the mission of
50 the Center.

20 INCREASE UI MAX BENEFIT
21 SECTION 11.6.(a) To maintain the rule of law with respect to State and federal
22 relations pertaining to employment security laws in North Carolina, any executive order issued
23 by the Governor that purports to expand unemployment insurance benefits, whether those
24 benefits will be paid from federal or State funds, is void ab initio unless the executive order is
25 issued upon authority that is conferred expressly by an act enacted by the General Assembly or
26 granted specifically to the Governor by the Congress of the United States.
27 SECTION 11.6.(b) Sections 1, 2, 3, and 4 of Executive Order No. 322, issued by
28 the Governor on October 16, 2024, and concurred to by the Council of State, are ratified and
29 shall terminate on March 1, 2025.

34 If this amount is less than fifteen dollars ($15.00), the individual is not eligible for benefits. The
35 weekly benefit amount may not exceed three hundred fifty dollars ($350.00).four hundred dollars

41 ENERGY SECURITY AND AFFORDABILITY
42 SECTION 11.7.(a) G.S. 62-110.9 reads as rewritten:
43 "§ 62-110.9. Requirements concerning reductions in emissions of carbon dioxide from
44 electric public utilities.
45 The Utilities Commission shall take all reasonable steps to achieve a seventy percent (70%)
46 reduction in emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2) emitted in the State from electric generating
47 facilities owned or operated by electric public utilities from 2005 levels by the year 2030 and that
48 result in carbon neutrality by the year 2050. For purposes of this section, (i) "electric public
49 utility" means any electric public utility as defined in G.S. 62-3(23) serving at least 150,000
50 North Carolina retail jurisdictional customers as of January 1, 2021, and (ii) "carbon neutrality"
51 means for every ton of CO2 emitted in the State from electric generating facilities owned or
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 251
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 operated by or on behalf of electric public utilities, an equivalent amount of CO2 is reduced,
2 removed, prevented, or offset, provided that the offsets are verifiable and do not exceed five
3 percent (5%) of the authorized reduction goal. In achieving the authorized carbon reduction
4 goals, goal, the Utilities Commission shall:
5 (1) Develop a plan, no later than December 31, 2022, 2026, with the electric
6 public utilities, including stakeholder input, for the utilities to achieve the
7 authorized reduction goals, goal of carbon neutrality by the year 2050, which
8 may, at a minimum, consider power generation, transmission and distribution,
9 grid modernization, storage, energy efficiency measures, demand-side
10 management, and the latest technological breakthroughs to achieve the least
11 cost path consistent with this section to achieve compliance with the
12 authorized carbon reduction goals goal (the "Carbon Plan"). The Carbon Plan
13 shall be reviewed every two years and may be adjusted as necessary in the
14 determination of the Commission and the electric public utilities.
15 (2) Comply with current law and practice with respect to the least cost planning
16 for generation, pursuant to G.S. 62-2(a)(3a), in achieving the authorized
17 carbon reduction goals goal and determining generation and resource mix for
18 the future. Any new generation facilities or other resources selected by the
19 Commission in order to achieve the authorized reduction goals goal for
20 electric public utilities shall be owned and recovered on a cost of service basis
21 by the applicable electric public utility except that:
22 a. Existing law shall apply with respect to energy efficiency measures
23 and demand-side management.
24 b. To the extent that new solar generation is selected by the Commission,
25 in adherence with least cost requirements, the solar generation selected
26 shall be subject to the following: (i) forty-five percent (45%) of the
27 total megawatts alternating current (MW AC) of any solar energy
28 facilities established pursuant to this section shall be supplied through
29 the execution of power purchase agreements with third parties
30 pursuant to which the electric public utility purchases solar energy,
31 capacity, and environmental and renewable attributes from solar
32 energy facilities owned and operated by third parties that are 80 MW
33 AC or less that commit to allow the procuring electric public utility
34 rights to dispatch, operate, and control the solicited solar energy
35 facilities in the same manner as the utility's own generating resources
36 and (ii) fifty-five percent (55%) of the total MW AC of any solar
37 energy facilities established pursuant to this section shall be supplied
38 from solar energy facilities that are utility-built or purchased by the
39 utility from third parties and owned and operated and recovered on a
40 cost of service basis by the soliciting electric public utility. These
41 ownership requirements shall be applicable to solar energy facilities
42 (i) paired with energy storage and (ii) procured in connection with any
43 voluntary customer program.
44 (3) Ensure any generation and resource changes maintain or improve upon the
45 adequacy and reliability of the existing grid.
46 (4) Retain discretion to determine optimal timing and generation and
47 resource-mix to achieve the least cost path to compliance with the authorized
48 carbon reduction goals, goal, including discretion in achieving the authorized
49 carbon reduction goals goal by the dates date specified in order to allow for
50 implementation of solutions that would have a more significant and material
51 impact on carbon reduction; provided, however, the Commission shall not
Page 252 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 exceed the dates date specified to achieve the authorized carbon reduction
2 goals goal by more than two years, except in the event the Commission
3 authorizes construction of a nuclear facility or wind energy facility that would
4 require additional time for completion due to technical, legal, logistical, or
5 other factors beyond the control of the electric public utility, or in the event
6 necessary to maintain the adequacy and reliability of the existing grid. In
7 making such determinations, the Utilities Commission shall receive and
8 consider stakeholder input."

18 PART XII. ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY

20 DEQ BASE BUDGET CORRECTIONS
21 SECTION 12.1.(a) To ensure the Department of Environmental Quality's budget
22 conforms with Chapter 143C of the General Statutes, the Department and the Office of State
23 Budget and Management, in consultation with the Fiscal Research Division, shall take all of the
24 following actions prior to the certification of the 2025-2027 budget under G.S. 143C-6-1(c):
25 (1) Remove all negative appropriations from the base budget.
26 (2) Remove all negative full-time equivalent positions from the base budget.
27 (3) Budget all one-time grants on a nonrecurring basis.
28 (4) Remove all intergovernmental transfers from "Other Admin Expenses."
29 (5) Budget all intergovernmental transfers as such with the correct amount
30 receipted to the corresponding expenditure.
31 (6) Correctly budget the base budget corrections enacted in the "Current
32 Operations Appropriations Act of 2023" (S.L. 2023-134).
33 (7) Accurately budget all special funds to not budget the expenditure of cash
34 balances that do not exist.
35 SECTION 12.1.(b) No budgetary action by the Department in accordance with
36 subsection (a) of this section shall increase the Department's net General Fund appropriation.
37 SECTION 12.1.(c) The Department shall report to the Fiscal Research Division on
38 all actions taken under this section within 30 days of the effective date of this act. This report
39 may be in the form of a revised "Worksheet I."

44 RECOVERY FUNDS PRIORITIZATION
45 SECTION 12.2.(a) Directive. – Recipients of funding from the State Fiscal
46 Recovery Fund for water, wastewater, and stormwater projects under Sections 12.13 and 12.14
47 of S.L. 2021-180, as amended, or Section 12.9 of S.L. 2022-74, as amended, shall prioritize
48 spending those funds prior to spending funds from other State or federal sources for other water,
49 wastewater, and stormwater projects. The Department of Environmental Quality and the Office
50 of State Budget and Management shall not approve payments or transfer funds for new water,
51 wastewater, and stormwater project funding through State or federal sources unless the recipient
Page 256 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 for funding is meeting all milestones necessary to spend their funding from the State Fiscal
2 Recovery Fund prior to December 31, 2026.
3
4 2023 WATER AND WASTEWATER GENERAL FUND DEADLINES
5 SECTION 12.2.(b) Deadlines for Project Completions. – Recipients of funding for
6 projects under Section 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134 shall comply with the following schedule:
7 (1) No later than December 31, 2027, provide to the Department of
8 Environmental Quality (Department) a completed request for funding form
9 with a project budget that describes a project that is eligible for funding under
10 applicable State or federal law and consistent with the purposes for the
11 funding as set forth in Section 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134.
12 (2) No later than December 31, 2029, enter into a construction contract for the
13 project.
14 (3) No later than June 30, 2032, expend all funding allocated under Section
15 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134.
16 SECTION 12.2.(c) Extension of Deadline. – The Department may extend the
17 applicable deadline set forth in subsection (b) of this section and set a new deadline with a date
18 certain, if the Department finds good cause for the recipient of funding failing to meet the
19 applicable deadline.
20 SECTION 12.2.(d) Reversion of Unspent Funds. – If a recipient for funding under
21 Section 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134 (i) fails to meet any of the deadlines set forth in subsection (b)
22 or (c) of this section or (ii) complies with the applicable deadline but there remains unexpended
23 or unbudgeted funds in excess of the needs of the eligible project, then unencumbered funds shall
24 revert in accordance with Section 12.2(c) of S.L. 2023-134 on the next business day after the
25 applicable deadline has passed.
26 SECTION 12.2.(e) Reallocation of Reverted Funds. – In reallocating funds reverted
27 under subsection (d) of this section, the Department shall prioritize other projects that are
28 allocated funds under Section 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134 that the State Water Infrastructure
29 Authority finds can no longer be completed due to unavoidable cost overruns. For purposes of
30 this subsection, an unavoidable cost overrun is an increase in the cost of a project since September
31 1, 2023, due to increases in labor, material, or engineering costs for the project as described in
32 the first request for funding submitted to the Department after that date. A change in project size
33 or scope is not an unavoidable cost overrun.
34 SECTION 12.2.(f) Reporting Requirement. – Beginning October 30, 2025, and no
35 later than 30 days after the end of each subsequent quarter thereafter, the Department shall report
36 to (i) the chairs of the House Appropriations Committee on Agriculture and Natural and
37 Economic Resources, (ii) the chairs of the Senate Appropriations Committee on Agriculture,
38 Natural, and Economic Resources, (iii) each member who represents a district with an active
39 project under Section 12.2(e) of S.L. 2023-134, and (iv) the Fiscal Research Division detailing,
40 at a minimum, each project's progress and funding status. This reporting requirement expires
41 when all funds are expended and those projects are completed.

43 2025 WATER SAFETY ACT
44
45 PFAS MITIGATION GRANTS
46 SECTION 12.3.(a) Mitigation Grants. – Article 9 of Chapter 130A of the General
47 Statutes is amended by adding a new Part to read:
48 "Part 9. PFAS Mitigation.
49 "§ 130A-310.80. Definitions.
50 In addition to the definitions in G.S. 130A-2 and G.S. 130A-290, the following definitions
51 apply in this Part:
Page 258 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (1) Distressed unit. – As defined in G.S. 159G-20.
2 (2) Fund. – The PFAS Mitigation Fund established in G.S. 130A-310.84.
3 (3) PFAS. – Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, including perfluorooctanoic
4 acid (PFOA), perfluorooctanesulfonate (PFOS), hexafluoropropylene oxide
5 dimer acid (HFPO-DA, also known as GenX), perfluorohexanesulfonic acid
6 (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), and perfluorobutanesulfonic acid
7 (PFBS).
8 (4) PFAS precursors. – PFAS, PAP (polyfluoroalkyl phosphate esters), FTA (fire
9 training area), FTS (6:2-fluorotelomersulfonic acid), and FBSA
10 (perfluorobutane sulfonamide).
11 (5) SWIA. – The State Water Infrastructure Authority established in Article 5 of
12 Chapter 159G of the General Statutes.
13 "§ 130A-310.82. Purpose.
14 The purpose of this Part is to provide funding to support the mitigation of the impacts of
15 PFAS on local public water and wastewater systems.
16 "§ 130A-310.84. PFAS Mitigation Fund.
17 (a) Fund Established. – The PFAS Mitigation Fund is established within the Department.
18 The purpose of the Fund is to support statewide efforts to detect, reduce, mitigate, and prevent
19 exposure to PFAS and PFAS precursors and to support scientific research and technology
20 development related to PFAS removal, treatment, monitoring, and precursor identification. The
21 fund consists of any funds appropriated to it by the General Assembly, and grants from federal
22 agencies or other non-State entities.
23 (b) Uses of Fund. – The Fund may only be used by SWIA to provide grants to units of
24 local government operating public water or wastewater treatment systems for any of the
25 following:
26 (1) PFAS sampling and monitoring in drinking water, wastewater, surface water,
27 and groundwater.
28 (2) Installation or upgrade of water treatment technologies for PFAS removal.
29 (c) Funding Criteria and Oversight. – SWIA shall establish criteria and application
30 procedures for local PFAS response grants and shall prioritize grants to public water systems and
31 public wastewater systems (i) for which contamination from PFAS has caused the greatest
32 impacts on public health and the environment and (ii) that are, or meet the criteria to be
33 categorized as, a distressed unit.
34 (d) Report. – SWIA shall report annually as a part of the report required by G.S. 159G-72
35 regarding projects funded under this section. The report shall include the project type (sampling
36 and monitoring, treatment technologies, or emergency response), the project recipient, a brief
37 description of the project, and the amount of funding provided."
38 SECTION 12.3.(b) Conforming Change. – G.S. 159G-71 reads as rewritten:
39 "§ 159G-71. State Water Infrastructure Authority; powers and duties.
40 The Authority has the following additional duties:
41 …
42 (13) To award grants to mitigate the impacts of PFAS on local public water and
43 wastewater systems."
44 SECTION 12.3.(c) Funding. – Funds appropriated in this act from the Stabilization
45 and Inflation Reserve (SIR) established in Section 2.2(q) of S.L. 2022-74 to the Department of
46 Environmental Quality are allocated to the PFAS Mitigation Fund established in Part 9 of Article
47 9 of Chapter 130A of the General Statutes, as enacted by subsection (a) of this section.
48
49 COLLABORATORY RESEARCH GRANTS
50 SECTION 12.3.(d) PFAS Research Funding. – Funds appropriated in this act from
51 the SIR to the North Carolina Collaboratory at the University of North Carolina (Collaboratory)
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 259
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 shall be used to support scientific research on PFAS and PFAS precursors, as defined in
2 G.S. 130A-310.80, conducted by or in collaboration with public or nonprofit academic
3 institutions, including any of the following:
4 (1) Detection methods for known and emerging PFAS and PFAS precursors.
5 (2) Fate and transport of PFAS and PFAS precursors in environmental media.
6 (3) Innovative PFAS remediation, filtration, removal, and destruction
7 technologies.
8 (4) Public health and toxicological impact assessments of PFAS and PFAS
9 precursors.
10 (5) Evaluation of the health impacts of PFAS mixtures found in the State's
11 drinking water to more closely model real-world public health scenarios.
12 (6) Replacement compounds for PFAS and PFAS precursors.
13 SECTION 12.3.(e) Directive. – The Collaboratory shall consult with affected
14 stakeholders, scientific experts, and State and local officials to ensure funding is targeted to
15 research in areas of highest environmental and public health impact.
16 SECTION 12.3.(f) Report. – The Collaboratory shall include in the report required
17 by G.S. 116-256 documentation of its use of the funds allocated by this section and updates
18 regarding the research funded by this section.

20 ALGAL BLOOM PROTECTION
21 SECTION 12.5.(a) Article 21 of Chapter 143 of the General Statutes is amended by
22 adding a new Part to read:
23 "Part 8E. Algal Control Grant Program.
24 "§ 143-215.73N. Algal Control Grant Program.
25 (a) Program; Purpose. – The Department of Environmental Quality shall establish the
26 Algal Control Grant Program (Program) to assist units of local government with the control,
27 prevention, and abatement of harmful algal blooms caused by cyanobacteria at public watercraft
28 launching or docking areas. The Department shall adopt rules for the disbursement of the grants
29 pursuant to this section.
30 (b) Grants. – To the extent funds are made available for the Program, the Department
31 shall award units of local government grants on a first-come, first-served basis, in accordance
32 with this section. The Department shall prioritize awarding grants to units of local government
33 that did not receive an award pursuant to the Program in the previous fiscal year. No entity shall
34 receive more than one grant per fiscal year. The Department shall establish criteria for initial and
35 continuing eligibility for participating entities.
36 (c) Local Match Requirement. – Units of local government shall match grant funds
37 received under this section in accordance with this subsection. A local match may include cash,
38 fee waivers, in-kind services, the donation of assets, the provision of infrastructure, or a
39 combination of these. Units of local government shall provide matching contributions as follows:
40 (1) For a local government in a development tier one area, as defined in
41 G.S. 143B-437.08, the State shall provide no more than three dollars ($3.00)
42 for every one dollar ($1.00) provided by the local government.
43 (2) For a local government in a development tier two area, as defined in
44 G.S. 143B-437.08, the State shall provide no more than two dollars ($2.00)
45 for every one dollar ($1.00) provided by the local government.
46 (3) For a local government in a development tier three area, as defined in
47 G.S. 143B-437.08, the State shall provide no more than one dollar ($1.00) for
48 every one dollar ($1.00) provided by the local government.
49 (d) Reporting. – No later than October 1, 2026, and every year thereafter that funds are
50 made available for the Program, the Department shall report to the Joint Legislative Oversight
51 Committee on Agriculture and Natural and Economic Resources on the entities receiving grants
Page 260 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 under the Program; the specific control, prevention, and abatement methods conducted with the
2 funds; and the impact of the Program on protecting the waters of the State from harmful algal
3 blooms in the previous fiscal year.
4 (e) Administrative Expenses. – The Department may use up to one hundred thousand
5 dollars ($100,000) in each fiscal year for administrative expenses."
6 SECTION 12.5.(b) G.S. 143-215.73F(b) is amended by adding a new subdivision
7 to read:
8 "(2a) To provide funding for grants issued pursuant to the Algal Control Grant
9 Program under Part 8E of Article 21 of this Chapter. Funding for algal control
10 projects is limited to one million two hundred thousand dollars ($1,200,000)
11 in each fiscal year."

13 BEACH AND INLET MANAGEMENT PLAN AND REPORT
14 SECTION 12.6.(a) Article 21 of Chapter 143 of the General Statutes is amended by
15 adding a new Part 8E, to be entitled "Beach and Inlet Management Planning." Section 4.9 of S.L.
16 2017-10 is repealed. Section 13.9 of S.L. 2000-67 is codified within Part 8E, as follows:
17 (1) Section 13.9(a) is codified as G.S. 143-215.73N, to be entitled "Findings."
18 (2) Sections 13.9(b), 13.9(c), and 13.9(d) are codified as subsections (a), (b), and
19 (c) of G.S. 143-215.73O, to be entitled "Beach and inlet management plan."
20 (3) Section 13.9(e) is repealed.
21 (4) Section 13.9(f) is codified as G.S. 143-215.73P, to be entitled "Federal funds;
22 matching."
23 SECTION 12.6.(b) Part 8E of Article 21 of Chapter 143 of the General Statutes, as
24 enacted by subsection (a) of this section, reads as rewritten:
25 "Part 8E. Beach and Inlet Management Planning.
26 "§ 143-215.73N. Findings.
27 The General Assembly makes the following findings:
28 (1) North Carolina has 320 miles of ocean beach, including some of the most
29 pristine and attractive beaches in the country.
30 (2) The balance between economic development and quality of life in North
31 Carolina has made our coast one of the most desirable along the Atlantic
32 Seaboard.
33 (3) North Carolina's beaches are vital to the State's tourism industry.
34 (4) North Carolina's beaches belong to all the State's citizens and provide
35 recreational and economic benefits to our residents statewide.
36 (5) Beach erosion can threaten the economic viability of coastal communities and
37 can significantly affect State tax revenues.
38 (6) The Atlantic Seaboard is vulnerable to hurricanes and other coastal storms,
39 and it is prudent to take precautions such as beach nourishment that protect
40 and conserve the State's beaches and reduce property damage and flooding.
41 (7) Beach renourishment as an erosion control method provides hurricane flood
42 protection, enhances the attractiveness of beaches to tourists, restores habitat
43 for turtles, shorebirds, and plants, and provides additional public access to
44 beaches.
45 (8) Federal policy previously favored and assisted voluntary movement of
46 structures threatened by erosion, but this assistance is no longer available.
47 (9) Relocation of structures threatened by erosion is sometimes the best available
48 remedy for the property owner and is in the public interest.
49 (10) Public parking and public access areas are needed for use by the general public
50 to enable their enjoyment of North Carolina's beaches.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 261
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (11) Acquisition of high erosion hazard property by local or State agencies can
2 reduce risk to citizens and property, reduce costs to insurance policyholders,
3 improve public access to beaches and waterways, and protect the
4 environment.
5 (12) Beach nourishment projects such as those at Wrightsville Beach and Carolina
6 Beach have been very successful and greatly reduced property damage during
7 Hurricane Fran.hurricanes and other coastal storms that have impacted the
8 State's coast.
9 (13) Because local beach communities derive the primary benefits from the
10 presence of adequate beaches, a program of beach management and
11 restoration should not be accomplished without a commitment of local funds
12 to combat the problem of beach erosion.
13 (14) The With limited exceptions, the State of North Carolina prohibits seawalls
14 and hardening the shoreline to prevent destroying the public's beaches.
15 (15) Beach nourishment is encouraged by both the Coastal Resources Commission
16 and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers as a method to control beach erosion.
17 (16) The Department of Environment and Natural Resources Environmental
18 Quality has statutory authority to assist local governments in financing beach
19 nourishment projects and is the sponsor of several federal navigation projects
20 that result in dredging beach-quality sand.
21 (17) It is declared to be a necessary governmental responsibility to properly
22 manage and protect North Carolina's beaches from erosion and that good
23 planning is needed to assure a cost-effective and equitable approach to beach
24 management and restoration, and that as part of a comprehensive response to
25 beach erosion, sound policies are needed to facilitate the ability of landowners
26 to move threatened structures and to allow public acquisition of appropriate
27 parcels of land for public beach access.
28 "§ 143-215.73O. Beach and inlet management plan.
29 (a) The Department of Environment and Natural Resources Environmental Quality shall
30 compile and evaluate information on the current conditions and erosion rates of beaches, on
31 coastal geology, and on storm and erosion hazards for use in developing a State plan and strategy
32 for beach management and restoration. The Department of Environment and Natural Resources
33 Environmental Quality shall make this information available to local governments for use in
34 land-use planning.
35 (b) The Department of Environment and Natural Resources shall develop a multiyear
36 beach management and restoration strategy and plan that does all of the following:
37 (1) Utilizes the data and expertise available in the Divisions of Water Resources,
38 Coastal Management, and Energy, Mineral, and Land Resources.
39 (2) Identifies the erosion rate at each beach community and estimates the degree
40 of vulnerability to storm and hurricane damage.
41 (3) Uses the best available geological and geographical information to determine
42 the need for and probable effectiveness of beach nourishment.
43 (4) Provides for coordination with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the North
44 Carolina Department of Transportation, the North Carolina Division of
45 Emergency Management, and other State and federal agencies concerned with
46 beach management issues.
47 (5) Provides a status report on all U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' beach protection
48 projects in the planning, construction, or operational stages.
49 (6) Makes maximum feasible use of suitable sand dredged from navigation
50 channels for beach nourishment to avoid the loss of this resource and to reduce
51 equipment mobilization costs.
Page 262 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition
General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (7) Promotes inlet sand bypassing where needed to replicate the natural flow of
2 sand interrupted by inlets.
3 (8) Provides for geological and environmental assessments to locate suitable
4 materials for beach nourishment.
5 (9) Considers the regional context of beach communities to determine the most
6 cost-effective approach to beach nourishment.
7 (10) Provides for and requires adequate public beach access, including
8 handicapped access.
9 (11) Recommends priorities for State funding for beach nourishment projects,
10 based on the amount of erosion occurring, the potential damage to property
11 and to the economy, the benefits for recreation and tourism, the adequacy of
12 public access, the availability of local government matching funds, the status
13 of project planning, the adequacy of project engineering, the
14 cost-effectiveness of the project, and the environmental impacts.
15 (11a) Includes a four-year cycle of planned maintenance and resiliency projects for
16 the State's beaches and inlets.
17 (12) Includes recommendations on obtaining the maximum available federal
18 financial assistance for beach nourishment.
19 (13) Is subject to a public hearing to receive citizen input.
20 (c) Each plan shall be as complete as resources and available information allow. The
21 Department of Environment and Natural Resources Environmental Quality shall revise the plan
22 every two years and shall submit the revised plan to the General Assembly no later than March
23 1 of each odd-numbered year. The Department may issue a supplement to the plan in
24 even-numbered years if significant new information becomes available.
25 "§ 143-215.73P. Federal funds; matching.
26 In the event that federal funds become available for planning and developing shore protection
27 projects, the State shall match those funds in accordance with the funding guidelines set out in
28 G.S. 143-215.71."
29 SECTION 12.6.(c) The Department of Environmental Quality shall provide an
30 interim report no later than March 1, 2026, on its progress toward updating the beach and inlet
31 management plan and meeting the March 1, 2027, deadline set forth in G.S. 143-215.73O(c), as
32 enacted by subsection (b) of this section. The report shall be provided to the Environmental
33 Review Commission, the Joint Legislative Oversight Commission on Agriculture and Natural
34 and Economic Resources, and the Fiscal Research Division.

3 DEQ PERMITTING TRANSPARENCY
4 SECTION 12.13. The Department of Environmental Quality shall track and report
5 on the work hours, duties, and output of its employees whose job description includes permitting
6 duties (permitting employees). On a quarterly basis, the Department of Environmental Quality
7 shall prepare and submit a performance report of its permitting employees to the Joint Legislative
8 Commission on Governmental Operations and the Office of the State Auditor. The report shall
9 include all of the following information for each permitting employee:
10 (1) Position title and number.
11 (2) If the employee has been in the position for less than six months, an indication
12 of this fact on the report.
13 (3) Total hours worked during the quarter.
14 (4) Total hours worked on permitting-related activities.
15 (5) If permitting-related activities account for less than ninety percent (90%) of
16 the employee's quarterly work hours, a brief list of other duties performed.
17 (6) All permit applications approved or denied, including all of the following
18 information for each application:
19 a. Applicant name.
20 b. Type of permit.
21 c. Date application was received.
22 d. Duration of information request period.
23 e. Date permit was issued or denied.

27 PART XIV. NATURAL AND CULTURAL RESOURCES

29 NC SYMPHONY CHALLENGE GRANT
30 SECTION 14.1.(a) Of the funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Natural
31 and Cultural Resources, the sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in recurring funds for each
32 year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall be allocated to the North Carolina Symphony as
33 provided in this section. It is the intent of the General Assembly that the North Carolina
34 Symphony raise at least seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in non-State funds for the 2025-2026
35 fiscal year and seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in non-State funds for the 2026-2027 fiscal
36 year. The North Carolina Symphony cannot use funds transferred from the organization's
37 endowment to its operating budget to achieve the fundraising targets set out in subsections (b)
38 and (c) of this section.
39 SECTION 14.1.(b) For the 2025-2026 fiscal year, the North Carolina Symphony
40 shall receive allocations from the Department of Natural and Cultural Resources as follows:
41 (1) Upon raising the initial sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in non-State
42 funding, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the sum of six hundred
43 thousand dollars ($600,000).
44 (2) Upon raising an additional sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in
45 non-State funding for a total amount of four million dollars ($4,000,000) in
46 non-State funds, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the sum of seven
47 hundred thousand dollars ($700,000).
48 (3) Upon raising an additional sum of three million dollars ($3,000,000) in
49 non-State funding for a total amount of seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in
50 non-State funds, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the final sum of
51 seven hundred thousand dollars ($700,000) in the 2025-2026 fiscal year.

15 SUNDAY OPENING STATE HISTORIC SITE PILOT PROGRAM
16 SECTION 14.2.(a) Program Established. – Funds appropriated in this act to the
17 Department of Natural and Cultural Resources (Department) for the Sunday Opening State
18 Historic Site Pilot Program (Program) shall be used by the Department to open and operate the
19 following State Historic Sites on Sundays during each site's peak season:
20 (1) Bentonville Battlefield.
21 (2) Brunswick Town/Fort Anderson.
22 (3) CSS Neuse.
23 (4) Charlotte Hawkins Brown Museum.
24 (5) Fort Fisher.
25 (6) Governor Charles B. Aycock Birthplace.
26 (7) Historic Bath.
27 (8) Historic Edenton.
28 (9) Historic Halifax.
29 (10) North Carolina State Capitol.
30 (11) Reed Gold Mine.
31 (12) Roanoke Island Festival Park.
32 (13) Somerset Place.
33 (14) Thomas Day State Historic Site.
34 SECTION 14.2.(b) Notice. – The Department shall publish, update, or provide
35 notice of the new operating hours pursuant to the Program established in subsection (a) of this
36 section.
37 SECTION 14.2.(c) Reports. – The Department shall submit the following reports to
38 the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Agriculture and Natural and Economic Resources:
39 (1) By October 1, 2026, an interim report with (i) actual costs by site during the
40 2025-2026 fiscal year, (ii) Sunday visitation numbers by site during the
41 2025-2026 fiscal year, and (iii) preliminary recommendations.
42 (2) By April 1, 2027, an interim report with any funding recommendations the
43 Department has for the upcoming biennium.
44 (3) By October 1, 2027, a final report on the implementation of the Program.
