7 REMOVE EXTRATERRITORIAL JURISDICTION AUTHORITY
8 SECTION 5.11.(a) G.S. 160D-201 reads as rewritten:
9 "§ 160D-201. Planning and development regulation jurisdiction.
10 (a) Cities. – All of the powers granted by this Chapter may be exercised by any city within
11 its corporate limits and within any extraterritorial area established pursuant to
12 G.S. 160D-202.limits.
13 …."
14 SECTION 5.11.(b) G.S. 160D-202 reads as rewritten:
15 "§ 160D-202. Municipal extraterritorial Transfer or relinquishment of jurisdiction.
16 (a) Geographic Scope. – Any city may exercise the powers granted to cities under this
17 Chapter within a defined area extending not more than one mile beyond its contiguous corporate
18 limits. In addition, a city of 10,000 or more population but less than 25,000 may exercise these
19 powers over an area extending not more than two miles beyond its limits and a city of 25,000 or
20 more population may exercise these powers over an area extending not more than three miles
21 beyond its limits. In determining the population of a city for the purposes of this Chapter, the city
22 council and the board of county commissioners may use the most recent annual estimate of
23 population as certified by the Secretary of the North Carolina Department of Administration.
24 Pursuant to G.S. 160A-58.4, extraterritorial municipal planning and development regulation may
25 be extended only from the primary corporate boundary of a city and not from the boundary of
26 satellite areas of the city.
27 (b) Authority in the Extraterritorial Area. – A city may not exercise any power conferred
28 by this Chapter in its extraterritorial jurisdiction that it is not exercising within its corporate limits.
29 A city may exercise in its extraterritorial area all powers conferred by this Chapter that it is
30 exercising within its corporate limits. If a city fails to extend a particular type of development
31 regulation to the extraterritorial area, the county may elect to exercise that particular type of
32 regulation in the extraterritorial area.
33 (c) County Approval of City Jurisdiction. – Notwithstanding subsection (a) of this
34 section, no city may extend its extraterritorial powers into any area for which the county has
35 adopted and is enforcing county zoning and subdivision regulations. However, the city may do
36 so where the county is not exercising both of these powers, or when the city and the county have
37 agreed upon the area within which each will exercise the powers conferred by this Chapter. No
38 city may extend its extraterritorial powers beyond one mile from its corporate limits without the
39 approval of the board or boards of county commissioners with jurisdiction over the area.
40 (d) Notice of Proposed Jurisdiction Change. – Any municipality proposing to exercise
41 extraterritorial jurisdiction under this Chapter shall notify the owners of all parcels of land
42 proposed for addition to the area of extraterritorial jurisdiction, as shown on the county tax
43 records. The notice shall be sent by first-class mail to the last addresses listed for affected
44 property owners in the county tax records. The notice shall inform the landowner of the effect of
45 the extension of extraterritorial jurisdiction, of the landowner's right to participate in a legislative
46 hearing prior to adoption of any ordinance extending the area of extraterritorial jurisdiction, as
47 provided in G.S. 160D-601, and of the right of all residents of the area to apply to the board of
48 county commissioners to serve as a representative on the planning board and the board of
49 adjustment, as provided in G.S. 160D-303. The notice shall be mailed at least 30 days prior to
50 the date of the hearing. The person or persons mailing the notices shall certify to the city council
Page 24 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 that the notices were sent by first-class mail, and the certificate shall be deemed conclusive in the
2 absence of fraud.
3 (e) Boundaries. – Any council exercising extraterritorial jurisdiction under this Chapter
4 shall adopt an ordinance specifying the areas to be included based upon existing or projected
5 urban development and areas of critical concern to the city, as evidenced by officially adopted
6 plans for its development. A single jurisdictional boundary shall be applicable for all powers
7 conferred in this Chapter. Boundaries shall be defined, to the extent feasible, in terms of
8 geographical features identifiable on the ground. Boundaries may follow parcel ownership
9 boundaries. A council may, in its discretion, exclude from its extraterritorial jurisdiction areas
10 lying in another county, areas separated from the city by barriers to urban growth, or areas whose
11 projected development will have minimal impact on the city. The boundaries specified in the
12 ordinance shall at all times be drawn on a map, set forth in a written description, or shown by a
13 combination of these techniques. This delineation shall be maintained in the manner provided in
14 G.S. 160A-22 for the delineation of the corporate limits and shall be recorded in the office of the
15 register of deeds of each county in which any portion of the area lies.
16 Where the extraterritorial jurisdiction of two or more cities overlaps, the jurisdictional
17 boundary between them shall be a line connecting the midway points of the overlapping area
18 unless the city councils agree to another boundary line within the overlapping area based upon
19 existing or projected patterns of development.
20 (f) County Authority Within City Jurisdiction. – The county may, on request of the city
21 council, exercise any or all of these the powers granted in this Chapter in any or all areas lying
22 within the city's corporate limits or within the city's specified area of extraterritorial
23 jurisdiction.limits.
24 (g) Transfer of Jurisdiction. – When a city annexes, annexes or a new city is incorporated
25 in, or a city extends its jurisdiction to include, in an area that is currently being regulated by the
26 county, the county development regulations and powers of enforcement shall remain in effect
27 until (i) the city has adopted such development regulations or (ii) a period of 60 days has elapsed
28 following the annexation, extension, annexation or incorporation, whichever is sooner. Prior to
29 the transfer of jurisdiction, the city may hold hearings and take any other measures consistent
30 with G.S. 160D-204 that may be required in order to adopt and apply its development regulations
31 for the area at the same time it assumes jurisdiction.
32 (h) Relinquishment of Jurisdiction. – When a city relinquishes jurisdiction over an area
33 that it is regulating under this Chapter to a county, the city development regulations and powers
34 of enforcement shall remain in effect until (i) the county has adopted such development
35 regulation or (ii) a period of 60 days has elapsed following the action by which the city
36 relinquished jurisdiction, whichever is sooner. Prior to the transfer of jurisdiction, the county
37 may hold hearings and take other measures consistent with G.S. 160D-204 that may be required
38 in order to adopt and apply its development regulations for the area at the same time it assumes
39 jurisdiction.
40 (i) Process for Local Government Approval. – When a local government is granted
41 powers by this section subject to the request, approval, or agreement of another local government,
42 the request, approval, or agreement shall be evidenced by a formally adopted resolution of the
43 governing board of the local government. Any such request, approval, or agreement can be
44 rescinded upon two years' written notice to the other governing boards concerned by repealing
45 the resolution. The resolution may be modified at any time by mutual agreement of the governing
46 boards concerned.
47 (j) Local Acts. – Nothing in this section shall repeal, modify, or amend any local act that
48 defines the boundaries of a city's extraterritorial jurisdiction by metes and bounds or courses and
49 distances.
50 (k) Effect on Vested Rights. – Whenever a city or county, pursuant to this section,
51 acquires jurisdiction over a territory that theretofore has been subject to the jurisdiction of another
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 25

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 local government, any person who has acquired vested rights in the surrendering jurisdiction may
2 exercise those rights as if no change of jurisdiction had occurred. The city or county acquiring
3 jurisdiction may take any action regarding such a development approval, certificate, or other
4 evidence of compliance that could have been taken by the local government surrendering
5 jurisdiction pursuant to its development regulations. Except as provided in this subsection, any
6 building, structure, or other land use in a territory over which a city or county has acquired
7 jurisdiction is subject to the development regulations of the city or county."

16 "§ 160A-58.4. Extraterritorial powers.
17 Satellite corporate limits shall not be considered a part of the city's corporate limits for the
18 purposes of extraterritorial land-use regulation pursuant to G.S. 160D-202 or abatement of public
19 health nuisances pursuant to G.S. 160A-193. However, a city's power to regulate land use
20 pursuant to Chapter 160D of the General Statutes or to abate public health nuisances pursuant to
21 G.S. 160A-193, shall be the same within satellite corporate limits as within its primary corporate
22 limits."
23 SECTION 5.11.(s) G.S. 160A-176.1(a) reads as rewritten:
24 "(a) A city may adopt ordinances to regulate and control swimming, surfing and littering
25 in the Atlantic Ocean adjacent to that portion of the city within its boundaries or within its
26 extraterritorial jurisdiction; boundaries; provided, however, nothing contained herein shall be
27 construed to permit any city to prohibit altogether swimming and surfing or to make these
28 activities unlawful."

43 SECTION 5.11.(w) Any provision in a local act that grants a city the power to
44 exercise extraterritorial planning jurisdiction under Article 19 of Chapter 160A of the General
45 Statutes, or its successor Chapter 160D of the General Statutes, is hereby repealed.

48 as stated:

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) For counties with a population between 25,001 and 75,000 according to the
2 last federal decennial census, October 1, 2027.
3 SECTION 5.11.(y) No city may expand its extraterritorial jurisdiction beyond the
4 territory that the city was exercising extraterritorial jurisdiction authority upon as of June 1, 2025.
5 SECTION 5.11.(z) Cities continuing to exercise extraterritorial jurisdiction
6 authority shall continue to appoint representation on boards in accordance with G.S. 160D-307.
7 SECTION 5.11.(aa) The relinquishment of jurisdiction over an area that a city is
8 regulating under the authority of extraterritorial planning jurisdiction under Article 19 of Chapter
9 160A of the General Statutes, or its successor Chapter 160D of the General Statutes, shall be
10 determined by the county in which the area lies, not the city which has been exercising
11 extraterritorial jurisdiction over the area, and becomes effective as follows:
12 (1) For counties with a population of 25,000 or less according to the last federal
13 decennial census, October 1, 2026.
14 (2) For counties with a population between 25,001 and 75,000 according to the
15 last federal decennial census, October 1, 2027.
16 SECTION 5.11.(bb) Nothing in this section shall be construed as prohibiting a city
17 from relinquishing jurisdiction over an area prior to the effective date set forth in subsection (aa)
18 of this section so long as the city complies with the provisions of Article 19 of Chapter 160A of
19 the General Statutes, or its successor Chapter 160D of the General Statutes.
20 SECTION 5.11.(cc) Upon relinquishment of jurisdiction over an area that a city is
21 regulating under the authority of extraterritorial planning jurisdiction under Article 19 of Chapter
22 160A of the General Statutes, or its successor Chapter 160D of the General Statutes:
23 (1) The city regulations and powers of enforcement shall remain in effect until
24 the earlier of the effective date of the land use regulations adopted by the
25 county with jurisdiction over the area or 60 days after the effective date set
26 forth in subsection (aa) of this section for that county. If the sixtieth day falls
27 on a holiday or weekend, the next business day shall be treated as the sixtieth
28 day. The county may hold hearings and take other measures that may be
29 required in order to adopt county regulations for the area prior to the effective
30 date set forth in subsection (aa) of this section for that county.
31 (2) Any person who has acquired vested rights under a permit, certificate, or other
32 evidence of compliance issued by the city may exercise those rights as if no
33 change of jurisdiction had occurred. The county acquiring jurisdiction may
34 take any action regarding the permit, certificate, or other evidence of
35 compliance that could have been taken by the city surrendering jurisdiction
36 pursuant to the city ordinances and regulations. Except as provided in this
37 section, any building, structure, or other land use in a territory over which a
38 county has acquired jurisdiction is subject to the ordinances and regulations
39 of the county. At least 180 days prior to the effective date set forth in
40 subsection (aa) of this section for the county in which any portion of a city
41 lies, the city shall notify the county of the following:
42 a. The boundaries of the city's extraterritorial jurisdiction in that county.
43 b. The existing land use regulations applying to that extraterritorial
44 jurisdiction in that county, including zoning and overlay maps.
45 c. Any pending requests for amendments or other changes to the existing
46 land use regulations applying to that extraterritorial jurisdiction in that
47 county.
48 d. Any vested rights with respect to properties in the extraterritorial
49 jurisdiction in that county.
50 SECTION 5.11.(dd) Sections 5.10 and 5.11 of this act shall have no effect on the
51 extraterritorial jurisdiction of law enforcement officers as authorized in any of the following:

9 PART V-A. HELENE RELATED FUNDING AND FLEXIBILITY
10
11 HELENE RELATED FUNDING: COMPETITIVE GRANT PRIORITIZATION
12 SECTION 5A.1.(a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, for the 2025-2027
13 fiscal biennium, the following programs and funds, which collectively represent over seven
14 hundred fifty million dollars ($750,000,000) in the base budget from competitive grant programs
15 over the biennium, shall prioritize applicants from counties that (i) were designated, in whole or
16 in part, by the United States Department of Housing and Urban Development as the most
17 impacted and distressed counties from Hurricane Helene and (ii) have a population of 300,000
18 or fewer based upon the 2023 Certified County Population Estimates from the State
19 Demographer:
20 (1) Needs-Based Public School Capital Fund, established in Article 38B of
21 Chapter 115C of the General Statutes. The matching requirement under
22 G.S. 115C-546.11 is waived for applicants qualifying under this subsection
23 for prioritization.
24 (2) Community Health Grant Program administered by the Department of Health
25 and Human Services, Division of Central Management, Office of Rural Health
26 (Rural Health).
27 (3) State-Designated Rural Health Centers Support Grant Program and Rural
28 Health Capital Grants Program administered by Rural Health.
29 (4) Agricultural Water Resources Assistance Program, established under Article
30 5 of Chapter 139 of the General Statutes.
31 (5) Utility Account, established under G.S. 143B-437.01.
32 (6) Rural Engagement & Investment Program under the Department of
33 Commerce, Rural Economic Development Division.
34 (7) Parks and Recreation Trust Fund, established under G.S. 143B-135.56.
35 (8) North Carolina Land and Water Fund, established under G.S. 143B-135.234.
36 (9) Grants administered by the North Carolina Arts Council, established under
37 Part 14 of Article 2 of Chapter 143B of the General Statutes.
38 (10) Grants awarded by the State Water Infrastructure Authority from the State
39 Drinking Water and Wastewater Reserves.
40 SECTION 5A.1.(b) Each agency overseeing the administration of funds from the
41 programs and funds listed in subsection (a) of this section shall require applicants seeking
42 prioritization to attest that (i) the application for funds is for repair, replacement, or construction
43 of equipment, buildings, or natural features due to damage or effects from Hurricane Helene,
44 including capacity-building, and (ii) the amount of funds requested is the amount of unmet need
45 above the amount paid by insurance and available federal aid.
46
47 HELENE RELATED FUNDING: STATE AGENCIES OPERATIONS
48 SECTION 5A.2. In order to augment State funds that are available for operational
49 needs of various State agencies resulting from recovery and relief efforts related to damage and
50 suffering caused by Hurricane Helene, the General Assembly has identified and transferred to
51 the Helene Fund funds from other reserves, in the amount of seven hundred million dollars
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 31

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 ($700,000,000), that are currently unutilized or underutilized and has used a portion of those
2 funds for multiple agencies, as provided elsewhere in this act, including the North Carolina
3 Community College System, the Department of Commerce, the Office of the Governor, and The
4 University of North Carolina.
5
6 HELENE RELATED FUNDING: SEWER/WATER
7 SECTION 5A.3. The General Assembly finds that the supplemental appropriations
8 provided by Congress in the American Relief Act of 2025 (P.L. 118-158) and allocated by the
9 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency to the Clean Water State Revolving Fund and the
10 Drinking Water State Revolving Fund for wastewater treatment works, drinking water facilities,
11 and decentralized wastewater treatment systems in the State impacted by Hurricane Helene
12 should be expeditiously used for repair and replacement of drinking water and wastewater
13 infrastructure damaged by Hurricane Helene.
14 Accordingly, the General Assembly has appropriated in this act for the 2025-2026
15 fiscal year the sum of six hundred eighty-five million six hundred thirteen thousand dollars
16 ($685,613,000) in federal disaster funding in this act, which is allocated in and for the following
17 amounts and uses:
18 (1) Two hundred fifty-three million six hundred eighty-one thousand dollars
19 ($253,681,000) to the Clean Water State Revolving Fund established in
20 G.S. 159G-22(b) (CWSRF) and four hundred nine million four hundred
21 twenty-two thousand dollars ($409,422,000) to the Drinking Water State
22 Revolving Fund established in G.S. 159G-22(c) (DWSRF) for funding of
23 projects consistent with applicable federal law and guidance to CWSRF and
24 DWSRF eligible entities that were damaged, can demonstrate impact, or
25 experienced a loss or disruption of a mission-essential function caused by
26 Hurricane Helene.
27 (2) Twenty-two million five hundred ten thousand dollars ($22,510,000) to the
28 CWSRF to improve the resilience of decentralized wastewater treatment
29 systems to flooding, to assess the potential to connect homes served by
30 decentralized wastewater treatment systems to centralized wastewater
31 systems, and to fund such connections.
32
33 HELENE RELATED FUNDING: TRANSPORTATION
34 SECTION 5A.4. In order to augment State funds that are available for needs
35 resulting from recovery and relief efforts related to damage and suffering caused by Hurricane
36 Helene, the General Assembly is identifying and reallocating transportation funds that are
37 unutilized or underutilized to meet the cash flow and federal matching requirements for
38 transportation infrastructure recovery in Helene impacted counties. To this end, as otherwise
39 provided in this act, the General Assembly has identified six hundred thirty-three million six
40 hundred thirty-nine thousand six hundred thirty dollars ($633,639,630) in Part XLIII of this act
41 for reallocation for uses consistent with the Highway Fund and this act.
42
43 HELENE RELATED FUNDING: PUBLIC SCHOOL CAPITAL
44 SECTION 5A.5. The State Controller shall transfer interest earned as of June 30,
45 2025, from the Needs-Based Public School Capital Fund established in Article 38B of Chapter
46 115C of the General Statutes, estimated at thirty-nine million six hundred thousand dollars
47 ($39,600,000), to the Department of Public Instruction for Capital Recovery Funds for the Public

18 PART VI. COMMUNITY COLLEGE SYSTEM
19
20 INSTITUTIONAL SUPPORT CONSOLIDATION
21 SECTION 6.1.(a) The State Board of Community Colleges shall consolidate
22 administrative functions held across the community colleges system into a new administrative
23 structure. The President of the Community Colleges System, a consultant hired by the State
24 Board, or individual community colleges may submit recommendations to the State Board, but
25 all decision-making power on the development of the new structure resides with the State Board.
26 The new structure developed pursuant to this section shall go into effect beginning with the
27 2026-2027 academic year. The State Board may use up to two million dollars ($2,000,000) in
28 nonrecurring funds appropriated in this act for the 2025-2026 fiscal year for the following:
29 (1) To contract with a third-party consultant to assist with development of the new
30 administrative structure.
31 (2) To conduct studies related to developing the new administrative structure.
32 (3) Other purposes the State Board deems relevant to developing the new
33 administrative structure.
34 SECTION 6.1.(b) The State Board shall submit a report to the Joint Legislative
35 Education Oversight Committee on the administrative structure developed pursuant to this
36 section no later than April 15, 2026. The report shall include at least the following:
37 (1) An overview of the new structure compared to the prior structure.
38 (2) A summary of efficiencies achieved by the new structure.
39

28 "General Provisions for State Administration.
29 "Part 1. Establishment and Administration of the North Carolina Community Colleges System.
30 "§ 115D-1. Statement of purpose.
31 The purposes of this Chapter are to provide for the establishment, organization, and
32 administration of a system of educational institutions throughout the State offering courses of
33 instruction in one or more of the general areas of two-year college parallel, technical, vocational,
34 and adult education programs, to serve as a legislative charter for such institutions, and to
35 authorize the levying of local taxes and the issuing of local bonds for the support thereof. The
36 major purpose of each and every institution operating under the provisions of this Chapter shall
37 be and shall continue to be the offering of vocational and technical education and training, and
38 of basic, high school level, academic education needed in order to profit from vocational and
39 technical education, for students who are high school graduates or who are beyond the
40 compulsory age limit of the public school system and who have left the public schools, provided,

42 Safety by a court of competent jurisdiction may, if approved by the director of the youth

44 if they are otherwise qualified for admission.

5 regulations and standards concerning the establishment, administration, and operation of
6 institutions as the State Board may deem necessary to insure the quality of educational programs,
7 to promote the systematic meeting of educational needs of the State, and to provide for the

9 The State Board of Community Colleges shall establish standards and scales for salaries and

11 shall be exempt from the provisions of the North Carolina Human Resources Act. Any and all

42 of the following requirements is met:
43 a. The person seeking the certificate is eligible for the certificate under
44 G.S. 20-11(n)(1) and is not subject to G.S. 20-11(n1).

48 board of trustees of each community college by a person who is denied a
49 driving eligibility certificate.

51 successful completion of a drug or alcohol treatment counseling program.

1 The State Board also shall develop policies as to when it is appropriate to

16 (b) In order to make instruction as accessible as possible to all citizens, the teaching of
17 curricular courses and of noncurricular extension courses at convenient locations away from
18 institution campuses as well as on campuses is authorized and shall be encouraged. A pro rata
19 portion of the established regular tuition rate charged a full-time student shall be charged a
20 part-time student taking any curriculum course. In lieu of any tuition charge, the State Board of

1 (b1) The State Board of Community Colleges shall not waive tuition and registration fees
2 for community college faculty or staff members. Community colleges may, however, use State
3 or local funds to pay tuition and registration fees for one course per semester for full-time
4 community college faculty or staff members employed for a nine-, ten-, eleven-, or twelve-month
5 term. Community colleges may also use State and local funds to pay tuition and registration fees

11 (c) No course of instruction shall be offered by any community college at State expense
12 or partial State expense to any captive or co-opted group of students, as defined by the State
13 Board of Community Colleges, without prior approval of the State Board of Community
14 Colleges. All course offerings approved for State prison inmates or prisoners in local jails must
15 be tied to clearly identified job skills, transition needs, or both. Approval by the State Board of

17 served by that institution. The State Board of Community Colleges may delegate to the President

24 class or program.

4 (y) The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt a policy to be applied uniformly
5 throughout the Community College System to provide that any student enrolled in a community
6 college who is a National Guard service member placed onto State active duty status during an
7 academic term shall be given an excused absence for the period of time the student is on active
8 duty. The policy shall further provide all of the following:
9 (1) The student shall be given the opportunity to make up any test or other work
10 missed during the excused absence.
11 (2) The student shall be given the option, when feasible, to continue classes and
12 coursework during the academic term through online participation for the
13 period of time the student is placed on active duty.
14 (3) The student shall be given the option of receiving a temporary grade of
15 "incomplete (IN)" or "absent from the final exam (AB)" for any course that
16 the student was unable to complete as a result of being placed on State active
17 duty status; however, the student must complete the course requirements
18 within the period of time specified by the community college to avoid
19 receiving a failing grade for the course.
20 (4) The student shall be permitted to drop, with no penalty, any course that the
21 student was unable to complete as a result of being placed on State active duty
22 status.
23 (z) The State Board of Community Colleges shall monitor community colleges for
24 compliance with Article 38 of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes. If the State Board determines
25 that a community college is in violation of Article 38, it shall report the identity of the community
26 college to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee.
27 "§ 115D-5.1. Workforce Development Programs.
28 (a) Community colleges shall assist in the preemployment and in-service training of
29 employees in industry, business, agriculture, health occupation and governmental agencies. Such
30 training shall include instruction on worker safety and health standards and practices applicable
31 to the field of employment. The State Board of Community Colleges shall make appropriate
32 regulations including the establishment of maximum hours of instruction which may be offered
33 at State expense in each in-plant training program. No instructor or other employee of a
34 community college shall engage in the normal management, supervisory and operational
35 functions of the establishment in which the instruction is offered during the hours in which the
36 instructor or other employee is employed for instructional or educational purposes.
37 (b) through (d) Repealed by Session Laws 2008-107, s. 8.7(a), effective July 1, 2008.
38 (e) There is created within the North Carolina Community College System the
39 Customized Training Program. The Customized Training Program shall offer programs and
40 training services to assist new and existing business and industry to remain productive, profitable,
41 and within the State. Before a business or industry qualifies to receive assistance under the
42 Customized Training Program, the President of the North Carolina Community College System
43 shall determine that:
44 (1) The business is making an appreciable capital investment;
45 (2) The business is deploying new technology;
46 (2a) The business or individual is creating jobs, expanding an existing workforce,
47 or enhancing the productivity and profitability of the operations within the
48 State; and
49 (3) The skills of the workers will be enhanced by the assistance.
50 (f) The Community Colleges System Office shall report no later than September 1 of
51 each year to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on:
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 43

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (1) The total amount of funds received by a company under the Customized
2 Training Program.
3 (1a) The types of services sought by the company, whether for new, expanding, or
4 existing industry.
5 (2) The amount of funds per trainee received by that company.
6 (3) The amount of funds received per trainee by the community college delivering
7 the training.
8 (4) The number of trainees trained by the company and community college.
9 (5) The number of years that company has been funded.
10 (f1) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the State Board of Community Colleges
11 may adopt guidelines that allow the Customized Training Program to use funds appropriated for
12 that program to support training projects for the various branches of the Armed Forces of the
13 United States.
14 (f2) Funds available to the Customized Training Program shall not revert at the end of a
15 fiscal year but shall remain available until expended. Up to ten percent (10%) of the
16 college-delivered training expenditures and up to five percent (5%) of the contractor-delivered
17 training expenditures for the prior fiscal year for Customized Training may be allotted to each
18 college for capacity building at that college.
19 (f3) Of the funds appropriated in a fiscal year for the Customized Training Programs, the
20 State Board of Community Colleges may approve the use of up to eight percent (8%) for the
21 training and support of regional community college personnel to deliver Customized Training
22 Program services to business and industry.
23 (g) The State Board shall adopt guidelines to implement this section. At least 20 days
24 before the effective date of any criteria or nontechnical amendments to guidelines, the State
25 Board must publish the proposed guidelines on the Community Colleges System Office's web
26 site and provide notice to persons who have requested notice of proposed guidelines. In addition,
27 the State Board must accept oral and written comments on the proposed guidelines during the 15
28 business days beginning on the first day that the State Board has completed these notifications.
29 For the purpose of this subsection, a technical amendment is either of the following:
30 (1) An amendment that corrects a spelling or grammatical error.
31 (2) An amendment that makes a clarification based on public comment and could
32 have been anticipated by the public notice that immediately preceded the
33 public comment.
34 "§ 115D-5.1A. Short-Term Workforce Development Grant Program.
35 (a) Program Established. – There is established the North Carolina Community College
36 Short-Term Workforce Development Grant Program (Program) to be administered by the State
37 Board of Community Colleges. The State Board shall adopt rules for the disbursement of the
38 grants pursuant to this section.
39 (b) Programs of Study. – The State Board of Community Colleges, in collaboration with
40 the Department of Commerce, shall determine the eligible programs of study for the Program,
41 according to the occupations that are in the highest demand in the State. The eligible programs
42 of study shall include programs such as architecture and construction, health sciences,
43 information technology, electrical line worker, and manufacturing programs and may include
44 other programs to meet local workforce needs.
45 (c) Award Amounts. – To the extent funds are made available for the Program, the State
46 Board of Community Colleges shall award grants in an amount of up to seven hundred fifty
47 dollars ($750.00) to students pursuing short-term, noncredit State and industry workforce
48 credentials. The State Board of Community Colleges shall establish criteria for initial and
49 continuing eligibility for students. At a minimum, students shall be required to qualify as a
50 resident for tuition purposes under the criteria set forth in G.S. 116-143.1 and in accordance with
Page 44 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 the coordinated and centralized residency determination process administered by the State
2 Education Assistance Authority.
3 (d) Report. – The State Board shall submit a report by April 1, 2024, and annually
4 thereafter, on the Program to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal
5 Research Division. The report shall contain, for each academic year and by programs of study,
6 the amount of grant funds disbursed and the number of eligible students receiving funds.
7 "§ 115D-5.2. Commercial fishing and aquaculture classes.
8 (a) The General Assembly urges all community colleges serving the coastal area of the
9 State to offer classes on commercial fishing and aquaculture.
10 (b) The North Carolina Community Colleges System Office shall provide technical
11 assistance to these colleges on offering such classes.
12 (c) The North Carolina Community Colleges System Office shall report to the Joint
13 Legislative Education Oversight Committee on any fiscal and administrative issues it identifies
14 that limit colleges' ability to offer such courses.
15 "§ 115D-5.5. Board Reserve Fund.

17 studies, pilot projects, start-up of new programs, and innovative ideas.
18 "Part 2. Administration of Local Community Colleges by State Board of Community Colleges.
19 "§ 115D-6. Withdrawal of State support.
20 The State Board of Community Colleges may withdraw or withhold State financial and
21 administrative support of any institutions subject to the provisions of this Chapter in the event
22 that:of any of the following:
23 (1) The required local financial support of an institution is not provided;provided.
24 (2) Sufficient State funds are not available;available.
25 (3) The officials of an institution refuse or are unable to maintain prescribed
26 standards of administration or instruction; orinstruction.
27 (4) Local educational needs for such an institution cease to exist.
28 "§ 115D-6.1. Administration of institutions.
29 (a) Policies. – The State Board of Community Colleges may adopt and execute such
30 policies, regulations, and standards concerning the establishment, administration, and operation
31 of institutions as the State Board may deem necessary to ensure the quality of educational
32 programs, to promote the systematic meeting of educational needs of the State, and to provide
33 for the equitable distribution of State and federal funds to the several institutions.
34 (b) Authority. – The State Board shall have the following authority with respect to
35 individual institutions:
36 (1) To approve sites, capital improvement projects, and budgets.
37 (2) To approve the selection of the chief administrative officer.
38 (3) To establish and administer standards for professional personnel, curricula,
39 admissions, and graduation.
40 (4) To regulate the awarding of degrees, diplomas, and certificates.
41 (5) To establish and regulate student tuition and fees within policies for tuition
42 and fees established by the General Assembly.
43 (6) To establish and regulate financial accounting procedures.
44 (c) Salaries. – The State Board shall establish standards and scales for salaries and
45 allotments paid from funds administered by the State Board, and all employees of the institutions
46 shall be exempt from the provisions of the North Carolina Human Resources Act. Any and all
47 salary caps set by the State Board for community college presidents shall apply only to the
48 State-paid portion of the salary. Except as otherwise provided by law, the employer contribution
49 rate on the local-paid portion of the salary, to be paid from local funds, shall be set by the State
50 Treasurer based on actuarial recommendations.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 45

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (d) Faculty Credentials. – The State Board of Community Colleges shall require each
2 community college to meet the faculty credential requirements of its accrediting agency for all
3 community college programs.
4 "§ 115D-6.2. Accreditation.Accreditation policy.
5 The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt a policy requiring community colleges
6 to be accredited in accordance with G.S. 115D-21.2.
7 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions apply in this section:
8 (1) Accreditation cycle. – The period of time during which a community college
9 is accredited.
10 (2) Accrediting agency. – An agency or association that accredits institutions of
11 higher education.
12 (3) Regional accrediting agency. – One of the following accrediting agencies:
13 a. Higher Learning Commission.
14 b. Middle States Commission on Higher Education.
15 c. New England Commission on Higher Education.
16 d. Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities.
17 e. Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on
18 Colleges.
19 f. Western Association of Schools and Colleges Accrediting
20 Commission for Community and Junior Colleges.
21 (b) Prohibit Consecutive Accreditation by an Accrediting Agency. – A community
22 college shall not receive accreditation by an accrediting agency for consecutive accreditation
23 cycles except as provided in subsection (c) of this section.
24 (c) Accreditation Transfer Procedure. – A community college that pursues accreditation
25 with a different accrediting agency in accordance with this section shall pursue accreditation with
26 a regional accrediting agency. If the community college is not granted candidacy status by any
27 regional accrediting agency that is different from its current accrediting agency at least three
28 years prior to the expiration of its current accreditation, the community college may remain with
29 its current accrediting agency for an additional accreditation cycle.
30 (d) Certain Programs Exempt. – The requirements of this section do not apply to
31 professional, departmental, or certificate programs at community colleges that have specific
32 accreditation requirements or best practices, as identified by the State Board of Community
33 Colleges.
34 (e) Cause of Action. – A community college may bring a civil action, as follows:
35 (1) Against any person who makes a false statement to the accrediting agency of
36 the community college, if all of the following criteria are met:
37 a. The statement, if true, would mean the community college is out of
38 compliance with its accreditation standards.
39 b. The person made the statement with knowledge that the statement was
40 false or with reckless disregard as to whether it was false.
41 c. The accrediting agency conducted a review of the community college
42 as a proximate result of the statement.
43 d. The review caused the community college to incur costs.
44 (2) A community college that prevails on a cause of action initiated pursuant to
45 this subsection shall be entitled to the following:
46 a. Costs related to the review conducted by the accrediting agency,
47 including for the following:
48 1. Additional hours worked by community college personnel.
49 2. Contracted services, including outside legal counsel.
50 3. Travel, lodging, and food expenses.
51 4. Fees required by the agency.
Page 46 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 b. Reasonable attorney fees.
2 c. Court costs.
3 "§ 115D-6.5. Notice of noncompliance; appointment of an interim board of trustees.
4 …
5 (c) Interim Board Assumption of Powers and Duties. – The adoption of the resolution to
6 remove the full board under this section shall have the effect of vacating the terms of all of the
7 members serving on the board of trustees. Notwithstanding G.S. 115D-12, the State Board of
8 Community Colleges shall appoint an interim five-member board of trustees for a period not to
9 exceed 12 months with input from the advisory committee listed in subsection (a) of this section.
10 To preserve local autonomy, the appointing authorities of the local administrative area of the
11 community college under G.S. 115D-12 shall make recommendations to the State Board on the
12 appointment of the members to the interim board of trustees. All appointees to the interim board
13 of trustees shall be residents of the administrative area of the institution for which they are
14 selected or of counties contiguous thereto with the exception of members provided for in
15 subsection (a) of G.S. 115D-12, Group Four. G.S. 115D-12(a)(3). At the end of the period of
16 service of the interim board of trustees, a board of trustees for the community college shall be
17 appointed in accordance with G.S. 115D-12. Initial terms of members of the new board of
18 trustees shall be staggered to align with the remainder of the vacated terms of the members of the
19 board of trustees.
20 …
21 "§ 115D-9.5. Bookstore sales.
22 Notwithstanding G.S. 66-58(c)(3) or any other provisions of law, the State Board of
23 Community Colleges may adopt rules governing the expenditure of funds derived from bookstore
24 sales by community colleges. These expenditures shall be consistent with the mission and
25 purpose of the Community College System. Profits may be used in the support and enhancement
26 of the bookstores, for student aid or scholarships, for expenditures of direct benefit to students,
27 and for other similar expenditures authorized by the board of trustees, subject to rules adopted
28 by the State Board. These funds shall not be used to supplement salaries of any personnel.
29 "§ 115D-9.10. Exchange of information with The University of North Carolina and North
30 Carolina public schools.
31 The State Board of Community Colleges shall comply with the provisions of
32 G.S. 116-11(10a) to plan and implement an exchange of information between the public schools
33 and the institutions of higher education in the State.
34 "§ 115D-9.15. Lease purchase and installment purchase contracts.
35 The State Board of Community Colleges shall review and approve lease purchase and
36 installment purchase contracts as provided under G.S. 115D-58.15(b). The State Board shall
37 adopt policies and procedures governing the review and approval process.
38 "§ 115D-9.20. Compliance review and requested information.
39 (a) Compliance Review. – The State Board of Community Colleges shall maintain an
40 accountability function that conducts periodic reviews of each community college operating
41 under the provisions of this Chapter. The purpose of the compliance review shall be to ensure
42 that (i) data used to allocate State funds among community colleges is reported accurately to the
43 System Office and (ii) community colleges are charging and waiving tuition and registration fees
44 consistent with law. The State Board of Community Colleges shall require the use of a
45 statistically valid sample size in performing compliance reviews of community colleges. All
46 compliance review findings that are determined to be material shall be forwarded to the college
47 president, local college board of trustees, the State Board of Community Colleges, and the State
48 Auditor. The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt rules governing the frequency,
49 scope, and standard of materiality for compliance reviews.
50 (b) Information to Department of Revenue. – The North Carolina Community Colleges
51 System Office shall provide the Department of Revenue with a list of all community colleges,
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 47

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 including name, address, and other identifying information requested by the Department of
2 Revenue. The North Carolina Community Colleges System Office shall update this list whenever
3 there is a change.
4 "§ 115D-9.25. Multicampus centers.
5 All multicampus centers approved by the State Board of Community Colleges shall receive
6 funding under the same formula. The State Board of Community Colleges shall not approve any
7 additional multicampus centers without identified recurring sources of funding. A community
8 college facility shall be considered a multicampus center if it meets the criteria established by the
9 State Board and is at least 4 miles away from the main campus of the community college and
10 other multicampus center locations.
11 "§ 115D-9.30. Service areas.
12 The State Board of Community Colleges shall review, at least every five years, service areas
13 that include counties assigned to more than one community college to determine the feasibility
14 of continuing to assign those counties to more than one community college. The State Board
15 shall revise service areas as needed to ensure that counties are served effectively. The State Board
16 shall report its findings and any revisions to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee
17 within 60 days of revisions being made.
18 "§ 115D-9.35. Athletic teams.
19 The State Board of Community Colleges shall monitor community colleges for compliance
20 with Article 38 of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes. If the State Board determines that a
21 community college is in violation of Article 38 of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes, it shall
22 report the identity of the community college to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight
23 Committee.
24 …
25 "Part 3. Community College Programs.
26 "§ 115D-10.5. Program funding.
27 (a) New Programs and Terminations of Programs. – A community college may not offer
28 a new program without the approval of the State Board of Community Colleges except that
29 approval shall not be required if the tuition for the program will fully cover the cost of the
30 program. If at any time tuition fails to fully cover the cost of a program that falls under the
31 exception, the program shall be discontinued unless approved by the State Board. If a proposed
32 new program would serve more than one community college, the State Board shall perform a
33 feasibility study prior to acting on the proposal. The State Board shall consider whether a regional
34 approach can be used when developing new programs and, to the extent possible, shall initiate
35 new programs on a regional basis. The State Board shall collect data on an annual basis on all
36 new programs and program terminations it approved and any regionalization of programs during
37 the year, including the specific reasons for which each program was terminated or approved.
38 (b) Recreation Extension Courses. – Funds appropriated to the Community Colleges
39 System Office as operating expenses for allocation to the institutions comprising the North
40 Carolina Community College System shall not be used to support recreation extension courses.
41 The financing of these courses by any institution shall be on a self-supporting basis, and
42 membership hours produced from these activities shall not be counted when computing full-time
43 equivalent students (FTE) for use in budget-funding formulas at the State level.
44 (c) Real Estate Continuing Education Courses. – Whenever a community college offers
45 real estate continuing education courses, the courses shall be offered on a self-supporting basis.
46 "§ 115D-10.10. College transfer program approval; standards for programs; annual
47 reporting requirements.
48 (a) The State Board of Community Colleges may approve the addition of the college
49 transfer program to a community college. If addition of the college transfer program to an
50 institution would require a substantial increase in funds, State Board approval shall be subject to
51 appropriation of funds by the General Assembly for this purpose.
Page 48 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (b) Addition of the college transfer program shall not decrease an institution's ability to
2 provide programs within its basic mission of vocational and technical training and basic academic
3 education.
4 (c) The State Board of Community Colleges shall develop appropriate criteria and
5 standards to regulate the addition of the college transfer program to institutions.
6 (d) The State Board of Community Colleges shall develop appropriate criteria and
7 standards to regulate the operation of college transfer programs.
8 (e) The Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina shall report to each
9 community college and to the State Board of Community Colleges in accordance with
10 G.S. 116-11(10b) on the academic performance of that community college's transfer students. If
11 the State Board of Community Colleges finds that college transfer students from a community
12 college are not consistently performing adequately at a four-year college, the Board shall review
13 the community college's program and determine what steps are necessary to remedy the problem.
14 The Board shall report annually to the General Assembly on the reports it receives and on what
15 steps it is taking to remedy problems that it finds.
16 (f) The Community Colleges System Office shall report annually by April 15 to the Joint
17 Legislative Education Oversight Committee, the State Board of Education, the Office of State
18 Budget and Management, and the Fiscal Research Division of the General Assembly on the
19 implementation of the UNC-NCCCS 2+2 E-Learning Initiative. This report shall include the
20 following:
21 (1) The courses and programs within the 2+2 E-Learning Initiative.
22 (2) The total number of prospective teachers that have taken or are taking part in
23 this initiative to date broken down by the current academic period and each of
24 the previous academic periods since the program's inception.
25 (3) The total number of teachers currently in the State's classrooms, by local
26 school administrative unit, who have taken part in this initiative.
27 (4) The change in the number of teachers available to schools since the program's
28 inception.
29 (5) The qualitative data from students, teachers, local school administrative unit
30 personnel, university personnel, and community college personnel as to the
31 impact of this initiative on our State's teaching pool.
32 (6) An explanation of the expenditures and collaborative programs between the
33 North Carolina Community College System and The University of North
34 Carolina, including recommendations for improvement.
35 "§ 115D-10.15. Workforce development programs.
36 Community colleges shall assist in the preemployment and in-service training of employees
37 in industry, business, agriculture, health occupation, and governmental agencies. Such training
38 shall include instruction on worker safety and health standards and practices applicable to the
39 field of employment. The State Board of Community Colleges shall make appropriate
40 regulations, including the establishment of maximum hours of instruction which may be offered
41 at State expense in each in-plant training program. No instructor or other employee of a
42 community college shall engage in the normal management, supervisory, and operational
43 functions of the establishment in which the instruction is offered during the hours in which the
44 instructor or other employee is employed for instructional or educational purposes.
45 "§ 115D-10.17. Customized Training Program.
46 (a) There is created within the North Carolina Community College System the
47 Customized Training Program. The Customized Training Program shall offer programs and
48 training services to assist new and existing business and industry to remain productive, profitable,
49 and within the State. Before a business or industry qualifies to receive assistance under the
50 Customized Training Program, the President of the North Carolina Community College System
51 shall determine the following:
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 49

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (1) The business is making an appreciable capital investment.
2 (2) The business is deploying new technology.
3 (3) The business or individual is creating jobs, expanding an existing workforce,
4 or enhancing the productivity and profitability of the operations within the
5 State.
6 (4) The skills of the workers will be enhanced by the assistance.
7 (b) The Community Colleges System Office shall report no later than September 1 of
8 each year to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the following:
9 (1) The total amount of funds received by a company under the Customized
10 Training Program.
11 (2) The types of services sought by the company, whether for new, expanding, or
12 existing industry.
13 (3) The amount of funds per trainee received by that company.
14 (4) The amount of funds received per trainee by the community college delivering
15 the training.
16 (5) The number of trainees trained by the company and community college.
17 (6) The number of years that company has been funded.
18 (c) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the State Board of Community Colleges
19 may adopt guidelines that allow the Customized Training Program to use funds appropriated for
20 that program to support training projects for the various branches of the Armed Forces of the
21 United States.
22 (d) Funds available to the Customized Training Program shall not revert at the end of a
23 fiscal year but shall remain available until expended. Up to ten percent (10%) of the
24 college-delivered training expenditures and up to five percent (5%) of the contractor-delivered
25 training expenditures for the prior fiscal year for Customized Training may be allotted to each
26 college for capacity building at that college.
27 (e) Of the funds appropriated in a fiscal year for the Customized Training Program, the
28 State Board of Community Colleges may approve the use of up to eight percent (8%) for the
29 training and support of regional community college personnel to deliver Customized Training
30 Program services to business and industry.
31 (f) The State Board shall adopt guidelines to implement this section. At least 20 days
32 before the effective date of any criteria or nontechnical amendments to guidelines, the State
33 Board must publish the proposed guidelines on the Community Colleges System Office's website
34 and provide notice to persons who have requested notice of proposed guidelines. In addition, the
35 State Board must accept oral and written comments on the proposed guidelines during the 15
36 business days beginning on the first day that the State Board has completed these notifications.
37 For the purpose of this subsection, a technical amendment is either of the following:
38 (1) An amendment that corrects a spelling or grammatical error.
39 (2) An amendment that makes a clarification based on public comment and could
40 have been anticipated by the public notice that immediately preceded the
41 public comment.
42 "§ 115D-10.19. Short-Term Workforce Development Grant Program.
43 (a) Program Established. – There is established the North Carolina Community College
44 Short-Term Workforce Development Grant Program (Program) to be administered by the State
45 Board of Community Colleges. The State Board shall adopt rules for the disbursement of the
46 grants pursuant to this section.
47 (b) Programs of Study. – The State Board of Community Colleges, in collaboration with
48 the Department of Commerce, shall determine the eligible programs of study for the Program,
49 according to the occupations that are in the highest demand in the State. The eligible programs
50 of study shall include programs such as architecture and construction, health sciences,
Page 50 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 information technology, electrical line worker, and manufacturing programs and may include
2 other programs to meet local workforce needs.
3 (c) Award Amounts. – To the extent funds are made available for the Program, the State
4 Board of Community Colleges shall award grants in an amount of up to seven hundred fifty
5 dollars ($750.00) to students pursuing short-term, noncredit State and industry workforce
6 credentials. The State Board of Community Colleges shall establish criteria for initial and
7 continuing eligibility for students. At a minimum, students shall be required to qualify as a
8 resident for tuition purposes under the criteria set forth in G.S. 116-143.1 and in accordance with
9 the coordinated and centralized residency determination process administered by the State
10 Education Assistance Authority.
11 (d) Report. – The State Board shall submit a report by April 1 annually on the Program
12 to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal Research Division. The
13 report shall contain, for each academic year and by programs of study, the amount of grant funds
14 disbursed and the number of eligible students receiving funds.
15 "§ 115D-10.21. Training programs for students with intellectual and developmental
16 disabilities.
17 (a) The State Board of Community Colleges shall establish a community college training
18 program for up to 15 community colleges. The program shall provide opportunities for
19 micro-credentials or other credentials that lead to increased employment outcomes for
20 individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD). To the extent funds are
21 appropriated for this purpose, the program shall improve the ability of participating community
22 colleges to offer training and educational components that include improving employability skills
23 and providing on-the-job training and apprenticeships with business and industry for individuals
24 with IDD. The goal of the program shall be to inform community colleges and address
25 cross-departmental supports within the individual community colleges on programs for
26 individuals with IDD related to at least the following:
27 (1) Establishing best practices for providing vocational training for individuals
28 with IDD.
29 (2) Providing financial and benefits counseling.
30 (3) Developing strategies on integrating assistive technology.
31 (4) Maximizing access, with supports, to credential and degree programs,
32 including micro-credentials that are established by the State Board.
33 (5) Identifying methods to increase orientation and integration of individuals with
34 IDD into the college community to the greatest extent possible.
35 (6) Determining a needs assessment, marketing, and evaluation to serve a broad
36 array of individuals with developmental and other similar disabilities or
37 learning challenges to assure adequate demand for new or existing programs.
38 (b) No later than May 1 of each year, the Community Colleges System Office shall report
39 on the funds appropriated to the System Office for the purposes of this section to the Joint
40 Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal Research Division. At a minimum,
41 the report shall address the impact of the program, the use of any additional positions created at
42 community colleges, professional development training for staff, and funding sources identified
43 for individuals with IDD to build programs at community colleges that support postsecondary
44 trainings and certifications that enable individuals with IDD to engage in competitive, sustainable
45 employment.
46 "§ 115D-10.25. Commercial fishing and aquaculture classes.
47 (a) The General Assembly urges all community colleges serving the coastal area of the
48 State to offer classes on commercial fishing and aquaculture.
49 (b) The North Carolina Community Colleges System Office shall provide technical
50 assistance to these colleges on offering such classes.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 51

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (c) The North Carolina Community Colleges System Office shall report to the Joint
2 Legislative Education Oversight Committee on any fiscal and administrative issues it identifies
3 that limit colleges' ability to offer such courses.
4 "§ 115D-10.30. Correction education programs.
5 (a) Approval. – No course of instruction shall be offered by any community college at
6 State expense or partial State expense to any captive or co-opted group of students, as defined by
7 the State Board of Community Colleges, without prior approval of the State Board. All course
8 offerings approved for State prison inmates or prisoners in local jails must be tied to clearly
9 identified job skills, transition needs, or both. Approval by the State Board shall be presumed to
10 constitute approval of both the course and the group served by that institution. The State Board
11 may delegate to the President the power to make an initial approval, with final approval to be
12 made by the State Board. A course taught without such approval will not yield any full-time
13 equivalent students, as defined by the State Board. Community colleges shall report full-time
14 equivalent (FTE) student hours for correction education programs on the basis of student
15 membership hours. Funds appropriated for community college courses for prison inmates shall
16 be used only for inmates in State prisons. The first priority for the use of these funds shall be to
17 restore the FTE for basic skills courses to the FY 2008-2009 level. Funds not needed for this
18 purpose may be used for continuing education and curriculum courses related to job skills
19 training. No community college shall operate a multi-entry/multi-exit class or program in a prison
20 facility, except for a literacy class or program. The State Board shall work with the Division of
21 Adult Correction and Juvenile Justice of the Department of Public Safety on offering classes and
22 programs that match the average length of stay of an inmate in a prison facility.
23 (b) Courses in Federal Prisons. – Courses in federal prisons shall not earn regular budget
24 full-time equivalents but may be offered on a self-supporting basis.
25 "§ 115D-10.35. Teacher residency licensure courses.
26 The North Carolina Community College System may offer courses, in accordance with
27 Article 17D of Subchapter V of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes, to individuals who choose
28 to enter the teaching profession through residency licensure.
29 "§ 115D-10.40. American Sign Language courses.
30 The State Board of Community Colleges shall develop curriculum and continuing education
31 standards for courses of instruction in American Sign Language and shall encourage community
32 colleges to offer courses in American Sign Language as a modern foreign language.
33 "§ 115D-10.45. Adult high school equivalency diploma test.
34 The State Board of Community Colleges may establish, retain, and budget fees charged to
35 students taking an adult high school equivalency diploma test, including fees for retesting. Fees
36 collected for this purpose shall be used only to (i) offset the costs of the test, including the cost
37 of scoring the test, (ii) offset the costs of printing adult high school equivalency diplomas, and
38 (iii) meet federal and State reporting requirements related to the test.
39 "§ 115D-10.50. Motorcycle Safety Instruction Program.
40 (a) There is created a Motorcycle Safety Instruction Program for the purpose of
41 establishing statewide motorcycle safety instruction to be delivered through the Community
42 Colleges System Office. The Program may be administered by a motorcycle safety coordinator
43 who shall be responsible for the planning, curriculum, and completion requirements of the
44 Program. The State Board of Community Colleges may elect a motorcycle safety coordinator
45 upon nomination of the President of the Community College System, and the compensation of
46 the motorcycle safety coordinator shall be fixed by the State Board upon recommendation of the
47 President of the Community College System pursuant to G.S. 115D-3. The State Board of
48 Community Colleges may contract with an appropriate public or private agency or person to
49 carry out the duties of the motorcycle safety coordinator.
50 (b) The Motorcycle Safety Instruction Program shall be implemented through the
51 Community Colleges System Office at institutions which choose to provide the Program. The
Page 52 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 motorcycle safety coordinator shall select and facilitate the training and certification of
2 instructors who will implement the Program.
3 "Part 4. Students.
4 "§ 115D-10.65. Accreditation of secondary school located in North Carolina shall not be a
5 factor in admissions, loans, scholarships, or other educational policies.
6 (a) For purposes of this section, the term "accreditation" shall include certification or any
7 other similar approval process.
8 (b) The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt a policy that prohibits any
9 community college from soliciting or using information regarding the accreditation of a
10 secondary school located in North Carolina that a person attended as a factor affecting
11 admissions, loans, scholarships, or other educational activity at the community college, unless
12 the accreditation was conducted by a State agency.
13 "§ 115D-10.70. Driving eligibility certificates.
14 (a) The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt the following rules to assist
15 community colleges in their administration of procedures necessary to implement G.S. 20-11 and
16 G.S. 20-13.2:
17 (1) To establish the procedures a person who is or was enrolled in a community
18 college must follow and the requirements that person must meet to obtain a
19 driving eligibility certificate.
20 (2) To require the person who is required under G.S. 20-11(n) to sign the driving
21 eligibility certificate to provide the certificate if he or she determines that one
22 of the following requirements is met:
23 a. The person seeking the certificate is eligible for the certificate under
24 G.S. 20-11(n)(1) and is not subject to G.S. 20-11(n1).
25 b. The person seeking the certificate is eligible for the certificate under
26 G.S. 20-11(n)(1) and G.S. 20-11(n1).
27 (3) To provide for an appeal through the grievance procedures established by the
28 board of trustees of each community college by a person who is denied a
29 driving eligibility certificate.
30 (4) To define exemplary student behavior and to define what constitutes the
31 successful completion of a drug or alcohol treatment counseling program.
32 (b) The State Board shall develop policies as to when it is appropriate to notify the
33 Division of Motor Vehicles that a person who is or was enrolled in a community college no
34 longer meets the requirements for a driving eligibility certificate. The State Board also shall adopt
35 guidelines to assist the presidents of community colleges in their designation of representatives
36 to sign driving eligibility certificates.
37 (c) The State Board shall develop a form for the appropriate individuals to provide their
38 written, irrevocable consent for a community college to disclose to the Division of Motor
39 Vehicles that the student no longer meets the conditions for a driving eligibility certificate under
40 G.S. 20-11(n)(1) or G.S. 20-11(n1), if applicable, in the event that this disclosure is necessary to
41 comply with G.S. 20-11 or G.S. 20-13.2. Other than identifying under which statutory subsection
42 the student is no longer eligible, no other details or information concerning the student's school
43 record shall be released pursuant to this consent.
44 "§ 115D-10.75. Excused absences for religious observances.
45 The State Board of Community Colleges shall direct each community college to adopt a
46 policy that authorizes a minimum of two excused absences each academic year for religious
47 observances required by the faith of a student. The policy may require that the student provide
48 written notice of the request for an excused absence a reasonable time prior to the religious
49 observance. The policy shall also provide that the student shall be given the opportunity to make
50 up any tests or other work missed due to an excused absence for a religious observance.
51 "§ 115D-10.76. Excused absences for National Guard service members.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 53

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 The State Board of Community Colleges shall adopt a policy to be applied uniformly
2 throughout the Community College System to provide that any student enrolled in a community
3 college who is a National Guard service member placed onto State active duty status during an
4 academic term shall be given an excused absence for the period of time the student is on active
5 duty. The policy shall further provide all of the following:
6 (1) The student shall be given the opportunity to make up any test or other work
7 missed during the excused absence.
8 (2) The student shall be given the option, when feasible, to continue classes and
9 coursework during the academic term through online participation for the
10 period of time the student is placed on active duty.
11 (3) The student shall be given the option of receiving a temporary grade of
12 "incomplete (IN)" or "absent from the final exam (AB)" for any course that
13 the student was unable to complete as a result of being placed on State active
14 duty status; however, the student must complete the course requirements
15 within the period of time specified by the community college to avoid
16 receiving a failing grade for the course.
17 (4) The student shall be permitted to drop, with no penalty, any course that the
18 student was unable to complete as a result of being placed on State active duty
19 status."
