30 SECTION 6.5.(c) Article 2 of Chapter 115D of the General Statutes is amended by
31 adding a new section to read:
32 "§ 115D-21.2. Accreditation.
33 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions apply in this section:
34 (1) Accreditation cycle. – The period of time during which a community college
35 is accredited.
36 (2) Accrediting agency. – An agency or association that accredits institutions of
37 higher education.
38 (3) Regional accrediting agency. – One of the following accrediting agencies:
39 a. Higher Learning Commission.
40 b. Middle States Commission on Higher Education.
41 c. New England Commission on Higher Education.
42 d. Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities.
43 e. Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on
44 Colleges.
45 f. Western Association of Schools and Colleges Accrediting
46 Commission for Community and Junior Colleges.
47 (b) Prohibit Consecutive Accreditation by an Accrediting Agency. – A community
48 college shall not receive accreditation by an accrediting agency for consecutive accreditation
49 cycles except as provided in subsection (c) of this section.
50 (c) Accreditation Transfer Procedure. – A community college that pursues accreditation
51 with a different accrediting agency in accordance with this section shall pursue accreditation with
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 55

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a regional accrediting agency. If the community college is not granted candidacy status by any
2 regional accrediting agency that is different from its current accrediting agency at least three
3 years prior to the expiration of its current accreditation, the community college may remain with
4 its current accrediting agency for an additional accreditation cycle.
5 (d) Certain Programs Exempt. – The requirements of this section do not apply to
6 professional, departmental, or certificate programs at community colleges that have specific
7 accreditation requirements or best practices, as identified by the State Board of Community
8 Colleges.
9 (e) Cause of Action. – A community college may bring a civil action, as follows:
10 (1) Against any person who makes a false statement to the accrediting agency of
11 the community college, if all of the following criteria are met:
12 a. The statement, if true, would mean the community college is out of
13 compliance with its accreditation standards.
14 b. The person made the statement with knowledge that the statement was
15 false or with reckless disregard as to whether it was false.
16 c. The accrediting agency conducted a review of the community college
17 as a proximate result of the statement.
18 d. The review caused the community college to incur costs.
19 (2) A community college that prevails on a cause of action initiated pursuant to
20 this subsection shall be entitled to the following:
21 a. Costs related to the review conducted by the accrediting agency,
22 including for the following:
23 1. Additional hours worked by community college personnel.
24 2. Contracted services, including outside legal counsel.
25 3. Travel, lodging, and food expenses.
26 4. Fees required by the agency.
27 b. Reasonable attorneys' fees.
28 c. Court costs."
29 SECTION 6.5.(d) G.S. 115D-21.5 is repealed.
30 SECTION 6.5.(e) Article 2 of Chapter 115D of the General Statutes is amended by
31 adding a new section to read:
32 "§ 115D-21.10. Curriculum courses taught throughout year.
33 Community colleges may teach curriculum courses at any time during the year, including the
34 summer term. Student membership hours from these courses shall be counted when computing
35 full-time equivalent students (FTE) for use in budget funding formulas at the State level."
36 SECTION 6.5.(f) Chapter 115D of the General Statutes is amended by adding a new
37 Article to read:
38 "Article 2B.
39 "High School Programs.
40 "§ 115D-30.1. Career and College Promise Program.
41 (a) There is established the Career and College Promise Program to allow pathways for
42 qualified high school students to take community college courses without the payment of tuition.
43 (b) Subject to the approval of the State Board of Community Colleges, local community
44 colleges may collaborate with public school units and nonpublic schools to offer courses through
45 the following programs:
46 (1) Academic transition pathways for qualified junior and senior high school
47 students that lead to a career technical education certificate, diploma, or State
48 or industry-recognized credential and academic transition pathways for
49 qualified freshmen and sophomore high school students that lead to a career
50 technical education certificate or diploma in one of the following:
51 a. Industrial and engineering technologies.
Page 56 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 b. Agriculture and natural resources.
2 c. Transportation technology.
3 d. Construction.
4 e. Business technologies.
5 (2) College transfer pathways requiring the successful completion of 30 semester
6 credit hours of transfer courses, including English and mathematics, for the
7 following students:
8 a. Qualified junior and senior high school students.
9 b. Qualified freshmen and sophomore high school students, if all of the
10 following requirements are met:
11 1. The student is determined to be academically gifted, have a
12 demonstrated readiness for the course material, and have the
13 maturity to justify admission to the community college by (i)
14 the community college president, (ii) the student's high school
15 principal or equivalent administrator, and (iii) the academically
16 gifted coordinator, if one is employed by the high school or
17 local school administrative unit.
18 2. The student participates in academic advising focused on the
19 implications of being admitted to college early with
20 representatives from the high school and the community
21 college.
22 3. The student's parent or guardian has given consent for the
23 student to participate.
24 "§ 115D-30.5. Evaluation of Career and College Promise Program.
25 (a) Evaluation. – In addition to the evaluation of cooperative innovative high schools by
26 the State Board of Education pursuant to G.S. 115C-238.55, the State Board of Community
27 Colleges, in conjunction with the State Board of Education and the Board of Governors of The
28 University of North Carolina, shall evaluate the success of students participating in the Career
29 and College Promise Program, including the College Transfer pathway and the Career and
30 Technical Education pathway.
31 (b) Metrics. – Success shall be measured by high school retention rates, high school
32 completion rates, high school dropout rates, certification and associate degree completion,
33 admission to four-year institutions, postgraduation employment in career or study-related fields,
34 and employer satisfaction of employees who participated in the programs. The evaluation shall
35 also include an analysis of the cost of students participating in each of the programs within the
36 Career and College Promise Program, including at least the following:
37 (1) Total enrollment funding, the number of budgeted full-time equivalent
38 students, and the number of students enrolled in courses through cooperative
39 innovative high schools, the College Transfer pathway, and the Career and
40 Technical Education pathway.
41 (2) The cost and number of waivers of tuition and registration fees provided for
42 students enrolled in courses through cooperative innovative high schools, the
43 College Transfer pathway, and the Career and Technical Education pathway.
44 (3) Any additional costs of a student attending courses on campus if a student is
45 not attending public school in a local school administrative unit for the
46 majority of the student's instructional time.
47 (c) Report. – The Boards shall jointly report by March 15 of each year to the Joint
48 Legislative Education Oversight Committee, the Senate Appropriations Committee on
49 Education/Higher Education, the House Appropriations Committee on Education, and the Fiscal
50 Research Division of the General Assembly. The report shall be combined with the evaluation
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 57

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 of cooperative innovative high schools required by G.S. 115C-238.55, and the Community
2 Colleges System Office shall be responsible for submitting the combined report.
3 "§ 115D-30.10. Cooperative innovative high schools.
4 Subject to the approval of the State Board of Community Colleges, local community colleges
5 may collaborate with local school administrative units to offer cooperative innovative high
6 school programs, as provided by Part 9 of Article 16 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes.
7 "§ 115D-30.15. Noncredit courses.
8 Subject to rules adopted by the State Board of Community Colleges, local community
9 colleges may provide for the following:
10 (1) During the summer quarter, persons less than 16 years old may be permitted
11 to take noncredit courses on a self-supporting basis.
12 (2) High school students may be permitted to take noncredit courses in safe
13 driving on a self-supporting basis during the academic year or the summer.
14 (3) High school students 16 years and older may be permitted to take noncredit
15 courses, except adult basic skills.
16 (4) Notwithstanding any other provision of this Article, qualified youth 15 years
17 and older may be permitted to enroll in courses, including
18 certification-eligible courses, in fire training pursuant to G.S. 95-25.5(n) and
19 on a specialized course list approved by the State Board of Community
20 Colleges in accordance with G.S. 115D-30.1.
21 "§ 115D-30.20. Gateway to College Program.
22 The purpose of the first semester of the Gateway to College Program is to address additional
23 support to successfully complete the Program. Students may need to take developmental courses
24 necessary for the transition to more challenging courses; therefore, the State Board of Community
25 Colleges shall (i) permit high school students who are enrolled in Gateway to College Programs
26 to enroll in developmental courses based on an assessment of their individual student needs by a
27 high school and community college staff team and (ii) include this coursework in computing the
28 budget FTE for the colleges.
29 "§ 115D-30.25. NC Career Coach Program.
30 (a) Purpose. – There is established the NC Career Coach Program to place community
31 college career coaches in high schools to assist students with determining career goals and
32 identifying community college programs that would enable students to achieve these goals.
33 (b) Memorandum of Understanding. – The board of trustees of a community college and
34 a local board of education of a local school administrative unit within the service area of the
35 community college shall enter into a memorandum of understanding for the placement of career
36 coaches employed by the board of trustees of the community college in schools within the local
37 school administrative unit. At a minimum, the memorandum of understanding shall include the
38 following:
39 (1) Requirement that the community college provides the following:
40 a. Hiring, training, and supervision of career coaches. The board of
41 trustees may include a local board of education liaison on the hiring
42 committee to participate in the decision making regarding hiring for
43 the coach positions.
44 b. Salary, benefits, and all other expenses related to the employment of
45 the career coach. The coach will be an employee of the board of
46 trustees and will not be an agent or employee of the local board of
47 education.
48 c. Development of pedagogical materials and technologies needed to
49 enhance the advising process.
50 d. Criminal background checks required by the local school
51 administrative unit for employees working directly with students.
Page 58 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 e. Agreement that, while on any school campus, the career coach will
2 obey all local board of education rules and will be subject to the
3 authority of the school building administration.
4 (2) Requirement that the local school administrative unit provides the following
5 to career coaches:
6 a. Access to student records, as needed to carry out the coach's job
7 responsibilities.
8 b. Office space on site appropriate for student advising.
9 c. Information technology resources, including, but not limited to,
10 internet access, telephone, and copying.
11 d. Initial school orientation and ongoing integration into the faculty and
12 staff community.
13 e. Promotion of school-wide awareness of coach duties.
14 f. Facilitation of the coach's access to individual classes and larger
15 assemblies for the purposes of awareness building.
16 (c) Application for NC Career Coach Program Funding. – The board of trustees of a
17 community college and a local board of education of a local school administrative unit within the
18 service area of the community college jointly may apply for available funds for NC Career Coach
19 Program funding from the State Board of Community Colleges. The State Board of Community
20 Colleges shall establish a process for award of funds as follows:
21 (1) Advisory committee. – Establishment of an advisory committee, which shall
22 include representatives from the NC Community College System, the
23 Department of Public Instruction, the Department of Commerce, and at least
24 three representatives of the business community, to review applications and
25 make recommendations for funding awards to the State Board.
26 (2) Application submission requirements. – The State Board of Community
27 Colleges shall require at least the following:
28 a. Evidence of a signed memorandum of understanding that meets, at a
29 minimum, the requirements of this section.
30 b. Evidence that the funding request will be matched with local funds in
31 accordance with the following:
32 1. Matching funds may come from public or private sources.
33 2. The match amount shall be determined based on the
34 development tier designation of the county in which the local
35 school administrative unit is located where the career coach is
36 assigned on the date of the award of funds by the State Board
37 of Community Colleges according to the following:
38 I. If located in a tier one county as defined in
39 G.S. 143B-437.08, no local match shall be required.
40 II. If located in a tier two county as defined in
41 G.S. 143B-437.08, one dollar ($1.00) of local funds for
42 every two dollars ($2.00) in State funds shall be
43 required.
44 III. If located in a tier three county as defined in
45 G.S. 143B-437.08, one dollar ($1.00) of local funds for
46 every one dollar ($1.00) in State funds shall be
47 required.
48 (3) Awards criteria. – The State Board of Community Colleges shall develop
49 criteria for consideration in determining the award of funds that shall include
50 the following:
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 59

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a. Consideration of the workforce needs of business and industry in the
2 region.
3 b. Targeting of resources to enhance ongoing economic activity within
4 the community college service area and surrounding counties.
5 c. Geographic diversity of awards.
6 (d) Annual Report. –
7 (1) The board of trustees of a community college that employs one or more career
8 coaches shall report annually to the State Board of Community Colleges on
9 implementation and outcomes of the Program, including the following
10 information:
11 a. Number of career coaches employed.
12 b. Number of local school administrative units served and names of
13 schools in which career coaches are placed.
14 c. Number of students annually counseled by career coaches.
15 d. Impact of career coaches on student choices, as determined by a valid
16 measure selected by the State Board of Community Colleges.
17 (2) The State Board of Community Colleges shall report annually no later than
18 October 1 to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the
19 following:
20 a. A compilation of the information reported by the board of trustees of
21 community colleges, as provided in subdivision (1) of this subsection.
22 b. Number and names of partnership applicants for NC Career Coach
23 Program funding.
24 c. Number, names, and amounts of those awarded NC Career Coach
25 Program funding."

31 SECTION 6.5.(h) Part 2 of Article 3 of Chapter 115D of the General Statutes is
32 amended by adding new sections to read:
33 "§ 115D-39.2. Pro rata tuition and uniform registration fees.
34 In order to make instruction as accessible as possible to all citizens, the teaching of curricular
35 courses and of noncurricular extension courses at convenient locations away from institution
36 campuses as well as on campuses is authorized and shall be encouraged. A pro rata portion of
37 the established regular tuition rate charged a full-time student shall be charged a part-time student
38 taking any curriculum course. In lieu of any tuition charge, the State Board of Community
39 Colleges shall establish a uniform registration fee, or a schedule of uniform registration fees, to
40 be charged students enrolling in extension courses for which instruction is financed primarily
41 from State funds.
42 "§ 115D-39.5. Tuition waivers.
43 (a) Allowed Tuition Waivers. – The State Board of Community Colleges shall not waive
44 tuition and registration fees for any individuals, except the State Board may, as provided by
45 general and uniform regulations, waive tuition and registration fees for the following:
46 (1) Persons not enrolled in elementary or secondary schools taking courses
47 leading to a high school diploma or equivalent certificate.
48 (2) Courses requested by the following entities that support the organizations'
49 training needs and are on a specialized course list approved by the State Board:
50 a. Volunteer fire departments.
51 b. Municipal, county, or State fire departments.

18 SECTION 6.5.(u) G.S. 115C-238.55 reads as rewritten:
19 "§ 115C-238.55. Evaluation of cooperative innovative high schools.
20 The State Board of Education and the governing Boards shall evaluate the success of students
21 in cooperative innovative high schools approved under this Part. Success shall be measured by
22 high school retention rates, high school completion rates, high school dropout rates, certification
23 and associate degree completion, admission to four-year institutions, postgraduation employment
24 in career or study-related fields, and employer satisfaction of employees who participated in and
25 graduated from the schools. The Boards shall jointly report by March 15 of each year to the Joint
26 Legislative Education Oversight Committee, the Senate Appropriations Committee on
27 Education/Higher Education, the House Appropriations Committee on Education, and the Fiscal
28 Research Division of the General Assembly on the evaluation of these schools. The report shall

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Any flexibility for innovation during the life of the technology, computer

7
8 COMMUNITY COLLEGE SEAMLESS SKILLS INITIATIVE
9 SECTION 6.8.(a) Initiative Established; Purpose. – There is established the
10 Community College Seamless Skills Initiative (Initiative). The purpose of the Initiative is to
11 create a competency-based education (CBE) model that seamlessly connects high school and
12 community college. By aligning learning experiences from high school with college-level
13 competencies, the Initiative enables students to explore career pathways, earn dual credit, and
14 fulfill computer science requirements while gaining credentials of value in high-demand
15 technology employment sectors through college credit.
16 SECTION 6.8.(b) Initiative Participants. – Fayetteville Technical Community
17 College (FTCC) and Wilkes Community College (WCC) shall partner to implement the duties
18 of the Initiative.
19 SECTION 6.8.(c) Project Commitments. – The Project shall commit to
20 accomplishing each of the following:
21 (1) Establish high-tech pathways that will integrate high school and community
22 college curricula for hands-on, project-based learning.
23 (2) Develop competency-based pathways that will create cross-curricular maps
24 for dual credit, fostering seamless transitions between secondary and
25 postsecondary education.
26 (3) Scale and replicate to be able to build a scalable framework for rapid
27 implementation across North Carolina.
28 (4) Empower student ownership by enabling students to set long-term learning
29 goals and to manage and own their credentials with a digital wallet.
30 (5) Facilitate student work experiences to accelerate learning and transition
31 students into work through "learn & earn" work-based learning in partnership
32 with public agencies and private employers.
33 (6) Address workforce needs by developing and sustaining a comprehensive
34 talent pipeline of skilled workers for high-demand technology fields in
35 occupations that depend upon informational technology and operational
36 technology for operational success.
37 (7) Develop statewide resources, including a guidebook to facilitate model
38 replication and a new talent development model to accelerate learning using
39 CBE.
40 SECTION 6.8.(d) Digital Wallet. – The participants in the Initiative shall contract
41 with a third-party entity to create a secure, interoperable digital wallet platform capable of
42 issuing, storing, verifying, and sharing learner credentials, including microcredentials,
43 certifications, transcripts, and verified skill records. The platform shall allow for credential
44 portability across educational institutions and employers, support open standards for
45 interoperability, and ensure privacy and security in compliance with applicable laws. The system
46 shall enable learners to manage a comprehensive, lifelong record of achievement that is
47 accessible, verifiable, and shareable with third parties through digital means. All credential and
48 learner data shall be owned and controlled by the student and shall not be stored in centralized
49 cloud infrastructure.
50 SECTION 6.8.(e) Support. – The Community Colleges System Office shall provide
51 ongoing technical support to community colleges participating in the Initiative. FTCC shall enter
Page 68 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 into a memorandum of understanding with WCC to jointly co-design the Program. As part of
2 this effort, the colleges shall engage an organization with demonstrated expertise in designing
3 and implementing learner-centered, modular, and competency-based high school programs
4 aligned with emerging and high-tech career pathways. The selected organization shall have a
5 history of co-designing stackable credentials with local education agencies and postsecondary
6 institutions and a proven track record of building digital infrastructure that supports
7 student-paced progression, interdisciplinary instruction, and authentic skill development. The
8 organization shall assist in the development of a comprehensive framework for the Program,
9 including course design, sequencing, credentialing structure, and related elements necessary to
10 support personalized student progression during the 2025-2026 school year.
11 SECTION 6.8.(f) Of the funds appropriated from the General Fund to the
12 Community Colleges System Office for the purposes in this section, the sum of two million seven
13 hundred fifty thousand dollars ($2,750,000) shall be allocated to FTCC and the sum of one
14 million dollars ($1,000,000) shall be allocated to WCC. Funds allocated for the purposes outlined
15 in this section shall not revert but shall remain available until the end of the 2028-2029 fiscal
16 year.
17
18 HIGH-COST WORKFORCE PROGRAMS START-UP FUNDS
19 SECTION 6.9.(a) Establishment of the Fund. – Of the funds appropriated to the
20 Community Colleges System Office (System Office) by this act for the 2025-2027 fiscal
21 biennium, the System Office shall establish the Fund for High-Cost Workforce Programs (Fund).
22 Any unexpended funds remaining in the Fund at the end of the fiscal year shall not revert to the
23 General Fund but shall remain available for the purposes set forth in this section. The Fund shall
24 be used to assist community colleges in starting new programs in high-demand career fields that
25 require significant start-up funds. Only programs offered at community colleges aligned with
26 high salary and high demand workforce sectors shall be eligible for the award of funds.
27 SECTION 6.9.(b) Applications. – The System Office shall establish an application
28 process for community colleges to apply for awards from the Fund no later than the beginning of
29 each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium. To be eligible to receive funds, colleges shall submit
30 to the System Office a completed application, which shall include at least the following
31 information:
32 (1) A description of the proposed new program requiring start-up funds.
33 (2) Documentation of industry demand for the program or documentation of
34 future local, regional, or statewide employment needs that will be met by the
35 program.
36 (3) Total cash cost to start the program and maintain the program over two fiscal
37 years.
38 (4) A plan for the fiscal sustainability of the new program.
39 SECTION 6.9.(c) Limitation on the Use of Funds. – A community college may only
40 apply for the award of funds to support one new program in each fiscal year. Funds shall remain
41 available to the community college for a period of two fiscal years.
42 SECTION 6.9.(d) Matching Funds. – A community college shall be required to
43 match a percentage of the total cash cost of the program with non-State funds based on a college's
44 total full-time equivalents (FTE) according to the following:
45 (1) Community colleges with a total FTE of greater than 6,500 shall be required
46 to match fifteen percent (15%) of the cost.
47 (2) Community colleges with a total FTE between 2,500 and 6,500 shall be
48 required to match ten percent (10%) of the cost.
49 (3) Community colleges with a total FTE below 2,500 shall be required to match
50 five percent (5%) of the cost.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 69

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 6.9.(e) Administration. – The System Office may adopt any regulations,
2 policies, or procedures regarding the application process, use of funds, eligibility requirements,
3 and any other rules necessary related to the administration of the Fund. The System Office may
4 use up to one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) each fiscal year for administrative costs for
5 establishing and implementing the program.
6 SECTION 6.9.(f) Report. – The System Office shall submit an initial report to the
7 Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee by December 1, 2026, and an annual report
8 thereafter for each year the System Office provides funds to community colleges from the Fund
9 on the programs receiving the funds, which shall include at least the following information:
10 (1) The community colleges that received funds, the amount of funds, and the
11 types of programs started.
12 (2) The use of funds by community colleges receiving awards, including costs
13 associated with student instruction, faculty salaries, instructional supplies,
14 related instructional equipment, and accreditation costs.
15 (3) Evaluation of the success of the new community college programs receiving
16 funds.

27 STREAMLINE LIMITED ENGLISH PROFICIENT ALLOTMENT
28 SECTION 7.8.(a) The title of Article 32F of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes
29 reads as rewritten:
30 "Supplemental School Funding.Funding and Other Allotments."
31 SECTION 7.8.(b) Article 32F of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
32 by adding a new section to read:
33 "§ 115C-472.30. Limited English proficient allotment.
34 To the extent funds are made available for this purpose, the State Board of Education shall
35 allocate funds to local school administrative units, charter schools, regional schools, and
36 laboratory schools operated under Article 29A of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes to provide
37 services to students with limited English proficiency. The State Board shall allocate these funds
38 under a formula that takes into account the average number of students in the units, charters,
39 regional schools, or laboratory schools over the past three years who have limited English
40 proficiency. Local school administrative units shall use funds allocated to them to pay for
41 classroom teachers, teacher assistants, tutors, textbooks, classroom materials/instructional
42 supplies/equipment, transportation costs, and professional development of teachers for students
43 with limited English proficiency. A county in which a local school administrative unit receives
44 funds under this section shall use the funds to supplement local current expense funds and shall
45 not supplant local current expense funds."
46 SECTION 7.8.(c) When making adjustments to allocations to local school
47 administrative units from the limited English proficient allotment for the 2025-2026 fiscal year,
48 no local school administrative unit with an average daily membership of 20,000 or fewer students
49 for the 2025-2026 school year shall receive a negative adjustment in excess of fifty thousand
50 dollars ($50,000) when compared to the allocation received during the 2024-2025 fiscal year
51 from that allotment.

33 "§ 115C-98.5. Challenges to supplementary and instructional materials.
34 (a) Local boards of education shall establish a community media advisory committee to
35 investigate and evaluate challenges to supplementary and instructional materials.
36 (b) At a minimum, the committee shall include the following:
37 (1) A principal from a high school, middle school, and elementary school,
38 respectively.
39 (2) A teacher from a high school, middle school, and elementary school,
40 respectively.
41 (3) A parent of a student in high school or middle school and a parent of a student
42 in elementary school.
43 (4) A school library media coordinator from a high school, middle school, and
44 elementary school, respectively.
45 (c) Challenges to instructional and supplemental materials shall be made in writing and
46 submitted to the local board of education. The challenge shall specify that the material being
47 challenged is one or more of the following:
48 (1) Obscene.
49 (2) Inappropriate to the age, maturity, or grade level of the students.
50 (3) Not aligned with the standard course of study.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 73

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (d) The local board of education and the media advisory committee shall only investigate
2 and evaluate challenges submitted by a parent of a student enrolled in a school governed by the
3 board, a teacher employed by the board, or a resident of the area of assignment for the board.
4 (e) Within two weeks of the filing of the challenge, the media advisory committee shall
5 hold a hearing and provide the challengers an opportunity to present their concerns to the
6 committee. The committee may, in the committee's discretion, request additional information on
7 the subject matter at the hearing from experts employed by the local school administrative unit.
8 Within two weeks of the hearing, the committee shall make a recommendation to the local board
9 of education on whether the challenge has merit and whether the challenged material should be
10 retained or removed as unfit material. The committee's determination shall be limited to
11 considerations of whether the material is unfit on the specific grounds of the material being (i)
12 obscene, (ii) inappropriate to the age, maturity, or grade level of the students, or (iii) not aligned
13 with the standard course of study.
14 (f) At the next meeting of the local board of education after the media advisory
15 committee's recommendation is received, the local board shall determine whether the challenge
16 has merit and whether the challenged material should be retained or removed as unfit material.
17 (g) The local board, at all times, has sole authority and discretion to determine whether a
18 challenge has merit and whether challenged material should be retained or removed. The decision
19 of the board is not appealable.
20 "§ 115C-99. Legal custodians of textbooks instructional materials furnished by State.
21 Local boards of education are the custodians of all textbooks instructional materials
22 purchased by the local boards with State funds. They shall provide adequate and safe storage
23 facilities for the proper care of these textbooks the instructional materials and emphasize to all
24 students the necessity for proper care of textbooks.instructional materials.
25 "§ 115C-100. Rental fees for textbooks instructional materials prohibited; damage fees
26 authorized.
27 No local board of education may charge any pupil a rental fee for the use of textbooks.
28 instructional materials. A pupil's parents or legal guardians may be charged damage fees for
29 abuse or loss of textbooks instructional materials under rules adopted by the State Board of
30 Education. All money collected from the sale of textbooks instructional materials purchased with
31 State funds under the provisions of this Part shall be paid annually as collected to the State Board
32 of Education.
33 "§ 115C-101. Duties and authority of superintendents of local school administrative units.
34 The superintendent of each local school administrative unit, as an official agent of the State
35 Board of Education, shall administer the provisions of this Part and the rules and regulations of
36 the Board insofar as they apply to his the local school administrative unit. The superintendent of
37 each local school administrative unit shall have authority to require the cooperation of principals
38 and teachers so that the children may receive the best possible service, and so that all the books
39 instructional materials and moneys may be accounted for properly. If any principal or teacher
40 fails to comply with the provisions of this section, his the superintendent shall withhold his the
41 salary vouchers of the principal until the duties imposed by this section have been performed.
42 If any superintendent fails to comply with the provisions of this section, the State
43 Superintendent, as secretary to the State Board of Education, shall notify the State Board of
44 Education and the State Treasurer. The State Board and the State Superintendent shall withhold
45 the superintendent's salary vouchers, and the State Treasurer shall make no payment until the
46 State Superintendent notifies him confirms that the provisions of this section have been complied
47 with.

18
19 STABILIZATION OF LOW-WEALTH ALLOTMENT
20 SECTION 7.10. The Department of Public Instruction shall examine the formula for
21 the supplemental funding in low-wealth counties allotment and develop a new funding model for
22 that allotment. The new model shall prioritize counties receiving more predictable allotments
23 year to year to avoid large differentials between consecutive school years. The model developed
24 pursuant to this section shall allocate no more than the funds allocated for low-wealth
25 supplemental funding in the 2025-2026 school year. The Department shall propose technical
26 adjustments for low-wealth supplemental funding to the State Board of Education for approval
27 before submitting the model to the Fiscal Research Division no later than February 15, 2026. The
28 technical adjustments shall include a list of any laws that would need to be adjusted or repealed
29 to allow for the new funding model to be implemented as well as a comparison of funding
30 received under the old model and the recommended new model, sorted by county.
31

26 "§ 115C-107.3. Child find.
27 (a) The Board shall require an annual census of all children with disabilities residing in
28 the State, subdivided for "identified" and "suspected" children with disabilities, to be taken in
29 each school year. Suspected children are those in the formal process of being evaluated or
30 identified as children with disabilities. The census shall be conducted annually and shall be
31 completed by October 15, submitted to the Governor and General Assembly and made available
32 to the public by January 15 annually. The census submitted to the General Assembly may be a
33 copy of any information or any report submitted to the federal government as part of compliance
34 with the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act pursuant to 20 U.S.C. § 1418.
35 (b) In taking the census, the Board requires the cooperation, participation, and assistance
36 of all local educational agencies. Therefore, each local educational agency shall cooperate and
37 participate with and assist the Board in conducting the census.
38 (c) The census shall include the number of children identified and suspected with
39 disabilities, their age, the nature of their disability, their county or city of residence, their local
40 school administrative unit residence, whether they are being provided special educational or
41 related services and if so by what local educational agency, the identity of each local educational
42 agency having children with disabilities in its care, custody, management, jurisdiction, control,
43 or programs, the number of children with disabilities being served by each local educational
44 agency, and any other information or data that the Board requires. The census shall be of children
45 with disabilities between the ages three through 21 but is not required to include children with
46 disabilities that have graduated from high school."

48 CHANGES TO LITERACY INSTRUCTION
49 SECTION 7.13.(a) G.S. 115C-83.6 reads as rewritten:
50 "§ 115C-83.6. Facilitating early grade reading proficiency.
Page 80 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Kindergarten, first, second, and third Kindergarten through fifth grade students shall
2 be assessed with valid, reliable, formative, and diagnostic reading assessments made available to
3 local school administrative units by the State Board of Education pursuant to
4 G.S. 115C-174.11(a). Difficulty with reading development identified through administration of
5 formative and diagnostic assessments shall be addressed with literacy interventions outlined in
6 the student's Individual Reading Plan. Parents or guardians of first and second grade students
7 offered a reading camp as a literacy intervention shall be encouraged to enroll their student in the
8 reading camp provided by the local school administrative unit. Parents or guardians of a student
9 identified as demonstrating reading comprehension below grade level shall make the final
10 decision regarding a student's reading camp attendance.
11 (a1) Kindergarten through third fifth grade reading assessments shall yield data that can
12 be used with the Education Value-Added Assessment System (EVAAS) to analyze student data
13 to identify root causes for difficulty with reading development and to determine actions to address
14 them.
15 (a2) The Department of Public Instruction shall provide for EVAAS analysis all formative
16 and diagnostic assessment data collected pursuant to this section for kindergarten through third
17 fifth grade. The Department shall use a uniform template for all data collected, and the template
18 shall be used each time data is provided. The template shall include clear designations for each
19 data component reported.
20 (b) Formative and diagnostic assessments and resultant literacy interventions shall
21 address oral language, phonological and phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and
22 comprehension using developmentally appropriate practices. These assessments may be
23 administered by computer or other electronic device.
24 (c) Local school administrative units are encouraged to partner with community
25 organizations, businesses, and other groups to provide volunteers, mentors, or tutors to assist
26 with the provision of literacy interventions that enhance reading development and proficiency."
27 SECTION 7.13.(b) G.S. 115C-83.6B(a) reads as rewritten:
28 "(a) An Individual Reading Plan (IRP) shall be developed for any student in kindergarten
29 through third fifth grade demonstrating difficulty with reading development based on the results
30 of either (i) the first diagnostic or formative assessment of the school year or (ii) the first
31 diagnostic or formative assessment of the second semester of the school year. The IRP shall be
32 continually adjusted based on multiple data sources as prescribed by the Department of Public
33 Instruction, indicating that the student is not progressing toward grade-level standards in one or
34 more major reading areas. Based on the most recently collected data, the IRP shall include the
35 following information, specific to the identified student:
36 (1) The specific reading skill deficiencies identified by assessment data.
37 (2) Goals and benchmarks for growth.
38 (3) The means by which progress will be monitored and evaluated.
39 (4) The specific additional literacy interventions the student will receive.
40 (5) The Science of Reading-based instructional programming the teacher will
41 implement.
42 (6) Any additional services the teacher deems appropriate to accelerate the
43 student's reading skill and development."
44 SECTION 7.13.(c) G.S. 115C-83.9(a) reads as rewritten:
45 "(a) Parents or guardians shall be notified in writing, and in a timely manner, that the
46 student shall be retained, unless he or she is exempt from mandatory retention for good cause, if
47 the student is not demonstrating reading proficiency by the end of third grade. Parents or
48 guardians shall receive this notice when a kindergarten, first, second, or third grade student (i) is
49 demonstrating difficulty with reading development; or (ii) is not reading at grade level.
50 Additionally, parents or guardians shall receive notice when a fourth or fifth grade student is
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 81

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 demonstrating difficulty with reading development or is not reading on grade level as determined

41 FISCAL RESPONSIBILITY AND K-12 TECH PLANNING
42 SECTION 7.14.(a) Part 3A of Article 8 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is
43 amended by adding new sections to read:
44 "§ 115C-102.10. Technology costs considerations.
45 The State Board of Education shall adopt rules requiring all public school units to evaluate
46 the following when acquiring technology, computer hardware, and software:
47 (1) The long-term cost of ownership, including costs of repairing the technology,
48 computer hardware, or software.
49 (2) Any flexibility for innovation during the life of the technology, computer
50 hardware, or software.
Page 82 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (3) Any anticipated resale or salvage value at the end of the target life cycle for
2 the technology, computer hardware, or software based on the average resale
3 or salvage value of similar technology, computer hardware, or software as a
4 percentage of the initial cost of purchase.
5 "§ 115C-102.11 Break/fix rate reporting requirement.
6 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions apply in this section:
7 (1) Break/fix rate. – The percentage obtained by dividing the number of school
8 technology devices reported as malfunctioning or needing repair due to
9 physical damage, hardware failure, or other breakage incidents prior to the
10 stated life cycle period, not covered by insurance or a policy plan period, by
11 the total number of school technology devices in operation during that period.
12 (2) School technology device. – Any electronic or computerized equipment
13 provided for educational purposes in a public school unit, including
14 computers, tablets, interactive whiteboards, and similar devices or anything
15 considered a digital device for purposes of the digital learning dashboard
16 pursuant to G.S. 115C-102.9.
17 (b) Each governing body of a public school unit shall submit a report on the following
18 information to the State Board of Education by August 15 annually:
19 (1) The break/fix rate of the school technology devices in the public school unit
20 for the previous school year.
21 (2) The total number of school technology devices currently in operation in the
22 public school unit.
23 (3) The total number of school technology devices in the public school unit
24 requiring repair that (i) underwent repair or (ii) were no longer in service
25 during the previous school year.
26 (4) The total amount of funds spent to repair or replace school technology devices
27 during the previous school year.
28 (c) The State Board of Education shall report to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight
29 Committee by November 15 annually on the break/fix rate of school technology devices across
30 all public school units based on the reports submitted by the governing bodies in accordance with
31 subsection (b) of this section. This report shall include a summary of the data reported by each
32 governing body and recommendations to reduce break/fix rates in the future."
33 SECTION 7.14.(b) The first reports from governing bodies of public school units
34 required by G.S. 115C-102.11(b), as enacted by this section, shall be submitted no later than
35 August 15, 2026, based on data collected during the 2025-2026 school year. The first report from
36 the State Board of Education required by G.S. 115C-102.11(c), as enacted by this section, shall
37 be submitted no later than November 15, 2026.

33 AUTOMATIC ENROLLMENT IN ADVANCED ENGLISH LANGUAGE ARTS
34 COURSES
35 SECTION 7.15.(a) G.S. 115C-81.36 reads as rewritten:
36 "§ 115C-81.36. Advanced courses in mathematics.mathematics and English Language
37 Arts.
38 (a) When practicable, local boards of education shall offer advanced learning
39 opportunities in mathematics in grades three through five, and advanced courses in mathematics
40 in all grades six and higher. For the purposes of this section, advanced learning opportunities are
41 those services and curricular modifications in mathematics and English Language Arts for
42 academically or intellectually gifted students approved as part of the local plan, as required by
43 G.S. 115C-150.7.G.S. 115C-150.7, and advanced courses are advanced courses in mathematics
44 and English Language Arts.
45 (a1) When advanced learning opportunities are offered in mathematics in grades three
46 through five, any student scoring at the highest level on the corresponding end-of-grade test shall,
47 for the next school year, be provided advanced learning opportunities in mathematics approved
48 for that student's grade level. No student who qualifies under this subsection shall be removed
49 from the advanced learning opportunity provided to the student unless a parent or guardian of the
50 student provides written consent for the student to be excluded or removed after being adequately
Page 84 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 informed that the student's placement was determined by the student's achievement on the
2 previous end-of-grade test.
3 (b) When advanced courses are offered in mathematics in grades six and higher, any
4 student scoring at the highest level on the corresponding end-of-grade or end-of-course test for
5 the mathematics course in which the student was most recently enrolled shall be enrolled in the
6 advanced course for the next mathematics course in which the student is enrolled. A student in
7 seventh grade scoring at the highest level on the seventh grade mathematics end-of-grade test
8 shall be enrolled in a high school level mathematics course in eighth grade. Local boards of
9 education may provide supplemental content enrichment, which may include the administration
10 of diagnostic assessments, to students enrolled in a high school level mathematics course. No

13 provides written consent for the student to be excluded or removed from that course after being
14 adequately informed that the student's placement was determined by the student's achievement

10 COMPETENCY-BASED HIGH SCHOOL/HEALTHCARE AND HIGH-TECH
11 PATHWAYS PROGRAM
12 SECTION 7.17.(a) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the
13 Competency-Based High School/Healthcare and High-Tech Pathways Program (Program). The
14 purpose of the Program is to create pathways that will utilize competency-based education
15 (CBE). Pathways will result in obtaining either an associate degree or an industry recognized
16 credential, certification, or licensure based on the student's goal of employment or enrollment.
17 Students will decide their college or career track at the end of their junior year with an initial
18 emphasis on healthcare preparation.
19 SECTION 7.17.(b) Participants. – Mooresville Graded School District (MGSD)
20 shall partner with Mitchell Community College (MCC) to implement the Program.
21 SECTION 7.17.(c) Program Time Line. – MGSD, in collaboration with MCC, shall
22 contract with an organization with demonstrated expertise in designing and implementing
23 learner-centered, modular, and competency-based high school programs that align with emerging
24 and high-tech career pathways. The organization shall have experience in co-designing stackable
25 credentials with local education agencies and higher education partners and a documented track
26 record of developing digital infrastructure that supports student-paced progression,
27 interdisciplinary learning, and real-world skill acquisition. This expert partner shall assist in
28 developing a framework for the Program, including course design, sequencing, credentialing
29 structure, and other elements necessary for personalized student progression during the
30 2025-2026 school year. Students will have the opportunity to participate in a pathway provided
31 by the Program by the 2027-2028 school year at the latest.
32 SECTION 7.17.(d) Program Flexibilities. – Notwithstanding any provision of law
33 to the contrary, the following flexibilities shall be available to Program participants:
34 (1) MGSD may offer Credit by Demonstrated Mastery assessments and CTE
35 Proof of Learning assessments outside of existing State testing windows.
36 (2) MCC may enroll MGSD students in community college courses prior to their
37 eleventh grade year without the student meeting the requirements for ninth
38 and tenth grade students pursuant to subdivision (4) of G.S. 115D-20.
39 (3) Students participating in the Program shall have access to all community
40 college courses at MCC, regardless of pathway selection.
41 (4) MGSD may replace any high school graduation credit requirement, except
42 those outlined in this subdivision, with either community college courses
43 offered by MCC or locally developed CBE courses that are consistent with
44 the student's pathway. MGSD shall not replace any graduation credit
45 requirements in the following subject areas:
46 a. English.
47 b. Mathematics.
48 c. Science.
49 d. Social Studies.
Page 86 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (5) MGSD may operate any high school in the local school administrative unit
2 consistent with the flexibilities of a school operating under a restart model
3 pursuant to G.S. 115C-105.37B(a)(2).
4 SECTION 7.17.(e) Selection of Third-Party Vendor. – MGSD shall select a
5 third-party vendor (Vendor) that is an educational support provider with a nationally recognized,
6 research-based instructional and leadership framework, including a High Reliability School
7 model, a taxonomy of educational objectives aligned to classroom strategies, and a system for
8 implementing personalized competency-based education. The provider shall have published
9 extensively in peer-reviewed and practitioner literature, have an established track record of
10 working directly with K-12 schools and districts across multiple states, and offer professional
11 development services, implementation support, and instructional resources developed and led by
12 a founding education researcher with over four decades of influence in curriculum, instruction,
13 and assessment.
14 SECTION 7.17.(f) MGSD Partnership with Vendor. – MGSD shall partner with the
15 Vendor selected pursuant to subsection (e) of this section to develop standards-aligned
16 proficiency scales for all content areas in grades nine through 12 to ensure educators in the
17 participating schools have the tools necessary to successfully transition to CBE approaches. The
18 Vendor shall provide professional development and coaching to the schools in MGSD to ensure
19 educators can engage in learning about CBE approaches, including implementation of the
20 proficiency scales developed by MGSD.
21 SECTION 7.17.(g) Funding. – Of the funds appropriated to the Department of Public
22 Instruction allocated to MGSD pursuant to this section, MGSD shall use the funds for the
23 following purposes:
24 (1) To contract with experts in CBE and designing stackable credentials pursuant
25 to subsection (c) of this section.
26 (2) To hire one additional staff member at MGSD to oversee implementation of
27 the Program.
28 (3) To partner with a Vendor pursuant to subsection (f) of this section.
29
30 LIGHTHOUSE MATH PROJECT
31 SECTION 7.18.(a) Project Established; Purpose. – There is established the
32 Lighthouse Math Project (Project). The purpose of the Project is to increase the percentage of
33 high school students who are (i) eligible to complete college level mathematics upon graduation
34 from high school and (ii) able to complete college level math either through dual enrollment
35 while in high school or within the first year of college enrollment.
36 SECTION 7.18.(b) Project Participants. – Wake Technical Community College
37 (Wake Tech) shall partner with Wake County Public School System (WCPSS) for high school
38 students at East Wake High School and Knightdale High School to be eligible to participate in
39 the Project at the Wake Tech East campus of Wake Tech. Wake Tech and WCPSS shall partner
40 with SparkNC to align the Project participants' ongoing efforts at the Wake Tech East campus
41 with the design and implementation of the Project.
42 SECTION 7.18.(c) Project Commitments. – WCPSS, in collaboration with Wake
43 Tech, shall contract with Khan Academy, Inc. (Khan), for use of the artificial intelligence student
44 tutoring program, Khanmigo. Students enrolling at participating high schools shall have access
45 to Khanmigo from the time of enrollment in the high school and continuing through their
46 attendance at Wake Tech if the student chooses to enroll at Wake Tech East. Wake Tech shall
47 collaborate with Khan to do the following:
48 (1) Develop and deliver at least one Khanmigo-centered gateway college math
49 course to be offered to high school students at participating high schools to be
50 completed as a college level math course. This course shall also be available
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 87

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 to students who graduate from participating high schools and enroll in the
2 courses at Wake Tech East.
3 (2) Develop a Khanmigo-centered developmental math course sequence that will
4 be provided by Wake Tech faculty to students from participating high schools
5 in one of the following formats:
6 a. As part of a Career and College Promise Career Ready Pathway.
7 b. As supplement to a Career and College Promise Pathway.
8 c. As a summer bridge program available to students prior to or
9 subsequent to their junior and senior years of high school.
10 d. As a community college course during the first semester of enrollment
11 at Wake Tech.
12 SECTION 7.18.(d) Modular Math Course. – As part of the Project, Khan, in
13 consultation with Wake Tech and WCPSS, shall work to develop a self-paced, competency-based
14 modular math course with appropriate student diagnostics and student assessments based on the
15 college readiness math competencies provided as part of the Project. It is the intent that students
16 successfully demonstrating college readiness math competencies will be eligible to enroll in
17 college level math courses.
18 SECTION 7.18.(e) Funding; DPI. – Of the funds appropriated from the General
19 Fund to the Department of Public Instruction, the sum of up to five hundred thousand dollars
20 ($500,000) in nonrecurring funds for the 2025-2026 fiscal year shall be used to contract with
21 Khan for licenses for the Khanmigo application and to create the course developed pursuant to
22 subdivision (1) of subsection (c) of this section. Funds appropriated for the purposes laid out in
23 this section shall not revert but shall remain available to accomplish the purposes of this section
24 until the end of the 2026-2027 fiscal year.
25 SECTION 7.18.(f) Funding; NCCCS. – Of the funds appropriated to the Community
26 Colleges System Office for the 2025-2026 fiscal year, the sum of one million dollars
27 ($1,000,000) in nonrecurring funds shall be used to establish time-limited positions at Wake
28 Tech. Two positions shall be assigned to work in each participating high school to assist in the
29 implementation of the courses offered by the Project. Funds appropriated for the purposes laid
30 out in this section shall not revert but shall remain available to accomplish the purposes of this
31 section until the end of the 2028-2029 fiscal year.
32
33 COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION AND HIGH SCHOOL REDESIGN
34 STRATEGIC NETWORK
35 SECTION 7.19. Mooresville Graded School District, Mitchell Community College,
36 Wake Technical Community College, Wake County Public School System, Fayetteville
37 Technical Community College, Wilkes Community College, and SparkNC shall collaborate to
38 create a Competency-Based Education and High School Redesign Strategic Network (Network).
39 The president of SparkNC, or the president's designee, shall serve as the chair of the Network.
40 The chair shall coordinate the operations of the Network and provide administrative and technical
41 support for the Network to the extent such support is necessary. The Network shall provide a
42 means for each entity to share what it has learned and developed regarding CBE and high school
43 redesign. The goals of the Network shall be to advance the goals of developing a CBE approach
44 to education across the State.
45
46 NORTH CAROLINA COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION INNOVATION GRANT
47 PROGRAM
48 SECTION 7.20.(a) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the North
49 Carolina Competency-Based Education Innovation Grant and Network Program (Program). The
50 purpose of the program is to provide funding and support to schools and districts to support the
51 transition to competency-based education (CBE) models that focus on mastery of learning rather
Page 88 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 than instructional time. The grant aims to promote innovative instructional practices, flexible
2 assessment models, and systemic shifts to ensure all students progress based on demonstrated
3 proficiency.
4 SECTION 7.20.(b) Program Objectives. – The objectives of the Program are as
5 follows:
6 (1) Redesign learning pathways. – Support schools and districts in developing
7 personalized, CBE models that allow students to progress at their own pace.
8 (2) Assessment innovation. – Utilize formative and performance-based
9 assessments that measure student mastery of skills and knowledge, including
10 the use of AI learning tools.
11 (3) Educator capacity building. – Provide common professional development for
12 educators on instructional strategies and assessment practices aligned with
13 transitioning to CBE.
14 (4) Develop strategies for statewide scaling and best practices. – Develop and
15 share resources, implementation strategies, and lessons learned to expand
16 CBE across North Carolina.
17 SECTION 7.20.(c) Phase One. – The first phase of the Program shall begin with the
18 2025-2026 school year and continue until the conclusion of the 2028-2029 school year. Public
19 school units shall submit applications and complete any planning or organizational duties during
20 the 2025-2026 school year with implementation of CBE in participating schools beginning with
21 the 2026-2027 school year.
22 SECTION 7.20.(d) Application. – The Department of Public Instruction shall create
23 an application for the Program and make the application available to all public school units no
24 later than 60 days after this act becomes law. Public school units shall submit their applications
25 to the Department no later than 60 days after the application becomes available. The Department
26 shall select 10 schools to participate in the Program no later than 60 days after the close of the
27 application period. When selecting schools to participate in the Program, the Department shall
28 prioritize schools that show a strong commitment to implementing CBE and innovation in
29 assessment. The Department shall develop criteria to determine which schools are most ready to
30 transition to CBE. The application shall include at least the following:
31 (1) A comprehensive plan outlining the school's vision for competency-based
32 education.
33 (2) A description of instructional and assessment shifts to be implemented.
34 (3) A budget for use of grant funds and a sustainability plan to continue utilizing
35 CBE after the conclusion of Phase One.
36 (4) Commitments from public school units leadership and stakeholders.
37 SECTION 7.20.(e) Grant Funds; Use; Training. – Grant awards shall be in the
38 amount of three hundred thirty thousand dollars ($330,000) to each participating school.
39 Participating schools shall use the sum of ninety-five thousand dollars ($95,000) each school
40 year to receive training on the implementation of CBE in the school. The remaining grant funds
41 shall be used for costs associated with the transition to CBE, including travel costs and CBE
42 resources. Grant funds shall remain available until the end of the 2028-2029 fiscal year.
43 SECTION 7.20.(f) Selection of Third-Party Vendor. – The Department shall select
44 a third-party vendor (Vendor) that is an educational support provider with a nationally
45 recognized, research-based instructional and leadership framework, including a High Reliability
46 School model, a taxonomy of educational objectives aligned to classroom strategies, and a system
47 for implementing personalized competency-based education. The provider must have published
48 extensively in peer-reviewed and practitioner literature, have an established track record of
49 working directly with K-12 schools and districts across multiple states, and offer professional
50 development services, implementation support, and instructional resources developed and led by
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 89

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a founding education researcher with over four decades of influence in curriculum, instruction,
2 and assessment.
3 SECTION 7.20.(g) Department Partnership with Vendor. – The Department shall
4 partner with the Vendor selected pursuant to subsection (f) of this section to develop publicly
5 available, standards-aligned proficiency scales for all content areas and grade levels to ensure
6 educators in the participating schools have the tools necessary to successfully transition to CBE
7 approaches. The Vendor shall provide annual professional development events throughout the
8 regions of the State to ensure educators can engage in learning about CBE approaches, including
9 implementation of the proficiency scales developed by the Department.
10 SECTION 7.20.(h) Department Duties. – The Department of Public Instruction shall
11 do the following as part of the Program:
12 (1) Develop, in collaboration with the Vendor, a CBE Network to support the
13 long-term transition to CBE. The CBE Network shall do the following:
14 a. Provide strategic guidance and policy recommendations for scaling
15 CBE statewide.
16 b. Facilitate collaboration among grant recipients, educators,
17 policymakers, and CBE industrial leaders.
18 c. Develop a long-term roadmap for CBE implementation statewide.
19 d. Offer professional learning communities and shared online resources
20 to support ongoing innovation and capacity building.
21 e. Develop methods of measuring proficiency for all courses offered as
22 part of the standard course of study in kindergarten through grade 12,
23 with support from the Vendor.
24 f. Disseminate developed proficiency measures to all public school units
25 and post standards developed to align with the proficiency measures
26 online on a publicly accessible website.
27 g. Conduct research and evaluation to inform best practices and
28 continuous improvement in participating schools.
29 (2) Provide support and technical assistance, professional learning opportunities,
30 and access to the CBE network for all grant recipients.
31 SECTION 7.20.(i) Reporting; Participants. – Each participating school shall submit
32 a quarterly progress report to the Department detailing implementation of CBE in the school. The
33 Department shall establish the due dates and mechanism for the submission of the progress
34 reports. Progress shall include available student learning outcome data, including evidence of
35 progress toward content mastery.
36 SECTION 7.20.(j) Reporting; Department. – The Department of Public Instruction
37 shall submit a final report on the outcomes of Phase One of the Program to the Joint Legislative
38 Education Oversight Committee by December 1, 2029. The report shall include the following:
39 (1) A summary of student outcomes reported by participating schools.
40 (2) Any challenges experienced by participating schools or the Department with
41 implementation of the Program.
42 (3) Recommendations for scaling the Program statewide.
43 (4) Any other information the Department deems relevant.
44 (5) Any information requested by the Committee.
45
46 AI ACADEMIC SUPPORT PROGRAM
47 SECTION 7.21. Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended by adding a new
48 Article to read:
49 "Article 42.
50 "Academic Support Programs.
51 "§ 115C-650. Establish AI Academic Support Program.
Page 90 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Purpose; Program. – There is established the AI Academic Support Program
2 (Program) to allow public school units to contract with Khan Academy, Inc., (Khan Academy)
3 for use of the academic support service Khanmigo, in grades six through 12. Khanmigo is an
4 application that uses artificial intelligence to support teachers with lesson plan development and
5 to support students with lesson comprehension.
6 (b) Funding. – Funds shall be allocated to each public school unit on the basis of average
7 daily membership (ADM) in grades six through 12 in the public school unit for the purposes set
8 out in this section.
9 (c) Funding Use. – Public school units that utilize funds pursuant to this section shall use
10 all funds to contract with Khan Academy for use of the Khanmigo service, any associated
11 professional development, and the rostering programs required pursuant to subsection (e) of this
12 section.
13 (d) Contract Term. – Public school units entering into contracts with Khan Academy shall
14 contract for a term of one year. At the end of each school year, each public school unit shall
15 evaluate the use of Khanmigo services in the public school unit prior to contracting with Khan
16 Academy for the subsequent school year. The evaluation shall include all of the information
17 required to be reported on by the Department of Public Instruction in subdivisions (2) through
18 (7) of subsection (h) of this section. Public school units shall submit their evaluations to the
19 Department of Public Instruction by July 15 of each year that funds are used pursuant to this
20 section.
21 (e) Class Rostering for Khanmigo. – All public school units that contract with Khan
22 Academy pursuant to this section shall use a secure, cloud-based, single sign-on, and class
23 rostering platform to manage access to digital instructional resources and student information
24 systems and to facilitate automated data integration between educational applications and school
25 district systems. Class rostering products used pursuant to this section must be approved by the
26 Department.
27 (f) Juvenile Justice. – The Department shall contract with Khan for 500 Khanmigo
28 licenses and training for use in schools operated by the Division of Juvenile Justice of the
29 Department of Public Safety. The Department of Public Instruction shall provide technical
30 support to the Division of Juvenile Justice for the use of Khanmigo. The Division of Juvenile
31 Justice shall comply with evaluation requirements pursuant to subsection (d) of this section.
32 (g) Study. – The Office of Learning Research at the North Carolina Collaboratory (OLR)
33 shall design and conduct a study to measure the effectiveness of using Khanmigo, including any
34 impact on student performance and growth. As part of the study, the Department of Public
35 Instruction shall aggregate and report to OLR on the evaluations submitted by public school units
36 and the Division of Juvenile Justice pursuant to subsections (d) and (f) of this section. OLR shall
37 collaborate with Khan Academy to aggregate data on student performance by teacher and by
38 subgroup, to the extent allowed by law. OLR shall provide the results of this study to the Joint
39 Legislative Education Oversight Committee no later than April 1, 2028.
40 (h) Report. – No later than February 15 of each year in which funds are made available
41 for the Program, the Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint Legislative
42 Education Oversight Committee at least the following information:
43 (1) The number public school units that contracted with Khan Academy pursuant
44 to this article.
45 (2) The number of students using the Khanmigo application.
46 (3) The average number of minutes and interactions students had with the
47 Khanmigo application weekly.
48 (4) The number of teachers using the application.
49 (5) The average number of minutes and interactions teachers had with the
50 Khanmigo application weekly.
51 (6) Any identified impact on student outcomes.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 91

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (7) Any identified impact on teacher performance.
2 (8) The number of moderation flags generated by the application."

4 AUTHORIZE NEW COOPERATIVE INNOVATIVE HIGH SCHOOLS AND CODIFY
5 SUPPLEMENTAL FUNDING
6 SECTION 7.22.(a) With the funds appropriated to the Department of Public
7 Instruction by this act for cooperative innovative high schools, the Department shall allocate to
8 local school administrative units the sum of six hundred thirty-five thousand dollars ($635,000)
9 in recurring funds for the 2025-2026 fiscal year in amounts consistent with those set forth in
10 G.S. 115C-238.54A, as enacted by this section, as supplemental funding for the following
11 cooperative innovative high schools for the 2025-2026 school year:
12 (1) Hawthorne Academy of Health Sciences.
13 (2) Martin Innovative Early College of Health Sciences.
14 (3) Moore County Cooperative Innovative High School.
15 SECTION 7.22.(b) Beginning with the 2025-2026 school year and for subsequent
16 school years thereafter, notwithstanding G.S. 115C-238.51A(c), G.S. 115C-238.54, and any
17 other provision of law to the contrary, Hawthorne Academy of Health Sciences, Martin
18 Innovative Early College of Health Sciences, and Moore County Cooperative Innovative High
19 School shall be permitted to operate in accordance with G.S. 115C-238.53 and G.S. 115C-238.54
20 as cooperative innovative high schools approved under G.S. 115C-238.51A(c) and shall be
21 subject to the evaluation requirements of G.S. 115C-238.55.
22 SECTION 7.22.(c) Part 9 of Article 16 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is
23 amended by adding a new section to read:
24 "§ 115C-238.54A. Cooperative innovative high schools – supplemental allotment funding
25 based on county development tier designation.
26 (a) Allocation of Funding. – The Department shall allocate cooperative innovative high
27 school supplemental allotment funds to local school administrative units with a cooperative
28 innovative high school approved pursuant to G.S. 115C-238.51A(c) based on developmental tier
29 area, as defined in G.S. 143B-437.08, as follows:
30 (1) Local school administrative units located in a development tier one area shall
31 be allocated funding as follows:
32 a. The sum of two hundred seventy-five thousand dollars ($275,000) in
33 recurring funds for each cooperative innovative high school in the unit.
34 b. For the Northeast Regional School of Biotechnology and Agriscience,
35 the Department shall allocate the sum of three hundred ten thousand
36 dollars ($310,000) in recurring funds from the regional school
37 supplemental allotment for the school for each fiscal year.
38 (2) Local school administrative units located in a development tier two area shall
39 be allocated the sum of two hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) in recurring
40 funds for each cooperative innovative high school in the unit.
41 (3) Local school administrative units located in a development tier three area shall
42 be allocated the sum of one hundred eighty thousand dollars ($180,000) in
43 recurring funds for each cooperative innovative high school in the unit.
44 (b) Applicability of Funds. – The allotment of funds to local school administrative units
45 pursuant to subsection (a) of this section shall include cooperative innovative high schools
46 approved pursuant to G.S. 115C-238.51A(c) operated by a local school administrative unit
47 regardless of not receiving allotments in a prior fiscal year. Funds shall not be allocated to local
48 school administrative units for cooperative innovative high schools approved by the State Board
49 pursuant to G.S. 115C-238.51A(b)."

40 SCHOOL BUSINESS SYSTEMS MODERNIZATION PLANS
41 SECTION 7.26.(a) For a public school unit to receive an allocation from the
42 nonrecurring funds appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction in this act for the
43 2025-2026 fiscal year for business systems modernization, the Department shall require the
44 public school unit to submit a letter of intent to the Department no later than September 1, 2026.
45 The letter of intent must (i) verify that the public school unit has not previously received funds
46 for business systems modernization, (ii) specify the platform the public school unit intends to
47 use, and (iii) state the projected implementation time line. Any platform chosen must integrate
48 with the Department's Licensure and Uniform Education Reporting System and analytics system.
49 SECTION 7.26.(b) Notwithstanding G.S. 143C-1-2(b), any funds that have been
50 appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction for the purpose of business systems
51 modernization that have not been obligated by June 30, 2031, shall revert to the General Fund.

11 FORMALIZE THE DIAPER BANK OF NORTH CAROLINA'S ROLE AS PROVIDER
12 OF FEMININE HYGIENE PRODUCTS FOR PUBLIC SCHOOLS
13 SECTION 7.28. G.S. 115C-377 reads as rewritten:
14 "§ 115C-377. Feminine Hygiene Products Grant Program.
15 (a) Program; Purpose. – The Department of Public Instruction shall establish the
16 Feminine Hygiene Products Grant Program (Program) to assist public school units participating
17 in the Program in providing provide students with feminine hygiene products at no charge to the
18 student. The Department shall run the Program in accordance with this section in each year in
19 which funds are made available for the purpose.
20 (b) Grants. – To the extent funds are made available for the Program, the Department of
21 Public Instruction shall award public school units grants of up to five thousand dollars ($5,000)
22 on a first-come, first-served basis, and the Department shall prioritize awarding grants to public
23 school units that did not receive an award pursuant to the Program in the previous fiscal year. No
24 public school unit shall receive more than one grant per fiscal year.
25 (b1) Participation. – The Department of Public Instruction shall develop a process through
26 which public school units can elect to participate in the Program for each school year.
27 (b2) Contract for Products. – The Department of Public Instruction shall contract with the
28 Diaper Bank of North Carolina to provide feminine hygiene products to all public school units
29 that elect to participate in the Program on a pro rata basis based on the number of female students
30 in grades six through 12 in the participating public school unit.
31 (c) Reporting. – No later than March 15, 2023, and every year thereafter that funds are
32 made available for 15 of each year of the Program, the Department shall report to the Joint
33 Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the public school units receiving grants products
34 under the Program, the specific number of feminine hygiene products purchased with the grant
35 funds, provided through the Program, the number of students served by the Program, and the
36 impact of the Program on student health and well-being."
