40 SPARKNC EXTENSION
41 SECTION 7.33. Section 7.62 of S.L. 2023-134, as amended by Section 3J.14 of S.L.
42 2024-57, reads as rewritten:
43 "SECTION 7.62.(a) There is established the SparkNC Pilot Program (Program) for
44 beginning with the 2023-2025 fiscal biennium. biennium continuing through the 2031-2033
45 fiscal biennium. The pilot program Program authorizes SparkNC, a North Carolina nonprofit
46 corporation, in partnership with selected public school units, to develop a nontraditional,
47 student-driven pathway through which students may select and complete modular learning
48 experiences that, when aggregated, will provide a competency-based equivalency to a traditional
49 elective course credit. SparkNC shall provide a menu of modular learning experiences that
50 include opportunities for work-based learning. For purposes of this section, "Learning
51 Accelerator" is either a High-Tech or Health Science Learning Accelerator. The
Page 98 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 competency-based elective credit shall be denoted on student transcripts as a Learning
2 Accelerator credit and be focused on science, technology, engineering, and mathematics
3 (STEM). A student may earn up to four credits in Learning Accelerators but may not complete
4 the same learning experience more than once for credit.
5 "SECTION 7.62.(b) Each public school unit partnering with SparkNC in accordance with
6 this section (partnering public school units) shall enter a memorandum of understanding with
7 SparkNC to meet certain requirements for the Program. These requirements shall include the
8 provision of a physical learning lab staffed by a learning lab facilitator, operated on a schedule
9 agreed upon by the public school unit and SparkNC, that will provide a site for collaborative
10 learning and networking. Learning lab facilitators shall facilitate interdistrict instruction, provide
11 student advising, design learning experiences, coordinate with industry partners, and validate
12 student work.
13 "SECTION 7.62.(c) Notwithstanding any State Board of Education rules, partnering public
14 school units shall award the elective credit in a Learning Accelerator to any student who
15 completes a combination of learning experiences determined by SparkNC to provide the
16 competency-based elective credit in that course upon verification of successful completion of the
17 learning experiences and integrity of student work products by the learning lab facilitator. The
18 elective credit shall be denoted as achieved competency on the student's transcript. A student's
19 participation in learning experiences but failure to earn elective credit shall not be denoted as a
20 fail on the student's transcript.
21 "SECTION 7.62.(d) The following provisions shall apply to the Program:
22 (1) Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-295 and any related State Board of Education
23 rules, learning lab facilitators shall not be required to hold teacher licensure
24 but shall meet the standards established by the memorandum of
25 understanding. Learning lab facilitators shall be the teacher of record for
26 students enrolled in the Program. Additional non-licensed personnel may be
27 contracted with on a full- or part-time basis for the purpose of providing
28 timely, real-world content, industry expertise, and student learning
29 experiences. Learning lab facilitators and contract personnel with the Program
30 shall be subject to the requirements of Part 6 of Article 22 of Chapter 115C of
31 the General Statutes (Criminal History Checks).
32 (2) For the purposes of student participation in the Program, the requirements of
33 Part 2 of Article 8 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes (Calendar) shall
34 not apply. Students may continue to participate in the Program and aggregate
35 learning experiences throughout the time the students are enrolled in the
36 public school unit and shall not be limited to a semester or school year.
37 Learning experiences may be provided to students in person, remotely, or
38 through asynchronous learning experiences.
39 (3) Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-316 or any other law or rule to the contrary,
40 public school units shall not be required to pay learning lab facilitators in
41 accordance to the salary schedule used for other teachers employed by the
42 public school unit.
43 (4) If a course in computer science is required for high school graduation,
44 completion of the competency-based elective credit of a High-Tech Learning
45 Accelerator shall be deemed to satisfy the requirement.
46 "SECTION 7.62.(e) For the 2023-2024 and 2024-2025 school years, all All public school
47 units are eligible to participate in the Program.
48 "SECTION 7.62.(f) The nonrecurring funds appropriated to the Department of Public
49 Instruction in the 2023-2024 and 2024-2025 fiscal years for the purposes of this section shall be
50 allocated as a directed grant to SparkNC to provide students a nontraditional pathway to earn
51 competency-based Learning Accelerator elective credits. Funds allocated pursuant to this section
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 99

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 shall not revert to the General Fund but shall remain available until the end of the 2025-2026
2 2026-2027 fiscal year. SparkNC shall utilize the grant to partner with and provide services in the
3 maximum number of public school units possible.
4 "SECTION 7.62.(g) SparkNC, in consultation with the partnering public school units, shall

47 STUDENT USE OF WIRELESS COMMUNICATION DEVICES
48 SECTION 7.34.(a) Article 7B of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
49 by adding a new Part to read:
50 "Part 8. Miscellaneous Requirements.
51 "§ 115C-76.100. Regulation of wireless communication devices.
Page 100 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) The following definitions apply in this section:
2 (1) Wireless communication device. – Any portable wireless device that has the
3 capability to provide voice, messaging, or other data communication between
4 two or more parties, including the following:
5 a. Cellular telephones.
6 b. Tablet computers.
7 c. Laptop computers.
8 d. Paging devices.
9 e. Two-way radios.
10 f. Gaming devices.
11 (2) Wireless communication policy. – A policy governing the use of wireless
12 communication devices by students.
13 (b) The governing body of a public school unit shall establish a wireless communication
14 policy. At a minimum, except as permitted in subsection (c) of this section, the policy shall
15 prohibit students from using, displaying, or having a wireless communication device turned on
16 during instructional time.
17 (c) A wireless communication policy shall allow student use of a wireless communication
18 device during instructional time as follows:
19 (1) If authorized by a teacher for educational purposes or for use in the event of
20 an emergency. The governing body may establish parameters to be followed
21 by a teacher in granting authorizations.
22 (2) As required by the student's individualized education program or section 504
23 (29 U.S.C. § 794) plan.
24 (3) As required to manage a student's health care, in accordance with a
25 documented medical condition.
26 (d) The governing body shall establish the consequences for violations of the wireless
27 communication policy, which may include confiscation of the wireless communication device
28 and disciplinary measures under the public school unit's Code of Student Conduct.
29 (e) No later than September 1 of the year in which this section becomes effective, every
30 public school unit shall send to the Department of Public Instruction a copy of the public school
31 unit's wireless communication policy. The public school unit shall provide the Department of
32 Public Instruction with a copy of the wireless communication policy anytime it is changed. No
33 later than October 1 of each year, the Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint
34 Legislative Education Oversight Committee the number of public school units that are in
35 compliance with this section and the name of any public school unit which is not in compliance
36 with this section."
37 SECTION 7.34.(b) This section applies beginning with the 2025-2026 school year.

39 PUBLIC SCHOOL ENROLLMENT STABILITY FOR MILITARY STUDENTS
40 SECTION 7.35. G.S. 115C-366(a9) reads as rewritten:
41 "(a9) A student who is not a domiciliary of a local school administrative unit that resides
42 with a parent or legal guardian that is on active military duty shall be permitted to register to
43 enroll in the public schools of that unit by remote means, including electronic means, prior to
44 commencement of the student's residency in the local school administrative unit if all of the
45 following apply:subject to the following conditions:
46 (1) To be eligible to register to enroll prior to becoming a domiciliary, all of the
47 following conditions must be met:
48 a. A parent or legal guardian is on active military duty and (i) is
49 transferred or pending transfer pursuant to an official military order to
50 a military installation or reservation in the State.State or (ii) is
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 101

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 separating from active duty service within the next 12 months and
2 intends to establish residency in the State.
3 (2)b. Upon request by the local school administrative unit where the student
4 seeks to register to enroll, a parent or legal guardian provides (i) a copy
5 of the official military order transferring to a military installation or
6 reservation located in the State.State or (ii) an official military
7 document showing an anticipated date of separation. Each local school
8 administrative unit may determine the document or documents
9 sufficient to establish an anticipated date of separation so long as the
10 document required is easily accessible by the parent or legal guardian.
11 (3)c. A parent or legal guardian completes and submits the local school
12 administrative unit's required enrollment forms and documentation,
13 except that proof of residency and documentation related to
14 disciplinary actions pursuant to G.S. 115C-366(a4) shall not be
15 required until the student transfers into the local school administrative
16 unit, at which time they shall be required prior to commencing
17 attendance.
18 (2) Once registered to be enrolled, the following apply:
19 a. A local school administrative unit shall make available to a student
20 who registers to enroll pursuant to this subsection the same
21 opportunities available to a student enrolled contemporaneously with
22 domicilia, such as requesting or applying for school assignment,
23 registering for courses, and applying for any other programs that
24 require additional request or application.
25 b. A student enrolled pursuant to this subsection may not attend school
26 in the local school administrative unit until proof of residency is
27 provided in accordance with the requirements of the local school
28 administrative unit.the later of (i) one year from the parent or legal
29 guardian's reporting-for-duty or separation date or (ii) the end of the
30 school year in which the student began attending the school. The
31 parent or legal guardian shall provide proof of residency before the
32 expiration of the allotted period of attendance provided in this
33 sub-subdivision.
34 c. If a student begins attending a school pursuant to this subsection in
35 grade 11 or 12, the local school administrative unit shall extend the
36 period of attendance allowed under sub-subdivision b. of this
37 subdivision to allow the student to attend the school through high
38 school graduation.
39 Nothing in this subsection shall be construed to curtail a local school administrative unit's
40 authority pursuant to G.S. 115C-366(a5)."

42 EXTENDED LEARNING AND INTEGRATED STUDENT SUPPORTS COMPETITIVE
43 GRANT PROGRAM
44 SECTION 7.36. Article 16 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended by
45 adding a new Part to read:
46 "Part 7A. Extended Learning and Integrated Student Supports Grant Program.
47 "§ 115C-238.35. Program; purpose.
48 (a) Program; Purpose. – There is established the Extended Learning and Integrated
49 Student Supports Grant Program (Program). Nonprofit corporations and nonprofit corporations
50 working in collaboration with local school administrative units operating relevant programs may
51 apply for the Program. The purpose of the Program is to fund high-quality, independently
Page 102 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 validated extended learning and integrated student support service programs for at-risk students
2 that raise standards for student academic outcomes by focusing on the following:
3 (1) Use of an evidence-based model with a proven track record of success.
4 (2) Inclusion of rigorous, quantitative performance measures to confirm
5 effectiveness of the program.
6 (3) Deployment of multiple tiered supports in schools to address student barriers
7 to achievement, such as strategies to improve chronic absenteeism, antisocial
8 behaviors, academic growth, and enhancement of parent and family
9 engagement.
10 (4) Alignment with State performance measures, student academic goals, and the
11 North Carolina Standard Course of Study.
12 (5) Prioritization in programs to integrate clear academic content, in particular,
13 science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) learning
14 opportunities or reading development and proficiency instruction.
15 (6) Minimization of student class size when providing instruction or instructional
16 supports and interventions.
17 (7) Expansion of student access to high-quality learning activities and academic
18 support that strengthen student engagement and leverage community-based
19 resources, which may include organizations that provide mentoring services
20 and private-sector employer involvement.
21 (8) Utilization of digital content to expand learning time, when appropriate.
22 (b) Program Requirements. – In each year in which sufficient funds are available, the
23 Department of Public Instruction shall administer the Program in accordance with this Part.
24 (c) Program Funding. – The Department shall use up to seven million dollars
25 ($7,000,000) from the At-Risk Student Services Alternative School Allotment each fiscal year
26 to fund the Program. Of the funds used to fund the Program, the Department may use up to two
27 hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) for each fiscal year for expenses of administering the
28 Program.
29 "§ 115C-238.36. Awards; eligible uses.
30 (a) Eligible Uses. – Grants shall be used to award funds for new or existing eligible
31 programs for at-risk students operated by nonprofit corporations and nonprofit corporations
32 working in collaboration with local school administrative units. Programs should focus on
33 serving (i) at-risk students not performing at grade level as demonstrated by statewide
34 assessments, (ii) students at risk of dropout, and (iii) students at risk of school displacement due
35 to suspension or expulsion as a result of antisocial behaviors. Priority consideration shall be given
36 to applications demonstrating models that focus services and programs in schools that are
37 identified as low-performing pursuant to G.S. 115C-105.37.
38 (b) Awards; Required Match. – Grant participants are eligible to receive grants for up to
39 two years in an amount of up to five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) each year. A grant
40 participant shall provide certification to the Department of Public Instruction that the grants
41 received under the Program shall be matched on the basis of three dollars ($3.00) in grant funds
42 for every one dollar ($1.00) in nongrant funds. Matching funds shall not include other State funds.
43 The Department shall also give priority consideration to an applicant that is a nonprofit
44 corporation working in partnership with a local school administrative unit resulting in a match
45 utilizing federal funds under Part A of Title I of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of
46 1965, as amended, or Title IV of the Higher Education Act of 1965, as amended, and other federal
47 or local funds. Matching funds may include in-kind contributions for up to fifty percent (50%)
48 of the required match.
49 (c) A nonprofit corporation may act as its own fiscal agent for the purposes of this
50 Program.
51 "§ 115C-238.37. Reporting requirements.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 103

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (a) Recipient Reporting. – No later than July 15 of each year in which a grant recipient
2 is participating in the Program, the recipient shall report to the Department of Public Instruction
3 on the expenditure of grant funds and the progress of the Program, including alignment with State
4 academic standards, data collection for reporting student progress, the source and amount of
5 matching funds, and other measures, before receiving funding for the next fiscal year. Grant
6 recipients shall also submit a final report on key performance data, including statewide test
7 results, attendance rates, graduation rates, and promotion rates, and financial sustainability of the
8 Program.
9 (b) Department Reporting. – No later than September 15 of each year of the Program, the
10 Department of Public Instruction shall report to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight
11 Committee on the Program, including recommendations regarding effective program models,
12 standards, and performance measures based on student performance, leveraging of
13 community-based resources to expand student access to learning activities, academic and
14 behavioral support services, and potential opportunities for the State to invest in proven models
15 for future grant programs."

17 TEACHER APPRENTICESHIP PROGRAM
18 SECTION 7.37.(a) Article 17D of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
19 by adding a new section to read:
20 "§ 115C-269.33. Teacher Apprenticeship Program.
21 (a) Definitions. – The following definitions shall apply in this section:
22 (1) Advanced Teaching Roles unit. – As defined in G.S. 115C-310.3(6).
23 (2) Apprentice. – A person who is employed as an apprentice by an apprenticeship
24 employer and meets all of the following criteria:
25 a. Holds a bachelor's degree.
26 b. Is eligible to hold or holds one of the following:
27 1. An emergency license.
28 2. A residency license.
29 3. A permit to teach issued by the Department in accordance with
30 rules adopted by the State Board of Education.
31 c. Submitted a Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA).
32 (3) Apprenticeship employer. – An Advanced Teaching Roles unit that meets all
33 of the following criteria:
34 a. Has a registered apprenticeship program under requirements
35 established by the United States Department of Labor.
36 b. Employs apprentices under the Program.
37 (4) Department. – The Department of Public Instruction.
38 (5) Program. – The Teacher Apprenticeship Program.
39 (6) RFP. – Request for proposals.
40 (b) Program Established; Purpose. – There is established the Teacher Apprenticeship
41 Program as a competitive grant program for the purpose of increasing the number of
42 professionally licensed teachers in the State and improving teacher competency, student
43 outcomes, and teacher retention in the State. The Department of Public Instruction shall
44 administer the Program in collaboration with ApprenticeshipNC as set forth in this section.
45 (c) Request for Proposals. – No later than November 1 of each year, ApprenticeshipNC
46 shall issue an RFP for the Program. Advanced Teaching Roles units may submit proposals by
47 January 1 of the following calendar year. Proposals shall include at least the following
48 information:
49 (1) A plan to establish a registered teacher apprenticeship program in
50 collaboration with ApprenticeshipNC, including at least the following
51 information:
Page 104 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a. The number of apprentices the unit intends to employ using grant
2 funds for State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment
3 expenses, or both.
4 b. Specific subject areas and grade levels in the local school
5 administrative unit with teacher shortages.
6 (2) A system of supports that would be provided for apprentices, including
7 qualifications of mentor teachers and a schedule of supervision.
8 (3) Alternative sources of funding to support apprenticeships that could be paired
9 with State funds received under the Program, including federal workforce
10 development funds.
11 (4) An explanation of how the unit would incorporate its registered teacher
12 apprenticeship program with its advanced teaching roles program to enhance
13 the learning environment for apprentices.
14 (5) Strategies to encourage candidates to accept an apprenticeship instead of
15 directly entering the teacher profession on a Residency License, Emergency
16 License, or permit to teach.
17 (d) Selection of Recipients. – By March 15 of each school year in which proposals are
18 submitted, ApprenticeshipNC shall review the proposals and select local school administrative
19 units to participate in the Program, beginning in the subsequent school year. ApprenticeshipNC
20 shall notify the Department of its selections, and the Department shall allocate funds to the
21 selected Advanced Teaching Roles units in accordance with subsection (e) of this section.
22 (e) Allocation of State Grant Funds. – To the extent funds are appropriated by the General
23 Assembly for this purpose, the Department shall allocate the funds for grants to apprenticeship
24 employers based on the number of apprentices the unit intends to employ receiving grant funds
25 for State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment expenses, or both, up to a
26 maximum of 10 total apprentices per unit, as follows:
27 (1) Up to seven thousand five hundred dollars ($7,500) per apprentice per year,
28 plus any additional funds necessary for benefits, to provide salary supplements
29 for apprentices in accordance subdivision (3) of subsection (f) of this section.
30 (2) Up to five thousand dollars ($5,000) per apprentice per year for the costs of
31 enrollment of an apprentice in an institution of higher education in accordance
32 with subdivision (5) of subsection (f) of this section.
33 (f) Program Requirements. – The following minimum requirements shall apply to
34 apprentices and apprenticeship employers under the Program:
35 (1) Number of apprentices. – An apprenticeship employer shall employ no more
36 than a maximum of 10 total apprentices who receive grant funds for
37 State-funded salary supplements, State-funded enrollment expenses, or both.
38 An apprenticeship employer may employ additional apprentices receiving
39 funds for salary supplements or education expenses from other sources.
40 (2) Funds for positions. – An apprenticeship employer may employ apprentices
41 using State funds allotted for teacher assistant positions, with roles and
42 responsibilities modified to meet the requirements of the Program, or other
43 available funds.
44 (3) Salary supplements. – As follows:
45 a. An apprenticeship employer shall provide the following salary
46 supplements:
47 1. For each apprentice, up to a maximum of seven thousand five
48 hundred dollars ($7,500) per apprentice.
49 2. For each mentor teacher, up to a maximum of five thousand
50 dollars ($5,000) per mentor teacher.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 105

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 b. An apprenticeship employer may provide a salary supplement for any
2 other teacher employed by the local school administrative unit who
3 agrees to become the teacher of record for additional students to
4 facilitate the operation of the registered apprenticeship program in that
5 unit, up to a maximum of three thousand dollars ($3,000).
6 (4) Mentors. – Each apprentice shall work full time in a classroom with a mentor
7 teacher who is a member of a team of teachers that is led by a teacher with an
8 advanced teaching role. For purposes of this subdivision, a mentor teacher
9 may include a classroom excellence teacher as defined in G.S. 115C-310.3(7).
10 (5) Enrollment costs. – An apprentice shall enroll in or remain enrolled in a
11 recognized educator preparation program. An apprenticeship employer may
12 provide up to a maximum of five thousand dollars ($5,000) per apprentice per
13 year for the costs of enrollment in an institution of higher education. These
14 funds shall only be used after the apprentice has exhausted all other
15 scholarships or grants covering the cost of attendance at an institution of
16 higher education, including federal Pell grants.
17 (6) Limitations. – An apprentice shall not do any of the following:
18 a. Serve as the teacher of record for any students.
19 b. Engage in substitute teaching for more than eight hours per week.
20 c. Work as an apprentice for more than three years.
21 (g) Term. – The Department shall award grant funds to selected Advanced Teaching
22 Roles units to serve as apprenticeship employers for one or more terms of three years. Prior to
23 the conclusion of a term, ApprenticeshipNC shall evaluate the success of the Program at the unit
24 and the compliance of the unit with the requirements of this section. At the conclusion of the
25 evaluation, ApprenticeshipNC may, in its discretion, renew the apprenticeship employer for an
26 additional term. Throughout the Program, an apprenticeship employer shall provide any
27 information or access requested by ApprenticeshipNC to evaluate the registered apprenticeship
28 program pursuant to this section.
29 (h) Emergency Position Conversion. – Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-105.25(b), an
30 apprenticeship employer may convert one position allocated to the unit for classroom teachers to
31 its dollar equivalent at the salary on the first step of the "A" Teachers Salary Schedule for every
32 one apprentice employed by the unit who is receiving State grant funds if all of the following are
33 met:
34 (1) The apprentice receiving State grant funds would have been eligible to fill the
35 vacant position using a residency license, emergency license, or permit to
36 teach but was instead hired into the apprentice position.
37 (2) The funds are only used for one or more of the following purposes in
38 accordance with the requirements of the Program:
39 a. Salary supplements for apprentices.
40 b. Salary supplements for mentor teachers.
41 c. Costs of enrollment in an institution of higher education.
42 d. Salary supplements for teachers identified in sub-subdivision b. of
43 subdivision (3) of subsection (f) of this section.
44 (i) Administration. – Of the funds appropriated to the Department of Public Instruction
45 for the Program for each fiscal year, the Department shall allocate the greater of fifteen percent
46 (15%) or three hundred thousand dollars ($300,000) to ApprenticeshipNC to do all of the
47 following in consultation with the Department:
48 (1) Outline the duties and responsibilities of apprentices, including on-the-job
49 training requirements.
Page 106 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Collaborate with recognized educator preparation programs to establish
2 education requirements for apprentices and revise curriculum requirements
3 for student teaching to include apprenticeships under the Program.
4 (3) Create minimum competencies for apprentices that reflect the progressive
5 acquisition of ability.
6 (4) Create resources that can be used by apprenticeship employers to select and
7 train mentor teachers, including the responsibilities of a mentor teacher and
8 background information on teacher apprenticeship programs.
9 (5) Develop a process to monitor apprentices in their first years of teaching after
10 successful completion of the Program to evaluate the qualities of teacher
11 candidates that correlate to successful outcomes and lower teacher turnover
12 rates.
13 (6) Assist apprenticeship employers with the following:
14 a. Combining State and federal funds to maximize the number of
15 apprentices in the Program.
16 b. Complying with applicable State and federal law.
17 (7) Develop a training module for mentor teachers that establishes standards for
18 mentor teachers under the Program and incorporates, where applicable, any
19 preexisting standards for mentor teachers.
20 (j) Report. – No later than March 15 of each year, ApprenticeshipNC shall report to the
21 Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the Program, including at least the
22 following information:
23 (1) The impact of the Program for each apprenticeship employer on the following:
24 a. The number of teachers, disaggregated by licensure type.
25 b. Student outcomes.
26 c. Teacher retention.
27 (2) Successful strategies and best practices used by apprenticeship employers.
28 (3) Any barriers to expanding the Program."

8 serving the current and future student population.

38 SECTION 7A.1.(b) Salary Supplements for Teachers Paid on This Salary Schedule.
39 –
40 (1) Licensed teachers who have NBPTS certification shall receive a salary
41 supplement each month of twelve percent (12%) of their monthly salary on
42 the "A" salary schedule.
43 (2) Licensed teachers who are classified as "M" teachers shall receive a salary
44 supplement each month of ten percent (10%) of their monthly salary on the
45 "A" salary schedule.
46 (3) Licensed teachers with licensure based on academic preparation at the
47 six-year degree level shall receive a salary supplement of one hundred
48 twenty-six dollars ($126.00) per month in addition to the supplement provided
49 to them as "M" teachers.
50 (4) Licensed teachers with licensure based on academic preparation at the
51 doctoral degree level shall receive a salary supplement of two hundred
Page 110 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 fifty-three dollars ($253.00) per month in addition to the supplement provided
2 to them as "M" teachers.
3 (5) Certified school nurses shall receive a salary supplement each month of ten
4 percent (10%) of their monthly salary on the "A" salary schedule.
5 (6) School counselors who are licensed as counselors at the master's degree level
6 or higher shall receive a salary supplement each month of one hundred dollars
7 ($100.00).
8 SECTION 7A.1.(c) For school psychologists, school speech pathologists who are
9 licensed as speech pathologists at the master's degree level or higher, and school audiologists
10 who are licensed as audiologists at the master's degree level or higher, the following shall apply:
11 (1) The first step of the salary schedule shall be equivalent to the sixth step of the
12 "A" salary schedule.
13 (2) These employees shall receive the following salary supplements each month:
14 a. Ten percent (10%) of their monthly salary, excluding the supplement
15 provided pursuant to sub-subdivision b. of this subdivision.
16 b. Three hundred fifty dollars ($350.00).
17 (3) These employees are eligible to receive salary supplements equivalent to those
18 of teachers for academic preparation at the six-year degree level or the
19 doctoral degree level.
20 (4) The twenty-sixth step of the salary schedule shall be seven and one-half
21 percent (7.5%) higher than the salary received by these same employees on
22 the twenty-fifth step of the salary schedule.
23 SECTION 7A.1.(d) Beginning with the 2014-2015 fiscal year, in lieu of providing
24 annual longevity payments to teachers paid on the teacher salary schedule, the amounts of those
25 longevity payments are included in the monthly amounts under the teacher salary schedule.
26 SECTION 7A.1.(e) A teacher compensated in accordance with this salary schedule
27 for the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall receive an amount equal to the greater of the following:
28 (1) The applicable amount on the salary schedule for the applicable school year.
29 (2) For teachers who were eligible for longevity for the 2013-2014 school year,
30 the sum of the following:
31 a. The salary the teacher received in the 2013-2014 school year pursuant
32 to Section 35.11 of S.L. 2013-360.
33 b. The longevity that the teacher would have received under the longevity
34 system in effect for the 2013-2014 school year provided in Section
35 35.11 of S.L. 2013-360 based on the teacher's current years of service.
36 c. The annual bonus provided in Section 9.1(e) of S.L. 2014-100.
37 (3) For teachers who were not eligible for longevity for the 2013-2014 school
38 year, the sum of the salary and annual bonus the teacher received in the
39 2014-2015 school year pursuant to Section 9.1 of S.L. 2014-100.
40 SECTION 7A.1.(f) As used in this section, the term "teacher" shall also include
41 instructional support personnel.

43 CONSOLIDATED TEACHER BONUS PROGRAM
44 SECTION 7A.2.(a) Article 20 of Chapter 115C of the General Statutes is amended
45 by adding the following new section to read:
46 "§ 115C-302.9. Teacher bonuses.
47 (a) Program. – The State Board of Education shall establish a consolidated teacher bonus
48 program to reward teacher performance and encourage student learning and improvement. To
49 attain this goal, to the extent funds are made available for this purpose, the Department of Public
50 Instruction shall administer bonus pay to qualifying teachers whose salaries are supported from
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 111

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 State funds in January of each year, based on data from the prior school year, in accordance with
2 this section.
3 (b) Definitions. – For purposes of this section, the following definitions shall apply:
4 (1) Eligible advanced course teacher. – A teacher of Advanced Placement
5 courses, International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme courses, or the
6 Cambridge Advanced International Certificate of Education (AICE) program
7 who meets the following criteria:
8 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, one or more
9 of the following:
10 1. A qualifying public school unit.
11 2. The North Carolina Virtual Public School program.
12 b. Taught one or more students who received a score listed in subsection
13 (c) of this section.
14 (2) Eligible career and technical education (CTE) teacher. – A teacher who meets
15 the following criteria:
16 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a qualifying
17 public school unit.
18 b. Taught one or more students who attained approved industry
19 certifications or credentials consistent with G.S. 115C-156.2.
20 (3) Eligible growth teacher. – A teacher who meets at least one of the following
21 criteria:
22 a. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a qualifying
23 public school unit and meets one of the following criteria:
24 1. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
25 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for third
26 grade reading from the previous school year.
27 2. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
28 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth
29 or fifth grade reading from the previous school year.
30 3. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the State
31 according to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth,
32 fifth, sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics from the
33 previous school year.
34 b. Is employed by, or retired having last held a position at, a local school
35 administrative unit and meets one of the following criteria:
36 1. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
37 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
38 to the EVAAS student growth index score for third grade
39 reading from the previous school year.
40 2. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
41 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
42 to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth or fifth
43 grade reading from the previous school year.
44 3. Is in the top twenty-five percent (25%) of teachers in the
45 teacher's respective local school administrative unit according
46 to the EVAAS student growth index score for fourth, fifth,
47 sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics from the previous
48 school year.
49 c. Was employed by a local school administrative unit that employed in
50 the previous school year three or fewer total teachers in that teacher's
51 grade level as long as the teacher has an EVAAS student growth index
Page 112 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 score from the previous school year of exceeded expected growth in
2 one of the following subject areas:
3 1. Third grade reading.
4 2. Fourth or fifth grade reading.
5 3. Fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, or eighth grade mathematics.
6 (4) EVAAS. – The Education Value-Added Assessment System.
7 (5) Predecessor bonus programs. – All of the following:

30 of S.L. 2023-134.
31 (6) Qualifying public school unit. – Any of the following:
32 a. A local school administrative unit.
33 b. A charter school.
34 c. A regional school.
35 d. A school providing elementary or secondary instruction operated by
36 The University of North Carolina under Article 29A of Chapter 116 of
37 the General Statutes.
38 (7) Qualifying teacher. – An eligible teacher who meets one of the following
39 criteria:
40 a. Remains employed teaching in the same qualifying public school unit
41 or, if an eligible advanced course teacher is only employed by the
42 North Carolina Virtual Public School program, remains employed
43 teaching in that program, at least from the school year the data is
44 collected until January 1 of the corresponding school year that the
45 bonus is paid.
46 b. Retired, between the last day of the school year in which the data is
47 collected and January 1 of the corresponding school year in which the
48 bonus is paid, after attaining one of the following:
49 1. The age of at least 65 with five years of creditable service.
50 2. The age of at least 60 with 25 years of creditable service.
51 3. Thirty years of creditable service.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 113

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (c) Advanced Course Bonuses. – A bonus in the amount of fifty dollars ($50.00) shall be
2 provided to qualifying advanced course teachers for each student taught in each advanced course
3 who receives the following score:
4 (1) For Advanced Placement courses, a score of three or higher on the College
5 Board Advanced Placement Examination.
6 (2) For International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme courses, a score of four
7 or higher on the International Baccalaureate course examination.
8 (3) For the Cambridge AICE program, a score of "E" or higher on the Cambridge
9 AICE program examinations.
10 (d) CTE Bonuses. – For qualifying career and technical education teachers, bonuses shall
11 be provided in the following amounts:
12 (1) A bonus in the amount of twenty-five dollars ($25.00) for each student taught
13 by a teacher who provided instruction in a course that led to the attainment of
14 an industry certification or credential with a twenty-five dollar ($25.00) value
15 ranking as determined under subsection (e) of this section.
16 (2) A bonus in the amount of fifty dollars ($50.00) for each student taught by a
17 teacher who provided instruction in a course that led to the attainment of an
18 industry certification or credential with a fifty dollar ($50.00) value ranking
19 as determined under subsection (e) of this section.
20 (e) CTE Course Value Ranking. – The Department of Commerce, in consultation with
21 the State Board, shall assign a value ranking for each industry certification and credential based
22 on academic rigor and employment value in accordance with this subsection. Fifty percent (50%)
23 of the ranking shall be based on academic rigor and the remaining fifty percent (50%) on
24 employment value. Academic rigor and employment value shall be based on the following
25 elements:
26 (1) Academic rigor shall be based on the number of instructional hours, including
27 work experience or internship hours, required to earn the industry certification
28 or credential, with extra weight given for coursework that also provides
29 community college credit.
30 (2) Employment value shall be based on the entry wage, growth rate in
31 employment for each occupational category, and average annual openings for
32 the primary occupation linked with the industry certification or credential.
33 (f) Statewide Growth Bonuses. – The Department shall provide bonuses to qualifying
34 teachers who are eligible teachers under sub-subdivision a. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b)
35 of this section, as follows:
36 (1) The sum of five million dollars ($5,000,000) shall be allocated for bonuses to
37 eligible teachers under sub-sub-subdivision a.1. of subdivision (3) of
38 subsection (b) of this section. These funds shall be distributed equally among
39 qualifying teachers.
40 (2) A bonus in the amount of two thousand dollars ($2,000) shall be awarded to
41 each qualifying teacher who is an eligible teacher under sub-sub-subdivision
42 a.2. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section.
43 (3) A bonus in the amount of two thousand dollars ($2,000) shall be awarded to
44 each qualifying teacher who is an eligible teacher under sub-sub-subdivision
45 a.3. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section.
46 (g) Local Growth Bonuses. – The Department shall provide bonuses to eligible teachers
47 under sub-subdivisions b. and c. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section, as follows:
48 (1) The sum of five million dollars ($5,000,000) shall be allocated for bonuses to
49 eligible EVAAS teachers under sub-sub-subdivisions b.1. and c.1. of
50 subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section. These funds shall be divided
51 proportionally based on average daily membership in third grade for each

1 local school administrative unit and then distributed equally among qualifying

11 (1) Bonus funds awarded to a teacher pursuant to subsection (c), subsection (d),

22 b.2., or c.2. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section may receive a
23 bonus under both subdivision (2) of subsection (f) and subdivision (2) of
24 subsection (g) of this section but shall not receive more than two bonuses
25 pursuant to subdivision (2) of subsection (f) and subdivision (2) of subsection
26 (g) of this section in any given school year.
27 (4) A qualifying teacher who is an eligible teacher under sub-sub-subdivision a.3.,
28 b.3., or c.3. of subdivision (3) of subsection (b) of this section may receive a
29 bonus under both subdivision (3) of subsection (f) and subdivision (3) of
30 subsection (g) of this section but shall not receive more than two bonuses
31 pursuant to subdivision (3) of subsection (f) and subdivision (3) of subsection
32 (g) of this section in any given school year.
33 (i) Bonuses Not Compensation. – Bonuses awarded to a teacher pursuant to this section
34 shall be in addition to any regular wage or other bonus the teacher receives or is scheduled to
35 receive. Notwithstanding G.S. 135-1(7a), the bonuses awarded under this section are not
36 compensation under Article 1 of Chapter 135 of the General Statutes, Retirement System for
37 Teachers and State Employees.
38 (j) Study and Report. – The State Board of Education shall study the effect of the

33 SUPPLEMENTAL FUNDS FOR TEACHER COMPENSATION
34 SECTION 7A.3.(a) Use of Funds. – For each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium,
35 except as provided in subsection (g) of this section, the State Board of Education shall allocate
36 funds pursuant to this section to eligible local school administrative units to provide salary
37 supplements to teachers and qualifying school administrators in those units. Allocation of salary
38 supplements among teachers and qualifying school administrators within each eligible local
39 school administrative unit, including whether a teacher or qualifying school administrator
40 receives a salary supplement and the amount of the supplement provided to that person, shall be
41 determined in the discretion of the local board of education of the eligible unit, except that no
42 individual salary supplement shall exceed the per teacher funding amount awarded to that unit
43 pursuant to subdivision (4) of subsection (c) of this section.
44 SECTION 7A.3.(b) Definitions. – As used in this section, the following definitions
45 shall apply:
46 (1) Adjusted market value of taxable real property. – A county's assessed taxable
47 real property value, using the latest available data published by the
48 Department of Revenue, divided by the county's sales assessment ratio
49 determined under G.S. 105-289(h).
50 (2) Composite value. – For each eligible county, the sum of the following:
Page 116 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 a. The taxable real property factor multiplied by sixty-five percent
2 (65%).
3 b. The median household income factor multiplied by twenty-five
4 percent (25%).
5 c. The effective tax rate factor multiplied by ten percent (10%).
6 (3) County allocation factor. – For each eligible county, the supplement factor for
7 that county divided by the sum of all supplement factors for the State.
8 (4) Effective tax rate. – The actual county tax rate multiplied by the most recent
9 annual sales assessment ratio for that county.
10 (5) Effective tax rate factor. – For each eligible county, the effective tax rate for
11 that county divided by the median effective tax rate in the State.
12 (6) Eligible county. – A county that has an adjusted market value of taxable real
13 property of less than seventy billion dollars ($70,000,000,000).
14 (7) Eligible local school administrative unit. – A local school administrative unit
15 located in whole or in part in an eligible county.
16 (8) Eligible school. – A public school that is located in an eligible county and
17 governed by a local school administrative unit.
18 (9) Maintenance of effort amount. – For each local school administrative unit in
19 each fiscal year, the supplant factor multiplied by the total State and non-State
20 funds expended for salaries for teachers from the fiscal year for which the
21 most recent salary data are available.
22 (10) Median household income. – A county's median household income for the
23 most recent 12 months for which data are available, as that term is used in
24 G.S. 143B-437.08.
25 (11) Median household income factor. – For each eligible county, the median
26 household income in the State divided by the median household income for
27 that county.
28 (12) Non-State funds. – Any funds held by a local school administrative unit, other
29 than nonrecurring federal funds received as a result of legislation enacted by
30 Congress in response to COVID-19, that are not State funds.
31 (13) Qualifying school administrator. – Any of the following:
32 a. Assistant principals paid pursuant to G.S. 115C-285(a)(8).
33 b. Principals paid pursuant to G.S. 115C-285(a)(8a).
34 (14) Supplant factor. – For each local school administrative unit in each fiscal year
35 of the fiscal biennium, the total non-State funds expended for salary
36 supplements for teachers in the 2020-2021 fiscal year divided by the total
37 State and non-State funds expended for salaries for teachers in the 2020-2021
38 fiscal year.
39 (15) Supplement factor. – For each eligible county, the composite value multiplied
40 by the number of State-funded teachers employed in a school in the county
41 that is governed by a local school administrative unit.
42 (16) Taxable real property factor. – For each eligible county, the median adjusted
43 market value of taxable real property in the State divided by the adjusted
44 market value of taxable real property for that county.
45 (17) Teacher. – Teachers and instructional support personnel.
46 SECTION 7A.3.(c) Allocation of Funds. – The State Board of Education shall
47 allocate funds for salary supplements to eligible local school administrative units according to
48 the following procedure:
49 (1) County allocation. – For each eligible county, the State Board shall determine
50 a county allocation by multiplying the county allocation factor for that county
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 117

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 by the funding amount appropriated pursuant to this section for the applicable
2 fiscal year.
3 (2) Per teacher funding amount. – For each eligible county, the State Board shall
4 determine a per teacher funding amount by dividing the county allocation
5 amounts determined pursuant to subdivision (1) of this subsection by the total
6 number of State-funded teachers employed in all eligible schools in that
7 county.
8 (3) Unit funding amount. – For each eligible local school administrative unit, the
9 State Board shall determine the funding amount for that unit based on the per
10 teacher funding amount or amounts for the eligible county or counties where
11 the unit is located. For each county with an eligible school governed by the
12 unit, the State Board shall multiply the applicable per teacher funding amount
13 for that county determined pursuant to subdivision (2) of this subsection by
14 the number of State-funded teachers employed in the eligible school in that
15 county. If the unit is located in multiple eligible counties, the State Board shall
16 aggregate those amounts.
17 (4) Allocation and funding cap. – The State Board shall allocate the amount
18 determined pursuant to subdivision (3) of this subsection to each eligible local
19 school administrative unit for each applicable fiscal year, up to a maximum of
20 five thousand dollars ($5,000) per State-funded teacher.
21 SECTION 7A.3.(d) Charter Schools. – Funds appropriated to the Department of
22 Public Instruction pursuant to this section shall be subject to the allocation of funds for charter
23 schools described in G.S. 115C-218.105. The General Assembly encourages charter schools
24 receiving funds pursuant to this section to provide salary supplements to teachers and qualifying
25 school administrators in the charter school in accordance with the requirements of this section.
26 SECTION 7A.3.(e) Formula for Distribution of Supplemental Funding Pursuant to
27 this Section Only. – The formula in this section is solely a basis for distribution of supplemental
28 funding to eligible local school administrative units and is not intended to reflect any measure of
29 the adequacy of the educational program or funding for public schools. The formula is also not
30 intended to reflect any commitment by the General Assembly to appropriate any additional
31 supplemental funds for eligible local school administrative units.
32 SECTION 7A.3.(f) Nonsupplant Requirement. – A local school administrative unit
33 that receives funds under this section shall use those funds to supplement non-State funds
34 provided for salary supplements for teachers and qualifying school administrators and shall not
35 use any State funds, including funds received under this section, Section 7A.4 of S.L. 2023-134,
36 or Section 7A.12 of S.L. 2021-180, to supplant non-State funds provided for salary supplements
37 for teachers and qualifying school administrators. For purposes of this section, a local school
38 administrative unit has supplanted non-State funds if the State Board finds that the amount of
39 non-State funds expended by the unit for salary supplements was less than ninety-five percent
40 (95%) of the maintenance of effort amount for the local school administrative unit.
41 SECTION 7A.3.(g) Nonsupplant Enforcement. – The State Board of Education shall
42 not allocate any funds under this section to a local school administrative unit if it determines that
43 the unit has supplanted non-State funds in violation of subsection (f) of this section.
44 SECTION 7A.3.(h) Reports. – No later than April 15 of each year of the 2025-2027
45 fiscal biennium, the State Board of Education shall report the following information for the
46 applicable fiscal year to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal
47 Research Division:
48 (1) A list of all eligible counties and eligible local school administrative units.
49 (2) Funds allocated to each eligible local school administrative unit.
50 (3) The percentage and amount of teachers and qualifying school administrators
51 in each eligible local school administrative unit receiving salary supplements.
Page 118 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (4) The average salary supplement amount in each eligible local school
2 administrative unit.
3 (5) The range of salary supplement amounts in each eligible local school
4 administrative unit.
5 (6) The effect of the salary supplements on the retention of teachers and
6 qualifying school administrators in eligible local school administrative units.
7 (7) The identity of any local school administrative unit that the State Board
8 determines has supplanted funds.

10 PRINCIPAL SALARY SCHEDULE
11 SECTION 7A.4.(a) The following annual salary schedule for principals shall apply
12 for each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium, beginning July 1, 2025:
13 2025-2027 Principal Annual Salary Schedule
14 Avg. Daily Membership Base Met Growth Exceeded Growth
15 0-200 $78,764 $86,640 $94,517
16 201-400 $82,702 $90,972 $99,242
17 401-700 $86,640 $95,305 $103,969
18 701-1,000 $90,579 $99,636 $108,695
19 1,001-1,600 $94,517 $103,969 $113,420
20 1,601+ $98,454 $108,300 $118,146
21 A principal's placement on the salary schedule shall be determined according to the
22 average daily membership of the school supervised by the principal, as described in subsection
23 (b) of this section, and the school growth scores, calculated pursuant to G.S. 115C-83.15(c), for
24 each school the principal supervised in one or more prior school years, as described in subsection
25 (c) of this section, regardless of a break in service, and provided the principal supervised each
26 school as a principal for at least a majority of the school year, as follows:
27 (1) A principal shall be paid according to the Exceeded Growth column of the
28 schedule if the school growth scores show the school or schools exceeded
29 expected growth in at least two of the prior three school years.
30 (2) A principal shall be paid according to the Met Growth column of the schedule
31 if any of the following apply:
32 a. The school growth scores show the school or schools met expected
33 growth in at least two of the prior three school years.
34 b. The school growth scores show the school or schools met expected
35 growth in at least one of the prior three school years and exceeded
36 expected growth in one of the prior three school years.
37 c. The principal supervised a school in at least two of the prior three
38 school years that was not eligible to receive a school growth score.
39 (3) A principal shall be paid according to the Base column if any of the following
40 apply:
41 a. The school growth scores show the school or schools did not meet
42 expected growth in at least two of the prior three school years.
43 b. The principal has not supervised any school as a principal for a
44 majority of the school year in at least two of the prior three school
45 years.
46 SECTION 7A.4.(b) For purposes of determining the average daily membership of a
47 principal's school, the allotted average daily membership for the school for the applicable school
48 year shall be used. For purposes of this section, the allotted average daily membership of a
49 principal's school shall include any prekindergarten students in membership at that school.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 119

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 7A.4.(c) For purposes of determining the school growth scores for each
2 school the principal supervised in one or more prior school years, the following school growth
3 scores shall be used during the following time periods:
4 (1) For the first six months of the applicable fiscal year, the school growth scores
5 from the first, second, and third years.
6 (2) For the second six months of the applicable fiscal year, the school growth
7 scores from the second, third, and fourth years.
8 (3) If a principal does not have a school growth score from any of the school years
9 identified in this subsection, the most recent available growth scores, up to the
10 fourth year, shall be used.
11 SECTION 7A.4.(d) Beginning with the 2017-2018 fiscal year, in lieu of providing
12 annual longevity payments to principals paid on the principal salary schedule, the amounts of
13 those longevity payments are included in the annual amounts under the principal salary schedule.
14 SECTION 7A.4.(e) A principal compensated in accordance with this section for the
15 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall receive an amount equal to the greater of the following:
16 (1) The applicable amount on the principal salary schedule for the applicable
17 fiscal year.
18 (2) For principals who were eligible for longevity in the 2016-2017 fiscal year,
19 the sum of the following:
20 a. The salary the principal received in the 2016-2017 fiscal year pursuant
21 to Section 9.1 or Section 9.2 of S.L. 2016-94.
22 b. The longevity that the principal would have received as provided for
23 State employees under the North Carolina Human Resources Act for
24 the 2016-2017 fiscal year based on the principal's current years of
25 service.
26 (3) For principals who were not eligible for longevity in the 2016-2017 fiscal
27 year, the salary the principal received in the 2016-2017 fiscal year pursuant to
28 Section 9.1 or Section 9.2 of S.L. 2016-94.
29 SECTION 7A.4.(f) For purposes of this section, the following definitions apply:
30 (1) First year. – The school year immediately preceding the second year.
31 (2) Fourth year. – The school year immediately preceding the applicable school
32 year.
33 (3) Second year. – The school year immediately preceding the third year.
34 (4) The applicable fiscal year. – The fiscal year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium
35 in which the principal is employed.
36 (5) The applicable school year. – The school year of the 2025-2027 fiscal
37 biennium in which the principal is employed.
38 (6) Third year. – The school year immediately preceding the fourth year.
39

43 "§ 115C-285.5. Bonuses for principals.

45 Instruction shall administer a bonus in each fiscal year to any principal who supervised a school
46 as a principal for a majority of the previous school year if that school was in the top fifty percent
47 (50%) of school growth in the State during the previous school year, calculated by the State
48 Board pursuant to G.S. 115C-83.15(c), as follows:
49 Principal Bonus Schedule
50 Statewide Growth Percentage Bonus
51 Top 5% $15,000
Page 120 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 Top 10% $10,000
2 Top 15% $5,000
3 Top 20% $2,500
4 Top 50% $1,000
5 A principal shall receive no more than one bonus pursuant to this section. The bonus shall be
6 paid at the highest amount for which the principal qualifies.
7 (b) The bonus awarded pursuant to this section shall be in addition to any regular wage
8 or other bonus the principal receives or is scheduled to receive.
9 (c) Notwithstanding G.S. 135-1(7a), the bonuses awarded pursuant to this section are not
10 compensation under Article 1 of Chapter 135 of the General Statutes, Retirement System for
11 Teachers and State Employees.
12 (d) It is the intent of the General Assembly that funds provided pursuant to this section
13 will supplement principal compensation and not supplant local funds.
14 (e) The bonus provided pursuant to this section shall be paid no later than October 31 of
15 each year to qualifying principals employed as of October 1 of that year."

17 ASSISTANT PRINCIPAL SALARIES
18 SECTION 7A.6.(a) For each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium, beginning July
19 1, 2025, assistant principals shall receive a monthly salary based on the salary schedule for
20 teachers who are classified as "A" teachers plus nineteen percent (19%). An assistant principal
21 shall be placed on the step on the salary schedule that reflects the total number of years of
22 experience as a certified employee of the public schools. For purposes of this section, an
23 administrator with a one-year provisional assistant principal's certificate shall be considered
24 equivalent to an assistant principal.
25 SECTION 7A.6.(b) Assistant principals with certification based on academic
26 preparation at the six-year degree level shall be paid a salary supplement of one hundred
27 twenty-six dollars ($126.00) per month and at the doctoral degree level shall be paid a salary
28 supplement of two hundred fifty-three dollars ($253.00) per month.
29 SECTION 7A.6.(c) Beginning with the 2017-2018 fiscal year, in lieu of providing
30 annual longevity payments to assistant principals on the assistant principal salary schedule, the
31 amounts of those longevity payments are included in the monthly amounts provided to assistant
32 principals pursuant to subsection (a) of this section.
33 SECTION 7A.6.(d) An assistant principal compensated in accordance with this
34 section for the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall receive an amount equal to the greater of the
35 following:
36 (1) The applicable amount on the salary schedule for the applicable year.
37 (2) For assistant principals who were eligible for longevity in the 2016-2017 fiscal
38 year, the sum of the following:
39 a. The salary the assistant principal received in the 2016-2017 fiscal year
40 pursuant to Section 9.1 or Section 9.2 of S.L. 2016-94.
41 b. The longevity that the assistant principal would have received as
42 provided for State employees under the North Carolina Human
43 Resources Act for the 2016-2017 fiscal year based on the assistant
44 principal's current years of service.
45 (3) For assistant principals who were not eligible for longevity in the 2016-2017
46 fiscal year, the salary the assistant principal received in the 2016-2017 fiscal
47 year pursuant to Section 9.1 or Section 9.2 of S.L. 2016-94.

44 ESTABLISH SCHOOL OF CIVIC LIFE AND LEADERSHIP
45 SECTION 8.2.(a) Chapter 116 of the General Statutes is amended by adding a new
46 Article to read:
47 "Article 31B.
48 "The School of Civic Life and Leadership.
49 "§ 116-258.1. The School of Civic Life and Leadership established.
50 (a) For purposes of this Article, the term "the School" refers to the School of Civic Life
51 and Leadership established pursuant to subsection (b) of this section.
Page 124 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (b) The Board of Trustees of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, in
2 consultation with the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina, the Provost of
3 the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, and faculty and administration officials at the
4 University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, shall establish the School of Civic Life and
5 Leadership as a separate reporting unit of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
6 "§ 116-258.2. Scope.
7 The School shall do at least the following:
8 (1) Provide course opportunities for students. Courses may focus on the
9 development of democratic competencies informed by American history, the
10 American political tradition, and the study of the great texts and traditions of
11 Western civilization that form the foundation of the American republic. The
12 purpose of these courses is to foster public discourse and civil engagement
13 necessary to promote democracy and benefit society.
14 (2) Develop programming to address the topics identified in subdivision (1) of
15 this section and provide resources to students, faculty, and the general public,
16 as needed.
17 "§ 116-258.3. Faculty.
18 (a) The Dean of the School shall be appointed by the Chancellor of the University of
19 North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with the consent of the Board of Trustees of the University of
20 North Carolina at Chapel Hill. Neither the Chancellor nor the Board of Trustees shall delegate
21 this responsibility to another party.
22 (b) All faculty hired by or appointed to the School shall be subject to the approval of the
23 Dean of the School.
24 (c) Faculty members may hold joint or courtesy appointments with other reporting units
25 of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. All joint and courtesy appointments shall be
26 made at the discretion of the Dean of the School."
27 SECTION 8.2.(b) For the 2025-2026 academic year, the following shall occur:
28 (1) The School of Civic Life and Leadership (the School) shall employ at least 20
29 faculty members hired from outside the University of North Carolina at
30 Chapel Hill. These faculty members shall be hired with permanent tenure or
31 be eligible to receive permanent tenure in accordance with policies adopted
32 by The Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina and the
33 University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
34 (2) The School shall not employ any additional faculty by joint or courtesy
35 appointment with other reporting units of the University of North Carolina at
36 Chapel Hill unless the school has employed at least 20 faculty members hired
37 from outside the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in accordance
38 with this subsection.
39 SECTION 8.2.(c) Notwithstanding G.S. 116-30.2, the recurring funds allocated to
40 the School of Civic Life and Leadership (the School) by this section beginning in the 2025-2026
41 fiscal year shall be used only to support the School and shall not be redirected for any other
42 purpose. In addition, these funds shall be used to supplement and not supplant any funds the
43 School would otherwise receive, including funds received by the School based on enrollment.
44 SECTION 8.2.(d) No later than November 15, 2026, the Board of Trustees of the
45 University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill shall report to the Joint Legislative Education
46 Oversight Committee and the Fiscal Research Division on the progress made toward establishing
47 the School of Civic Life and Leadership (the School), including at least the following
48 information:
49 (1) Courses and other programming provided by the School.
50 (2) Faculty hired by the School, including the number of faculty members hired
51 from outside the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 125

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (3) Uses of funds appropriated to the School pursuant to this section.
2 (4) Any other matter the Board deems relevant to the progress of establishing the
3 School.

5 FUNDING REDUCTION FOR CERTAIN CENTERS AND INSTITUTES
6 SECTION 8.3.(a) For each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium, in order to
7 maximize the receipt of federal funds and improve the quality of research provided in this State,
8 the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina shall reduce by at least thirty-three
9 million six hundred sixty thousand dollars ($33,660,000) the total recurring funds allocated to
10 the constituent institutions of The University of North Carolina for centers or institutes at those
11 institutions that are identified by the Board of Governors as low-performing, redundant, failing
12 to maximize the receipt of federal grant funds, or are otherwise in conflict with federal law, State
13 law, the policies of the Board of Governors, or the vision and purpose of The University of North
14 Carolina. These reductions shall not be allocated by the Board of Governors in an
15 across-the-board method but shall be done in a manner that recognizes the importance of the
16 academic missions and differences among the identified constituent institutions. As a part of this
17 process, the Board of Governors shall require the constituent institutions of The University of
18 North Carolina to evaluate the centers and institutes at those institutions and provide
19 recommendations to the Board of Governors on reductions that would be appropriate under this
20 section.
21 SECTION 8.3.(b) No later than April 1 of each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal
22 biennium, the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina shall report on the
23 implementation of the reductions required in this section for that fiscal year to the Joint
24 Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal Research Division.
25

27 SECTION 8.4. Part 4B of Article 1 of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes is
28 repealed.

30 ACADEMIC PROGRAM REVIEW
31 SECTION 8.5.(a) For purposes of this section, the term "identified constituent
32 institution" refers to every constituent institution of The University of North Carolina except the
33 following:
34 (1) The North Carolina School of Science and Mathematics.
35 (2) The University of North Carolina at Asheville.
36 (3) The University of North Carolina at Greensboro.
37 SECTION 8.5.(b) No later than June 30, 2026, in order to reduce the recurring funds
38 appropriated to the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina in the 2026-2027
39 fiscal year and allocated to identified constituent institutions by the sum of at least twenty million
40 dollars ($20,000,000) in accordance with this section, each identified constituent institution, in
41 collaboration with The University of North Carolina System Office, shall review and reduce or
42 eliminate academic programs or other curriculum at that institution that are low-performing,
43 redundant, have insufficient enrollment, produce an insufficient return on investment, or are
44 otherwise in conflict with the policies of the Board of Governors or the vision and purpose of
45 The University of North Carolina.
46 SECTION 8.5.(c) The University of North Carolina System Office shall report on
47 the implementation of the reductions required in this section for the 2026-2027 fiscal year to the
48 Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the Fiscal Research Division no later than
49 April 1, 2027.

51 ceases operation. Upon request, the local school administrative unit

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 shall maintain the facilities and premises of the laboratory school and
2 keep them in good repair and tenantable condition by providing all
3 routine custodial services and routine facilities maintenance services,

5 maintenance of exterior landscaping and snow removal, and timely
6 repair of the facilities and premises. The chancellor is authorized to
7 execute the lease agreement and memoranda of agreement for the
8 operation of a laboratory school.

12 administrative unit and attend the laboratory school, including any
13 students who are homeless and require assistance pursuant to 42
14 U.S.C. § 11301, et seq., the McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance

17 a laboratory school student resides in the unit or how the unit's
18 transportation policies and practices are applied to other students and
19 (ii) upon request, include providing transportation of students and
20 personnel for laboratory school extracurricular activities and
21 educational trips in the same manner as other schools in the unit for
22 that school year.

24 hundred percent (100%) muscadine grape juice is made available to
25 students as a part of the school's nutrition program or through the

41 reimbursement funds.

43 percent (100%) muscadine grape juice is made available to
44 students as part of the school's nutrition program or through the
45 operation of the school's vending facilities.

50 1. Services required by the Department of Public Instruction for
51 children with disabilities.

41 "§ 116-43.30. Advanced Placement courses for secondary school students.
42 (a) It is the intent of the State to enhance accessibility and encourage secondary school
43 students to enroll in and successfully complete more rigorous Advanced Placement courses to
44 enable success in postsecondary education for all students. To attain this goal, to the extent funds
45 are made available for this purpose, secondary school students with the greatest financial need
46 who are enrolled in the North Carolina School of Science and Mathematics and the high school
47 academic program at the University of North Carolina School of the Arts shall be exempt from
48 paying any fees for registration and administration of examinations for Advanced Placement
49 courses in which the student is enrolled, regardless of the score the student achieves on an
50 examination.

15 ESTABLISH OFFICE OF LEARNING RESEARCH
16 SECTION 8.13.(a) Article 31A of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes is amended
17 by adding the following new section to read:
18 "§ 116-257. Office of Learning Research.
19 (a) Office of Learning Research Established. – There is established the Office of
20 Learning Research (OLR) to identify and evaluate the efficacy and efficiency of programs,
21 activities, initiatives, procedures, and any other factors related to elementary and secondary
22 education in the State. The OLR shall be housed within the Collaboratory.
23 (b) Funding and Duties of the OLR. – Funding allocated to the Collaboratory for the OLR
24 shall be administered by the Collaboratory pursuant to the provisions of G.S. 116-255(c). These
25 funds shall be used to do at least the following:
26 (1) Provide information and support needed by elementary and secondary public
27 schools, university leaders, and elected officials to make evidence-based
28 decisions.
29 (2) Collaborate with constituent institutions of The University of North Carolina
30 and other stakeholders to implement innovative policies and programs to
31 accelerate learning for all students.
32 (3) Work with external research resources and partners to evaluate local, State,
33 and federal programs in order to establish metrics and assess return on
34 investment.
35 (4) Support the operations of the OLR.
36 (c) Access to Information. – All units of State and local government, including the State
37 Board of Education, the Department of Public Instruction, and public school units, shall provide
38 access to the OLR to records, data, processes, personnel, and any other information deemed
39 relevant by the Collaboratory to carry out its duties pursuant to G.S. 116-255(b). The access
40 provided to the Collaboratory pursuant to this subsection shall be in addition to any access
41 provided related to funding received by the Collaboratory under G.S. 116-255(c)."
42 SECTION 8.13.(b) Section 2A.8 of S.L. 2024-57 reads as rewritten:
43 "SECTION 2A.8.(a) OLR Established. OLR Funds. – There is appropriated from the
44 General Fund to the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina the sum of one
45 million five hundred thousand dollars ($1,500,000) in recurring funds for the 2024-2025 fiscal
46 year to be allocated to the North Carolina Collaboratory to establish and operate the Office of
47 Learning Research (OLR), beginning in the 2024-2025 fiscal year. The purpose of OLR is to
48 identify and evaluate the efficacy and efficiency of programs, activities, initiatives, procedures,
49 and any other factors related to elementary and secondary education in the State.

30 information deemed relevant by the Office or the Collaboratory, to the extent otherwise permitted
31 under State and federal law, to carry out the provisions of this section.

33 the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee on the progress made in establishing and
34 operating the OLR pursuant to this section. For each fiscal year OLR is in operation, the
35 Collaboratory shall include in the annual report required by G.S. 116-256 information on the
36 activities of OLR from the prior fiscal year."

3 UNIVERSITY OF NORTH CAROLINA SYSTEM FACULTY REALIGNMENT
4 INCENTIVE PROGRAM
5 SECTION 8.15. Part 1 of Article 1 of Chapter 116 of the General Statutes is amended
6 by adding the following new section to read:
7 "§ 116-17.4. Faculty Realignment Incentive Program.
8 (a) For purposes of this section, the following definitions shall apply:
9 (1) Identified faculty member. – A full-time, tenured faculty member employed
10 by a constituent institution of The University of North Carolina who meets all
11 of the following criteria:
12 a. Is at least 55 years of age.
13 b. Meets either of the following criteria:
14 1. Is eligible to commence retirement with an early or service
15 retirement allowance under the Teachers' and State Employees'
16 Retirement System (TSERS).
17 2. Is vested in the Optional Retirement Program (ORP) for The
18 University of North Carolina.
19 c. Does not receive disability or workers' compensation benefits.
20 (2) Program. – The Faculty Realignment Incentive Program established pursuant
21 to this section.
22 (b) There is established the Faculty Realignment Incentive Program to authorize the
23 Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina to permit constituent institutions of The
24 University of North Carolina to award severance payments to identified faculty members to
25 provide long-term cost-savings and improved operational efficiencies for The University of
26 North Carolina. To the extent funds are made available for this purpose, funds for the Program
27 shall be distributed among constituent institutions based on criteria established by the President
28 of The University of North Carolina. The Program shall meet at least the following requirements:
29 (1) An identified faculty member shall be selected to receive a payment under the
30 Program in the discretion of the constituent institution where the identified
31 faculty member is employed.
32 (2) Severance payments shall be equivalent to the identified faculty member's
33 base salary from the prior academic year.
34 (3) Severance payments shall be exempt from payroll deductions for retirement
35 contributions and shall not be considered compensation for purposes of the
36 supplemental plans administered by The University of North Carolina or plans
37 administered by the Supplemental Retirement Board of Trustees under
38 G.S. 135-96.
39 (4) If an identified faculty member does not qualify for the full employer premium
40 contribution for retiree health coverage provided under TSERS or ORP, then
41 the constituent institution where the identified faculty member is employed
42 may provide the faculty member, in addition to a severance payment, an
43 amount equivalent to 12 months of the full employer contribution to the
44 employee health insurance premium.
45 (c) December 1 of each year, the Board of Governors shall report at least the following
46 information on the Program to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee and the
47 Fiscal Research Division, disaggregated by constituent institution:
48 (1) The number of identified faculty members that received funds under the
49 Program.
50 (2) The total amount paid out by the Program."
51
