29 NC SYMPHONY CHALLENGE GRANT
30 SECTION 14.1.(a) Of the funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Natural
31 and Cultural Resources, the sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in recurring funds for each
32 year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall be allocated to the North Carolina Symphony as
33 provided in this section. It is the intent of the General Assembly that the North Carolina
34 Symphony raise at least seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in non-State funds for the 2025-2026
35 fiscal year and seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in non-State funds for the 2026-2027 fiscal
36 year. The North Carolina Symphony cannot use funds transferred from the organization's
37 endowment to its operating budget to achieve the fundraising targets set out in subsections (b)
38 and (c) of this section.
39 SECTION 14.1.(b) For the 2025-2026 fiscal year, the North Carolina Symphony
40 shall receive allocations from the Department of Natural and Cultural Resources as follows:
41 (1) Upon raising the initial sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in non-State
42 funding, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the sum of six hundred
43 thousand dollars ($600,000).
44 (2) Upon raising an additional sum of two million dollars ($2,000,000) in
45 non-State funding for a total amount of four million dollars ($4,000,000) in
46 non-State funds, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the sum of seven
47 hundred thousand dollars ($700,000).
48 (3) Upon raising an additional sum of three million dollars ($3,000,000) in
49 non-State funding for a total amount of seven million dollars ($7,000,000) in
50 non-State funds, the North Carolina Symphony shall receive the final sum of
51 seven hundred thousand dollars ($700,000) in the 2025-2026 fiscal year.

15 SUNDAY OPENING STATE HISTORIC SITE PILOT PROGRAM
16 SECTION 14.2.(a) Program Established. – Funds appropriated in this act to the
17 Department of Natural and Cultural Resources (Department) for the Sunday Opening State
18 Historic Site Pilot Program (Program) shall be used by the Department to open and operate the
19 following State Historic Sites on Sundays during each site's peak season:
20 (1) Bentonville Battlefield.
21 (2) Brunswick Town/Fort Anderson.
22 (3) CSS Neuse.
23 (4) Charlotte Hawkins Brown Museum.
24 (5) Fort Fisher.
25 (6) Governor Charles B. Aycock Birthplace.
26 (7) Historic Bath.
27 (8) Historic Edenton.
28 (9) Historic Halifax.
29 (10) North Carolina State Capitol.
30 (11) Reed Gold Mine.
31 (12) Roanoke Island Festival Park.
32 (13) Somerset Place.
33 (14) Thomas Day State Historic Site.
34 SECTION 14.2.(b) Notice. – The Department shall publish, update, or provide
35 notice of the new operating hours pursuant to the Program established in subsection (a) of this
36 section.
37 SECTION 14.2.(c) Reports. – The Department shall submit the following reports to
38 the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Agriculture and Natural and Economic Resources:
39 (1) By October 1, 2026, an interim report with (i) actual costs by site during the
40 2025-2026 fiscal year, (ii) Sunday visitation numbers by site during the
41 2025-2026 fiscal year, and (iii) preliminary recommendations.
42 (2) By April 1, 2027, an interim report with any funding recommendations the
43 Department has for the upcoming biennium.
44 (3) By October 1, 2027, a final report on the implementation of the Program.

32 UNDERWATER ARCHEOLOGY HOBBY LICENSE
33 SECTION 14.5.(a) Article 3 of Chapter 121 of the General Statutes is amended by
34 adding a new section to read:
35 "§ 121-25.2. Underwater archaeology hobby license.
36 (a) Hobby License Established. – The underwater archaeology hobby license authorizes
37 licensees to conduct noncommercial exploration of abandoned shipwrecks and the bottoms of
38 the navigable waters of the State and to recover by hand underwater archaeological artifacts to
39 which the State has title under G.S. 121-22. This license shall be issued to individual State
40 residents and nonresidents for terms of either one year or three years, at the option of the
41 applicant.
42 (b) Restrictions. – Holders of a hobby license shall not do any of the following:
43 (1) Recover or remove more than 10 underwater archaeological artifacts per day.
44 (2) Recover or remove archaeological artifacts buried deeper than 3 inches below
45 the upper substrate of the bottoms of navigable waters.
46 (3) Recover or remove underwater archaeological artifacts that are too fragile or
47 structurally unstable to be conserved intact.
48 (4) Recover or remove underwater archaeological artifacts using mechanical
49 dredging or lifting devices.
50 (5) Use magnetic devices to explore abandoned shipwreck sites.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 271

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (6) Destroy, rearrange, alter, or interfere with the structural integrity of an
2 abandoned shipwreck, including moving or removing timbers, fittings,
3 fastenings, or machinery.
4 (7) Explore, access, or recover artifacts from any of the following:
5 a. Sites that the Department has authorized persons to conduct
6 exploration, recovery, or salvage operations pursuant to a permit
7 issued under G.S. 121-25.
8 b. Sites listed on the National Register of Historic Places or the North
9 Carolina Register of Historic Places.
10 c. Any site managed by and under the jurisdiction of a federal agency,
11 unless the licensee has obtained prior permission from that federal
12 agency.
13 d. Any site that the Department has closed to the public for the purpose
14 of conducting or supervising the surveillance, protection, preservation,
15 survey, or systematic underwater archaeological recovery of
16 underwater materials defined in G.S. 121-22.
17 (c) Reporting Requirements. –
18 (1) Post-retrieval report. – Holders of a hobby license shall submit to the
19 Department a post-retrieval report no later than 10 days following the end of
20 the calendar quarter in which the licensee recovered underwater
21 archaeological artifacts. The post-retrieval report shall include a list of each
22 artifact recovered, including a description of the artifact and the location
23 where the artifact was removed, and any additional information required by
24 the Department.
25 (2) Annual report. – Holders of a hobby license shall submit an annual report to
26 the Department by December 31 of each year that includes a description of
27 the exploration and recovery activities carried out by the licensee during the
28 calendar year, and any additional information required by the Department.
29 Licensees shall submit an annual report even if the licensee did not engage in
30 any exploration activities during the calendar year.
31 (d) Relinquishment of Title. – Notwithstanding G.S. 121-4(12) or any other law
32 pertaining to the disposition of State property, the Department shall review each post-retrieval
33 report submitted by a licensee and, no later than 60 days after receiving a post-retrieval report,
34 shall relinquish title held by the State to all underwater archaeological artifacts recovered
35 pursuant to this section and properly documented in a post-retrieval report submitted by the
36 licensee; provided, however, that the Department may decline to relinquish title upon
37 determining that the licensee violated any provision of this section or any rules adopted by the
38 Department pursuant to this section.
39 (e) Grounds for Suspension, Revocation, or Denial of a Hobby License. – The
40 Department may deny, suspend, or revoke a hobby license for any of the following reasons:
41 (1) The employment of fraud, deceit, or misrepresentation in obtaining or
42 attempting to obtain a license or the renewal of a license.
43 (2) A history of conviction of a State or federal crime that bears upon an
44 individual's fitness to conduct activities related to the exploration,
45 surveillance, protection, preservation, and archeological recovery of property
46 subject to the exclusive dominion and control of the State under G.S. 121-22,
47 as determined by the Department pursuant to G.S. 121-25.1.
48 (3) A history of noncompliance with this Article or any rules adopted by the
49 Department, any conditions of a hobby license or permit issued by the
50 Department under G.S. 121-25, or any other State or federal law pertaining to
51 the exploration, recovery, or salvage of archeological or historic sites.
Page 272 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (4) Selling any underwater archaeological artifacts recovered pursuant to this
2 section.
3 (f) Fees. – The Department may establish fees for issuing and renewing hobby licenses,
4 but those fees shall not exceed the following amounts:
5 (1) One-year hobby license:
6 a. State Residents $20.00.
7 b. Nonresidents $30.00.
8 (2) Three-year hobby license:
9 a. State Residents $50.00.
10 b. Nonresidents $75.00."
11 SECTION 14.5.(b) G.S. 121-25 reads as rewritten:
12 "§ 121-25. License to conduct exploration, recovery or salvage operations.
13 (a) Any Except as provided in G.S. 121-25.2, any qualified person, firm or corporation
14 desiring to conduct any type of exploration, recovery or salvage operations, in the course of
15 which any part of a derelict vessel or its contents or other archaeological site may be removed,
16 displaced or destroyed, shall first make application to the Department of Natural and Cultural
17 Resources and obtain a permit or license to conduct such operations. If the Department of Natural
18 and Cultural Resources shall find that the granting of such permit or license is in the best interest
19 of the State, it may grant such applicant a permit or license for such a period of time and under
20 such conditions as the Department may deem to be in the best interest of the State. Such permit
21 or license may include but need not be limited to any of the following:
22 (1) Payment of monetary fee to be set by the Department.
23 (2) That a portion or all of the historic material or artifacts be delivered to custody
24 and possession of the Department.
25 (3) That a portion of all of such relics or artifacts may be sold or retained by the
26 licensee.
27 (4) That a portion or all of such relics or artifacts may be sold or traded by the
28 Department.
29 Permits or licenses may be renewed upon or prior to expiration upon such terms as the
30 applicant and the Department may mutually agree. Holders of permits or licenses shall be
31 responsible for obtaining permission of any federal agencies having jurisdiction, including the
32 United States Coast Guard, the United States Department of the Navy and the United States Army
33 Corps of Engineers prior to conducting any salvaging operations.
34 …."

1 TOPSAIL ISLAND PRESERVATION FUNDS
2 SECTION 14.6. Funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Natural and

8 funds provided to it under this section. DNCR shall transfer these funds to the General Fund.

33 prosecuting witness, the following costs shall be assessed and collected. No costs may be
34 assessed when a case is dismissed. Only upon entry of a written order, supported by findings of

36 assessed under this section or (ii) waive or reduce costs assessed under subdivision (7), (8), (8a),

41 waiver of all or part of the order of court costs at least 15 days prior to hearing. Notice shall be

14 STUDY INCREASING ENERGY EFFICIENCIES
15 SECTION 19.2.(a) Study. – The Department of Public Safety, in collaboration with
16 the Department of Justice, the Department of Adult Correction and Juvenile Justice, the State
17 Highway Patrol, and the State Bureau of Investigation, shall study what energy-saving initiatives
18 may be implemented by each department, including any agency or division controlled by or
19 located for administrative purposes in the department, to reduce energy costs incurred by each
20 department. For purposes of this subsection, the term "energy-saving initiatives" includes repairs,
21 renovations, or other improvements to any building or facility owned, controlled, or otherwise
22 occupied by a department and any agency or division controlled by or located for administrative
23 purposes in the department.
24 SECTION 19.2.(b) Report. – The Department of Public Safety shall submit a report
25 on the findings of the study required under subsection (a) of this section, including any legislative
26 recommendations, to the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Justice and Public Safety by
27 March 1, 2026.

30 SENATE CONFIRMATION OF ADJUTANT GENERAL
31 SECTION 19.7.(a) G.S. 127A-19 reads as rewritten:
32 "§ 127A-19. Adjutant General.
33 (a) The military head of the militia shall be the Adjutant General who shall hold the rank
34 of major general with federal recognition at time of appointment or attain the rank of major
35 general pursuant to this section. The Adjutant General shall be appointed by the Governor in the
36 Governor's capacity as commander in chief of the militia, in consultation with the Secretary of
37 Public Safety, and Safety. The appointment is subject to the process in accordance with
38 G.S. 143B-9 as if the Adjutant General was a head of a principal State department. The Adjutant
39 General shall serve at the pleasure of the Governor. The Adjutant General, while holding this
40 office, shall be a member of the active North Carolina National Guard. If an appointed Adjutant
41 General does not attain the rank of major general with federal recognition within a reasonable
42 period of time from the date of appointment, the Governor shall replace the Adjutant General
43 with an appointee who meets the criteria in in, and appointed in accordance with, this section. A
44 "reasonable period of time" shall take into account time in grade requirements for promotion or
45 promotions and administrative periods necessary to complete the promotion process.
46 (b) In order to be eligible for appointment as Adjutant General, a person shall be a
47 resident of the State of North Carolina and meet all of the following requirements:
48 (1) The person shall have a total of at least 10 years of commissioned service in
49 any component or components of the Armed Forces of the United States.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 287

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) The person shall have a minimum of three years commissioned service in the
2 Army or Air National Guard within the six-year period previous to the
3 appointment date.
4 (3) The person, at the time of appointment, shall be one of the following:
5 a. A major general with federal recognition or who is eligible for federal
6 recognition.
7 b. A brigadier general with federal recognition or who is eligible for
8 federal recognition and who is eligible for promotion to major general
9 with federal recognition.
10 c. A colonel with federal recognition or who is eligible for federal
11 recognition; who is eligible for promotion and federal recognition as a
12 brigadier general; and who is eligible for promotion to major general
13 with federal recognition.
14 (4) The person shall have completed all service school or other criteria for
15 promotion to general officer with federal recognition.
16 (5) The person shall have a minimum of 12 months in command of either (i) an
17 Army or Air National Guard unit or (ii) a unit in any component of the Armed
18 Forces of the United States.
19 (c) Subject to the approval of the Governor and in consultation with the Secretary of
20 Public Safety, the Adjutant General may appoint:
21 (1) A deputy adjutant general, who may hold the same rank as the Adjutant
22 General.
23 (2) Two assistant adjutants general for the Army National Guard and an assistant
24 adjutant general for the Air National Guard, each of whom may hold the rank
25 of brigadier general.
26 The appointees authorized by this subsection shall serve at the pleasure of the Governor. The
27 Adjutant General may also employ staff members and other personnel as authorized by the
28 Secretary and funded."
29 SECTION 19.7.(b) This section is effective when it becomes law.

31 MILITARY JUDGES OF THE NORTH CAROLINA NATIONAL GUARD
32 APPOINTMENT MODIFICATIONS
33 SECTION 19.8.(a) G.S. 127A-50 reads as rewritten:
34 "§ 127A-50. Summary courts-martial.
35 (a) In the North Carolina National Guard, not in the service of the United States, summary
36 courts-martial may be appointed by any of the following:
37 (1) Any person who may convene a general or special court-martial.
38 (2) The commander of a battalion, comparable or higher command of the North
39 Carolina Army National Guard, provided that the commander is an officer of
40 the grade of major or above.
41 (3) The commander of a detached squadron, comparable or higher command of
42 the North Carolina Air National Guard, provided that the commander is an
43 officer of the grade of major or above.
44 (b) The court acting under this section shall consist of one officer who shall have the
45 power to administer oaths and try enlisted personnel of each respective command for breaches
46 of discipline and violations of laws governing those organizations. These courts shall also have
47 the power to impose punishments in like manner and to the extent prescribed by the Uniform
48 Code of Military Justice and Manual for Courts-Martial, United States, as shall be in use by the
49 Armed Forces of the United States at the time of the offense, except that no officer, the State
50 military judge, and a judge advocate detailed to the court as a hearing officer. A summary
51 court-martial shall have the authority to impose fines of not more than five hundred dollars
Page 288 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 ($500.00), to impose forfeitures of two-thirds pay for one month, to restrict to limits, to impose
2 extra duty, and to reduce the rank of enlisted persons E7 and above by up to two ranks and
3 enlisted persons E6 and below to the rank of E1.
4 (c) No court acting under this section shall have the authority to impose confinement as
5 part of a sentence.
6 (d) There shall be no right during summary courts-martial to demand trial by
7 court-martial."
8 SECTION 19.8.(b) G.S. 127A-50.1 reads as rewritten:
9 "§ 127A-50.1. Military judges.
10 The Adjutant General shall appoint military judges to preside over courts-martial of the North
11 Carolina National Guard not in federal service. Minimum requirements for appointment as a
12 military judge are:are the following:
13 (1) Certification as a military judge by the Judge Advocate General of the United
14 States Army, Air Force, Navy, Marines, or Coast Guard.
15 (2) Designation as a judge advocate by the Judge Advocate General of the United
16 States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines, or Coast Guard.
17 (3) Membership in the North Carolina National Guard, the National Guard of
18 another state, or the active or reserve components of the Armed Forces of the
19 United States.
20 (4) A member in good standing for at least 10 years of either of the following:
21 a. The bar of the highest court of this State or any other state.
22 b. The bar of a federal court.
23 (5) Hold the rank of lieutenant colonel or above."
24 SECTION 19.8.(c) Subsection (a) of this section is effective when it becomes law
25 and applies to summary courts-martial initiated on or after that date. Subsection (b) of this section
26 is effective when it becomes law and applies to military judges serving on or after that date,
27 except the requirements of G.S. 127A-50.1, as amended by subsection (b) of this section, shall
28 only apply to appointments made on or after that date. The remainder of this section is effective
29 when it becomes law.

38 (3a) Disaster recovery period. – The period following a disaster response period in
39 which the State Emergency Response Team provides for the rapid and orderly
40 rehabilitation of persons and restoration of property by focusing on restoration
41 of critical infrastructure, rebuilding of communities, and revitalization of
42 economic activity.
43 (3b) Disaster response period. – The initial 30-day period after a state of emergency
44 is declared in which the State Emergency Response Team is activated and
45 provides for the prompt and efficient rescue, care, and treatment of threatened
46 or affected persons in order to save lives, prevent further injury, and provide
47 basic life necessities to impacted persons. The disaster response period may
48 be extended for an additional 30 days by the Governor with the concurrence
49 of the Council of State. The State Emergency Response Team may also
50 engage in disaster recovery efforts during the disaster response period as
51 needed.

5 LIMIT USE OF COMMUNITY PROGRAM FUNDS
6 SECTION 19.10.(a) Funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Public
7 Safety for the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium for community program contracts, that are not required
8 for or used for community program contracts, may be used only for the following:
9 (1) Other statewide residential programs that provide Level 2 intermediate
10 dispositional alternatives for juveniles.
11 (2) Statewide community programs that provide Level 2 intermediate
12 dispositional alternatives for juveniles.
13 (3) Regional programs that are collaboratives of two or more Juvenile Crime
14 Prevention Councils which provide Level 2 intermediate dispositional
15 alternatives for juveniles.
16 (4) The Juvenile Crime Prevention Council funds to be used for the Level 2
17 intermediate dispositional alternatives for juveniles listed in
18 G.S. 7B-2506(13) through (23).
19 SECTION 19.10.(b) Funds appropriated by this act to the Department of Public
20 Safety for the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium for community programs may not be used for staffing,
21 operations, maintenance, or any other expenses of youth development centers or detention
22 facilities.
23 SECTION 19.10.(c) The Department of Public Safety shall submit an electronic
24 report by October 1 of each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium on all expenditures made in
25 the preceding fiscal year from the miscellaneous contract line in Fund Code 1230 to the chairs of
26 the House of Representatives Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety and the
27 Senate Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety and the Fiscal Research Division.
28 The report shall include all of the following: an itemized list of the contracts that have been
29 executed, the amount of each contract, the date the contract was executed, the purpose of the
30 contract, the number of juveniles that will be served and the manner in which they will be served,
31 the amount of money transferred to the Juvenile Crime Prevention Council fund, and an itemized
32 list of grants allocated from the funds transferred to the Juvenile Crime Prevention Council fund.

22 PART XX. STATE BUREAU OF INVESTIGATION
23
24 USE OF SEIZED AND FORFEITED PROPERTY
25 SECTION 20.1.(a) Seized and forfeited assets transferred to the State Bureau of
26 Investigation (SBI) during the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium pursuant to applicable federal law shall
27 be credited to the budget of the SBI and shall result in an increase of law enforcement resources
28 for the SBI. The SBI shall make the following reports to the chairs of the House of
29 Representatives Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety and the Senate
30 Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety:
31 (1) A report upon receipt of any assets.
32 (2) A report that shall be made prior to use of the assets on their intended use and
33 the departmental priorities on which the assets may be expended.
34 (3) A report on receipts, expenditures, encumbrances, and availability of these
35 assets for the previous fiscal year, which shall be made no later than
36 September 1 of each year.
37 SECTION 20.1.(b) The General Assembly finds that the use of seized and forfeited
38 assets transferred pursuant to federal law for new personnel positions, new projects, acquisition
39 of real property, repair of buildings where the repair includes structural change, and construction
40 of or additions to buildings may result in additional expenses for the State in future fiscal periods.
41 Therefore, the SBI is prohibited from using these assets for such purposes without the prior
42 approval of the General Assembly.
43 SECTION 20.1.(c) Nothing in this section prohibits State law enforcement agencies
44 from receiving funds from the United States Department of Justice, the United States Department
45 of the Treasury, and the United States Department of Health and Human Services.
46
47 NO TRANSFER OF POSITIONS TO OTHER STATE AGENCIES
48 SECTION 20.2.(a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, and except as
49 otherwise provided in subsection (b) of this section, the Office of State Budget and Management
50 shall not transfer any positions, personnel, or funds from the State Bureau of Investigation to any
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 293

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 other State agency during the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium unless the transfer was included in the
2 base budget for one or both fiscal years of the biennium.
3 SECTION 20.2.(b) This section shall not apply to consolidation of information
4 technology positions into the Department of Information Technology pursuant to
5 G.S. 143B-1325.

7 TECHNICAL CORRECTIONS RELATED TO MAKING THE STATE BUREAU OF

9 SECTION 20.3.(a) G.S. 18B-902(b) reads as rewritten:
10 "(b) Investigation. – Before issuing a new permit, the Commission, with the assistance of
11 the ALE Division, shall investigate the applicant and the premises for which the permit is
12 requested. The Commission may request the assistance of local ABC officers in investigating
13 applications. An applicant shall cooperate fully with the investigation.
14 The Department of Public Safety State Bureau of Investigation (Bureau) may provide a
15 criminal record check to the ALE Division for a person who has applied for a permit through the
16 Commission. The ALE Division shall provide to the Department of Public Safety, Bureau, along
17 with the request, the fingerprints of the applicant, any additional information required by the
18 Department of Public Safety, Bureau, and a form signed by the applicant consenting to the check
19 of the criminal record and to the use of the fingerprints and other identifying information required
20 by the State or national repositories. The applicant's fingerprints shall be forwarded to the State
21 Bureau of Investigation used for a search of the State's criminal history record file, and the State
22 Bureau of Investigation shall forward a set of the fingerprints to the Federal Bureau of
23 Investigation for a national criminal history check. The ALE Division and the Commission shall
24 keep all information pursuant to this subsection privileged, in accordance with applicable State
25 law and federal guidelines, and the information shall be confidential and shall not be a public
26 record under Chapter 132 of the General Statutes.
27 The Department of Public Safety Bureau may charge each applicant a fee for conducting the
28 checks of criminal history records authorized by this subsection."
29 SECTION 20.3.(b) G.S. 74C-8.1(a) reads as rewritten:
30 "(a) Authorization. – Upon receipt of an application for a license, registration,
31 certification, or permit, the Board shall conduct a background investigation to determine whether
32 the applicant meets the requirements for a license, registration, certification, or permit set out in
33 G.S. 74C-8(d). The Department of Public Safety State Bureau of Investigation (Bureau) may
34 provide a criminal record check to the Board for a person who has applied for a new or renewal
35 license, registration, certification, or permit through the Board. The Board shall provide to the
36 Department of Public Safety, Bureau, along with the request, the fingerprints of a new applicant,
37 and the Department of Public Safety Bureau shall provide a criminal record check based upon
38 the applicant's fingerprints. The Board may request a criminal record check from the Department
39 of Public Safety Bureau for a renewal applicant based upon the applicant's fingerprints in
40 accordance with policy adopted by the Board. The Board shall provide any additional information
41 required by the Department of Public Safety Bureau and a form signed by the applicant
42 consenting to the check of the criminal record and to the use of the fingerprints and other
43 identifying information required by the State or national repositories. The applicant's fingerprints
44 shall be forwarded to the State Bureau of Investigation used for a search of the State's criminal
45 history record file, and the State Bureau of Investigation shall forward a set of the fingerprints to
46 the Federal Bureau of Investigation for a national criminal history check. The Department of
47 Public Safety Bureau may charge each applicant a fee for conducting the checks of criminal
48 history records authorized by this subsection.
49 The Board may require a new or renewal applicant to obtain a criminal record report from
50 one or more reporting services designated by the Board to provide criminal record reports.
51 Applicants are required to pay the designated reporting service for the cost of these reports."

23 SECTION 20.3.(d) G.S. 84-24 reads as rewritten:
24 "§ 84-24. Admission to practice.
25 For the purpose of examining applicants and providing rules and regulations for admission
26 to the Bar including the issuance of license therefor, there is hereby created the Board of Law
27 Examiners, which shall consist of 11 members of the Bar, elected by the Council, who need not
28 be members of the Council. No teacher in any law school, however, shall be eligible. The
29 members of the Board of Law Examiners elected from the Bar shall each hold office for a term
30 of three years.
31 The Board of Law Examiners shall elect a member of the Board as chair thereof, and the
32 Board may employ an executive secretary and provide such assistance as may be required to
33 enable the Board to perform its duties promptly and properly. The chair and any employees shall
34 serve for a period of time determined by the Board.
35 The examination shall be held in the manner and at the times as the Board of Law Examiners
36 may determine.
37 The Board of Law Examiners shall have full power and authority to make or cause to be made
38 such examinations and investigations as may be deemed by it necessary to satisfy it that the
39 applicants for admission to the Bar possess the qualifications of character and general fitness
40 requisite for an attorney and counselor-at-law and to this end the Board of Law Examiners shall
41 have the power of subpoena and to summons and examine witnesses under oath and to compel
42 their attendance and the production of books, papers and other documents and writings deemed
43 by it to be necessary or material to the inquiry and shall also have authority to employ and provide
44 assistance as may be required to enable it to perform its duties promptly and properly. Records,
45 papers, and other documents containing information collected and compiled by the Board or its
46 members or employees as a result of investigations, inquiries, or interviews conducted in
47 connection with examinations or licensing matters, are not public records within the meaning of
48 Chapter 132 of the General Statutes.
49 All applicants for admission to the Bar shall be fingerprinted to determine whether the
50 applicant has a record of criminal conviction in this State or in any other state or jurisdiction. The
51 information obtained as a result of the fingerprinting of an applicant shall be limited to the official
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 295

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 use of the Board of Law Examiners in determining the character and general fitness of the
2 applicant.
3 The Department of Public Safety State Bureau of Investigation (Bureau) may provide a
4 criminal record check to the Board of Law Examiners for a person who has applied for a license
5 through the Board. The Board shall provide to the Department of Public Safety, Bureau, along
6 with the request, the fingerprints of the applicant, any additional information required by the
7 Department of Public Safety, Bureau, and a form signed by the applicant consenting to the check
8 of the criminal record and to the use of the fingerprints and other identifying information required
9 by the State or national repositories. The applicant's fingerprints shall be forwarded to the State
10 Bureau of Investigation used for a search of the State's criminal history record file, and the State
11 Bureau of Investigation shall forward a set of the fingerprints to the Federal Bureau of
12 Investigation for a national criminal history check. The Board shall keep all information pursuant
13 to this subsection privileged, in accordance with applicable State law and federal guidelines, and
14 the information shall be confidential and shall not be a public record under Chapter 132 of the
15 General Statutes.
16 The Department of Public Safety Bureau may charge each applicant a fee for conducting the
17 checks of criminal history records authorized by this section.
18 The Board of Law Examiners, subject to the approval of the Council, shall by majority vote,
19 from time to time, make, alter, and amend such rules and regulations for admission to the Bar as
20 in their judgment shall promote the welfare of the State and the profession: Provided, that no
21 change in the educational requirements for admission to the Bar that establishes an additional or
22 greater requirement shall become effective until two years after the date of the adoption of the
23 change.
24 All rules and regulations, and modifications, alterations and amendments thereof, shall be
25 recorded and promulgated as provided in G.S. 84-21 in relation to the certificate of organization
26 and the rules and regulations of the Council.
27 Whenever the Council shall order the restoration of license to any person as authorized by
28 G.S. 84-32, it shall be the duty of the Board of Law Examiners to issue a written license to the
29 person, noting thereon that the license is issued in compliance with an order of the Council,
30 whether the license to practice law was issued by the Board of Law Examiners or the Supreme
31 Court in the first instance.
32 Appeals from the Board shall be had in accordance with rules or procedures as may be
33 approved by the Supreme Court as may be submitted under G.S. 84-21 or as may be promulgated
34 by the Supreme Court."

21 information required by the Department of Public Safety, Bureau, and a form signed by the
22 applicant consenting to the check of the criminal record and to the use of the fingerprints and
23 other identifying information required by the State or national repositories. The applicant's
24 fingerprints shall be forwarded to the State Bureau of Investigation used for a search of the State's
25 criminal history record file, and the State Bureau of Investigation shall forward a set of the
26 fingerprints to the Federal Bureau of Investigation for a national criminal history check. The
27 Board shall keep all information pursuant to this subsection privileged, in accordance with
28 applicable State law and federal guidelines, and the information shall be confidential and shall
29 not be a public record under Chapter 132 of the General Statutes.
30 The Department of Public Safety Bureau may charge each applicant a fee for conducting the
31 checks of criminal history records authorized by this subsection."

7 "§ 14-208.9. Change of address; change of academic status or educational employment
8 status; change of online identifier; change of name.
9 (a) If a person required to register changes address, the person shall report in person and
10 provide written notice of the new address not later than the third business day after the change to
11 the sheriff of the county with whom the person had last registered. If the person moves to another
12 county, the person shall also report in person to the sheriff of the new county and provide written
13 notice of the person's address not later than the tenth day after the change of address. Upon receipt
14 of the notice, the sheriff shall immediately forward this information to the Department of Public
15 Safety. Bureau. When the Department of Public Safety Bureau receives notice from a sheriff that
16 a person required to register is moving to another county in the State, the Department of Public
17 Safety Bureau shall inform the sheriff of the new county of the person's new residence.
18 (b) If a person required to register intends to move to another state, the person shall report
19 in person to the sheriff of the county of current residence at least three business days before the
20 date the person intends to leave this State to establish residence in another state or jurisdiction.
21 The person shall provide to the sheriff a written notification that includes all of the following
22 information: the address, municipality, county, and state of intended residence.
23 (1) If it appears to the sheriff that the record photograph of the sex offender no
24 longer provides a true and accurate likeness of the sex offender, then the
25 sheriff shall take a photograph of the offender to update the registration.
26 (2) The sheriff shall inform the person that the person must comply with the
27 registration requirements in the new state of residence. The sheriff shall also
28 immediately forward the information included in the notification to the
29 Department of Public Safety, Bureau, and the Department of Public Safety
30 Bureau shall inform the appropriate state official in the state to which the
31 registrant moves of the person's notification and new address.
32 (b1) A person who indicates his or her intent to reside in another state or jurisdiction and
33 later decides to remain in this State shall, within three business days after the date upon which
34 the person indicated he or she would leave this State, report in person to the sheriff's office to
35 which the person reported the intended change of residence, of his or her intent to remain in this
36 State. If the sheriff is notified by the sexual offender that he or she intends to remain in this State,
37 the sheriff shall promptly report this information to the Department of Public Safety.Bureau.
38 (c) If a person required to register changes his or her academic status either by enrolling
39 as a student or by terminating enrollment as a student, then the person shall, within three business
40 days, report in person to the sheriff of the county with whom the person registered and provide
41 written notice of the person's new status. The written notice shall include the name and address
42 of the institution of higher education at which the student is or was enrolled. The sheriff shall
43 immediately forward this information to the Department of Public Safety.Bureau.
44 (d) If a person required to register changes his or her employment status either by
45 obtaining employment at an institution of higher education or by terminating employment at an
46 institution of higher education, then the person shall, within three business days, report in person
47 to the sheriff of the county with whom the person registered and provide written notice of the
48 person's new status not later than the tenth day after the change to the sheriff of the county with
49 whom the person registered. The written notice shall include the name and address of the
50 institution of higher education at which the person is or was employed. The sheriff shall
51 immediately forward this information to the Department of Public Safety.Bureau.

5 relevant information based on registration information or notice of a change
6 of academic or educational employment status. If an institution of higher

11 "§ 14-208.15. Certain statewide registry information is public record: access to statewide
12 registry.
13 (a) The information in the statewide registry that is public record is the same as in
14 G.S. 14-208.10. The Department of Public Safety Bureau shall release any other relevant
15 information that is necessary to protect the public concerning a specific person, but shall not
16 release the identity of the victim of the offense that required registration under this Article.
17 (b) The Department of Public Safety Bureau shall provide free public access to automated
18 data from the statewide registry, including photographs provided by the registering sheriffs, via
19 the Internet. The public will be able to access the statewide registry to view an individual
20 registration record, a part of the statewide registry, or all of the statewide registry. The
21 Department of Public Safety Bureau may also provide copies of registry information to the public
22 upon written request and may charge a reasonable fee for duplicating costs and mailings costs.
23 (c) Upon request of an institution of higher education, the Sheriff of the county in which
24 the educational institution is located shall provide a report containing the registry information for
25 any registrant who has stated that the registrant is a student or employee, or expects to become a
26 student or employee, of that institution of higher education. The Department of Public Safety
27 Bureau shall provide each sheriff with the ability to generate the report from the statewide
28 registry. The report shall be provided electronically without charge. The institution of higher
29 education may receive a written report upon payment of reasonable duplicating costs and mailing
30 costs.
31 "§ 14-208.15A. Release of online identifiers to entity; fee.
32 (a) The Department of Public Safety Bureau may release registry information regarding
33 a registered offender's online identifier to an entity for the purpose of allowing the entity to
34 prescreen users or to compare the online identifier information with information held by the entity
35 as provided by this section.
36 (b) An entity desiring to prescreen its users or compare its database of registered users to

38 Public Safety Bureau to access the information. An entity that complies with the criteria
39 developed by the Department of Public Safety Bureau regarding the release and use of the online
40 identifier information and pays the fee may screen new users or compare its database of registered
41 users to the list of online identifiers of persons in the statewide registry as frequently as the
42 Department of Public Safety Bureau may allow for the purpose of identifying a registered user
43 associated with an online identifier contained in the statewide registry.
44 (c) The Department of Public Safety Bureau may charge an entity that submits a request
45 for the online identifiers of persons in the statewide registry an annual fee of one hundred dollars
46 ($100.00). Fees collected under this section shall be credited to the Department of Public Safety
47 Bureau and applied to the cost of providing this service.
48 (d) The Department of Public Safety Bureau shall develop standards regarding the release

50 information obtained from the statewide registry shall not be disclosed for any purpose other than

13 had last registered. Upon receipt of the notice, the sheriff shall immediately forward this
14 information to the Department of Public Safety. Bureau. If the juvenile moves to another county
15 in this State, the Department of Public Safety Bureau shall inform the sheriff of the new county
16 of the juvenile's new residence.
17 …

12 "§ 143B-1208.19. Criminal Information Network.Division of Criminal Information.
13 (a) The Department of Public Safety State Bureau of Investigation (Bureau) is authorized
14 to establish, devise, maintain and operate a system for receiving and disseminating to
15 participating agencies information collected, maintained and correlated under authority of
16 G.S. 143B-902. G.S. 143B-1208.16. The system shall be known as the Criminal Information
17 Network.Division of Criminal Information (DCI).
18 (b) The Department of Public Safety Bureau is authorized to cooperate with the Division
19 of Motor Vehicles, Department of Administration, and other State, local and federal agencies and
20 organizations in carrying out the purpose and intent of this section, and to utilize, in cooperation
21 with other State agencies and to the extent as may be practical, computers and related equipment
22 as may be operated by other State agencies.
23 (c) The Department of Public Safety, Bureau, after consultation with participating
24 agencies, shall adopt rules and regulations governing the organization and administration of the
25 Criminal Information Network, DCI, including rules and regulations governing the types of
26 information relating to the administration of criminal justice to be entered into the system, and
27 who shall have access to such information. The rules and regulations governing access to the
28 Criminal Information Network DCI shall not prohibit an attorney who has entered a criminal
29 proceeding in accordance with G.S. 15A-141 from obtaining information relevant to that
30 criminal proceeding. The rules and regulations governing access to the Criminal Information
31 Network DCI shall not prohibit an attorney who represents a person in adjudicatory or
32 dispositional proceedings for an infraction from obtaining the person's driving record or criminal
33 history.
34 (d) The Department Bureau may impose monthly fees on participating agencies. The
35 monthly fees collected under this subsection shall be used to offset the cost of operating and
36 maintaining the Criminal Information Network.DCI. The fee amount varies depending upon the
37 type of device. For a desktop device, the monthly fee is twenty-five dollars ($25.00) per device.
38 For a mobile device, the fee is twelve dollars ($12.00) per device.
39 (1) The Department may impose a monthly circuit fee on agencies that access the
40 Criminal Information Network through a circuit maintained and operated by
41 the Department of Public Safety. The amount of the monthly fee is three
42 hundred dollars ($300.00) plus an additional fee amount for each device
43 linked to the Network. The additional fee amount varies depending upon the
44 type of device. For a desktop device after the first seven desktop devices, the
45 additional monthly fee is twenty-five dollars ($25.00) per device. For a mobile
46 device, the additional monthly fee is twelve dollars ($12.00) per device.
47 (2) The Department may impose a monthly device fee on agencies that access the
48 Criminal Information Network through some other approved means. The
49 amount of the monthly device fee varies depending upon the type of device.
50 For a desktop device, the monthly fee is twenty-five dollars ($25.00) per
51 device. For a mobile device, the fee is twelve dollars ($12.00) per device."

37 SCHOOL SAFETY GRANTS
38 SECTION 20.7.(a) Definitions. – For the purposes of this section, the following
39 definitions shall apply:
40 (1) Community partner. – A public or private entity, including, but not limited to,
41 a nonprofit corporation or a local management entity/managed care
42 organization (LME/MCO), that partners with a public school unit to provide
43 services or pay for the provision of services for the unit.
44 (2) School health support personnel. – School psychologists, school counselors,
45 school nurses, and school social workers.
46 SECTION 20.7.(b) Program; Purpose. – The Executive Director of the Center for
47 Safer Schools shall establish the School Safety Grants Program (Program) for the 2025-2027
48 fiscal biennium. The purpose of the Program shall be to improve safety in public school units by
49 providing grants in each fiscal year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium for (i) services for students
50 in crisis, (ii) school safety training, (iii) safety equipment in schools, and (iv) subsidizing the
51 School Resource Officer Grants Program.
Page 310 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 20.7.(c) Grant Applications. – A public school unit may submit an
2 application to the Executive Director of the Center for Safer Schools for one or more grants
3 pursuant to this section in each year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium. The application shall
4 include an assessment, to be performed in conjunction with a local law enforcement agency, of
5 the need for improving school safety within the public school unit that would receive the funding
6 or services. The application shall identify current and ongoing needs and estimated costs
7 associated with those needs.
8 SECTION 20.7.(d) Criteria and Guidelines. – The Executive Director of the Center
9 for Safer Schools shall develop criteria and guidelines for the administration and use of the grants
10 pursuant to this section, including any documentation required to be submitted by applicants. In
11 assessing grant applications, the Executive Director shall consider at least all of the following
12 factors:
13 (1) The level of resources available to the public school unit that would receive
14 the funding.
15 (2) Whether the public school unit has received other grants for school safety.
16 (3) The overall impact on student safety in the public school unit if the identified
17 needs are funded.
18 SECTION 20.7.(e) Grants for Students in Crisis. – Of the funds appropriated by this
19 act for the grants provided in this section, the Executive Director of the Center for Safer Schools,
20 in consultation with the Department of Health and Human Services, shall award grants to public
21 school units to contract with community partners to provide or pay for the provision of any of
22 the following crisis services:
23 (1) Crisis respite services for parents or guardians of an individual student to
24 prevent more intensive or costly levels of care.
25 (2) Training and expanded services for therapeutic foster care families and
26 licensed child placement agencies that provide services to students who (i)
27 need support to manage their health, welfare, and safety and (ii) have any of
28 the following:
29 a. Cognitive or behavioral problems.
30 b. Developmental delays.
31 c. Aggressive behavior.
32 (3) Evidence-based therapy services aligned with targeted training for students
33 and their parents or guardians, including any of the following:
34 a. Parent-child interaction therapy.
35 b. Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy.
36 c. Dialectical behavior therapy.
37 d. Child-parent psychotherapy.
38 (4) Any other crisis service, including peer-to-peer mentoring, that is likely to
39 increase school safety. Of the funds appropriated by this act for the grants
40 provided in this section, the Executive Director shall use no more than three
41 hundred fifty thousand dollars ($350,000) in each fiscal year of the 2025-2027
42 fiscal biennium for the services identified in this subdivision.
43 SECTION 20.7.(f) Grants for Training to Increase School Safety. – Of the funds
44 appropriated by this act for the grants provided in this section, the Executive Director of the
45 Center for Safer Schools, in consultation with the Department of Health and Human Services,
46 shall award grants to public school units to contract with community partners to address school
47 safety by providing training to help students develop healthy responses to trauma and stress. The
48 training shall be targeted and evidence-based and shall include any of the following services:
49 (1) Counseling on Access to Lethal Means (CALM) training for school health
50 support personnel, local first responders, and teachers on the topics of suicide
51 prevention and reducing access by students to lethal means.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 311

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Training for school health support personnel on comprehensive and
2 evidence-based clinical treatments for students and their parents or guardians,
3 including any of the following:
4 a. Parent-child interaction therapy.
5 b. Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy.
6 c. Behavioral therapy.
7 d. Dialectical behavior therapy.
8 e. Child-parent psychotherapy.
9 (3) Training for students and school employees on community resilience models
10 to improve understanding and responses to trauma and significant stress.
11 (4) Training for school health support personnel on Modular Approach to
12 Therapy for Children with Anxiety, Depression, Trauma, or Conduct
13 problems (MATCH-ADTC), including any of the following components:
14 a. Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy.
15 b. Parent and student coping skills.
16 c. Problem solving.
17 d. Safety planning.
18 (5) Any other training, including the training on the facilitation of peer-to-peer
19 mentoring, that is likely to increase school safety. Of the funds appropriated
20 by this act for the grants provided in this section, the Executive Director shall
21 use no more than three hundred fifty thousand dollars ($350,000) in each fiscal
22 year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium for the services identified in this
23 subdivision.
24 SECTION 20.7.(g) Grants for Safety Equipment. – Of the funds appropriated by this
25 act for the grants provided in this section, the Executive Director of the Center for Safer Schools
26 shall award grants to public school units for (i) the purchase of safety equipment for school
27 buildings and (ii) training associated with the use of safety equipment purchased pursuant to this
28 subsection. Notwithstanding G.S. 115C-218.105(b), charter schools may receive grants for
29 school safety equipment pursuant to this subsection.
30 SECTION 20.7.(h) Subsidizing School Resource Officer Grants Program. – If the
31 Executive Director of the Center for Safer Schools receives applications for grants for school
32 resource officers under G.S. 143B-1208.20 in excess of the amount of funding appropriated for
33 school resource officer grants in the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium, the Executive Director may use
34 the funds appropriated for the grants provided for in this section to cover the unmet need for
35 school resource officer grants.
36 SECTION 20.7.(i) Supplement Not Supplant. – Grants provided to public school
37 units pursuant to the Program shall be used to supplement and not to supplant State or non-State
38 funds already provided for these services.
39 SECTION 20.7.(j) Administrative Costs. – Of the funds appropriated to the Center
40 for Safer Schools by this act for the grants provided in this section, the Executive Director of the
41 Center for Safer Schools may retain a total of up to one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) in
42 each fiscal year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium for administrative costs associated with the
43 Program.
44 SECTION 20.7.(k) Disbursement. – The Executive Director of the Center for Safer
45 Schools may enter into a memorandum of understanding with the Department of Public
46 Instruction to disburse grants awarded under this section.
47 SECTION 20.7.(l) Program Report. – No later than April 1 of each fiscal year in
48 which funds are awarded pursuant to this section, the Executive Director of the Center for Safer
49 Schools shall report on the Program to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee, the
50 Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Health and Human Services, the Joint Legislative
51 Oversight Committee on Justice and Public Safety, the Joint Legislative Commission on
Page 312 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 Governmental Operations, the Senate Appropriations/Base Budget Committee, the House
2 Committee on Appropriations, and the Fiscal Research Division. The report shall include at least
3 the following information:
4 (1) The identity of each public school unit and community partner that received
5 grant funds through the Program.
6 (2) The amount of funding received by each entity identified pursuant to
7 subdivision (1) of this subsection.
8 (3) The services, training, and equipment purchased with grant funds by each
9 entity that received a grant.
10 (4) Recommendations for the implementation of additional effective school
11 safety measures.

13 PART XXI. STATE HIGH PATROL

15 STATE CAPITOL POLICE/CREATION OF RECEIPT-SUPPORTED POSITIONS
16 SECTION 21.1.(a) Creation of Receipt-Supported Positions Authorized. – The State
17 Capitol Police may contract with State agencies for the creation of receipt-supported positions to
18 provide security services to the buildings occupied by those agencies.
19 SECTION 21.1.(b) Annual Report Required. – No later than September 1 of each
20 fiscal year, the State Capitol Police shall report to the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on
21 Justice and Public Safety the following information for the fiscal year in which the report is due:
22 (1) A list of all positions in the State Capitol Police. For each position listed, the
23 report shall include at least the following information:
24 a. The position type.
25 b. The agency to which the position is assigned.
26 c. The source of funding for the position.
27 (2) For each receipt-supported position listed, the contract and any other terms of
28 the contract.
29 SECTION 21.1.(c) Additional Reporting Required Upon Creation of
30 Receipt-Supported Positions. – In addition to the report required by subsection (b) of this section,
31 the State Capitol Police shall report the creation of any position pursuant to subsection (a) of this
32 section to the chairs of the House of Representatives Appropriations Committee on Justice and
33 Public Safety and the Senate Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety and to the
34 Fiscal Research Division within 30 days of the position's creation. A report submitted pursuant
35 to this section shall include at least all of the following information:
36 (1) The position type.
37 (2) The agency to which the position is being assigned.
38 (3) The position salary.
39 (4) The total amount of the contract.
40 (5) The terms of the contract.
41 SECTION 21.1.(d) Format of Reports. – Reports submitted pursuant to this section
42 shall be submitted electronically and in accordance with any applicable General Assembly
43 standards.

45 USE OF SEIZED AND FORFEITED PROPERTY
46 SECTION 21.2.(a) Seized and forfeited assets transferred to the State Highway
47 Patrol during the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium pursuant to applicable federal law shall be credited
48 to the budget of the State Highway Patrol and shall result in an increase of law enforcement
49 resources for the State Highway Patrol. The State Highway Patrol shall make the following
50 reports to the chairs of the House of Representatives Appropriations Committee on Justice and
51 Public Safety and the Senate Appropriations Committee on Justice and Public Safety:
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 313

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (1) A report upon receipt of any assets.
2 (2) A report that shall be made prior to use of the assets on their intended use and
3 the departmental priorities on which the assets may be expended.
4 (3) A report on receipts, expenditures, encumbrances, and availability of these
5 assets for the previous fiscal year, which shall be made no later than
6 September 1 of each year.
7 SECTION 21.2.(b) The General Assembly finds that the use of seized and forfeited
8 assets transferred pursuant to federal law for new personnel positions, new projects, acquisition
9 of real property, repair of buildings where the repair includes structural change, and construction
10 of or additions to buildings may result in additional expenses for the State in future fiscal periods.
11 Therefore, the State Highway Patrol is prohibited from using these assets for such purposes
12 without the prior approval of the General Assembly.
13 SECTION 21.2.(c) Nothing in this section prohibits State law enforcement agencies
14 from receiving funds from the United States Department of Justice, the United States Department
15 of the Treasury, and the United States Department of Health and Human Services.
16
17 NO TRANSFER OF POSITIONS TO OTHER STATE AGENCIES
18 SECTION 21.3.(a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, and except as
19 otherwise provided in subsection (b) of this section, the Office of State Budget and Management
20 shall not transfer any positions, personnel, or funds from the State Highway Patrol to any other
21 State agency during the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium unless the transfer was included in the base
22 budget for one or both fiscal years of the biennium.
23 SECTION 21.3.(b) This section shall not apply to consolidation of information
24 technology positions into the Department of Information Technology pursuant to
25 G.S. 143B-1325.

6 "§ 143B-1760. North Carolina Center for Missing Persons established.
7 There is established within the Department of Public Safety State Highway Patrol the North
8 Carolina Center for Missing Persons, which shall be organized and staffed in accordance with
9 applicable laws. The purpose of the Center is to serve as a central repository for information

17 "§ 143B-1769. Provision of toll-free service; instructions to callers; communication with
18 law-enforcement agencies.
19 The Center shall provide a toll-free telephone line for anyone to report the disappearance of
20 any individual or the sighting of any missing child or missing person. The Center personnel shall
21 instruct the caller, in the case of a report concerning the disappearance of an individual, of the
22 requirements contained in G.S. 143B-1014 G.S. 143B-1764 of first having to submit a missing
23 person report on the individual to the law-enforcement agency having jurisdiction of the area in
24 which the individual became or is believed to have become missing. Any law-enforcement
25 agency may retrieve information imparted to the Center by means of this phone line. The Center
26 shall directly communicate any report of a sighting of a missing person or a missing child to the
27 law-enforcement agency having jurisdiction in the area of disappearance or sighting.
28 "§ 143B-1770. Improper release of information; penalty.
29 Any person working under the supervision of the Director of Victims and Justice Services
30 who knowingly and willfully releases, or authorizes the release of, any data, information, or
31 records maintained or possessed by the Center to any agency, entity, or person other than as
32 specifically permitted by Subpart B this Part or in violation of any rule adopted by the Secretary
33 Commander of the State Highway Patrol is guilty of a Class 2 misdemeanor.

10 (a) There is established within the North Carolina Center for Missing Persons the Missing

12 is to provide a statewide system for the rapid dissemination of information regarding a missing
13 person or missing child aged 65 or older who is believed to be suffering from dementia,
14 Alzheimer's disease, or a cognitive impairment that, in light of the person's or child's missing

17 makes them unable to meet their own needs or to seek help without assistance.

20 no more than 72 hours have passed since the person or child went missing, the Center or law
21 enforcement agency shall issue an alert providing for rapid dissemination of information
22 statewide regarding the missing person or missing child. person. The Center or law enforcement
23 agency shall make every effort to disseminate the information as quickly as possible when the
24 person's or child's status as missing has been reported to a law enforcement agency.agency,

28 subsection (a) of this section and shall provide education and training to encourage radio and
29 television broadcasters to participate in the alert. The guidelines and procedures shall ensure that
30 specific health information about the missing person or missing child is not made public through
31 the alert or otherwise.

33 Transportation and develop a procedure for the use of overhead permanent changeable message
34 signs to provide information on the missing person or missing child meeting the criteria of this
35 section when information is available that would enable motorists to assist in the recovery of the
36 missing person or missing child. person. The Center and the Department of Transportation shall
37 develop guidelines for the content, length, and frequency of any message to be placed on an
38 overhead permanent changeable message sign.

22 "§ 143B-1775. North Carolina Ashanti Alert established.
23 (a) There is established within the North Carolina Center for Missing Persons the Ashanti
24 Alert. The purpose of the Ashanti Alert is to provide a statewide system for the rapid
25 dissemination of information regarding a missing person over 18 years of age that is suspected
26 to have been abducted and there is both abductor and vehicle information available.
27 (b) If the Center receives a request that involves a missing person as described in
28 subsection (a) of this section, and at the time of receipt no more than 72 hours have passed since
29 the person went missing, the Center shall issue an alert providing for rapid dissemination of
30 information statewide regarding the missing person. The Center shall make every effort to
31 disseminate the information as quickly as possible when the person's status as missing has been
32 reported to a law enforcement agency, including procedures for the use of the Emergency Alert
33 System and the Wireless Emergency Alert.
34 (c) The Center shall adopt guidelines and develop procedures for issuing a 24-hour alert
35 for missing persons as described in subsection (a) of this section and shall provide education and
36 training to encourage radio and television broadcasters to participate in the alert.
37 (d) The Center shall consult with the Department of Transportation and develop a
38 procedure for the use of overhead permanent changeable message signs to provide information
39 on the missing person or missing child meeting the criteria of this section when information is
40 available that would enable motorists to assist in the recovery of the missing person or missing
41 child. The Center and the Department of Transportation shall develop guidelines for the content,
42 length, and frequency of any message to be placed on an overhead permanent changeable
43 message sign.

49 weather event.

15 "§ 143B-1726. Statewide radio system authorized; use of telephone lines in emergencies.
16 (a) The Commander of the State Highway Patrol is hereby authorized and directed to set
17 up and maintain a statewide radio system, with adequate broadcasting stations so situate as to
18 make the service available to all parts of the State for the purpose of maintaining radio contact
19 with the members of the State Highway Patrol and other officers of the State, to the end that the
20 traffic laws upon the highways may be more adequately enforced and that the criminal use of the
21 highways may be prevented. The Commander of the State Highway Patrol, Patrol is hereby
22 authorized to establish a plan of operation in accordance with Federal Communication
23 Commission rules so that all certified law-enforcement officers within the State may use the law
24 enforcement emergency frequency of 155.475MHz.
25 (b) The Commander of the State Highway Patrol is likewise authorized and empowered
26 to arrange with the various telephone companies of the State for the use of their lines for
27 emergency calls by the members of the State Highway Patrol, if it shall be found practicable to
28 arrange apparatus for temporary contact with said the telephone circuits along the highways of
29 the State.
30 (c) In order to make this service more generally useful, the various boards of county
31 commissioners and the governing boards of the various cities and towns are hereby authorized
32 and empowered to provide radio receiving sets in the offices and vehicles of their various officers,
33 and such these expenditures are declared to be a legal expenditure of any funds that may be
34 available for police protection.
35 (d) Each user of the statewide radio system created pursuant to this section shall be
36 charged an annual user fee of twenty-five dollars ($25.00), except that the statewide radio
37 manager of each county may exempt up to 100 users each year from paying this fee. The fee shall
38 be paid to the State Highway Patrol and used by the State Highway Patrol for costs associated
39 with maintaining and operating the statewide radio system created pursuant to this section."
40 SECTION 21.5.(b) This section becomes effective January 1, 2026, and applies to
41 users of the statewide radio system on or after that date.

25 "§ 143-128. Requirements for certain building contracts.
26 …
27 (b) Separate-prime contracts. – When the State, county, municipality, or other public
28 body uses the separate-prime contract system, it shall accept bids for each subdivision of work
29 for which specifications are required to be prepared under subsection (a) of this section and shall
30 award the respective work specified separately to responsible and reliable persons, firms or
31 corporations regularly engaged in their respective lines of work. When the estimated cost of work
32 to be performed in any single subdivision or branch for which separate bids are required by this
33 subsection is less than twenty-five thousand dollars ($25,000), the same may be included in the
34 contract for one of the other subdivisions or branches of the work, irrespective of total project
35 cost. The contracts shall be awarded to the lowest responsible, responsive bidders, taking into
36 consideration quality, performance, and the time specified in the bids for performance of the
37 contract, and compliance with G.S. 143-128.2. contract. Bids may also be accepted from and
38 awards made to separate contractors for other categories of work.
39 …
40 (d) Single-prime contracts. – All bidders in a single-prime project shall identify on their
41 bid the contractors they have selected for the subdivisions or branches of work for:
42 (1) Heating, ventilating, and air conditioning;
43 (2) Plumbing;
44 (3) Electrical; and
45 (4) General.
46 The contract shall be awarded to the lowest responsible, responsive bidder, taking into
47 consideration quality, performance, and the time specified in the bids for performance of the
48 contract, and compliance with G.S. 143-128.2. contract. A contractor whose bid is accepted shall
49 not substitute any person as subcontractor in the place of the subcontractor listed in the original
50 bid, except (i) if the listed subcontractor's bid is later determined by the contractor to be
51 nonresponsible or nonresponsive or the listed subcontractor refuses to enter into a contract for
Page 322 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 the complete performance of the bid work, or (ii) with the approval of the awarding authority for
2 good cause shown by the contractor. The terms, conditions, and requirements of each contract
3 between the contractor and a subcontractor performing work under a subdivision or branch of
4 work listed in this subsection shall incorporate by reference the terms, conditions, and
5 requirements of the contract between the contractor and the State, county, municipality, or other
6 public body.
7 When contracts are awarded pursuant to this section, the public body shall make available to
8 subcontractors the dispute resolution process as provided for in subsection (f1) of this section.
9 (d1) Dual bidding. – The State, a county, municipality, or other public entity may accept
10 bids to erect, construct, alter, or repair a building under both the single-prime and separate-prime
11 contracting systems and shall award the contract to the lowest responsible, responsive bidder
12 under the single-prime system or to the lowest responsible, responsive bidder under the
13 separate-prime system, taking into consideration quality, performance, compliance with
14 G.S. 143-128.2, and time specified in the bids to perform the contract. In determining the system
15 under which the contract will be awarded to the lowest responsible, responsive bidder, the public
16 entity may consider cost of construction oversight, time for completion, and other factors it
17 considers appropriate. The bids received as separate-prime bids shall be received, but not opened,
18 one hour prior to the deadline for the submission of single-prime bids. The amount of a bid
19 submitted by a subcontractor to the general contractor under the single-prime system shall not
20 exceed the amount bid, if any, for the same work by that subcontractor to the public entity under
21 the separate-prime system. The provisions of subsection (b) of this section shall apply to
22 separate-prime contracts awarded pursuant to this section and the provisions of subsection (d) of
23 this section shall apply to single-prime contracts awarded pursuant to this section.

26 "§ 143-135.5. State policy; cooperation in promoting the use of small, minority, physically
27 handicapped and women contractors; purpose.construction contracts.
28 (a) It is the policy of this State to encourage and promote the use of small, minority,
29 physically handicapped and women contractors in State construction projects. All State agencies,
30 institutions and political subdivisions shall cooperate with the Department of Administration and
31 all other State agencies, institutions and political subdivisions in efforts to encourage and
32 promote the use of small, minority, physically handicapped and women contractors in achieving
33 the purpose of this Article, which is the effective and economical construction of public
34 buildings.

36 business with, any business that, within the last two years, has been finally found by a court or
37 an administrative agency of competent jurisdiction to have unlawfully discriminated on the basis
38 of race, gender, religion, national origin, age, physical disability, or any other unlawful basis in
39 its solicitation, selection, hiring, or treatment of another business."
