32 PART XXIV. OFFICE OF STATE AUDITOR
33
34 FLOODPLAIN BUYOUT PUBLIC/PRIVATE PARTNERSHIP PILOT PROGRAM
35 SECTION 24.1.(a) Pilot Program. – Of the funds appropriated to the Office of the
36 State Auditor, the nonrecurring sum of fifteen million dollars ($15,000,000) in the 2025-2026
37 fiscal year and twenty million dollars ($20,000,000) in the 2026-2027 fiscal year shall be used
38 for the Floodplain Buyout Public/Private Partnership Pilot Program (Program) in accordance
39 with this section. The purpose of the Program is to utilize innovative valuation methods and
40 streamlined administrative processes to modernize the State's floodplain buyout strategies,
41 reduce future flood damage, and enhance floodplain functionality. The Office of the State
42 Auditor shall allocate one million dollars ($1,000,000) of those funds from the 2025-2026 fiscal
43 year to the Board of Governors of The University of North Carolina for the North Carolina
44 Collaboratory (Collaboratory) for the purposes set forth in subsection (d) of this section. The
45 Office of the State Auditor and the Program Director shall seek to commence the operations of
46 the Program within six months of this section becoming law.
47 SECTION 24.1.(b) Program Director. – The State Auditor shall designate a State
48 employee within the Office of the State Auditor to serve as the Program Director and oversee the
49 Program. The Program Director shall do all of the following:
50 (1) Negotiate and execute a performance-based contract with a private partner in
51 accordance with this section.
Page 326 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) Ensure the release of funds for the Program align with established and defined
2 milestones set forth in the contract with the private partner.
3 (3) Coordinate with other State agencies, as necessary, to access data, planning
4 resources, and other support, including the Department of Public Safety.
5 (4) Ensure consistency with the Flood Resiliency Blueprint being developed by
6 the Department of Environmental Quality in planning and conducting the
7 Program, except where the General Assembly has directed otherwise.
8 (5) Monitor and evaluate the Program's effectiveness and compliance with this
9 section.
10 SECTION 24.1.(c) Private Partner. – The private partner contracted with by the
11 Office of the State Auditor for the participation in the Program shall do all of the following:
12 (1) Conduct buyout operations, including negotiations and contracting with
13 property owners for buyouts, real estate closings that transfer title to local land
14 trusts, demolition of existing structures as appropriate, and ecological
15 restoration.
16 (2) Meet and satisfy any predefined contractual milestones set forth in the
17 contract.
18 (3) Consider local zoning ordinances and development plans where buyouts are
19 proposed.
20 SECTION 24.1.(d) Collaboratory/Research and Valuation Development. – The
21 Collaboratory shall do all of the following:
22 (1) Establish buyout zone values based on projections of future damages and
23 enhanced floodplain functionality.
24 (2) Utilize appropriate investment assessment methods to define the total value of
25 the contract entered into under this section.
26 (3) Determine the efficacy of other State efforts to reduce future flood damage
27 with an emphasis on cost-benefit outcomes.
28 SECTION 24.1.(e) Evaluation. – The Program Director and the Office of the State
29 Auditor shall evaluate the effectiveness of the Program based on benchmarks and outcomes based
30 on the Collaboratory's valuation research as set forth in subsection (d) of this section and
31 compared to existing and historic State-run buyout programs.
32 SECTION 24.1.(f) The Office of the State Auditor shall submit a report to the Joint
33 Legislative Oversight Committee on General Government, the Joint Legislative Commission on
34 Governmental Operations, and the Fiscal Research Division on the Program implementation and
35 the Program's progress on or before October 1, 2025. Within one year of the Program's
36 completion, the Office of the State Auditor and the Program Director shall submit a report to the
37 same committees and the Fiscal Research Division on findings, recommendations, and any
38 proposed legislative recommendations or adjustments based on the Program's results, including
39 all projects completed, average cost of buyout for properties, types of properties, locations, and
40 any other metrics and outcomes the Program Director deems appropriate.
41
42 THE DAVE ACT
43 SECTION 24.2.(a) This section shall be known as "The Division of Accountability,
44 Value, and Efficiency (DAVE) Act."
45 SECTION 24.2.(b) The State Auditor shall establish a Division of Accountability,
46 Value, and Efficiency (Division) within the Department of the State Auditor. The powers and
47 duties of the Division shall be to effectuate the assessment and recommendations required by this
48 section, and the State Auditor shall organize and administer the Division in such a manner as the
49 State Auditor may deem necessary to conduct the Division's work accordingly.
50 SECTION 24.2.(c) No later than October 1, 2025, every State agency shall report to
51 the Division both of the following:
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 327

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (1) An explanation of how the agency, including each division or office within
2 that agency, utilizes public monies to execute its powers and duties under law.
3 (2) A description of all positions within that agency that have remained vacant for
4 six months or more as of the effective date of this section. The description
5 shall include the original position vacancy dates, the dates of any postings or
6 repostings of the positions, and an explanation for the length of the vacancies.
7 SECTION 24.2.(d) The Division shall assess the continued need for each State
8 agency and the vacant positions within each agency. The assessment shall be based on a review
9 of the reports submitted pursuant to this section and any other information the Division deems
10 relevant. In gathering and assessing relevant information, the Division may consult with the Joint
11 Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations. The Division may also employ
12 individuals to utilize artificial intelligence and other appropriate tools for the purpose of
13 examining any of the following with respect to State agencies and their budgets:
14 (1) Amounts spent, including the entities receiving funds and the intended
15 purpose of the amounts spent.
16 (2) The effectiveness of any amount spent in achieving the intended purpose of
17 that spending.
18 (3) Duplicative spending.
19 (4) Any other factor demonstrating the fiscal soundness or effectiveness of the
20 State agency or lack thereof.
21 SECTION 24.2.(e) No later than December 31, 2025, the Division shall report the
22 results of the assessment conducted pursuant to this section to the General Assembly. The
23 Division's report shall include, at minimum, recommendations on both of the following:
24 (1) Any State agencies, or any divisions or offices within a State agency, that
25 should be dissolved based on the Division's assessment.
26 (2) Any State agency positions that should be eliminated based on the Division's
27 assessment.
28 SECTION 24.2.(f) The Division, in its discretion, may annually require any or all
29 State agencies to submit a report with the information required by this section, and may reassess
30 such information on an annual basis in accordance with this section, and report the results of the
31 assessment to the General Assembly.
32 SECTION 24.2.(g) This section is effective when it becomes law and expires
33 December 31, 2028, at which time the Division shall terminate.
34
35 GDAC AVAILABILITY FOR AUDITOR
36 SECTION 24.3.(a) Of the funds appropriated to the Office of the State Auditor in
37 this act, the sum of up to seven hundred fifty thousand dollars ($750,000) in nonrecurring funds
38 for the 2025-2026 fiscal year shall be used to collaborate with the Government Data Analytics
39 Center (GDAC) to implement analytical capabilities to ensure efficiency in State government
40 agencies and assist the State Auditor in meeting its statutory responsibilities under G.S. 147-64.6.
41 These funds shall be used to fund detection analytics, software, information reporting, managed
42 services, and technical infrastructure. The Office of the State Auditor shall coordinate with the
43 GDAC and utilize the subject matter expertise and technical infrastructure available through
44 existing GDAC public-private partnerships for fraud detection and analytics infrastructure.
45 SECTION 24.3.(b) GDAC shall, at the direction of the State Auditor, expedite data
46 sharing agreements with State agencies in accordance with G.S. 143B-1385(c) to assist the
47 Division of Accountability, Value, and Efficiency, established in this act, in completing its
48 assessments and reports of State agencies as required by law.

50 STATE AUDITOR/ASSESSMENT OF IT SECURITY STANDARDS &

Page 328 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 24.4.(a) Article 5A of Chapter 147 of the General Statutes is amended by
2 adding a new section to read:
3 "§ 147-64.6E. Security practices of State information technology systems.
4 (a) After assessing the security practices of a State agency's information technology
5 system, the Office of the State Auditor may include in its report to the agency a time frame, not
6 to exceed 90 days, in which the agency shall implement the Auditor's recommendations, if any
7 are made. Within 14 days after the expiration of the time frame specified in the report, the agency
8 shall, in writing, report to the State Auditor the actions it has taken to implement the
9 recommendations. The Auditor may, in his or her discretion, extend the time frame designated
10 in the report.
11 (b) If the State Auditor determines that a State agency has not, to his or her satisfaction,
12 implemented the recommendations included in the report, the State Auditor shall, in writing,
13 notify the State Chief Information Officer (hereinafter "State CIO") and the agency of the same.
14 Upon receiving the notification, the State CIO and the Department of Information Technology
15 (hereinafter "DIT") shall withhold all approvals required by Article 15 of Chapter 143B of the
16 General Statutes for any projects, contracts, budgets, or procurements for the agency until the
17 State Auditor determines all recommendations have been satisfactorily implemented, unless
18 approval from the State CIO or the DIT is required to implement the State Auditor's
19 recommendations.
20 (c) In a case where an agency's approvals are withheld as provided in subsection (b) of
21 this section, the agency shall, in writing, notify the State Auditor when it has taken the necessary
22 actions to implement the State Auditor's recommendations. Upon finding that the agency has
23 satisfactorily implemented the recommendations, the State Auditor shall, in writing, notify the
24 State CIO, and the State CIO and the DIT shall cease withholding approvals.
25 (d) Any action by the State CIO or the DIT to withhold approvals pursuant to subsection
26 (b) of this section shall not be subject to the provisions of G.S. 143B-1342."
27 SECTION 24.4.(b) G.S. 143B-1377 reads as rewritten:
28 "§ 143B-1377. State CIO approval of security standards and risk assessments.
29 …
30 (e) Nothing in this section shall be construed to preclude the Office of the State Auditor
31 from assessing the security practices of State information technology systems as part of its
32 statutory duties and responsibilities.responsibilities, including the provisions of G.S. 147-64.6E."

42 (1) S.L. 2021-180.

Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 329

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 25.1.(b) The funds set out in subsection (a) of this section may be
2 reallocated only when all of the following conditions are met:
3 (1) The appropriated funds have not been expended by December 31, 2025.
4 (2) There is a reasonable expectation that the funds will not be expended before
5 the deadline established by applicable federal law or guidance.
6 (3) The reallocation is made to support one or more SFRF related activities
7 authorized and receiving appropriations under this act or one of the acts listed
8 above in subsection (a) of this section. Reallocated funds shall not be used for
9 any new activity, purpose, or program.
10 (4) The funds were not appropriated for a broadband project or activity.
11 SECTION 25.1.(c) To the extent the Office of State Budget and Management is
12 aware of any unappropriated SFRF funds, including interest earned, that remain unexpended and
13 may be reallocated to another eligible project, the OSBM shall report that information to the
14 Fiscal Research Division not later than January 15, 2026.
15 SECTION 25.1.(d) At least 30 days prior to executing the reallocation of funds as
16 proposed by NCPRO under subsection (a) of this section, the Office of State Budget and
17 Management shall report to the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations and
18 the Fiscal Research Division on the proposed plan to reallocate the funds, including the amounts
19 to be reallocated and the projects to which the funds will be reallocated. The OSBM shall submit
20 a monthly report to the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations and the Fiscal
21 Research Division on all reallocated SFRF expenditures.
22 SECTION 25.1.(e) Any funds remaining after the reallocation of funds authorized
23 in subsection (a) and subsection (b) of this section shall be allocated to the State Treasurer up to
24 an amount equal to the remaining unreimbursed COVID-19 related expenses incurred by the
25 North Carolina State Health Plan for Teachers and State Employees between March 3, 2021, and
26 December 31, 2024.
27 SECTION 25.1.(f) If the deadline for the expenditure of SFRF funds is extended to
28 June 30, 2027, or later, by the federal government, the provisions of this section shall be void
29 and have no effect.

21 STATE AGENCIES/ELECTRONIC PAYMENTS
22 SECTION 27.2.(a) G.S. 66-58.12 reads as rewritten:
23 "§ 66-58.12. Agencies may provide access to services through electronic and digital
24 transactions; fees authorized.
25 (a) Public agencies are encouraged to maximize citizen and business access to their
26 services through the use of electronic and digital transactions. A public agency may determine,
27 through program and transaction analysis, which of its services may be made available to the
28 public through electronic means, including the Internet. The agency shall identify any inhibitors
29 to electronic transactions between the agency and the public, including legal, policy, financial,
30 or privacy concerns and specific inhibitors unique to the agency or type of transaction. An agency
31 shall not provide a transaction through the Internet that is impractical, unreasonable, or not
32 permitted by laws pertaining to privacy or security.
33 (b) An agency may charge a fee to cover its costs of permitting a person to complete a
34 transaction through the World Wide Web internet or other means of electronic access. The
35 transaction fee may be applied on a per transaction basis and may be calculated either as a flat
36 fee or a percentage fee, as determined under an agreement between a person and a public agency.
37 The fee may be collected by the agency or by its third party agent.fee. Neither the flat fee nor the
38 percentage fee shall exceed two percent (2%) of the total amount of each transaction. An agency
39 shall not charge a surcharge for accepting electronic payments. For purposes of this subsection,
40 the following definitions apply:
41 (1) Surcharge. – A fee added to a payment by charge card, credit card, debit card,
42 or by electronic funds transfer for the convenience of making the electronic
43 payment or for any other purpose not authorized by law.
44 (2) Transaction fee. – A fee charged by a payment processor to a State agency for
45 processing a charge card, credit card, or debit card payment.
46 (c) The fee flat fee or percentage fee imposed under subsection (b) of this section must
47 be approved by the State Chief Information Officer. The revenue derived from the fee must be
48 credited to a nonreverting agency reserve account. The funds in the account may be expended
49 only for e-commerce initiatives and projects approved by the State Chief Information Officer.
50 For purposes of this subsection, the term "public agencies" does not include a county, unit, special
51 district, or other political subdivision of government. The State Chief Information Officer shall
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 331

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 report any fees imposed under subsection (b) of this section and expenditures for e-commerce
2 initiatives and projects to the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations and the
3 Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on Information Technology.
4 (d) This section does not apply to the Judicial Department."

10 It is the policy of the State of North Carolina that all agencies, institutions, departments,

12 Budget Act, Chapter 143C of the General Statutes, shall devise techniques and procedures for

33 "§ 147-86.22. Statewide accounts receivable program.
34 (a) Program. – The State Controller shall implement a statewide accounts receivable
35 program. As part of this program, the State Controller shall do all of the following:
36 (1) Monitor the State's accounts receivable collection efforts.
37 (2) Coordinate information, systems, and procedures between State agencies to
38 maximize the collection of past-due accounts receivable.
39 (3) Adopt policies and procedures for the management and collection of accounts
40 receivable by State agencies.
41 (3a) In consultation and coordination with the Department of Administration and
42 the State Chief Information Officer, enter into a statewide term contract for
43 electronic payment processing services.
44 (4) Establish procedures for writing off accounts receivable.
45 (b) Electronic Payment. – Notwithstanding the provisions of G.S. 147-86.20 and
46 G.S. 147-86.21, this subsection applies to debts owed a community college, a local school
47 administrative unit, an area mental health, developmental disabilities, and substance abuse
48 authority, and the Administrative Office of the Courts, and to debts payable to or through the

Page 332 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (b1) Policies Established. – The State Controller shall establish policies that allow
2 accounts receivable to be payable under certain conditions by electronic payment. The policies
3 shall provide that transaction fees for electronic payments may be imposed as provided in
4 G.S. 66-58.12, unless otherwise provided for by law. These policies shall be established with the
5 concurrence of the State Treasurer. In addition, any policies that apply to debts payable to or
6 through the office of a clerk of superior court or a magistrate shall be established with the
7 concurrence of the Administrative Officer of the Courts. The Administrative Officer of the Courts
8 may also establish policies otherwise authorized by law that apply to these debts as long as those
9 policies are not inconsistent with the Controller's policies. State agencies shall use the vendor or
10 vendors under the statewide term contract for electronic payments allowed under the policies
11 established under this subsection, unless explicitly exempted by the State Controller, in
12 concurrence with the State Treasurer or the Administrative Officer of the Courts, as applicable.
13 A condition of payment by electronic payment is receipt by the appropriate State agency of
14 the full amount of the account receivable owed to the State agency. A debtor who pays by
15 electronic payment may be required to pay any fee or charge associated with the use of electronic
16 payment.
17 (b2) Payment Processor Fees. – The policies established by the State Controller under
18 subsection (b1) of this section and the terms of the statewide term contract executed pursuant to
19 subdivision (3a) of subsection (a) of this section may authorize a vendor providing payment
20 processing services to retain their transaction fee at the time each transaction is made instead of
21 submitting the full amount of the account receivable owed to the State agency; provided,
22 however, the transaction fee shall not exceed two percent (2%) of the total amount of each
23 transaction. The State Controller may also establish policies and authorize contracts that provide
24 a State agency may require a vendor to pay the full amount of the account receivable owed to the
25 State agency, on a schedule agreed to by the agency and vendor, and thereafter the vendor shall
26 be reimbursed for the transaction fees owed to it by the State. In the case of reimbursement, the
27 transaction fee owed to the vendor shall not exceed two percent (2%) of the full amount of the
28 account receivable owed to the State agency. Fees associated with processing electronic
29 payments may be paid out of the General Fund and Highway Fund if the payment of the fee by
30 the State is economically beneficial to the State and the payment of the fee by the State has been
31 approved by the State Controller and State Treasurer.
32 (b3) Consult General Assembly. – The State Controller and State Treasurer shall consult
33 with the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations before establishing policies
34 that allow accounts receivable to be payable by electronic payment and before authorizing fees
35 associated with electronic payment to be paid out of the General Fund and Highway Fund.
36 (b4) Payments Not Honored. – A payment of an account receivable that is made by
37 electronic payment and is not honored by the issuer of the card or the financial institution offering
38 electronic funds transfer does not relieve the debtor of the obligation to pay the account
39 receivable.
40 (c) Collection Techniques. – The State Controller, in conjunction with the Office of the
41 Attorney General, shall establish policies and procedures to govern techniques for collection of
42 accounts receivable. These techniques may include use of credit reporting bureaus, judicial
43 remedies authorized by law, and administrative setoff by a reduction of a tax refund pursuant to
44 the Setoff Debt Collection Act, Chapter 105A of the General Statutes, or a reduction of another
45 payment, other than payroll, due from the State to a person to reduce or eliminate an account
46 receivable that the person owes the State.
47 The State Controller shall negotiate a contract with a third party to perform an audit and
48 collection process of inadvertent overpayments by State agencies to vendors as a result of pricing
49 errors, neglected rebates and discounts, miscalculated freight charges, unclaimed refunds,
50 erroneously paid excise taxes, and related errors. The third party shall be compensated only from
51 funds recovered as a result of the audit. Savings realized in excess of costs shall be transferred
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 333

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 from the agency to the Office of State Budget and Management and placed in a special reserve
2 account for future direction by the General Assembly. Any disputed savings shall be settled by
3 the State Controller. This paragraph does not apply to the purchase of medical services by State
4 agencies or payments used to reimburse or otherwise pay for health care services.
5 (d) Annual Report. – The State Controller shall report annually to the Joint Legislative
6 Commission on Governmental Operations and the Fiscal Research Division on the revenue
7 deposited into Special Reserve Account 24172 and the disbursement of that revenue."
8 SECTION 27.2.(c) The State Controller, in concurrence with the State Treasurer and
9 the Administrative Officer of the Courts, and any State agency subject to a contract with a vendor
10 to provide electronic payment processing services shall make every effort allowed by law to
11 amend the terms of those contracts to include a provision that the transaction fee paid to the
12 vendor shall not exceed two percent (2%) of the total amount of each transaction or two percent
13 (2%) of the full amount of the account receivable owed to the State. Upon the expiration,
14 amendment, or renewal of the contract for electronic payment processing services, the State
15 Controller, in concurrence with the State Treasurer and the Administrative Officer of the Courts,
16 and any State agency shall include, as part of the new, amended, or renewed contract, a provision
17 which explicitly states that the amount of the transaction fee to be paid by the State or the State
18 agency shall not exceed two percent (2%) of the total amount of each transaction or two percent
19 (2%) of the full amount of the account receivable owed to the State.

14 MAKE APPLYING FOR STATE JOBS EASIER
15 SECTION 31A.1.(a) Article 5 of Chapter 126 of the General Statutes is amended by
16 adding a new section to read:
17 "§ 126-14.3A. Increasing efficiency of State job application process.
18 (a) The Office of State Human Resources (OSHR) shall streamline the job application
19 process for State positions by enabling applicants to upload resumes or website profiles. An
20 electronic tool shall be utilized to import information from these documents into the State job
21 application format, making the process more efficient while still collecting information necessary
22 for merit-based hiring under G.S. 126-14.2 and G.S. 126-14.3. Applicants remain responsible for
23 ensuring all information required for initial screening appears correctly in their completed State
24 job application after importing their resume or profile.
25 (b) For job applications requiring references, supplemental questions, or other
26 information not typically found on resumes and not needed for initial screening, State agencies
27 may collect this information later in the selection process, such as during job interviews.
28 (c) Beginning in 2026 and then annually thereafter, the OSHR shall present the State
29 application form and demonstrate the import process to the State Human Resources Commission
30 to receive the Commission's informal feedback."
31 SECTION 31A.1.(b) G.S. 126-5(c7) reads as rewritten:

5 PERMANENT HIRING OF CERTAIN EMPLOYEES/SPECIFIC CONDITIONS
6 SECTION 31A.2.(a) Article 1 of Chapter 126 of the General Statutes is amended by
7 adding a new section to read:
8 "§ 126-6.4. Temp-to-perm hiring.
9 (a) The Council of State, the executive branch agencies, the Community College System
10 Office, and The University of North Carolina may directly hire temporary employees into vacant
11 positions if all of the following conditions are met:
12 (1) The permanent position to be filled must be vacant.
13 (2) The temporary employee must have worked for a minimum of six months in
14 a substantially equivalent role with satisfactory performance. This six-month
15 period excludes any mandatory breaks required under G.S. 126-6.3.
16 (3) The temporary employee must meet the minimum education and experience
17 requirements established for the position classification and their salary must
18 be set within the approved classification range.
19 (4) The temporary employee must have been originally hired through the North
20 Carolina Office of State Human Resources Temporary Solutions Program.
21 The Director of the Office of State Human Resources may waive the requirements specified in
22 subdivision (3) of this subsection, including both the minimum education and experience
23 requirements and the requirement that salary be set within the classification range.
24 (b) Unless otherwise provided, a hiring under this section is exempt from the provisions
25 of this Chapter, including any procedural or substantive requirements, including publicly posting
26 the position, requiring an application, holding an interview or new reference checks, selecting
27 the applicants from the pool of the most qualified persons, or following the priorities for certain
28 types of applicants under State law. This exemption for the hiring process does not affect whether
29 the position is subject to Chapter 126 of the General Statutes once the employee is hired.
30 (c) A hiring under this section is not exempt from G.S. 126-14, 126-14.1, or 126-14.5 or
31 from Article 6 or 7 of Chapter 126 of the General Statutes."
32 SECTION 31A.2.(b) This section is effective when it becomes law.
33
34 GRANT EMPLOYING AGENCY FLEXIBILITY/HIRING/PAY/CLASSIFICATION
35 SECTION 31A.3. Effective July 1, 2025, Article 1 of Chapter 126 of the General
36 Statutes is amended by adding a new section to read:
37 "§ 126-3.1. Employing agency flexibility.
38 (a) For the purposes of this section, an "employing agency" means the Council of State,
39 State agencies in the executive branch of government, the Community College System Office,
40 and The University of North Carolina.
41 (b) An employing agency is granted flexibility, notwithstanding other provisions of this
42 Chapter, to do the following:
43 (1) Offer qualified applicants for employment the option to have their
44 applications considered for future positions at the same agency and at other
45 agencies within the same or comparable classification.
46 (2) Permit agencies to recruit and hire applicants from job postings that apply to
47 all vacancies in a particular classification across all State agencies.
48 (3) Classify or reclassify positions according to the State Human Resources
49 Commission (SHRC) classification system, provided employees meet the
50 minimum requirements for the classification.
Page 338 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (4) Establish employee salaries within SHRC-determined salary ranges for
2 respective position classifications.
3 (c) Nothing in this section diminishes the powers of the State Human Resources
4 Commission or the Director of the Office of State Human Resources under any other provision
5 of this Chapter or relating to corrective actions taken when an employing agency fails to comply
6 with this section."
7
8 MODERNIZE AND SIMPLIFY THE STATE HUMAN RESOURCES ACT
9 SECTION 31A.4. By March 15, 2026, the Office of State Human Resources
10 (OSHR) shall submit a report to the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, the Speaker of the
11 House of Representatives, and the Fiscal Research Division with recommended changes to
12 Chapter 126 of the General Statutes to modernize the North Carolina Human Resources Act,
13 simplify its provisions, and align it with contemporary human resources best practices. At a
14 minimum, the report shall address legislative changes needed to:
15 (1) Streamline hiring processes.
16 (2) Enhance recruitment strategies and increase the attractiveness of State
17 government employment.
18 (3) Develop and maintain a high-quality, well-trained State workforce.
19 (4) Improve State employee retention rates.
20 The OSHR shall collaborate with State agencies, local governments, and other
21 relevant stakeholders to develop these legislative proposals.

26 "Fund"), a special fund within the Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. The Fund shall
27 be maintained as a special fund and shall be administered by the Department to carry out the
28 operations and maintenance of the State's veterans' cemeteries. Interest accruing from the monies
29 in the Fund shall be credited to the Fund. The Fund shall consist of the following sources of

38 Division on all of the following:

40 known issues that require maintenance and/or repair in the upcoming State
41 fiscal year.
42 (2) The total funds spent at each of the State's veterans' cemeteries for
43 maintenance and/or repair and any other expenses in the prior State fiscal year
44 and the source of the funds.
45 (3) The number of full- and part-time employees assigned to work at each of the
46 State's veterans' cemeteries in the prior State fiscal year.
47 (4) The number of veterans and the legal spouses and eligible dependents of
48 veterans who were interred at each of the State's veterans' cemeteries in the
49 prior State fiscal year, and the type of interment for each veteran, legal spouse,
50 and eligible dependent."

8 the grant contract requirements. In such a case, the OSBM is authorized to disburse grant funds

10 ($750,000) in a fiscal year because the amount disbursed is for both the prior fiscal year and the
11 current fiscal year.

24 "Part 10. State Veterans Home.
25 …
26 "§ 143B-1291. Establishment.Establishment of State veterans homes; closing homes.
27 (a) Establishment. – The State of North Carolina shall construct, maintain, and operate
28 veterans homes for the aged and infirm veterans resident in this State under the administrative
29 authority and control of the Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. There is vested in the
30 Department any and all the powers and authority that may be necessary to enable it to establish
31 and operate the homes and to homes; provided, however, funds to construct, maintain, and
32 operate the homes shall be pursuant to appropriation by the General Assembly except as provided
33 in G.S. 143B-1294(c). The Department shall issue rules necessary to operate the homes in
34 compliance with applicable State and federal statutes and regulations. The Department may enter
35 into contracts to construct and maintain veterans homes in accordance with the provisions of
36 Articles 3, 3C, 3D, and 8 of Chapter 143 of the General Statutes and procedures established by
37 the Division of Purchase and Contract and the Office of State Construction. The Department may
38 enter into contracts to operate veterans homes as provided in G.S. 143B-1295.
39 (b) Report Condition Assessment Results. – If the Department determines, based upon
40 an assessment conducted by the Office of State Construction, the Department, or an entity with
41 whom the Department has contracted to conduct the assessment, that a State veterans home
42 requires repair in order to maintain the home in a safe and habitable condition, the Department
43 shall, not later than 24 hours after receiving the assessment report, submit a report of the
44 assessment findings to the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee on General Government, the
45 House Appropriations Committee on General Government, the Senate Appropriations
46 Committee on General Government and Information Technology, and the Fiscal Research
47 Division. The report shall, at a minimum, include the name of the State agency or other entity
48 that conducted the assessment, the reason for the assessment, the dangerous conditions found,
49 the Department's recommendations for remedying the dangerous conditions, and the estimated
50 costs of remedying the dangerous conditions.

1 "§ 143B-1293. North Carolina Veterans Home Trust Fund.

12 "§ 143B-1294. Funding.
13 …
14 (c) All funds received by the Department shall be deposited in the North Carolina
15 Veterans Home Trust Fund, except for any funds deposited into special agency accounts
16 established pursuant to G.S. 143B-1293(d)(3). The Veterans' Affairs Commission shall authorize
17 the expenditure of all funds from the North Carolina Veterans Home Trust Fund. The Veterans'
18 Affairs Commission may delegate authority to the Assistant Secretary of Veterans Affairs for the
19 expenditure of funds from the North Carolina Veterans Home Trust Fund for operations of the
20 State Veterans Nursing Homes. The delegation of authority shall apply only to the person holding
21 the office of Secretary of the Department at the time the vote is undertaken, and a new vote to
22 delegate authority must be undertaken by the Commission each time a person is appointed to
23 serve as Secretary or designated to serve as chair of the Commission under G.S. 143B-1221.
24 "§ 143B-1295. Contracted operation of homes.
25 The Department of Military and Veterans Affairs, in consultation with the Veterans' Affairs
26 Commission Commission, may contract with persons or other nongovernmental entities to
27 operate each State veterans home. Contracts for the procurement of services to manage,
28 administer, and operate any State veterans home shall be awarded on a competitive basis through
29 the solicitation of proposals and through the procedures established by statute and the Division
30 of Purchase and Contract. A contract may be awarded to the vendor whose proposal is most
31 advantageous to the State, taking into consideration cost, program suitability, management plan,
32 excellence of program design, key personnel, corporate or company resources, financial
33 condition of the vendor, experience and past performance, and any other qualities deemed
34 necessary by the Veterans' Affairs Commission Department and set out in the solicitation for
35 proposals. Any contract awarded under this section shall not exceed five years in length. The
36 Veterans' Affairs Commission Department is not required to select or recommend the vendor
37 offering the lowest cost proposal but shall select or recommend the vendor who, in the opinion
38 of the Commission, Department, offers the proposal most advantageous to the veterans and the
39 State of North Carolina.
40 "§ 143B-1296. Program staff.
41 The Department shall appoint and fix the salary of an Administrative Officer a Program
42 Director for the State veterans home program. The Administrative Officer shall be an honorably
43 discharged veteran who has served in active military service in the Armed Forces of the United
44 States for other than training purposes. Program Director does not have to be a veteran, but
45 preference shall be given to veteran applicants in accordance with G.S. 128-15. The
46 Administrative Officer Program Director shall direct the establishment of the State veterans
47 home program, coordinate the master planning, land acquisition, and construction of all State
48 veterans homes under the procedures of established by the Office of State Construction, and
49 oversee the ongoing operation of said the veterans homes. The Division Department may hire
50 any required additional administrative staff to help assist with administrative and operational
51 responsibilities at each established State veterans home.

11 TAX FRAUD ANALYTICS
12 SECTION 36.5. Of the funds appropriated in this act to the Department of Revenue,
13 the sum of four million four hundred thousand dollars ($4,400,000) in recurring funds for each
14 fiscal year of the 2025-2027 fiscal biennium shall be used to continue and expand the
15 Department's tax fraud analysis contract through the Government Data Analytics Center
16 (GDAC). These funds shall be used in each fiscal year to fund detection analytics, software,
17 information reporting, collections case management, collections optimization, managed services,
18 and technical infrastructure. The Department of Revenue shall continue to coordinate with the
19 GDAC and utilize the subject matter expertise and technical infrastructure available through
20 existing GDAC public-private partnerships for fraud detection and analytics infrastructure.

11 NORTH CAROLINA SMALL BUSINESS ENTERPRISE PROGRAM
12 SECTION 37.3.(a) G.S. 147-36 reads as rewritten:
13 "§ 147-36. Duties of Secretary of State.
14 It is the duty of the Secretary of State:
15 …
16 (8a) To certify businesses eligible to participate in the North Carolina Small
17 Business Enterprise Program and to maintain a record of those businesses
18 certified.
19 …."
20 SECTION 37.3.(b) Article 4 of Chapter 147 of the General Statutes is amended by
21 adding a new section to read:
22 "§ 147-54.7B. North Carolina Small Business Enterprise Program; certification.
23 (a) The Department of the Secretary of State shall establish a certification program for
24 small businesses, which shall be known as the North Carolina Small Business Enterprise
25 Program. The purpose of the Program is to provide small businesses with access to State contracts
26 and educational opportunities and resources. To be certified as a small business, the business
27 shall:
28 (1) Be headquartered in this State.
29 (2) Have an annual net income that is less than one million five hundred thousand
30 dollars ($1,500,000), after the cost of goods sold is deducted.
31 (3) Have 100 or fewer employees.
32 (4) Be organized for profit.
33 (b) A small business certification shall be renewed every two years. The Department shall
34 not charge a fee for certification under this section.
35 (c) The Department shall publish a list of the small businesses certified under this section
36 on its website for use by State departments, agencies, and institutions; political subdivisions of
37 the State; and other interested persons. Only businesses certified in accordance with this section
38 shall be considered by State departments, agencies, and institutions and political subdivisions of
39 the State as small businesses for purposes of participation in State and public contracts under
40 Article 3 and Article 8 of Chapter 143 of the General Statutes; provided, however, nothing in this
41 section shall be construed to require the use of a small business in a State or public contract if the
42 small business is not the lowest responsible, responsive bidder or otherwise fails to meet the
43 requirements of the contract proposal.
44 (d) The Department may adopt rules to implement the provisions of this section in
45 accordance with Chapter 150B of the General Statutes."
46 SECTION 37.3.(c) The Department of Administration and the Office of the
47 Secretary of State shall enter into a memorandum of understanding which provides that the Office
48 shall provide to the Department the names of and other relevant information about small
49 businesses certified under G.S. 147-54.7B, as enacted in subsection (b) of this section, for
50 inclusion in the State's e-procurement service.
Page 352 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 SECTION 37.3.(d) Not later than 15 days after the date this act becomes law, the
2 Department of Administration shall provide to the Office of the Secretary of State the names of
3 and other relevant information about small businesses that were certified by the North Carolina
4 Small Business Enterprise Program prior to the date this act becomes law for inclusion on the
5 Office's website, as provided in G.S. 147-54.7B, as enacted in subsection (b) of this section.

26 SECTION 38.2.(b) Part 4 of Article 6 of Chapter 147 of the General Statutes, as
27 created and amended by Section 38.1 of this act, reads as rewritten:
28 "Part 4. North Carolina Investment Authority.
29 "§ 147-70.1. Creation of Investment Authority.
30 (a) Creation. – The North Carolina Investment Authority is created as a body corporate
31 and politic having the powers and jurisdiction as provided under this Article or any other law.
32 The Investment Authority is a State agency for the performance of essential governmental and
33 public functions. The Investment Authority is located within, but independent from the control
34 of, the Department of State Treasurer. The Investment Authority shall have perpetual succession.
35 (b) Independence. – The Investment Authority, in carrying out its statutory
36 responsibilities, shall be independent of any fiscal control exercised by the Director of the
37 Budget, the Department of Administration, and the Department of State Treasurer, including for
38 organizational, staffing, procurement, and budgetary purposes. Except as provided under
39 subsection (c) of this section and unless otherwise explicitly provided by law, the Investment
40 Authority is exempt from the State Budget Act, and the provisions of Chapter 143C of the
41 General Statutes do not apply to the Investment Authority.
42 (c) Fiduciary Funds. – In order for the Investment Authority to effectively operate the
43 investment programs under its management, all funds while under management of the Investment
44 Authority are Fiduciary Funds described under subdivisions (8) through (10) of G.S. 143C-1-3(a)
45 and shall be accounted for as specified in G.S. 147-69.3(f).
46 "§ 147-70.2. Powers and duties of the Investment Authority.
47 (a) In addition to the authority granted to the Investment Authority under this Article or
48 any other law, the Investment Authority shall have all of the powers necessary to execute the
49 provisions of this Part, including, at a minimum, the following powers:
50 (1) The right to sue and be sued.
Page 354 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (2) To take, demand, receive, and possess all kinds of real and personal property
2 necessary and proper for its purposes.
3 (3) To bargain, sell, grant, alienate, or dispose of all real and personal property as
4 it may lawfully acquire.
5 (b) The Investment Authority shall have the right to acquire fidelity bonds, fiduciary
6 insurance, directors' and officers' insurance, or errors and omissions coverage, as determined by
7 the Investment Authority board. This right is independent of any purchase of insurance by the
8 State Treasurer under G.S. 147-67.1.
9 (c) Pursuant to G.S. 143B-1320(b), the Investment Authority shall be exempt from the
10 provisions of Article 15 of Chapter 143B of the General Statutes.
11 "§ 147-70.3. Taxation of Investment Authority.
12 (a) Property owned or acquired by the Authority is exempt from all taxes imposed by the
13 State or any political subdivision of the State.
14 (b) The Investment Authority shall not be subject to State income taxes.
15 (c) This section shall not be construed to apply in any way to individual members of the
16 Board of Directors or any employee of the Investment Authority.
17 "§ 147-70.4. Confidentiality of Investment Authority records.
18 Any record or other information received or generated by the Investment Authority in order
19 to negotiate at arm's length investment transactions that constitute a trade secret, as defined in
20 G.S. 66-152, is not public record and is exempt from the requirements of Chapter 132 of the
21 General Statutes until the applicable negotiation is completed and unless the record or
22 information substantiates a conflict with the duties of the Investment Authority under
23 G.S. 147-70.6(a).
24 "§ 147-70.5. Criminal record checks.
25 (a) The Investment Authority may obtain from the State and National Repositories of
26 Criminal Histories or from any other lawful source the criminal history of any of the following
27 individuals:
28 (1) A current or prospective permanent or temporary employee of the Investment
29 Authority.
30 (2) A contractor with the Investment Authority.
31 (3) An employee or agent of a contractor with the Investment Authority who is
32 performing or will perform work for the Investment Authority.
33 (4) A volunteer of the Investment Authority.
34 (5) Any other individual otherwise engaged by the Investment Authority who will
35 have access to health or financial information or data maintained by the
36 Investment Authority that is confidential or otherwise nonpublic.
37 (b) The Investment Authority may deny employment to or dismiss any individual
38 identified under subdivisions (1), (2), (4), and (5) of subsection (a) of this section who refuses to
39 consent to a criminal history record check or to the use of fingerprints or other identifying
40 information required by the State or National Repositories of Criminal Histories. Any refusal
41 shall constitute just cause for the employment denial or the dismissal from employment.
42 (c) The Investment Authority may extend a conditional offer of employment pending the
43 results of a criminal history record check authorized by this section.
44 …
45 "§ 147-71.1. Board of Directors.
46 (a) Membership. – The Investment Authority shall be governed by a Board of Directors.
47 The Board of Directors shall consist of the following voting members:
48 (1) The State Treasurer, who shall serve as an ex officio member.
49 (2) One member appointed by the General Assembly upon the recommendation
50 of the Speaker of the House of Representatives in accordance with
51 G.S. 120-121.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 355

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (3) One member appointed by the General Assembly upon the recommendation
2 of the President Pro Tempore of the Senate in accordance with G.S. 120-121.
3 (4) One member appointed by the Governor, subject to confirmation by the
4 General Assembly by joint resolution.
5 (5) One member appointed by the State Treasurer, subject to confirmation by the
6 General Assembly by joint resolution.
7 (b) Terms. – The four appointive directors of the Board of Directors shall be appointed
8 for staggered six‑year terms, except for the initial term. The initial term of the director appointed
9 by the President Pro Tempore of the Senate is one year. The initial term of the director appointed
10 by the Speaker of the House of Representatives is two years. The initial term of the director
11 appointed by the State Treasurer is three years. The initial term of the director appointed by the
12 Governor is four years. An appointive director whose term has expired but whose qualified
13 successor has not been appointed shall continue to serve on the Board of Directors until a
14 qualified successor is duly appointed, including by the State Treasurer after a holdover period of
15 six months or more as provided for under subsection (e) of this section.
16 (c) Qualifications to Serve. – No appointed director of the Board of Directors shall hold
17 any other public office in North Carolina, except that an appointed director may also have
18 membership on either or both of the Boards of Trustees under G.S. 128-28 and G.S. 135-6. All
19 appointed members of the Board of Directors shall have expert knowledge of investments and a
20 minimum of a 10-year track record of successful management in pension, endowment, or other
21 relevant investment management fields.
22 (d) Disqualifications to Serve. – An individual is not eligible to serve on the Board of
23 Directors if any of the following apply to that individual:
24 (1) The individual has been indicted or charged with, been convicted of, pleaded
25 guilty or nolo contendere to, or forfeited bail concerning a felony, or a
26 misdemeanor involving fraud, theft, or dishonesty under the laws of any
27 jurisdiction in the United States.
28 (2) The individual has had a judgment entered against him or her by a court of
29 competent jurisdiction in a civil matter involving a breach of fiduciary duties.
30 (3) The individual has been the subject of an adverse action by the Securities and
31 Exchange Commission which resulted in any sanction, payment of a fine,
32 injunction, or other negative finding, whether individually or as a partner,
33 principal member, managing director, or other position of leadership of any
34 entity subject to the penalty or finding.
35 (4) The individual, or the individual's spouse or immediate family member, is or
36 becomes employed by the Department of State Treasurer or by a service
37 provider engaged to invest or assist in the oversight of assets overseen by the
38 Investment Authority.
39 (5) The individual, or the individual's spouse or immediate family member, is an
40 endorser, obligor, or provider of surety for, or is a borrower of, any money
41 loaned to or borrowed from the assets overseen by the Board of Directors.
42 (d1) Removal of Appointive Members. – A duly appointed member of the Board of
43 Directors may be removed by the applicable appointing authority for misfeasance, malfeasance,
44 or nonfeasance.
45 (e) Vacancies. – Any vacancy in a position held by an appointive member shall be filled
46 by a new appointment made by the applicable appointing authority for the vacant seat. If a seat
47 on the Board of Directors is vacant or held over for six months or more without an appointment
48 by the applicable appointing authority of an individual meeting the qualifications in this section,
49 then the State Treasurer may nominate a member for approval by the Board of Directors. Any
50 individual appointed to fill a vacancy shall serve only for the unexpired term. A vacancy
Page 356 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 automatically occurs upon the death or resignation of a member of the Board of Directors or upon
2 the failure of a member of the Board of Directors to do any of the following:
3 (1) Attend meetings for three consecutive meetings unless excused by majority
4 vote of the other Board of Directors members.
5 (2) Cure a conflict of interest within 30 days of identification of the conflict.
6 (3) Agree to abide by the ethics policy adopted by the Board of Directors.
7 (f) Reappointment. – Any member of the Board of Directors is eligible for
8 reappointment, except that no appointive member of the Board of Directors may serve for more
9 than two consecutive, full, six-year terms without at least a one-year break in membership on the
10 Board of Directors.
11 (g) Oath. – Each appointive member of the Board of Directors shall take an oath of office
12 to administer the duties of office faithfully and impartially, and a record of the oath shall be filed
13 in the office of the Secretary of State.
14 (h) Officers. – The following shall apply to officers of the Board of Directors:
15 (1) The State Treasurer shall serve as chair of the Board of Directors.
16 (2) The State Treasurer shall designate a vice-chair from among the remaining
17 members of the Board of Directors. The term of the vice-chair extends to the
18 earlier of either three years or the date of expiration of the vice-chair's then
19 current term as a member of the Board of Directors. In the absence of the State
20 Treasurer or the Treasurer's designee, the vice-chair shall preside over the
21 proceedings of the Board of Directors.
22 (3) The Board of Directors shall appoint and prescribe the duties of a secretary,
23 who need not be a member of the Board of Directors. The secretary is the
24 custodian of all books, documents, and papers filed with the Board of
25 Directors and the minute book or journal of the Board of Directors. The
26 secretary shall keep a record of the proceedings of the Board of Directors. The
27 secretary has the authority to make copies of all minutes and other records and
28 documents of the Board of Directors.
29 (i) Designees. – The State Treasurer is authorized to appoint a designee. No other
30 member of the Board of Directors is authorized to appoint a designee.
31 (j) Compensation and Reimbursement. – Members of the Board of Directors shall
32 receive no compensation for their services. For attendance at meetings of the Board of Directors
33 or any committee of the Board of Directors, and for other services for the Investment Authority,
34 members of the Board of Directors shall receive per diem, subsistence, and travel allowances in
35 accordance with G.S. 138-5 or G.S. 138-6, as appropriate.
36 (k) Meetings and Voting. – The Board of Directors shall meet at least quarterly. A
37 meeting may be called by the State Treasurer or by a majority of the Board of Directors. The
38 State Treasurer or the Treasurer's designee shall establish the agenda for each meeting. A
39 minimum of three members of the Board of Directors is required for quorum. The affirmative
40 vote of a majority of the members of the Board of Directors present at a meeting of the Board of
41 Directors that has been duly called and held is required for any action taken by the Investment
42 Authority, except that the State Treasurer's vote shall prevail in the event of a tied vote.
43 "§ 147-71.2. Duties of the Board of Directors.
44 (a) Investment-Related Powers and Duties. – The Board of Directors has all of the
45 following investment-related powers and duties:
46 (1) The Board of Directors has the authority to approve all of the following:
47 a. Investment policy statements to include investment objectives,
48 strategic asset allocation, and policy benchmarks.
49 b. Risk budgets, including related limits for key risk indicators.
50 c. The appointment of a master global custodian bank.
51 d. Annual operating budgets for investment programs.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 357

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 e. Market-oriented compensation plans.
2 (2) The Board of Directors shall periodically review all of the following:
3 a. Investment performance and investment manager appointment and
4 termination activities.
5 b. Investment strategies, policies, and tactical considerations.
6 c. Asset liability studies.
7 d. Performance benchmarks and key risk indicators.
8 e. Audited investment financial statements and audit reports pursuant to
9 G.S. 147-69.9.
10 f. Independent evaluation of governance, operations, and investment
11 practices.
12 g. Periodic cost-effectiveness studies of the investment programs.
13 (3) The Board of Directors shall appoint a Chief Investment Officer of the
14 Investment Authority.
15 (4) With respect to Retirement Systems' assets, at least biennially, the Board of
16 Directors shall approve an absolute risk operating range. The absolute risk
17 operating range shall be expressed in equity and debt allocation equivalency
18 terms and shall meet all of the following criteria:
19 a. The range is deemed appropriate in seeking to maximize long-term
20 returns.
21 b. The risk is not considered undue relative to other similarly situated
22 U.S. public pension funds. An assessment of compliance with this
23 requirement related to undue risk shall be construed in a manner
24 consistent with subsections (c) and (d) of G.S. 147-70.6.
25 c. In setting the range, the Board of Directors has taken into
26 consideration all of the factors affecting the funding of the Retirement
27 Systems and each of the Retirement Systems' ability to meet its
28 financial obligations.
29 (5) The Board of Directors shall utilize the approved absolute risk operating range
30 under subdivision (4) of this subsection to recommend investment return
31 assumptions to (i) the Board of Trustees of the Local Governmental
32 Retirement System, (ii) the Board of Trustees of the Teachers' and State
33 Employees' Retirement System, and (iii) the actuaries engaged to prepare
34 annual actuarial valuations.
35 (b) Annual Internal Budget. – The Board of Directors shall not approve an annual internal
36 budget for the Investment Authority that exceeds three basis points of a rolling three-year average
37 of total assets invested by the Investment Authority, unless the Investment Authority reasonably
38 determines that, because of special circumstances, including applicable investment restrictions,
39 it is clearly not prudent to do so. The annual internal budget includes expenditures directly
40 associated with services retained by the Investment Authority in accordance with subsection (c)
41 of this section and employee compensation and benefits. The Investment Authority's approved
42 annual internal budget as well as the Investment Authority's actual spending for the prior fiscal
43 year shall be annually reported to the Joint Legislative Commission on Governmental Operations,
44 the House of Representatives Appropriations Committee, the Senate Appropriations/Base
45 Budget Committee, and the Fiscal Research Division.
46 (c) Authority to Contract for Services. – Notwithstanding Article 3 of Chapter 143 of the
47 General Statutes, G.S. 114-2.3, and G.S. 147-17, the Investment Authority is authorized to
48 independently retain the services of appraisers, auditors, actuaries, attorneys, investment
49 consultants, statisticians, custodians, information technology professionals, or other persons or
50 firms possessing specialized skills or knowledge necessary for the proper administration of
51 investment programs created pursuant to this section.
Page 358 Senate Bill 257-Second Edition

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 (d) Setting of Compensation Plans. – In order to promote achievement of long-term
2 investment objectives and to retain key public employees with investment functions, the
3 Investment Authority is authorized to establish, consistent with the Investment Authority's
4 fiduciary duties, market-oriented compensation plans, including salaries and performance-related
5 bonuses, for employees possessing specialized skills or knowledge necessary for the proper
6 administration of investment programs. The design and administration of those compensation
7 plans shall be based on compensation studies conducted by a nationally recognized firm
8 specializing in public fund investment compensation. The compensation and other associated
9 employee benefits shall be apportioned directly from the investment program. The Investment
10 Authority shall report the salaries and bonuses paid to the Joint Legislative Oversight Committee
11 on General Government annually.
12 "§ 147-71.3. Liability of Board of Directors.
13 An individual serving on the Board of Directors shall be immune individually from civil
14 liability for monetary damages, except to the extent covered by insurance, for any act or failure
15 to act arising out of that service, except where any of the following apply:
16 (1) The individual was not acting within the scope of that individual's official
17 duties.
18 (2) The individual was not acting in good faith.
19 (3) The individual committed gross negligence or willful or wanton misconduct
20 that resulted in the damages or injury.
21 (4) The individual derived an improper personal financial benefit, either directly
22 or indirectly, from the transaction.
23 (5) The individual incurred the liability from the operation of a motor vehicle.
24 …
25 "§ 147-72.1. Chief Investment Officer.
26 (a) Principal Executive Officer. – The Chief Investment Officer is the Investment
27 Authority's principal executive officer and is responsible to the Board of Directors.
28 (b) Appointment and Term. – The CIO shall be appointed by a majority vote of the Board
29 of Directors, and any vacancy may be so filled by the Board of Directors. An individual appointed
30 as the CIO shall have expert knowledge of investments and a minimum of a 15-year track record
31 of successful management in pension, endowment, or other relevant investment management
32 arenas. The term of employment and compensation of the CIO is set by the Board of Directors,
33 except that each term of employment shall be limited to five years or less. The CIO is eligible
34 for multiple terms of employment without interruption. The CIO may be removed from office by
35 the Board of Directors.
36 (c) Employment of Staff. – The Chief Investment Officer shall employ staff necessary to
37 assist the CIO and the Board of Directors in carrying out duties and responsibilities under this
38 Article or as prescribed in any other law. Unless otherwise provided by law, Investment Authority
39 employees shall serve at the pleasure of the CIO and any vacancies in these positions may be
40 filled by the CIO. The CIO may designate managerial, professional, and policy-making positions
41 as exempt from the North Carolina Human Resources Act, in accordance with G.S. 126-5(c1).
42 Compensation of employees is set by the CIO within the limits set by the compensation plan
43 approved by the Board of Directors under G.S. 147-71.2.
44 (d) Contract Negotiation. – The CIO may negotiate, renegotiate, and execute contracts
45 with third parties in the performance of the CIO's duties and responsibilities under this Article.
46 Any delegation of authority by the Board of Directors shall require Board of Directors approval
47 and shall reserve certain strategic decisions and extraordinary investment decisions to the Board
48 of Directors. Contract execution with master global custodian banks and external auditors shall
49 be done only after approved by the Board of Directors.
50 …
51 "§ 147-73.2. Ethics policies.
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 359

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 To ensure that the State Treasurer's Investment Authority investment programs operate under
2 a strong governance framework with rigorous internal controls and a high degree of operational
3 transparency and are managed with the highest ethical and professional standards and in the most
4 efficient and effective manner possible, the State Treasurer, after consultation with the
5 Investment Advisory Committee, is authorized and required to Board of Directors shall adopt
6 policies and procedures on the following topics:
7 (1) Requiring that the Department of State Treasurer's Investment Management
8 Division Investment Authority adopt a code of ethics.
9 (2) Requiring all employees of the Department Investment Authority who have
10 responsibility for matters related to investments to be provided with training
11 with respect to the discharge of their duties and responsibilities to the funds.
12 (3) Governing gifts to employees of the Department Investment Authority who
13 have responsibility for matters related to investments.
14 (4) Imposing limitations on external investment managers' use of placement
15 agents and other persons that appear before the Department Investment
16 Authority to ensure that these persons play only a proper role in investment
17 opportunities.
18 (5) As a component of the investment due diligence, negotiations, and contracting
19 process, requiring an independent assessment of whether circumstances exist
20 that create a material risk that professional judgement or actions regarding a
21 potential investment arrangement's recommendation, approval, or execution
22 have been or will be unduly influenced by a direct or indirect personal
23 interest."
24 SECTION 38.2.(c) Rules, codes of ethics, policies, and procedures adopted by the
25 State Treasurer in effect on June 30, 2025, that are impacted by the change in authority from the
26 State Treasurer or Department of State Treasurer to the Investment Authority under this section
27 shall remain in effect until amended by law, amended by the Investment Authority, or repealed.
28 SECTION 38.2.(d) Before January 1, 2026, when the Investment Authority shall
29 begin to manage investments as provided under Section 38.3 of this act, funds appropriated to
30 the Department of State Treasurer and funds available to the Department of State Treasurer under
31 G.S. 147-69.3 may be used to pay any expenses of the Investment Authority.
32 SECTION 38.2.(e) G.S. 147-65.2, as created by Section 38.1(b) and Section 38.1(n)
33 of this act, reads as rewritten:
34 "§ 147-65.2. Salary of State Treasurer.Treasurer and certain Department employees.
35 (a) State Treasurer. – The salary of the State Treasurer shall be as established in the
36 Current Operations Appropriations Act. In addition to the salary set by the General Assembly in
37 the Current Operations Appropriations Act, longevity pay shall be paid on the same basis as is
38 provided to employees of the State who are subject to the North Carolina Human Resources Act.
39 (b) In order to promote achievement of long term investment objectives and to retain key

41 Authority shall consider all relevant material factors he or she considers
42 relevant to the decision consistent with the Treasurer's Investment Authority's
43 fiduciary duties under G.S. 147-69.7, G.S. 147-70.6, including financial,
44 operational, and investment expertise and resources, alignment of interests
45 and investor protections, transparency and repeatability of investment process,
46 risk controls, and cost-effectiveness.
47 (3) For any third-party investment management arrangements, the investment
48 manager must have total assets under management of at least one hundred
49 million dollars ($100,000,000) at the inception of the investment management
50 arrangement with the State Treasurer.

26 "§ 147-69.2A. Investments; special funds held by the State Treasurer.
27 (a) Firm to Administer Special Fund. – Following a public procurement process, a
28 designee of the Governor, a designee of the State Treasurer, a designee of the Speaker of the
29 House of Representatives, and a designee of the President Pro Tempore of the Senate shall jointly
30 and unanimously Venture Capital Multiplier Fund. – The Investment Authority may select a
31 third-party professional investment management firm, subject to the rules and regulations of the
32 U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, to administer a special fund created to invest assets
33 described in G.S. 147-69.2(b)(12)c. of the Escheats Fund and select investment opportunities
34 appropriate for receiving allocations from the Venture Capital Multiplier Fund on the basis of
35 potential return on investment and the risks attendant thereto. The State Treasurer Investment
36 Authority shall assign professional and clerical staff to assist in the oversight of the Venture
37 Capital Multiplier Fund. All costs for the third-party investment management firm and the
38 professional and clerical staff shall be borne by the Venture Capital Multiplier Fund pursuant to
39 G.S. 147-69.3(f). The State Treasurer Investment Authority shall discharge his or her its duties
40 with respect to the Venture Capital Multiplier Fund as a fiduciary consistent with
41 G.S. 147-69.7.G.S. 147-70.6.

43 concerning the Fund are public records governed by Chapter 132 of the General Statutes and any
44 applicable provisions of the General Statutes protecting confidential information.

48 of interests such that (i) the designees of the State Treasurer and Governor who selected the
49 third-party investment management firm, (ii) the staff of the State Treasurer overseeing the Fund,
50 and (iii) the third-party investment management firm's employees selecting or overseeing Fund
51 investments do not provide services for compensation (as an employee, consultant, or otherwise),

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 within two years after the end of their service to the Fund, to any entity in which an investment
2 from the Fund was made.
3 (b1) Conflict of Interest Policy. – The Investment Authority shall adopt a policy to prevent
4 conflicts of interest. This policy shall include a provision prohibiting all of the following
5 individuals from providing services for compensation to any entity in which an investment from
6 the Venture Capital Multiplier Fund was made within two years after the end of that individual's
7 service to the Fund:

38 "§ 147-69.2E. Investments in digital assets.
39 (a) The following definitions apply in this section:
40 (1) Designated funds. – Any of the funds described in G.S. 147-69.1(b) and
41 G.S. 147-69.2(a).
42 (2) Digital asset. – A virtual currency, cryptocurrency, native electronic asset,
43 stablecoin, nonfungible token, or any other asset that is only digital and that
44 confers economic, proprietary, or access rights or powers.
45 (3) Private key. – A unique element of cryptographic data used for signing
46 transactions on a blockchain that is known to the owner of the unique element.
47 (4) Secure custody solution. – A technological product or a blended product and
48 service that employs advanced security measures to safeguard private keys
49 and prevent unauthorized access.
50 (b) The Investment Authority may invest the cash of the designated funds in digital assets
51 only after approval by the Board of Directors. The approval shall be based on an independent
Senate Bill 257-Second Edition Page 371

General Assembly Of North Carolina Session 2025
1 assessment by a third-party consultant that all of the following requirements for proposed
2 investments have been met:
3 (1) The digital assets are maintained with a secure custody solution.
4 (2) The risk and reward profile, including under adverse scenarios, is appropriate
5 for the designated fund's circumstances from a total portfolio perspective.
6 (3) The control environment meets institutional investment industry requirements
7 for independent risk and compliance oversight, operational robustness, and
8 regulatory compliance.
9 (c) An investment in digital assets from any of the designated funds shall not exceed, in
10 the aggregate, five percent (5%) of the balance of the designated fund.

4 "§ 147-69.5. Local Government Law Enforcement Special Separation Allowance Fund.
5 The Local Government Law Enforcement Special Separation Allowance Fund is established
6 as a fund in the Office of the State Treasurer under the management of the Treasurer. Investment
7 Authority. The Fund consists of contributions made by entities authorized to make contributions
8 to the Fund and interest and other investment income earned by the Fund. Contributions to the
9 Fund are irrevocable. Assets of the Fund may be used only to provide law enforcement special
10 separation allowance benefits to individuals who are former employees of a unit of local
11 government that contributes to the Fund and are entitled to law enforcement special separation
12 allowance payable by the unit. The assets of the Fund are not subject to the claims of creditors
13 of an entity that contributes to the Fund.

28 "§ 147-69.8. Annual report on new investment authority.
29 Whenever the General Assembly broadens the investment authority of the State Treasurer
30 Investment Authority as to the General Fund, the Teachers' and State Employees' Retirement
31 System, the Consolidated Judicial Retirement System, the Firefighters' and Rescue Squad
32 Workers' Pension Fund, the Local Governmental Employees' Retirement System, the Legislative
33 Retirement System, the North Carolina National Guard Pension Fund, or any idle funds, the State
34 Treasurer Investment Authority shall annually report in detail to the General Assembly the
35 investments made under such new authority, including the returns on those investments, earnings,
36 changes to value, and gains and losses in disposition of such investments. The report shall be
37 made no later than the first six months of each calendar year, covering performance in the prior
38 fiscal year. As to each type of new investment authority, the report shall be made for at least four
39 years. To the extent the information required by this section is also required in the reports under
40 G.S. 147-69.12, the State Treasurer Investment Authority may combine reports or make
41 cross-reference to those reports.

6 SECTION 38.3.(c) G.S. 147-70.6, as created by Section 38.1 of this act, reads as
7 rewritten:
8 "§ 147-70.6. Discharge of duties to funds.
9 (a) The State Treasurer Investment Authority, including the Board of Directors, shall
10 discharge his or her all duties with respect to each fund or investment program held by the
11 Investment Authority to the credit of the State Treasurer, including each of the funds, funds
12 enumerated in G.S. 147-69.2 as follows:G.S. 147-69.1 and G.S. 147-69.2, in all of the following
13 manners:
14 (1) Solely in the interest of the intended beneficiaries of the fund, if any.
15 (2) For the exclusive purpose of carrying out the purpose of the fund, including
16 providing benefits to participants and beneficiaries, and paying reasonable
17 expenses of administering the fund.
18 (3) With the care, skill, and caution that a prudent investor would use after
19 considering the purposes, distribution requirements, and other circumstances
20 then prevailing.
21 (4) Impartially, taking into account any differing interests of participants and
22 beneficiaries.
23 (5) Incurring only costs that are appropriate and reasonable.
24 (6) In accordance with a good-faith interpretation of the provisions of
25 G.S. 147-69.2 and any other applicable law governing the fund.
26 (b) In investing and managing assets of the any fund or investment program pursuant to
27 subsection (a) of this section, the State Treasurer:Investment Authority shall do all of the
28 following:
29 (1) Shall consider Consider all of the following circumstances:
30 a. General economic conditions.
31 b. The possible effect of inflation or deflation.
32 c. The role that each investment or course of action plays within the
33 overall portfolio of the fund.
34 d. The expected total return from income and the appreciation of capital.
35 e. Needs for liquidity, regularity of income, and preservation or
36 appreciation of capital.
37 f. With respect to the Retirement Systems defined in G.S. 147-69.2(b)(8)
38 and any other pension plans, the adequacy of funding for the
39 Retirement Systems or other pension plan based on reasonable
40 actuarial factors.
41 g. The purpose of the fund, if established.
42 (2) Shall diversify Diversify the investments of the fund fund, unless the State
43 Treasurer Investment Authority reasonably determines that, because of
44 special circumstances, including applicable investment restrictions, it is
45 clearly prudent not to do so.
46 (3) Shall make Make a reasonable effort to verify facts relevant to the investment
47 and management of assets of the funds.
48 (4) Shall invest only in those investments authorized by law consistent with the

35 BROADBAND FUND FLEXIBILITY
36 SECTION 40.1.(a) G.S. 143B-1373.2 is repealed.
37 SECTION 40.1.(b) G.S. 143B-1374 is repealed.
38 SECTION 40.1.(c) The Department of Information Technology shall use funds
39 appropriated for the Growing Rural Economies with Access to Technology program for fixed
40 wireless and satellite broadband grants, established in G.S. 143B-1373.2 to award grants to
41 eligible entities to purchase installation materials for satellite internet service. Installation
42 materials must be for the grantee's own use and not for distribution to other parties. The
43 Department shall prioritize grant applicants that will deploy installation materials in one of the
44 39 counties designated as a disaster area due to Hurricane Helene. The Department may also give
45 priority to grantees that offer emergency services, disaster relief, educational services, or
46 economic development.
47 SECTION 40.1.(d) For the purposes of this section, an eligible entity is one of the

51 (3) A volunteer fire department.

9 estimate and shall be completed within a reasonable time frame mutually agreed to by the

12 are filed within 90 days of one another. A pole owner may deviate from the time limits specified

20 whose salary is set by Part VII-A of this act or by this Part, pursuant to the North Carolina Human

25 required to implement these bonuses shall revert to the General Fund. The compensation bonuses
26 awarded by this section are not compensation for the purposes of any retirement system
27 administered by the State. The compensation bonuses awarded by this section are not part of
28 annual salary and shall be paid out separately. The compensation bonus shall be awarded to
29 eligible State-funded permanent employees without regard to an employee's placement within
30 the salary range, including employees at the top of the salary range. The compensation bonus
31 shall be adjusted pro rata for permanent part-time employees.
